title: The Rain That Brought Her
content:
The rain came down like the sky had split open.

Xu Ming looked up from his mortar and pestle, the rhythm of grinding ginseng interrupted by the sudden roar against the tile roof. Water sluiced down the windows of the old pharmacy, turning the afternoon into dusk. He wiped his hands on his apron and went to flip the sign from OPEN to CLOSED.

No one would come in this weather. The last customer had left an hour ago, clutching a paper-wrapped bundle of astragalus root for her mother's weak lungs.

The bell above the door chimed.

Xu Ming froze, hand still on the sign.

She stood in the doorway, water streaming from the hem of her white dress, from the tips of her long black hair. The rain had soaked through everything — the silk clung to her like a second skin, revealing the sharp line of her collarbones, the curve of her waist. She didn't seem to notice. Or care.

She stepped inside, and the pharmacy felt smaller.

"I need herbs," she said.

Her voice was low, smooth. The kind of voice that belonged in a tea house at midnight, not a traditional medicine shop on a rainy Tuesday afternoon.

Xu Ming found his voice. "We're about to close."

"Then open again."

She walked past him, leaving wet footprints on the wooden floor. Her bare feet made no sound. He noticed she wasn't wearing shoes — had she walked through the storm like that? The rain had washed away any scent of perfume, but something else lingered. Jasmine. Wet earth. Something older.

She stopped at the counter and turned to face him.

Xu Ming was not a man given to poetry. He was thirty-two years old, had spent fifteen years learning the properties of herbs and the pulse points of the human body, and his idea of a good evening was a pot of oolong tea and a medical text from the Ming dynasty. He had never believed in love at first sight.

But standing in the dim light of his shop, with rain still dripping from her chin, she was the most beautiful thing he had ever seen.

"What do you need?" he asked, surprising himself with how steady his voice was.

"Century ginseng root."

The words hung in the air.

Xu Ming blinked. "I'm sorry?"

"Century ginseng root. Panax ginseng that has grown for one hundred years. Dried and prepared according to the methods of the Song dynasty."

He almost laughed. "That doesn't exist."

"It does."

"Miss, century ginseng hasn't been available in the legitimate market for eight hundred years. The last confirmed harvest was — "

"I know when it was."

She held his gaze. Her eyes were strange — pale gold in the center, darkening to brown at the edges. Like amber. Like something that had been watching the world for a very long time.

"I have one," Xu Ming said.

The words came out before he could stop them.

He didn't know why he said it. The ginseng root wasn't for sale. It was locked in the steel cabinet behind the counter, wrapped in silk and stored in a wooden box lined with lead. It had been in his family for four generations. His great-grandfather had bought it from a monk who claimed to have found it in the mountains of Changbai, growing in the shadow of a waterfall that only appeared during the fifth moon.

Xu Ming had always thought it was a family legend. A story to tell at New Year dinners.

But the root was real. He'd held it once, when he was eighteen and his father had shown him the family's greatest treasure. It looked like a man — arms, legs, a gnarled face that seemed to shift in the candlelight.

"What would you offer for it?" he asked.

The woman smiled. It was a small thing, barely a curve of her lips, but it changed her face entirely. She looked younger. Hungrier.

"How much do you want?"

"Money doesn't matter."

"What matters, then?"

He didn't know. He only knew that he couldn't let her leave. That if she walked out that door, the rain would swallow her, and he would spend the rest of his life wondering if she had been real.

"Tell me why you need it," he said.

She tilted her head. Water dripped from her hair onto the counter.

"I'm dying," she said.

Xu Ming's medical training kicked in before his heart could react. He scanned her — pale skin, dark circles under her eyes, the way her fingers trembled slightly as she gripped the edge of the counter. She was thin. Too thin. The dress hung loose on her frame.

"What's your diagnosis?"

"Does it matter?"

"I'm a pharmacist. I need to know what I'm treating."

"You're a pharmacist. You sell plants. You don't treat anything."

The words stung because they were true. He compounded prescriptions, weighed out herbs, gave advice on dosages. But he wasn't a doctor. He couldn't diagnose.

Still.

"Let me take your pulse."

She stared at him for a long moment. The rain hammered against the roof. Somewhere down the street, a car horn blared.

"Fine."

She held out her arm.

Xu Ming's fingers touched her wrist. Her skin was cold — not the cold of rain, but the cold of winter. Of something that had never been warm. He pressed down, searching for her pulse.

It took him thirty seconds to find it.

One beat. Then nothing. Then another beat, thirty seconds later.

He looked up at her.

"What are you?"

"Give me the root," she said, "and I'll tell you."

Xu Ming should have refused. Should have called the police, or a hospital, or an exorcist. His hand was still on her wrist, feeling that impossible heartbeat, and every rational part of his brain was screaming at him to let go.

But her eyes were amber in the dim light, and her skin was cold, and she smelled like jasmine and rain and something ancient.

He let go of her wrist.

Then he unlocked the steel cabinet.

The box was where it had always been — wrapped in faded red silk, tied with a cord of braided horsehair. He carried it to the counter and set it down between them.

"Open it," she said.

He untied the cord. Unfolded the silk.

The ginseng root lay in the box like a sleeping child.

It was larger than he remembered. Nearly two feet long, the body twisted and gnarled into the shape of a man. The face was clearer now — eyes closed, mouth slightly open, as if it was about to speak. The roots that should have been thin and fibrous were thick, almost muscular. Fingers. Toes.

Xu Ming had held this root once, fourteen years ago. It had felt like wood. Dry and dead.

Now it felt like something was breathing inside it.

The woman reached out and touched it.

"Beautiful," she whispered.

"Who are you?"

She looked up at him, and for a moment, her eyes flickered. The amber turned to gold, and the gold turned to something that wasn't human. Vertical pupils. A slit where a pupil should be.

"I am Bai Suyin," she said. "And I have been looking for this for three hundred years."

Xu Ming's hand moved to the box, to close it, to take it back.

But her hand was faster.

She grabbed his wrist. Her fingers were cold and strong, and he felt something pass between them — a jolt, like static electricity, but deeper. It went through his skin, his muscle, his bone, and settled somewhere in his chest.

"Don't be afraid," she said.

"I'm not afraid."

"Liar."

She smiled again, and this time it reached her eyes. The gold faded, and she looked human again. Beautiful. Dying.

"Take it," he said.

The words came out of him like they belonged to someone else.

"Take the ginseng. It's yours."

Bai Suyin's smile faded.

"Why?"

"Because you need it."

"That's not a reason."

"It's the only one I have."

She studied him for a long moment. The rain had slowed outside, settling into a steady rhythm. Water dripped from the eaves. The pharmacy smelled of wet wood and herbs and her.

"You don't know what I am," she said.

"No."

"You don't know what I'll become."

"No."

"Then why?"

Xu Ming thought about it. Really thought about it. He was a practical man. A careful man. He had never done anything reckless in his life.

But when he looked at her, he felt like he was standing on the edge of something vast. Something that had been waiting for him.

"Because I want to find out," he said.

Bai Suyin picked up the ginseng root. She held it like it was alive, like it might wake up and speak to her. She closed her eyes and pressed it against her chest.

When she opened them again, the gold was back. Brighter this time.

"You'll regret this," she said.

"Probably."

"I'm not a good person."

"Neither am I."

"You're the best person I've met in a hundred years."

She said it like it was a curse.

Then she turned and walked toward the door. Her bare feet made no sound on the wooden floor. The rain had stopped, and the sun was breaking through the clouds, casting long shadows across the street.

She paused at the threshold.

"My trial," she said, without turning around. "It's you."

"What?"

But she was already gone.

Xu Ming stood in the empty pharmacy, the steel cabinet open, the silk wrapper lying empty on the counter. The bell above the door was still. The rain had stopped completely.

He looked down at his wrist, where her fingers had touched him.

There was a mark there. White. Pale against his skin. It looked like a scale.

No.

It looked like a bite.

title: The Taste of Smoke
content:
Xu Ming didn't sleep that night.

He lay in his narrow bed above the pharmacy, staring at the water stain on the ceiling that looked like a map of some forgotten country. The woman's face burned behind his eyelids. Her voice, low and smooth as river stones, kept repeating in his skull.

*I'll be back tomorrow.*

He'd said she could. He'd said it like a fool.

At 3 AM, he gave up on sleep and went downstairs. The shop smelled of ginseng and dried chrysanthemum, the familiar scents that had been his entire world for six years. His father had run this place before him. His grandfather too. Three generations of Xu men, grinding herbs and wrapping packages, never once encountering a woman who asked for thousand-year-old formulas.

He pulled out the medical texts. Not the ones on the shelf for customers, but the leather-bound books his grandfather had kept locked in the cabinet beneath the counter. The characters were faded, written in a hand that predated the Communist era.

*On the Transformation of Serpents.*

His grandfather had never mentioned this book. Had never mentioned any of this.

Xu Ming turned the brittle pages by the light of his phone. The text described symptoms he'd never seen in any modern medical journal — skin that shimmered in moonlight, eyes that changed color with emotion, a pulse that beat twice for every human heartbeat. The treatments were wild. Teas of ghost mushrooms. Powders made from crushed jade. Bloodletting at specific lunar phases.

He read until the sky turned gray.

The woman came back at noon.

Xu Ming heard the bell above the door before he saw her. He'd been standing behind the counter for two hours, heart hammering at every customer who walked in. Old Mrs. Chen for her arthritis medicine. A young mother for pediatric cough syrup. A delivery man dropping off boxes of angelica root.

Then the bell rang, and the air changed.

She wore a white dress today, simple and elegant, with a pale blue cardigan that made her look almost ordinary. Her hair was pinned up, revealing the long line of her neck. A silver pendant rested at her collarbone — a snake eating its own tail.

"You came back," Xu Ming said. Stupid. Of course she came back.

"Did you think I wouldn't?" She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. Up close, he noticed things he'd missed yesterday. The way she held perfectly still between movements, like a predator conserving energy. The faint scent of lotus flowers and something older, something that reminded him of his grandfather's incense burner.

"I looked at the book you mentioned." He kept his voice steady. "The one about transformation."

Her smile vanished. "You read it."

"My grandfather had it locked away."

"He was wise." She stepped closer, and Xu Ming forced himself not to step back. "What else did he have locked away, Xu Ming?"

The way she said his name made his skin prickle. Like she already knew everything about him. Like she'd known him for longer than two days.

"Why do you need the formula?" he asked.

"Because I want to live."

"You're alive now."

She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Am I? Tell me, pharmacist. When was the last time you felt truly alive? When your heart raced so fast you thought it would burst? When the world went sharp and clear and every second mattered?"

Xu Ming thought about his days. The slow grind of measuring herbs. The quiet evenings watching TV in his room. The only time his heart had raced in years was when he'd seen her yesterday.

"I'm alive enough," he said.

"No. You're waiting." She leaned forward, and the lotus scent grew stronger. "You've been waiting your whole life without knowing what you're waiting for. I know that feeling better than anyone."

"Because you've been waiting a thousand years?"

Silence.

The bell above the door rang, and a customer walked in — a man in his fifties, looking for cold medicine. Xu Ming served him mechanically, hands moving through the familiar motions while his mind raced. The woman waited by the window, watching the street with an expression he couldn't read.

When the customer left, she spoke without turning around.

"The formula. Do you have it?"

"I can make it." The words came out before he could stop them. "But I need to know what I'm making it for."

She turned. Her eyes caught the light, and for just a moment, he saw something flicker in them. Something that wasn't human. His heart slammed against his ribs.

"For a thousand years, I've cultivated," she said. "Meditated in caves beneath mountains. Swam in waters so deep the sun never reaches. Learned the ways of humans by watching them live and die in the span of my seasons." She touched the pendant at her throat. "I've done everything a snake can do to shed this form. But there's one thing I cannot do alone."

"What?"

"Love." She said it like a curse. "And be loved in return. A mortal heart, freely given. That's the last step. The heart trial."

Xu Ming felt like the floor had dropped away beneath him. "You want me to..."

"Fall in love with me, Xu Ming. Yes."

The absurdity of it hit him like a physical blow. A woman walks into his pharmacy, asks for ancient herbs, and now she wants his heart? He almost laughed. Almost.

"Why me?" he asked instead.

"Because you can see me." She stepped closer. "Most humans look at me and see a pretty woman. They feel attraction, desire, curiosity. But they don't *see*. You do. You saw something in me yesterday that made you look twice. Made you look past the surface."

"Maybe I just thought you were beautiful."

"Many people think I'm beautiful. That's not the same." She was close enough now that he could see the faint pattern on her skin, almost invisible, like scales beneath the surface. "You saw the truth of me. You just don't know it yet."

Xu Ming's hands shook. He gripped the counter to steady himself. "This is insane."

"Yes."

"Impossible."

"Also yes."

"And if I say no?"

She was silent for a long moment. The pendant caught the light again, the snake eating its own tail, endless cycle, no beginning, no end.

"Then I find someone else," she said quietly. "And I forget I ever met you."

The words should have been a relief. Instead, they hit him like a punch to the chest. He didn't want her to forget him. He didn't want to go back to the gray, quiet days of measuring herbs and watching TV alone.

"One date," he heard himself say. "One date, and I'll think about the formula."

Her eyes widened. For the first time, she looked genuinely surprised. "A date?"

"You said you understand humans. Prove it. Take me somewhere. Show me you're not just a beautiful face with impossible stories." He didn't know where the boldness came from. Some deep part of him that had been sleeping for years, finally waking up. "One date. Then I decide."

She studied him like she was reading a book written in a language she barely understood. "You're braver than you look."

"Or stupider."

"Maybe both." She smiled, and this time it reached her eyes. The shift in her expression made her look younger. More human. "Tomorrow night. Wear something nice."

Then she was gone, the door swinging shut behind her, leaving Xu Ming alone with the scent of lotus flowers and his own racing heart.

---

He spent the next day in a daze.

Every customer who walked through the door made him jump. Every ring of the phone made him reach for it, expecting her voice. He mixed the wrong herbs twice, had to throw out a thousand yuan worth of medicine because his hands wouldn't stop shaking.

At 7 PM, he closed the shop early for the first time in his life.

His wardrobe was pathetic. Three polo shirts, two button-ups for funerals and weddings, a jacket he'd bought five years ago. He chose the best combination — dark jeans, a white button-up, the jacket — and stared at himself in the mirror.

He looked like a pharmacist going to a dinner party. Which was exactly what he was.

The date was set for 8 PM at a restaurant near West Lake. She'd texted him the address from a number he didn't recognize. *Bai Suyin*, the contact name said. He'd saved it with trembling fingers.

At 7:45, he locked the pharmacy and stepped onto the street.

The night air was cool, carrying the smell of water and exhaust fumes. Hangzhou never truly got dark — the city lights painted everything in shades of orange and blue. He walked toward the lake, trying to calm his breathing.

What was he doing? Going on a date with a woman who claimed to be a snake spirit. A thousand-year-old serpent who needed his love to become human. It was insane. Delusional. The plot of a bad web novel.

But when he saw her waiting by the restaurant entrance, all his doubts evaporated.

She wore red tonight. A dress that caught the light like water, with traditional dragon patterns embroidered in gold thread. Her hair was down, falling past her shoulders in waves that seemed to move on their own. When she saw him, she smiled.

"You came."

"I said I would."

"I know." She stepped closer, and he caught the lotus scent again. "But most humans say things they don't mean."

"I'm not most humans."

"No." She looked at him, and there was something ancient in her gaze. Something that made him feel like a child standing before a mountain. "You're not."

The restaurant was elegant — white tablecloths, candles, a view of the lake through floor-to-ceiling windows. She ordered without looking at the menu, speaking Mandarin so perfect it sounded like poetry. Xu Ming ordered blindly, too nervous to read the characters.

They talked. About his work. About her travels — though she was careful with her words, never saying anything that couldn't be explained away. She was charming. Warm. Every time she laughed, Xu Ming felt something crack open inside him.

Halfway through dinner, she reached across the table and touched his hand.

Her skin was cool. Not cold, like death, but cool like river water on a summer day. He looked down at her fingers resting on his, then up at her face.

"Thank you," she said softly.

"For what?"

"For giving me a chance." She squeezed his hand. "Most humans run."

"Maybe I should."

"But you won't." Her eyes held his. "Because you feel it too. This connection. Like we've known each other before."

He did feel it. That was the terrifying part. Sitting across from her, holding her hand, he felt like he was coming home to a place he'd never been.

"How is this possible?" he whispered.

"Nothing is impossible." She leaned closer. "That's the first thing you need to understand about my world, Xu Ming. There are no rules. Only choices."

Their food arrived. They ate, and talked, and laughed, and Xu Ming felt himself falling deeper with every passing minute. She told him about mountains she'd climbed, rivers she'd crossed, sunrises she'd watched from peaks so high the air was thin enough to kill a human. She never said how she'd survived those places. She didn't need to.

After dinner, they walked by the lake.

The water was dark, reflecting the lights of the city like scattered jewels. Tourists milled about, couples holding hands, families taking photos. Xu Ming and Bai Suyin walked in silence, her arm linked through his.

"I should tell you something," she said.

"Another secret?"

"A warning." She stopped

title: The Scales Beneath Her Skin
content:
The rain came again that night.

Xu Ming lay awake in his narrow bed above the shop, listening to it drum against the tile roof. The sound was wrong — too rhythmic, too deliberate. Each drop struck like a finger tapping glass.

He'd closed the shop at six. Cleaned the mortar and pestle. Restocked the ginseng drawer. Normal things. Human things.

But his hands still remembered her wrist.

The pulse that shouldn't exist. The cold that wasn't death-cold but something else. Like touching a river in winter and finding it flowing.

He sat up. The clock on his nightstand read 2:47 AM.

The shop was silent. The rain had stopped.

Xu Ming swung his legs over the edge of the bed and pressed his feet to the cold floor. The wood groaned beneath him — old, familiar, trustworthy. He'd lived in this room since he was nineteen, apprenticing under Old Chen. The walls knew his shape. The ceiling knew his ceiling.

Something tapped at the window.

He turned.

A moth pressed against the glass. White. The size of his palm. Its wings beat once, twice, then stilled.

Xu Ming didn't move.

The moth's body was wrong. Too long. Too segmented. And its eyes — he could see them even in the dark — were not insect eyes. They were human. Black and wet and watching.

He blinked.

The moth was gone.

The window showed only his reflection. Pale. Tired. A man who needed sleep and herbs and the simple mathematics of prescriptions.

He went back to bed.

But he didn't sleep.

---

Morning came gray and damp.

Xu Ming descended the stairs at six, same as always. The shop smelled of wood and medicine and the particular must of old Hangzhou, where the river breathed through every wall. He opened the shutters. Unlocked the door. Swept the threshold.

A man stood at the counter.

Xu Ming stopped mid-sweep. "I didn't hear you come in."

The man smiled. He was old — seventy, maybe eighty — with a shaved head and robes of coarse brown cloth. A Buddhist monk. But not the kind Xu Ming saw at the temples by West Lake, the ones with smartphones and polite donation boxes.

This monk's robes were patched. His sandals were mud-caked. And his eyes — pale, almost colorless — held something that made Xu Ming's throat tighten.

"You are Xu Ming," the monk said. Not a question.

"Can I help you?"

"I need herbs." The monk placed a slip of paper on the counter. His fingers were long. The nails were clean but yellowed. "These specific ones."

Xu Ming picked up the paper.

The handwriting was ancient — not Chinese characters but something older, more angular. He recognized it because Old Chen had taught him, had made him memorize the old scripts for the old remedies.

The list was impossible.

Black cohosh from a grave that faced north. Snake's tongue fern picked under a waning moon. Red peony root dried in the belly of a dead bird.

Xu Ming set the paper down. "I don't have these."

"You do not need to have them." The monk's voice was soft, almost kind. "You only need to know who asked for them."

A beat of silence.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

The monk's smile didn't change, but something in his eyes shifted. Like clouds passing over a winter sun. "The woman who came yesterday. The one with the white dress and the old hunger. She asked for herbs as well, didn't she?"

Xu Ming's hand tightened on the broom.

"She asked for thousand-year lichen. For silver-stemmed lotus. For the blood of a virgin rooster." The monk's voice dropped. "She is not what she appears to be, young pharmacist."

"She's a customer."

"She is a serpent."

The word hung in the air. Heavy. Old. Wrong.

Xu Ming forced himself to breathe. "I think you should leave."

"I have hunted her for thirty lifetimes." The monk didn't move. "I have seen her shed skins across dynasties. I have watched her crawl up from the mud of what was once a fishing village, learning to walk on two legs, learning to speak, learning to mimic a soul. She is clever. She is patient. And she has chosen you."

"Chosen me for what?"

The monk's smile vanished. "Her heart trial."

The words meant nothing. Everything. Xu Ming felt them settle into his bones like cold water.

"She must love a mortal to complete her transformation," the monk continued. "She must make you love her. And when she succeeds, when she breaks through the final barrier, she will become something that should not exist. A snake that walks as a god."

"Get out."

"Her name is not human. Her name is Bai Suyin — White Serpent's Shadow. She was old when your ancestors were painting their bodies with mud. She will be old when your descendants are dust."

Xu Ming stepped forward. His hands were shaking. "I said get out."

The monk studied him for a long moment. Then he reached into his robe and pulled out a small cloth pouch. He set it on the counter.

"Tie this to your bedpost tonight. If she comes, you will see her true face."

Then he turned and walked out into the gray morning.

The door swung shut.

Xu Ming stood alone in the shop. The pouch sat on the counter like a live thing. He didn't touch it.

But he didn't throw it away either.

---

At noon, she came back.

The bell above the door chimed. Xu Ming looked up from the ledger he'd been staring at for two hours without reading a single word.

Bai Suyin stood in the doorway.

Today she wore a pale green dress that matched the rain-washed sky. Her hair was loose, falling in dark waves past her shoulders. She looked younger than yesterday. Softer. The sharp edges of her beauty had blurred into something almost ordinary.

Almost.

"Good morning," she said.

"You came yesterday."

"I did." She walked to the counter. Her movements were smooth, unhurried. "I realized I forgot something."

"What?"

She reached into her pocket and pulled out a small jade pendant. It was carved in the shape of a coiled snake, its scales catching the light.

"This is for you."

Xu Ming looked at the pendant. Then at her face. "Why?"

"Because you helped me." She smiled — a small, careful smile. "And because I want you to have it."

"I don't take gifts from customers."

"It's not a gift." She set the pendant on the counter. "It's payment. For the herbs yesterday. And for future herbs I might need."

He should refuse. He knew he should refuse. The monk's words were still burning in his skull. Serpent. Thirty lifetimes. Heart trial.

But her eyes were brown, not black. Her skin was warm, not cold. And when she smiled, she looked like a woman, just a woman, standing in a quiet pharmacy on a gray Hangzhou morning.

Xu Ming picked up the pendant.

The jade was warm. Warmer than it should have been. And when he held it, he felt something — a pulse, a breath, a heartbeat that wasn't his own.

"What are you?" he asked.

Bai Suyin's smile faltered. Just for a moment. Then it returned, smooth and practiced.

"I'm someone who needs your help."

"Help with what?"

She was quiet for a long time. The shop's clock ticked. The rain began again, soft against the window.

"Help becoming human," she said.

The words landed like stones.

Xu Ming stared at her. Waited for the punchline. The laugh. The explanation that made sense.

She didn't laugh.

"You're serious."

"Yes."

"You're telling me you're not human."

"I'm telling you I want to be."

He set the pendant down. His hand was steady, but his voice wasn't. "A monk came this morning. He told me you were a serpent. That you've been alive for a thousand years. That you're using me for something called a heart trial."

Bai Suyin's face went still. Not surprised. Not alarmed. Still.

"What else did he tell you?"

"That he's hunted you for thirty lifetimes."

"He has." She looked down at her hands. "And he will keep hunting me until one of us dies. That is the nature of our... arrangement."

"Arrangement?"

"He was my teacher. Once. A long time ago." Her voice was flat. Empty. "I was a snake who had awakened to cultivation. He was a monk who believed all non-humans were evil. He tried to kill me. I escaped. The chase became... everything."

Xu Ming leaned against the counter. His legs felt weak. "This is insane."

"Yes."

"None of this is real."

"It is real." She looked up, and her eyes were wet. "I am real. Whatever else I am, whatever I was, I am real."

"Then prove it."

She didn't move.

"Show me," he said. "Show me what you are."

Bai Suyin held his gaze. Then she reached up and unbuttoned the first button of her dress.

Xu Ming stepped back. "What are you—"

"You asked to see."

She unbuttoned the second button. The third. Then she pulled the fabric aside, just enough to expose her collarbone.

He saw skin. Smooth. Pale. Human.

Then he saw it move.

Under the skin, something shifted. A ripple. A pulse. Scales — white and translucent — pushed up from beneath, sliding across her collarbone like water flowing uphill.

They covered her shoulder. Her neck. The corner of her jaw.

And for one terrible, beautiful moment, Xu Ming saw her.

Not the woman. Not the disguise.

The serpent underneath.

He stumbled back. His hip hit the counter, sending bottles clattering.

Bai Suyin rebuttoned her dress. The scales sank back beneath her skin. When she looked up again, her eyes were brown and wet and human.

"I didn't want you to see that," she whispered.

Xu Ming couldn't speak.

"But you needed to. Before you decided." She picked up the jade pendant and pressed it into his hand. "Keep this. If you want me gone, leave it by the river tonight. I will never come back. If you want me to stay..."

She didn't finish.

She turned and walked out of the shop, the door swinging shut behind her.

Xu Ming stood alone.

The pendant was warm in his palm. Warm like blood. Warm like the body of something that should not exist but did, had existed for a thousand years, and had chosen him to be part of whatever came next.

He looked at the pouch the monk had left.

He looked at the pendant.

Then he looked at the door she had walked through, and the rain beyond it, and the city that had suddenly become a stranger.

He didn't know what to choose.

But something in his chest — something that felt like fear, or hunger, or the first flutter of something ancient waking up — had already made its decision.

title: The Bell That Never Stopped Ringing
content:
Xu Ming didn't sleep that night.

He lay in his narrow bed above the shop, staring at the water-stained ceiling, listening to the rain. The herbs were still spread across his workbench downstairs — white peony, cassia twig, licorice root. She'd known exactly what she wanted. Not a single hesitation.

And that smell.

He'd washed his hands three times. The scent of gardenia and something deeper, something that made his chest ache, still clung to his skin.

At 3 AM, he gave up.

The shop was quiet. Rain drummed against the windows. He turned on the overhead light and sat at his workbench, pulling out the old pharmacopoeia his grandfather had left him. Hand-bound, yellowed pages. Recipes for things modern medicine had forgotten.

He flipped to the section on rare herbs.

*Baihua sheshecao — Old Snake Tongue. Used to treat poisonous bites. Can induce visions in those with weak constitutions. Discontinued in standard practice after the Cultural Revolution.*

A note in his grandfather's cramped handwriting: *Do not prepare alone. Do not prepare at night.*

Xu Ming stared at the words. His grandfather had never been a superstitious man.

He turned another page.

*Shijian cao — Time Grass. Legend states it only grows where a spirit has bled. Medicinal use unverified. According to folk records, the last recorded harvest was in 1127 AD.*

Xu Ming closed the book.

He'd seen the dates. Every herb she'd asked for had a record of use before the Song Dynasty. Then the records stopped. Not because the herbs had gone extinct — because they'd stopped being used.

By humans.

He rubbed his face. This was insane. He was a pharmacist. He treated old ladies with arthritis and office workers with back pain. He didn't believe in spirits, or legends, or anything that couldn't be measured and dosed.

But she'd touched his hand, and the world had gone quiet.

---

She came back the next evening.

Xu Ming was closing up when the bell above the door chimed. He turned, and there she was. Long white dress this time, rain beading on her shoulders like pearls. Her hair was loose, falling past her waist.

"I hope I'm not too late," she said.

"Shop's closed."

"I know." She stepped inside anyway, bringing that scent with her. Gardenia. Rain. Something older than both. "I need more."

"Bai Suyin." He said her name carefully, tasting it. "You asked for herbs yesterday that haven't been used in a thousand years. You paid with vintage coins. You touched my hand and everything went black."

She didn't flinch.

"And now you're back."

"Yes."

"Explain."

She tilted her head, studying him. Her eyes were dark, almost black in the dim light. No — not quite black. When she moved, he caught a flash of something pale. Gold, maybe. The color of aged honey.

"If I tell you the truth," she said slowly, "you won't believe me. And if you do believe me, you'll be in danger."

"I'm already in danger." He gestured at the rain outside. "You found me. That means either you're stalking me, or you know something I don't."

A ghost of a smile crossed her lips. "Both."

"Then start talking."

She walked past him, her fingers trailing across his workbench. She stopped at the pharmacopoeia, still open to the page on Time Grass.

"You've been reading."

"I have a grandfather who wrote notes like he expected someone to need them."

Her fingers traced the faded characters. "He was right."

Xu Ming waited.

Bai Suyin turned to face him. In the overhead light, her skin seemed to glow — not like a woman in good health, but like the moon through clouds. Pale. Distant. Beautiful in a way that hurt to look at.

"I'm not human," she said.

The words hung in the air.

"I've known for a long time," she continued, "but the knowing and the saying are different. I'm a snake spirit. A thousand years of cultivation. I chose this form, this face, this voice. I chose to walk among humans and learn your ways."

Xu Ming didn't speak. His hands were steady on the counter, but his heart was hammering.

"This city — Hangzhou — it's where I was born. Not as a human. As a serpent." Her voice dropped, taking on a cadence that wasn't quite human. "I lived in the waters of West Lake before the first pagoda was built. I watched dynasties rise and fall. I learned the old cultivation methods from a master who died before your great-grandfather's great-grandfather was born."

"You're a snake."

"I'm a cultivator who happened to be born a snake. There's a difference."

His practical mind screamed at him. This was impossible. Spirits weren't real. But his hands remembered the cold shock of her touch, the way the world had fallen silent, the scent that followed him into his dreams.

"Why are you here?"

"I need to become human." Her voice cracked on the word. "Truly human. Not just this borrowed skin. A soul that can be reborn. A heart that can love and die and love again."

"And I'm supposed to help you?"

"You're my heart trial."

The words meant nothing to him. She must have seen the confusion on his face because she stepped closer, close enough that he could see the faint pattern on her skin — scales, so fine they were almost invisible, tracing up her collarbone into her jaw.

"The final stage of cultivation," she said. "A snake spirit who wishes to transcend must find a mortal and experience true emotion. Love. The kind that burns. The kind that endures." Her lips pressed together. "The kind that breaks you open and remakes you."

"You chose me."

"No." She shook her head. "The heart chooses. I only followed where it led."

Xu Ming wanted to laugh. Wanted to tell her she was crazy, that he was a pharmacist who couldn't even keep his goldfish alive, that he didn't believe in fate or destiny or any of it.

But he remembered the silence.

"I don't love you," he said.

"Not yet."

"And if I never do?"

She smiled, and it was the saddest thing he'd ever seen. "Then I remain a snake. My cultivation fails. I return to the lake and wait another thousand years for another chance."

"Or?"

"Or I find another mortal. But the heart doesn't lie. If I choose wrong, the trial kills me." She touched her chest. "The cultivation process is already consuming my core. I have six months. Maybe less."

Six months.

Xu Ming looked at this impossible woman in his closed pharmacy, and every rational part of his brain screamed at him to call the police, to run, to pretend this had never happened.

"I've told you the truth," she said. "Now I need you to decide. Help me, or walk away. But if you walk away, don't come back. Another day near you, and I won't be able to leave."

"What do you need?"

"The herbs are for stabilizing my form. Human bodies are fragile — I have to maintain this shape every day. The old recipes work, but I need a pharmacist who can prepare them correctly."

"And the other thing?"

She looked at him. "Your presence. Your touch. Your voice. The trial requires proximity. I need to be near you for the cultivation to take hold."

"Near me how?"

Her dark eyes held his. "Living with you. Breathing the same air. Sleeping under the same roof."

Xu Ming opened his mouth to refuse.

And then the temple bell rang.

It came from outside, from the direction of Lingyin Temple on the hill. Deep. Resonant. Wrong — because temple bells don't ring at night, and this one was ringing with a force that shook the glass in his windows.

Bai Suyin went pale.

Not figuratively. Her skin literally drained of color until she was whiter than her dress. Her eyes snapped to the door, and he saw it — that flash of gold, unmistakable now, her pupils elongating into slits.

"No," she whispered.

The bell rang again. Closer.

"What is it?"

"He's found me." She backed away from the door, her hands pressed against her chest. "I thought I had more time. I thought the wards would hold—"

The third ring shook the floor.

Xu Ming grabbed her arm. Her skin was cold, cold like river water in winter, cold like something that had never been truly warm.

"Who found you?"

"Fahai." She said the name like a curse. "The Abbot of Lingyin. He's hunted me for a thousand years. He knows what I am. He knows what I'm trying to become."

The bell stopped.

Silence. Heavy. Waiting.

And then the shop door swung open without anyone touching it.

A man stood in the doorway. Robe of brown hemp, shaved head, prayer beads of yellowed bone. He was young — no older than thirty — but his eyes held the weight of centuries. He carried no weapon. He didn't need one.

"Bai Suyin," he said, his voice flat as a blade. "I've tracked you through three lifetimes. You won't escape this time."

She pressed herself against Xu Ming's back. He felt her trembling.

"Leave her alone," he said.

The abbot's gaze shifted to him. Cold. Measuring. "You don't know what you're holding, boy. That thing in your arms has devoured men's souls. She's worn their skins. She's tricked, lied, and murdered her way through a thousand years."

"That's not true," Bai Suyin whispered.

"Isn't it?" Fahai stepped forward. The temperature in the room dropped. "Tell him about the scholar of Kaifeng. Tell him about the merchant of Suzhou. Tell him about the monk you seduced and left to die in a cave."

Xu Ming felt her grip tighten on his shirt.

"I was different then," she said. "I was wild. I didn't know—"

"You knew." Fahai raised his hand, and the prayer beads began to glow. "And you'll pay for every life you've taken."

The light erupted.

Xu Ming threw himself in front of Bai Suyin. The light hit him like a physical force, throwing him against the counter. Glass shattered. Herbs scattered. Pain exploded through his ribs.

"Xu Ming!" She was at his side, her hands on his face, her eyes wide with terror. Not for herself. For him.

"Interesting." Fahai lowered his hand. "A mortal shields a demon. How noble." His voice hardened. "How foolish."

He raised his hand again.

Bai Suyin stepped in front of Xu Ming. Her body changed — scales rippling across her skin, her jaw unhinging, her eyes burning gold. The shop filled with the smell of rain and old stone.

"If you touch him," she said, and her voice was no longer human, "I'll show you what a thousand-year serpent can do."

Fahai smiled.

And the bell began to ring again, this time so loud that Xu Ming's vision went white.

The last thing he saw was Bai Suyin turning to him, her golden eyes filled with tears, her mouth forming words he couldn't hear.

Then nothing.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER FIVE: The Taste of Forbidden Fruit

The rain started at midnight.

Xu Ming sat at his desk, the yellowed pages of the medical text spread before him like the wings of a dead bird. Thunder rolled across West Lake, distant and heavy. He'd been reading the same passage for three hours.

*White Snake Strain. Once harvested from the mountains of Emei. Believed extinct since the Tang Dynasty.*

The words blurred. He rubbed his eyes and checked his phone for the tenth time.

No messages.

Three days since Bai Suyin had walked out of his shop. Three days since she'd kissed him on the bridge. Three days since his world had cracked open and something ancient had crawled out.

He'd tried to find her. Asked neighbors, checked the temples around the lake, even called the police station. No one had seen a woman matching her description. It was as if she'd never existed.

But the herbs were real.

He opened the locked drawer beneath his desk, took out the bundle wrapped in silk. The leaves inside were dark green, almost black, with silver veins that caught the lamplight. He'd never seen anything like them. When he'd pressed one to his nose, the scent had been something between jasmine and old copper.

The bell above his shop door chimed.

Xu Ming's heart kicked. He shoved the bundle back in the drawer, slammed it shut, and stood.

"Sorry, we're—"

The words died in his throat.

Bai Suyin stood in the doorway. Her dress was soaked through, white fabric clinging to her skin. Water dripped from her hair, ran down her face like tears she wouldn't shed. Her eyes were different tonight. Not warm. Hungry.

"I shouldn't have come," she said.

"Where have you been?"

"Running."

"From what?"

She stepped inside, closed the door behind her without turning around. The lock clicked. "From the truth. I thought I could leave. Start over somewhere else. Let you forget me."

"Three days isn't enough to forget you."

"I know." She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "I have been walking the mountains around the lake. I climbed to the top of Leifeng Pagoda and sat in the rain. I tried to feel nothing. But you are here, Xu Ming. In my chest like a thorn."

He crossed to her. She didn't move as he took her hand, didn't flinch when his fingers touched her wrist.

Her pulse was wrong.

Too slow. Too steady. Like a drum beating once every ten seconds.

"Your heart," he said.

"Is beating. That is enough."

"No. I'm a pharmacist. I know what a healthy pulse sounds like. Yours is—"

"What it is." She pulled her hand away. "I came to tell you the truth. All of it. And then you can decide if you still want to touch me."

The rain hammered the roof. Water leaked through a crack in the frame, dripping onto the floor in steady rhythm.

"One thousand years ago," she said, "I was a snake."

Xu Ming stood very still.

"I was born in the ice caves of Kunlun. My mother was already dead when I hatched. I learned to hunt in darkness. I learned to kill before I learned to bask in the sun." She spoke without emotion, like she was reading a history of someone else's life. "I was not special. There were thousands like me. But I was lucky. A cultivator found me when I was starving, half-frozen in a blizzard. She took me in. Taught me to breathe the energy of the world. To draw qi into my body and make it my own."

"A snake," Xu Ming repeated.

"Yes. I have been human for barely six months. This body is borrowed from a woman who drowned in the Qiantang River. I found her before the fish did." She met his eyes. "I wore her like a coat."

He should have run. Should have screamed. Should have grabbed the iron pestle from his mortar and held it like a weapon.

Instead, he said: "Why me?"

"Because you are my heart trial."

"Your what?"

"When a spirit cultivates toward humanity, there is a final gate. One obstacle that cannot be passed through meditation or study." She stepped closer. Rainwater pooled at her feet. "To become human, I must love a mortal. Truly love him. And he must love me back. If I succeed, I shed the last of my serpent nature and walk among you forever."

"And if you fail?"

"Then I revert. Snake again. No memory of what I almost became. No hope of trying again." Her voice dropped. "I have waited a thousand years for this. I will not fail."

The words hung between them.

Xu Ming thought of his quiet shop. His carefully organized herbs. His routine that never changed because change had always terrified him.

He thought of her fingers on his skin. Her laugh by the lake. The way she'd looked at him like he was the only thing in the world worth seeing.

"You could have lied to me," he said. "Seduced me. Made me fall in love without ever knowing what you were."

"Yes."

"Why didn't you?"

"Because if you love a lie, it is not love." She touched his face. Her fingers were cold. "I want you to look at me and see the snake. See the monster. See every cold-blooded thing I have done to survive. And still choose me."

Thunder cracked overhead. The lights flickered.

"I don't know if I can," Xu Ming said honestly.

"Then let me show you something."

She stepped back, raised her hands, and began to glow.

The light came from beneath her skin, pale and shimmering like moonlight on water. Her eyes changed first — the irises turning vertical, the pupils dilating into black slits. Scales rippled across her cheekbones, silver-white and gleaming.

Xu Ming stumbled backward. His hip hit the desk, sending papers scattering.

She was beautiful. She was terrifying. She was something that should not exist.

"This is what I am," she said. Her voice had changed too. Something underneath the words, a low vibration that made the bottles on his shelves rattle. "This is the truth beneath the skin."

The transformation spread down her neck. Her fingers elongated, nails turning into claws. Behind her, something moved — a shape in the air, a suggestion of massive coils that couldn't possibly fit in his small shop.

"Stop," Xu Ming whispered.

She froze.

"Please. Stop."

The scales faded. Her eyes returned to human brown. The presence behind her dissolved like smoke. She stood before him again, just a woman in a wet dress, trembling slightly.

"That was only a fraction," she said. "If I chose to show you my true form, I would fill this room. Break the walls. You would see what a thousand years of cultivation looks like when it stops pretending."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because Fahai knows I am here."

The name hit him like cold water.

"The monk from the temple?"

"He is not a monk. He is a hunter. He has been pursuing me for eight hundred years, across every incarnation, every life I have tried to build." Her jaw tightened. "He killed my teacher. He burned the mountain where I was born. He has made it his holy purpose to destroy any spirit that seeks to become human."

"He's why you came to me."

"Yes. I need protection. A mortal holding. Somewhere he cannot find me." She looked at him with desperate eyes. "But more than that, I need you to love me. Not because of the trial. Because if you love me, truly, I can fight him. If you love me, I can win."

Xu Ming's hands were shaking.

He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs for coughs and fevers. His life was small and safe and predictable.

And now a thousand-year-old snake spirit was asking him to be her shelter.

"I need time," he said.

"We do not have time." She grabbed his wrist, pressed his palm flat against her chest. Her heart beat once. Twice. The space between each pulse was an eternity. "He has already found my trail. He will be here before the rain stops."

"How do you know?"

"Because I can smell him." Her nostrils flared. "Burning incense. Old blood. He walks the city like a wolf, and he is getting closer."

The shop's back door exploded inward.

Wood splintered. Glass shattered. And a figure stepped through the wreckage, robes dripping wet, prayer beads clicking in the dark.

"白素贞."

The voice was deep. Calm. Absolute.

"你逃不掉了."

Bai Suyin's face went white.

Xu Ming didn't speak Chinese well, but he caught the words she'd taught him by the lake. His name. Her name.

*You can't escape.*

The man in the doorway raised his head, and Xu Ming saw his eyes for the first time.

They were not human eyes. They were gold. Burning gold, like coins heated in a forge.

"I am Abbot Fahai," the man said, switching to Mandarin with a perfect, terrible smile. "And you, pharmacist, are harboring a demon."

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Gold in His Eyes

The rain had stopped. Xu Ming noticed that first—the sudden silence on the roof, the drip-drip from the broken door frame becoming a trickle rather than a flood. The world had gone still, holding its breath.

Fahai stood in the splintered doorway, water streaming from his robes. He was taller than Xu Ming remembered from the temple, or perhaps he had always been this tall and the shadows had hidden it. His shaved head gleamed wet, and his prayer beads clicked as he rotated them—once, twice, a rhythm like a heartbeat.

"I said," Fahai repeated, his Mandarin crisp and cultured, "you are harboring a demon."

Bai Suyin moved. Not backward, not forward. She shifted sideways, putting herself between Xu Ming and the door. Her wet dress clung to her body, and Xu Ming saw something ripple beneath her skin—not scales this time, but muscle, sinew, the coiled readiness of a predator.

"He is innocent," she said. "He knows nothing."

"He knows enough." Fahai's golden eyes never left Xu Ming. "He has touched you. Breathed your air. Let you drink his tea. The contamination is already complete."

"Contamination?" Xu Ming's voice cracked. He was still holding the pestle, his knuckles white around the ceramic. "I haven't been contaminated by anything."

Fahai laughed. It was a beautiful sound, warm and melodic, utterly wrong coming from a face that hard. "You think she is the contamination? No, child. You are the contamination. Your mortality. Your weakness. Your brief, flickering life that means nothing in the span of eternity." He stepped forward, and his wet sandals left no marks on the floor. "She has spent a thousand years trying to become what you are born as. Pathetic, isn't it?"

"Don't." Bai Suyin's voice dropped low, almost a growl. "Don't speak to him."

"Or what? You'll show me your true form?" Fahai spread his arms. "Please. I have carved my name into the bones of a hundred spirits who thought they could frighten me. You are not special, snake. You are simply the one who has eluded me longest."

Xu Ming's mind raced. He needed time. Needed to think. Needed to understand what was happening in his shop, among his herbs and his scales and his quiet life.

"You said you killed her teacher," Xu Ming said.

Fahai's eyes flickered. Surprise, maybe. Or interest. "She told you that much?"

"You burned a mountain."

"I burned many mountains." Fahai's smile thinned. "Her teacher was a white snake who had lived two thousand years. She had almost completed her transformation. She had found a mortal to love, a fisherman who sang to her every evening by the river. It was quite romantic." His voice dripped with contempt. "I waited until the night of their union. When she was vulnerable. When her power was open, shared, unprotected. Then I struck."

Bai Suyin made a sound. It was small, barely audible, but Xu Ming heard it. A whimper. A break in the thousand-year composure.

"You cut off her head," she whispered.

"Her cultivation was too strong. I couldn't destroy her core directly. But a snake's head, separated from her body..." Fahai shrugged. "The fisherman watched. I made sure of that. He saw her face turn human as she died, saw her eyes find his, saw her mouth form his name one last time. He threw himself into the river the next day. I considered that a mercy."

Xu Ming's stomach turned. The pestle felt heavy in his hand, useless. What was ceramic against a man who had killed two-thousand-year-old spirits?

"Why?" Xu Ming asked. "Why do you hunt them?"

"Because they should not exist." Fahai's golden eyes blazed. "The boundary between human and spirit was drawn by Heaven itself. Every creature that crosses it is an abomination, a crack in the wall that holds back chaos. I am not a murderer, pharmacist. I am a repairman."

"You're a butcher," Bai Suyin spat.

"And you are a fugitive." Fahai's prayer beads stopped moving. "Eight hundred years I have tracked you. Eight hundred years of your little lives, your little loves, your little attempts to become something you were never meant to be. This ends tonight."

He raised his hand.

The air changed.

Xu Ming felt it first in his chest—a pressure, like being underwater. The bottles on his shelves began to rattle. The herbs in their drawers vibrated. The lights flickered once, twice, then went dark.

But there was light. Gold light, pouring from Fahai's palm. It illuminated his face from below, casting shadows upward, making him look like a demon himself.

"Bai Suyin," he intoned, "by the authority vested in me by the Celestial Court, I pronounce your sentence. For the crime of perverting Heaven's order, for the sin of stealing human form, for the transgression of loving mortal flesh—"

"Stop quoting scripture at me." Bai Suyin's voice was ice. "You've never had authority from anyone. You're a rogue, Fahai. A madman who convinced himself the voices in his head were Heaven's commands."

The gold light flickered.

"The voices," Fahai said slowly, "have never been wrong."

He unleashed the light.

It was not fire. It was not lightning. It was something between, a stream of pure energy that roared across the shop, incinerating everything it touched. A shelf of dried herbs became ash. A wooden counter blackened and crumbled. The glass case containing Xu Ming's antique scales melted into a puddle of green slag.

Bai Suyin moved.

She was fast—faster than anything Xu Ming had ever seen. She twisted, her body bending at angles that should have broken bones, and the light passed inches from her face. It struck the wall behind her, and the brick glowed red, then white, then collapsed into molten stone.

"Xu Ming, get out!" she shouted.

He couldn't move. His legs had locked, his brain refusing to process what his eyes were seeing. This was his shop. His father's shop. The place where he had learned to measure herbs by feel, to recognize sickness by smell, to heal with patience and precision. And it was being destroyed.

Fahai raised his hand again.

Bai Suyin charged.

She crossed the distance in a blink, her hand extended, fingers curved into claws. She was aiming for his throat—Xu Ming saw the killing intent in her eyes, the desperation of a cornered animal.

Fahai caught her wrist.

He stopped her momentum completely, as if she had run into a wall. His fingers wrapped around her arm, and Xu Ming saw scales ripple across her skin where he touched.

"Still weak," Fahai said. "Still incomplete. You haven't completed the trial. You haven't been loved. You're barely more than a snake wearing a human suit."

He twisted.

Bai Suyin screamed.

It was not a human scream. It was high and thin, a whistle of pain that made Xu Ming's ears bleed. Her body convulsed, and for a moment, Xu Ming saw her true form flicker over her skin—scales, fangs, eyes that were vertical slits of gold.

"I should take your head here," Fahai said, his voice conversational. "End this farce. But no—that would be too quick. You've wasted my time, snake. Eight hundred years of my life, chasing you across continents, watching you build your little nests and find your little mortals. I want you to suffer."

He raised his other hand.

The gold light gathered again.

Xu Ming moved.

He didn't think. Didn't plan. His body acted before his mind caught up, driven by something deeper than reason. He stepped forward, raised the pestle, and brought it down on Fahai's arm.

Ceramic met divine power.

The pestle exploded.

Shards flew everywhere, cutting Xu Ming's face, his hands, his arms. He felt blood hot on his skin, felt something sharp embedded in his cheek. But the blow had done something—Fahai's grip loosened, just a fraction, and Bai Suyin wrenched free.

She grabbed Xu Ming's collar and pulled him backward, through the shattered remains of his shop, toward the back room. Toward the stairs that led to his apartment above.

"Run," she hissed. "Don't look back. Don't stop."

They ran.

Behind them, Fahai laughed. It echoed through the ruined shop, through the broken walls, through the smoke and ash. It was the laugh of someone who knew the end of the story and was simply waiting for the characters to catch up.

"You can run," he called after them. "But the rain has stopped. The trail is fresh. And I have waited eight hundred years."

Xu Ming stumbled up the stairs, Bai Suyin's hand tight on his arm. He could feel her trembling, could feel the heat of her skin, could smell something strange on her—something like ozone, like burnt metal, like the air before a thunderstorm.

They reached his apartment.

The door was unlocked. He pushed it open, and they fell inside, gasping, bleeding, terrified.

"We can't stay here," Bai Suyin said. "He'll find us. He always finds us."

"Then where do we go?"

She looked at him. Her eyes were human again, but something moved beneath them—a deeper light, a primal awareness.

"There's a place," she said. "A temple. Hidden in the mountains west of here. My teacher built it before Fahai found her. She warded it against his sight."

"Your teacher's temple? The one he burned?"

"No." Bai Suyin's voice dropped to a whisper. "The one she built in secret. The one he never found."

She reached into her dress and pulled out a chain. At the end of it hung a jade pendant, small and green, carved into the shape of a coiled serpent.

"She gave this to me before she died," Bai Suyin said. "She said it would show me the way home, when the time came."

"The time for what?"

Bai Suyin's hand closed around the jade. When she opened her fingers, the pendant was glowing—soft, pulsing, like a heartbeat.

"The time for the war to end."

Below them, Fahai's footsteps echoed up the stairs.

Xu Ming looked at the glowing jade, at the trembling woman, at the shattered door that would not hold for more than a minute.

He had been a pharmacist. He had mixed herbs for coughs and fevers. His life had been small and safe and predictable.

And now a thousand-year-old snake spirit was asking him to follow her into the mountains, to a hidden temple, to a war that had been raging for eight centuries.

"What are we waiting for?" he asked.

Bai Suyin smiled. It was the first genuine smile he had seen on her face, and it transformed her—made her look young, hopeful, almost human.

"Nothing," she said. "We were waiting for you."

She grabbed his hand.

The jade pendant flared.

And the world dissolved into green light.

title: The Price of Sanctuary
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes swept across the shop like floodwater. Xu Ming's shadow stretched and twisted against the wall. Every bottle on every shelf cast a dozen smaller shadows, each one sharp enough to cut.

"I am not a demon." Bai Suyin's voice came from behind him, low and steady. "You know what I am. You have always known."

"I know what you *were*." Fahai stepped through the shattered doorframe. His robes didn't stir. The rain didn't touch him. He moved like something that had forgotten how to be wet. "A serpent of the West Lake. A creature of instinct and hunger. You consumed the essence of three scholars during the Tang Dynasty, did you not? Their souls still drift inside you. I have heard them screaming."

"That was six hundred years before I found the path of cultivation."

"And yet you carry their weight."

Xu Ming's hands moved before his brain caught up. He stepped sideways, placing his body between the monk and Bai Suyin. The action was so absurd, so completely useless, that he almost laughed. What was he going to do? Throw herbal remedies at a man whose eyes burned like molten gold?

"Get out of my shop," Xu Ming said.

Fahai's smile didn't waver. "Brave. Stupid, but brave. I have killed men for less than standing in my way."

"He is my chosen." Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's shoulder. Her fingers were cold, but they gripped him with surprising strength. "The heart trial has begun. Harm him, and you sever the connection. You know the Celestial Laws as well as I do. A spirit's trial cannot be interrupted by force without consequence."

"The Celestial Laws." Fahai's laugh was soft, almost gentle. "Do you think I care about laws made by bureaucrats who have never faced what I have faced? Who have never seen what spirits do to mortals when they tire of playing human?"

He raised his hand. The prayer beads clicked together, and the sound was wrong. It echoed. It multiplied. It sounded like a hundred monks chanting in a room that could only fit one.

"Last chance, serpent. Release the mortal. Return to your lake. I will confine you for another three hundred years, and when you emerge, perhaps you will have learned humility."

"Or what?" Bai Suyin stepped forward, matching his calm with her own. "You will trap me in a pagoda again? You will drown me in holy water? You have tried all of this before, Fahai. Eight hundred years of attempts. Eight hundred years of failure. I am still here. I am still growing stronger. And you are still just a man."

"I am not a man."

The prayer beads stopped moving.

"I am a weapon."

The shop exploded into chaos.

Fahai moved faster than anything Xu Ming had ever seen. One moment he was at the door. The next, he was beside Bai Suyin, his hand closing around her throat. But she was already gone, dissolving into white mist that reformed behind the counter. Her arm swept across the shelf, sending jars of herbs flying. They shattered against Fahai's face, filling the air with the smell of angelica and ginseng.

"Running?" Fahai wiped his face calmly. "You used to fight."

"I am conserving my strength." Bai Suyin's voice came from everywhere and nowhere. "For when it matters."

Xu Ming's training kicked in. Three years as a pharmacist. Countless nights alone in this shop. He knew every bottle, every drawer, every hidden space. His hand found the back wall, slid along the wood paneling until his fingers caught the hidden latch. The false panel swung open, revealing the safe behind it.

Not a safe for money. A safe for the precious herbs. The ones that cost more than most people made in a year. The ones that could kill or cure depending on dosage.

He grabbed the first thing his hand touched. A small silk pouch, heavy with dried roots. He didn't look. He just threw.

The pouch hit Fahai in the chest. It burst open, releasing a cloud of dark brown powder. The monk inhaled before he could stop himself. His eyes widened.

"Aconite," Xu Ming said. "Processed with wine and honey. The monks at Lingyin Temple buy it from me for their arthritis remedies."

Fahai's face went slack. His hand dropped to his side. The golden light in his eyes flickered, dimmed, went out.

For three seconds, he was just a man.

Bai Suyin moved. She crossed the distance in a blur of white silk. Her hand plunged into Fahai's chest—not through his flesh, but *into* his shadow. She pulled. Something dark and writhing came out in her grip, like a snake made of smoke.

"Your dharma protection," she hissed. "I have it."

Fahai's eyes snapped open. The gold returned, hotter than before. "You have a *piece* of it."

He grabbed her wrist. The smoke snake screamed and dissolved. Bai Suyin stumbled backward, clutching her hand. When she opened it, Xu Ming saw blisters forming on her palm. Burn marks. Holy wounds.

"You forget," Fahai said, straightening his robes. "I have been hunting your kind since before your grandmother's grandmother was born. I know every trick. Every escape. Every hidden weakness."

"You could not have survived aconite," Xu Ming said. "I processed that root myself. One breath should have paralyzed you."

"It did." Fahai touched his chest. "For a moment. But I have been cultivating as long as your serpent has. Longer, perhaps. My body is no longer entirely mortal."

He reached into his sleeve and pulled out something Xu Ming had never seen before. A bell. Small, brass, tarnished with age. It had no clapper, no handle, no markings.

"You know what this is," Fahai said, looking at Bai Suyin.

Her face had lost all color. "Where did you get that?"

"From your teacher's corpse. After I burned her mountain."

"You lying *bastard*." Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "She had no bell. She was a water spirit. She had no—"

"She kept it hidden. Even from you." Fahai held the bell up to the light. "It is a Soul Summoner. Forged during the Liang Dynasty. It calls to spirits who have bound themselves to mortal bodies, and it *unbinds* them."

He shook the bell.

No sound came out.

But Bai Suyin screamed.

She doubled over, clutching her stomach. Her skin flickered, scales rippling across her arms and neck. Her eyes turned vertical, slitted, reptilian. Her mouth opened, and a forked tongue lashed the air.

"Stop," she gasped. "Please. *Stop.*"

Fahai shook the bell again.

Bai Suyin's body convulsed. Her legs gave out. She collapsed to the floor, writhing, her human form warping and twisting like clay in a potter's hands. The white mist that had been her companion now tore away from her in shreds, dissolving into the air.

Xu Ming's legs moved before he could think. He ran at Fahai, fists raised, ready to die.

Fahai backhanded him without looking.

Xu Ming hit the wall. His vision exploded into stars. His ribs screamed. He tasted blood.

"The mortal is not part of this equation," Fahai said, still watching Bai Suyin. "But if he insists on interfering, I will remove him. Permanently."

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice was barely a whisper. "Run. Get out. Find the White Lotus Society. They will—"

"I will kill you both if you speak another word." Fahai shook the bell a third time.

Bai Suyin's scream turned into something animal. Her body dissolved into white mist, then reformed, then dissolved again. She was losing control. Losing herself. The thousand years of cultivation were being stripped away, one layer at a time.

Xu Ming pushed himself up. His legs were shaking. His ribs felt like broken glass. But he had one thing left. One card he had never played.

He reached into his pocket and pulled out the jade vial Bai Suyin had given him the night she arrived.

"What is that?" Fahai's eyes narrowed.

"A gift." Xu Ming uncorked the vial. A drop of red liquid fell onto his finger. It burned. It glowed. It smelled like iron and flowers and the oldest thing he had ever known. "Her blood. She gave it to me the night she came here. She said if I ever needed her, truly needed her, I should drink it."

"You would consume a spirit's blood?" Fahai laughed. "You would become a vessel? A sacrifice? Fool. It will kill you."

"Maybe." Xu Ming raised the vial to his lips. "But if I die, I die standing."

He drank.

The world stopped.

The first thing he felt was heat. Not burning heat, but *life* heat. A thousand years of life, compressed into a single drop of blood. It hit his stomach and exploded outward, filling every cell, every nerve, every fiber of his being. His heart stopped. Started again. Stopped. Started at a rhythm that was not human.

Then he saw her.

Not with his eyes. With something deeper. He saw Bai Suyin as she truly was: not a woman in a white dress, but a vast, coiled presence at the bottom of a lake, scales glittering like moonlight, eyes ancient and patient and hungry. He saw her teacher. He saw the mountain that burned. He saw a thousand years of solitude, of longing, of trying to become something she was not born to be.

And she saw him.

*Xu Ming.*

Her voice was inside his head, soft and stunned.

*You actually drank it.*

"Did I do something stupid?" he whispered.

*Yes. The stupidest thing anyone has ever done for me.*

He felt her warmth wrap around him. Her presence filled the cracks in his bones, sealed the wounds in his flesh. She was healing him. Protecting him. And as she did, he felt something else.

He felt her power.

It was not gentle. It was not beautiful. It was a hurricane, a wildfire, a thousand years of cultivation compressed into a single, terrible point. And it was *his*.

He opened his eyes.

Fahai was staring at him. The golden light in his eyes had gone dark. His face was pale. For the first time, he looked afraid.

"What have you done?" Fahai whispered.

"I don't know," Xu Ming said. "But I'm about to find out."

He raised his hand. The white mist that had been Bai Suyin's body flowed toward him, wrapped around his arm, coiled into his palm. He felt her there, whole and waiting.

"Together," he said.

*Together.*

He turned to face Fahai.

And the bell in the monk's hand shattered.

Not from any force. Not from any blow. It simply cracked, split, fell apart like dry clay. Fahai stared at the fragments in his palm, his expression shifting from shock to fury to something that looked almost like grief.

"You have bonded with her," he said. "Completely. Irrevocably. You have tied your mortal soul to her spirit."

"Yes."

"Then you have condemned yourself." Fahai's voice was flat. Empty. "She is a serpent. A creature of instinct. She will consume you, body and soul, and when she does, I will be there to finish what you started."

He turned and walked toward the shattered door.

"Where

title: The Bond Beneath the Skin
content:
The shattered door lay in pieces across Xu Ming's floor, rain mixing with the splinters. Abbot Fahai stood in the opening, his golden eyes fixed on Bai Suyin with the patience of a hunter who had tracked his prey for centuries.

Xu Ming's pharmacist training took over. His hand moved to the counter, fingers brushing against the mortar and pestle. Useless. What could herbs do against a man whose eyes burned like molten coins?

"You have violated sacred law," Fahai said, stepping forward. His robes barely rustled. "A spirit who has fed on human essence cannot be allowed to complete her transformation."

"I have fed on nothing." Bai Suyin's voice was steel. "I have killed no one. I have taken no life."

"Then explain the blood on your hands." Fahai reached into his robe and withdrew a scroll, yellowed with age. He unrolled it, revealing characters that glowed faintly red. "Three hundred years ago, Suzhou. A merchant found dead with his throat torn out. Fifty years after that, Nanjing. A scholar, same wound pattern. One hundred years ago—"

"Those were not me."

"Denial is meaningless." Fahai's fingers traced the scroll. "The Heavenly Registry records every death caused by spirits. Your essence signature is on each one."

Xu Ming found his voice. "She's been with me. Every night. She hasn't left."

"Foolish mortal." Fahai's golden eyes flicked to him. "A spirit of her power can project herself across miles while her body lies still. She could have killed a hundred men while you slept beside her and you would never know."

Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's. Her fingers were cold. "He's lying. Those deaths were caused by another. A black serpent who has been hunting me as long as Fahai has."

"Convenient." Fahai smiled. "A phantom enemy, always one step ahead, always responsible for your crimes."

"It's the truth."

"Then prove it." Fahai snapped his fingers. The air rippled. Two figures materialized behind him—monks in crimson robes, their faces hidden by hoods. They carried chains made of brass, etched with scripture.

Xu Ming stepped in front of Bai Suyin. "This is my shop. You don't have a warrant. You don't have authority—"

"I have authority given by heaven itself." Fahai raised his hand, and Xu Ming felt his legs give way. His knees hit the floor. Not from force—from weight. As if the air itself had become lead.

"Xu Ming!" Bai Suyin grabbed his shoulder.

"Stay back," he gasped. "Don't—"

"You're hurting him," she said, her voice dropping low. Too low. A vibration that made the herbs in their jars shiver. "Let him go."

Fahai's smile widened. "There she is. The beast beneath the skin."

"Let him go, or I will show you exactly what a thousand years of cultivation looks like."

"You'll destroy half the city. Kill hundreds of mortals. Prove everything I've ever said about you." Fahai tilted his head. "Go ahead. I've been waiting eight hundred years for you to lose control."

Xu Ming couldn't breathe. The pressure on his chest was crushing. He saw Bai Suyin's eyes flicker—white scales appearing at the corners, her pupils narrowing to vertical slits.

She was going to do it. She was going to transform.

"No," he whispered.

She heard him. Her gaze snapped to his.

"Don't," he said, forcing the word out through compressed lungs. "He wants you to. Don't give him what he wants."

The scales receded. Her eyes returned to human. But when she spoke, her voice held something Xu Ming had never heard before—not anger, not fear, but a terrible calm.

"You're right, Fahai. I won't fight you here. Not in front of witnesses. Not with mortals in danger." She raised her chin. "Take me."

Fahai's smile vanished. "What?"

"Take me to the Heavenly Court. Let me stand before the Jade Emperor and plead my case. If you're so certain of my guilt, let the Celestial Bureaucracy judge me."

"You think they will show mercy?"

"I think you're afraid to let me speak." Bai Suyin stepped forward, hands raised. "You've hunted me for eight hundred years, but you've never once let me testify. Why is that, Fahai? What are you hiding?"

Something flickered in those golden eyes. Just for a moment. Doubt. Then it was gone.

"Clever," he said. "Very clever. You delay. You scheme. But it changes nothing." He gestured to the crimson monks. "Bind her."

The chains wrapped around her wrists before Xu Ming could move. The brass hissed where it touched her skin, leaving red marks. Bai Suyin's face tightened, but she made no sound.

"You can't just take her," Xu Ming said, finding his feet at last. "There are laws. Procedures. She has rights—"

"She is not a citizen. She is not even human." Fahai turned to face him fully. "You, however, are. And harboring a spirit is punishable by—"

"Leave him alone." Bai Suyin's voice cut through. "He didn't know what I was. He's innocent."

"He knew enough." Fahai's hand shot out, grabbing Xu Ming's wrist. His grip was like iron. "You will be tested. If your soul is clean, you will be released. If you have been corrupted by her essence..." He smiled. "Well. Let's hope that's not the case."

"Don't touch him!" Bai Suyin lunged, but the chains flared with golden light. She screamed—a sound that was only partly human—and collapsed to her knees.

"Stop!" Xu Ming tried to pull free, but Fahai's grip only tightened.

"One more word, snake, and I will burn his soul from his body where you watch." Fahai's eyes were flat. Cold. "You know I can."

Bai Suyin was silent, but her whole body trembled. Xu Ming saw scales rippling beneath her skin, fighting to emerge.

"Good." Fahai released Xu Ming and stepped back. "Take the spirit to the monastery. I'll deal with the mortal after."

The crimson monks flanked Bai Suyin, chains dragging her toward the shattered door. She looked back once. Her eyes met Xu Ming's.

*Find me,* she mouthed. *Please.*

Then she was gone, swallowed by the rain.

Fahai lingered. He studied Xu Ming with those terrible golden eyes. "You love her."

Xu Ming said nothing.

"Pitiful. She chose you because you were weak. Easy to manipulate. A quiet life with no ambition, no hunger, no fire." Fahai shook his head. "You are not her heart trial. You are her hiding place. And now you've served your purpose."

He walked to the door, then paused. "If you're wise, you'll forget everything. Return to your herbs and your prescriptions. Pretend none of this happened."

"And if I'm not wise?"

Fahai laughed. It was a terrible sound, devoid of warmth. "Then you'll learn why spirits fear my name."

He stepped into the rain and vanished.

Xu Ming stood alone in his ruined shop. The door hung crooked. Rain soaked his floor. Herbs lay scattered everywhere, crushed and useless.

He looked at his hands. They were still shaking.

*Find me,* she had said.

But where? How? He was a pharmacist. He didn't know about monasteries or Heavenly Courts or immortal politics. He didn't know anything.

Then he saw it.

On the counter, half-hidden under a broken jar of ginseng root, was a piece of paper. He picked it up. It was damp, but the characters were still legible.

It was a prescription. Not for any herb he knew.

*Black serpent scale. Phoenix tail feather. Tear of a mortal who loves without hope.*

Below it, in smaller characters:

*The monastery of Thunderclap Peak. Come at midnight, when the moon hides its face. Bring the tear.*

Xu Ming's blood ran cold.

The tear of a mortal who loves without hope.

He pressed his palm to his chest. His heart was pounding. But he knew, with terrible certainty, that he would go.

Because she was right.

He did love her.

And he had no hope at all.

---

The rain continued through the night. Xu Ming worked by candlelight, gathering supplies. Herbs he recognized. Herbs he didn't. A flask of wine. A knife. A small jade pendant that had belonged to his mother—he didn't know why he brought it, only that it felt right.

At eleven, he put on his coat and stepped into the streets.

Hangzhou was empty. The rain had driven everyone inside. The streetlights cast yellow pools on wet asphalt, and his footsteps echoed off buildings that had stood for centuries.

He walked toward the mountain.

Thunderclap Peak was an hour's hike, even in good weather. In this rain, it would take longer. He didn't care.

He thought about Bai Suyin. About the way she smiled when she tasted his tea. About her hands sorting herbs with impossible precision. About the weight of her head on his shoulder when she fell asleep in the shop.

About the scales.

About the fact that she was a thousand years old and he was twenty-seven.

About the fact that none of that mattered.

He was still walking.

The trail began at the edge of the city, winding up through bamboo groves that bent and swayed in the wind. The rain turned to mist. The temperature dropped. Xu Ming's breath fogged in front of him.

He thought about Fahai's eyes. Those golden, inhuman eyes.

*What are you hiding?* Bai Suyin had asked.

It was a good question.

He reached the monastery at eleven forty-five.

It was older than he expected. Stone walls covered in moss. A gate made of wood so dark it was almost black. No lights. No sound. Just the rain and the wind and the weight of something ancient pressing down on him.

He pushed the gate.
