It opened.

Inside, the courtyard was empty. A single pagoda rose in the center, its roof curved like a serpent's spine. Xu Ming walked toward it, his heart hammering.

The door to the pagoda was open.

He stepped inside.

The interior was lit by a single candle. And there, chained to a pillar in the center of the room, was Bai Suyin.

She was changed.

Her skin was pale as moonlight. Her eyes were white, no pupils, no irises. And her hair—her beautiful black hair—had turned silver, falling around her like a waterfall of mercury.

"Xu Ming," she whispered. "You came."

"I brought the tear."

She shook her head slowly. "It's a trap. Fahai knew you would come. He's waiting."

"Let him wait." Xu Ming crossed the room, his shoes echoing on stone. He reached for her chains.

"Don't touch them!" she cried.

Too late.

The moment his fingers brushed the brass, golden light exploded. Pain shot through his arm, up his shoulder, into his chest. He fell backward, gasping.

And Fahai stepped out of the shadows.

"Foolish mortal," he said. "Did you truly think I would leave her unguarded?"

Xu Ming struggled to his feet. His arm was numb. "Let her go."

"Or what? You'll throw herbs at me?" Fahai

title: The Price of a Heartbeat
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the damage was done. Xu Ming had seen what lurked beneath the monk's skin, and it was not holiness.

"I am not here to harm you, pharmacist." Fahai stepped over the shattered remains of the back door. His robes were wet, but he moved without sound, without disturbing the debris. "I am here to save you."

Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's. Her fingers were ice cold. "Don't listen to him. He will say anything."

"Will I?" Fahai stopped at the counter that separated them. He placed both palms flat on the wood, and Xu Ming noticed his nails were trimmed to the quick, the cuticles scarred from old battles. "Tell me, Xu Ming. Did she explain what the heart trial costs?"

"She told me enough."

"Did she tell you that when a snake spirit completes her transformation, she must consume the heart of her chosen mortal? Not metaphorically. Literally." Fahai's voice dropped to a whisper. "She will eat your heart while it is still beating, and she will absorb your life force into her own. That is the price of her humanity."

Xu Ming's blood turned to ice. He looked at Bai Suyin.

She did not deny it.

"Is that true?" His voice cracked on the last word.

"Only if I fail." Bai Suyin's grip on his hand tightened. "Only if the trial breaks. If I complete it properly—if I truly love you and you truly love me—the transformation is pure. I become human without sacrifice."

"And if the love is not real?"

Silence.

Fahai laughed, a sound like stones grinding together. "She cannot answer because the answer damns her. The heart trial is a gamble. Every spirit who attempts it knows the stakes. Either she succeeds and becomes human, or she fails and consumes you. There is no middle ground."

"You speak of purity, Abbot." Bai Suyin's voice turned sharp, cold as winter steel. "But you know nothing of what it means to love. You burned my teacher alive and called it mercy. You drowned an entire village to flush me out, and called it justice. Do not lecture me on the cost of transformation."

The air thickened. Xu Ming felt pressure building behind his temples, like the moment before a storm breaks.

"That village was infested with water spirits," Fahai said calmly. "They were draining the life from the villagers. I did what was necessary."

"You did what you always do. You chose destruction over understanding."

"And you chose to seduce a mortal for your own gain." Fahai's eyes flickered gold again. "We are both monsters, Bai Suyin. The difference is that I admit what I am."

Xu Ming stepped between them. His legs were shaking. His heart hammered against his ribs. But he stood his ground.

"Everyone out. Now."

Both of them looked at him like he had grown a second head.

"You cannot command me, mortal." Fahai's voice dripped contempt.

"This is my shop. My property. And I am telling you both to leave before I call the police."

"The police cannot stop me."

"They can stop her." Xu Ming pointed at Bai Suyin. "She's the one who needs to stay hidden. You said it yourself—she needs a mortal holding. So if I throw her out, she has nowhere to go. And if you destroy my property, I file a report, and suddenly there's a paper trail connecting an abbot to a break-in."

Fahai's face went blank. Then, slowly, he smiled.

"You are smarter than you look, pharmacist."

"I'm just tired of being talked around." Xu Ming grabbed his phone from the counter. "I'm calling 911. You have ten seconds to decide how you want to explain yourselves."

Bai Suyin stared at him with something new in her eyes. Respect, maybe. Or fear.

Fahai bowed, a shallow, mocking gesture. "Very well. I will withdraw. For now." He turned to leave, then paused at the broken door. "But remember, Xu Ming. She has told you nothing but half-truths since the moment she met you. Ask her about the last mortal she loved. Ask her what happened to him."

He stepped into the rain and vanished.

The shop fell silent. Water dripped through the broken doorframe, pooling on the floor. Xu Ming's hands were shaking so badly he had to put the phone down.

"The last mortal I loved." Bai Suyin's voice was barely audible. "His name was Zhang Yun. He was a scholar, gentle and kind. I met him in the Ming Dynasty, four hundred years ago."

Xu Ming waited.

"I thought I could complete the trial with him. I thought I loved him enough. But Fahai found us before the transformation was complete. He attacked while I was still changing. I could not control my power." Tears slid down her face. "I crushed Zhang Yun in my coils before I even realized what was happening. I held him as he died. I felt his ribs break beneath my scales."

The image hit Xu Ming like a physical blow. A woman. A snake. A man dying in an embrace meant to be love.

"I am not that creature anymore," Bai Suyin said. "I have spent four hundred years learning control. Learning restraint. I would rather die than hurt you."

"But you might not have a choice."

"No. I might not."

Xu Ming walked to the front door and locked it. Then he returned to the counter and poured himself a glass of water he did not drink.

"You said Fahai killed your teacher. Burned your mountain. Drowned a village." He turned to face her. "Why is he still alive?"

"He is protected by a relic. A golden staff blessed by the Buddha himself. It makes him immune to spirit attacks." Her jaw tightened. "I have tried to kill him in every lifetime. I have failed in every lifetime."

"So this is a stalemate."

"For now."

"But he knows where you are. He'll be back."

"Yes."

Xu Ming set down the glass. His hands had stopped shaking. Somewhere in the chaos, his fear had burned away, replaced by something harder. Something that felt almost like acceptance.

"Then we need a plan."

"I have been planning for a thousand years. There is no plan that works."

"Then we need a new plan." Xu Ming walked to the back of the shop, where his grandmother's old books were kept. He pulled one down—a medical text from the Qing Dynasty, handwritten, filled with remedies for ailments that no longer existed. "My grandmother believed that every poison had an antidote. Every curse had a cure. If she was right, then Fahai has a weakness. Something that can break his relic's protection."

Bai Suyin's eyes widened. "I have searched for centuries. There is no record—"

"Then we make a record." Xu Ming flipped through the pages. "Tell me everything you know about the staff. Where it came from. Who made it. What prayers were used to bless it."

"Why does that matter?"

"Because blessing and curse are the same thing, just facing different directions. If we know how the staff was blessed, we might find a way to undo it."

Bai Suyin was silent for a long moment. Then she walked to his side, her wet dress trailing water across the floor.

"The staff was forged during the Tang Dynasty. A monk named Faqing spent forty years meditating in a cave, subsisting on nothing but dew and wild herbs. When he emerged, he carried the staff. It was said that Buddha himself had blessed it in a dream."

"Can it be stolen?"

"Fahai never lets it leave his sight. He sleeps with it in his hands. He eats with it across his knees."

"Can it be destroyed?"

"Fire cannot touch it. Water cannot rust it. Metal cannot scratch it." Bai Suyin's voice dropped. "I once watched him stand in a dragon's breath for three minutes. The staff did not even grow warm."

Xu Ming closed the book. "Then we don't destroy it. We change it."

"What?"

"Your heart trial. You said it's about love. Real love. So what if we find a way to complete it faster? To force the transformation before Fahai can attack?"

"That is not how it works. The trial cannot be rushed—"

"Then we don't rush it. We weaponize it." Xu Ming's mind was racing now, connecting dots that had no business being connected. "Fahai hunts spirits because he believes they are evil. But if a spirit could prove itself worthy of humanity—if the Buddha himself recognized that transformation—wouldn't that undermine everything he stands for?"

Bai Suyin stared at him. "You want to use my trial as a weapon against him."

"I want to use your love as a weapon against him."

Something flickered in her eyes. Hope, perhaps. Or fear of hope.

"It has never been done," she whispered.

"Good." Xu Ming grabbed his coat. "Then we're doing something new."

"Where are you going?"

"To find answers." He paused at the door. "You said Fahai walked the city like a wolf. If he's tracking you, he's not just using magic. He's using information. Someone is helping him on the ground."

"The temples. His order has spies everywhere."

"Then we need our own source of information." Xu Ming opened the door. Rain splashed against the pavement. "I know a man. He runs an antique shop on Hefang Street. He deals in things that should not exist."

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin caught his arm. "If you leave this shop, Fahai will know. He has eyes everywhere. He will follow you."

"Then I'll lead him away from you."

"That is suicide."

"No." Xu Ming met her eyes. "That's a distraction."

He stepped into the rain and disappeared into the night.

Bai Suyin stood alone in the shattered shop, her heart pounding against her ribs. For the first time in a thousand years, someone had chosen to protect her instead of the other way around.

She did not know whether to laugh or weep.

So she did neither. She waited.

And somewhere in the darkness of Hangzhou, Fahai watched Xu Ming's progress through a bowl of still water, his golden eyes tracking the pharmacist's every step.

"Interesting," he murmured. "Very interesting."

He reached into his robe and pulled out a small bronze bell. He rang it once.

From the shadows behind him, a figure emerged. Tall. Hooded. Silent.

"Follow him. Do not engage. Report everything."

The figure bowed and vanished into the rain.

Fahai looked back at the water bowl. Xu Ming was walking faster now, almost running. Heading toward Hefang Street. Toward danger.

"Let us see how far your courage takes you, little pharmacist."

He smiled.

And in the reflection of the water, his smile was not human at all.

title: The Third Heartbeat
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes swept across the shop like a blade. Xu Ming felt it pass through him—cold, searching, hungry. It lingered on the shelves of herbs, the mortar and pestle, the prescription notes pinned to the wall. Then it settled on Bai Suyin.

She had not moved. Her hand was still pressed against his chest, her heartbeat still echoing in his palm. But something had changed in her posture. The fear was gone. In its place was a stillness so absolute it felt like the room had stopped breathing.

"Eight hundred years," she said, her voice flat. "And you still don't know when to knock."

Fahai's smile did not waver. He stepped fully into the shop, his robes brushing against shattered glass. The prayer beads in his hand clicked with each step—one, two, three—a rhythm that felt like a countdown. He was taller than Xu Ming remembered. Broader. The kind of man who could fill a doorway without trying.

"The snake hides in a pharmacy," Fahai said, almost amused. "How poetic. You always did have a taste for the mundane."

"I have a taste for survival."

"Then you understand why I must end this." He raised his hand. The prayer beads began to glow—a sickly yellow light that pulsed like a heartbeat. "You have had a thousand years to complete your trial. You have failed. Every life, every incarnation, you reach for humanity and fall short. It is time to accept what you are."

"And what am I, according to you?"

"A serpent. A thing of instinct and hunger. No matter how you dress yourself, no matter how many mortals you charm, you will always be what you were born as." His voice softened, almost kind. "I am not your enemy, Bai Suyin. I am your liberation."

Xu Ming felt her hand tighten against his chest. Her nails bit through his shirt. He looked down and saw that they were elongating again—not the full transformation, but a hint of it. A warning.

"Don't," he whispered.

She looked at him. Her eyes were still human brown, but there was something moving in the depths of them, like a shadow passing beneath still water.

"You heard him," she said. "He wants to kill me."

"I heard him say he wants to save you."

"Same thing. To Fahai, they are the same thing."

Fahai took another step forward. The golden light from his eyes intensified, casting long shadows across the room. The bottles on Xu Ming's shelves began to rattle again, but this time it was not from Bai Suyin's power. It was from his.

"You have until the third heartbeat," Fahai said. "Release the mortal. Shed your human skin. Return to the wild, and I will let you live."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then I will purify you here. In front of your chosen one. So he can see exactly what he has been holding."

Bai Suyin turned to face him fully. She released Xu Ming's hand and stepped forward, placing herself between him and the monk. Her wet dress clung to her body, and for a moment Xu Ming saw her not as a woman or a spirit, but as something older. Something that had survived millennia of hunters and fires and drownings.

"You think I am afraid of you?" she said. "I have faced dynasties. I have watched empires turn to dust. I have been burned, stabbed, drowned, and buried. And I am still here. You are just another man with a holy book and a grudge."

Fahai's smile vanished. The prayer beads stopped glowing. For a moment, the shop was silent except for the rain hammering against the roof.

Then he moved.

Xu Ming did not see him cross the room. One moment Fahai was at the door. The next, he was directly in front of Bai Suyin, his hand wrapped around her throat, lifting her off the ground.

"No," Xu Ming said.

But the word was meaningless. He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs. He did not fight monks who glowed with golden light.

Bai Suyin did not struggle. She hung in Fahai's grip like a doll, her feet dangling, her face calm. Her eyes met Xu Ming's.

"Run," she said.

"I won't."

"Run, Xu Ming. This is not your fight."

"Yes it is." He grabbed the nearest thing—a jar of ginseng—and threw it at Fahai's head. It shattered against the monk's skull. Fahai did not even blink.

But the distraction was enough.

Bai Suyin twisted in his grip. Her hand shot up and caught his wrist. There was a crack—sharp, wet, final—and Fahai's grip loosened. She dropped to the floor, rolled, and came up with a shard of glass in her hand.

"You broke my wrist," Fahai said. He sounded almost impressed.

"You're lucky I didn't take the arm."

Fahai laughed. It was a terrible sound, hollow and old. "You still think this is a fight between equals. I have been hunting your kind for longer than you have been alive, snake. I know every trick. Every escape. Every weakness."

He flexed his broken wrist. The bones realigned with a series of pops that made Xu Ming's stomach turn. The golden light flared, and when it faded, the wrist was whole.

"You see?" Fahai said. "I am not what I was when we last met. I have grown. Evolved. While you spent centuries playing house with mortals, I spent them learning how to kill gods."

Bai Suyin's face went white. "You're lying."

"I never lie." He reached into his robe and pulled out a small bronze mirror. It was ancient, crusted with verdigris, but when he held it up, the surface reflected nothing. "Do you know what this is?"

She did not answer. But Xu Ming saw her hands start to shake.

"This is the Mirror of True Sight," Fahai continued. "Forged from the bronze of a temple that burned with a thousand monks inside. It reflects only the truth. And I have been holding it for seven hundred years, waiting for the moment I could use it on you."

He raised the mirror.

Bai Suyin screamed.

It was not a human scream. It was high and piercing, a sound that seemed to vibrate in Xu Ming's bones. She collapsed to her knees, her hands covering her face. The mirror's surface rippled, and Xu Ming saw something move inside it—a shape, white and vast, coiling through darkness.

"What are you doing to her?" Xu Ming demanded.

"Showing her what she truly is." Fahai's voice was calm, almost gentle. "She has spent so long wearing human skin that she has forgotten the monster beneath. I am reminding her."

The shape in the mirror grew clearer. Xu Ming saw scales. A massive head. Eyes that glowed like moons. It was beautiful and terrible, and he understood, suddenly, why Bai Suyin had been afraid to show him her true form.

Because it was not a woman in that mirror. It was a god. Ancient. Hungry. And utterly inhuman.

"Stop," Xu Ming said.

"Or what?"

"I said stop."

He grabbed the mirror from Fahai's hand.

It burned him instantly. The bronze seared his palm like hot iron, and he dropped it with a cry. The mirror clattered to the floor, cracked, and went dark.

Bai Suyin gasped. Her hands fell from her face. She was crying—real tears, human tears—and she looked at him with an expression he could not read.

"You touched it," she whispered.

"I had to."

"Xu Ming, I could have—"

"Shut up." He pulled her to her feet. His hand was still smoking, the skin blistered and red, but he did not let go. "We're leaving. Now."

Fahai watched them, his golden eyes unreadable. "You cannot run from me. I will find you. I will always find you."

"Then find us," Xu Ming said. "But do it somewhere that isn't my shop."

He dragged Bai Suyin towards the back door. She stumbled after him, still weak, still shaking. The rain hit them like a wall as they stepped outside, cold and relentless.

"Where are we going?" she asked.

"I don't know."

"You don't know?"

"I'm making this up as I go." He pulled her down the alley, past dumpsters and broken streetlights, towards the river. "You said you needed me to love you. You said that would let you fight him. So tell me—"

He stopped. Turned to face her.

"What do I need to do?"

She stared at him. The rain had washed the tears from her face, but her eyes were still red. She looked younger than she had in the shop. Vulnerable.

"You need to choose," she said.

"Choose what?"

"Whether you want to love a woman or a snake. Because you cannot have both. Fahai is right about one thing—I am not human. I wear this skin like a coat, and I can shed it at any moment. If you want to love me, you have to love the thing underneath. The monster."

She waited. The rain fell. The city hummed around them, indifferent to their small drama.

Xu Ming thought of the mirror. The shape in the darkness. The eyes that glowed like moons.

He thought of the way she had held his hand when she showed him the truth of her heart.

"I don't know what I choose," he said. "But I know I'm not done trying."

Bai Suyin smiled. It was a sad smile, tired and ancient.

"That is the most honest thing anyone has ever said to me."

She reached up and touched his face. Her fingers were cold. Her touch was light.

And then she kissed him.

It was not gentle. It was desperate, hungry, tasting of rain and copper and something older, something that hummed in his blood like a second heartbeat.

When she pulled back, her eyes were no longer brown.

They were gold.

"Fahai," she said.

Xu Ming turned.

The monk stood at the end of the alley, his broken mirror in one hand, his prayer beads glowing in the other. Behind him, the streetlights flickered and died.

"I gave you a chance," Fahai said. "Now I give you nothing."

He raised his hand.

The prayer beads exploded into light.

And Xu Ming felt the world fall away.

title: The Scales Between Us
content:
The golden eyes held me for exactly three seconds before I moved.

I didn't think. I acted. My hand found the jar of powdered ghost pepper—the one I kept for particularly stubborn patients who refused to taste their medicine—and I threw it directly into Fahai's face.

He didn't flinch. The powder passed through him like smoke through a screen.

"Clever," he said. "But ineffective."

Bai Suyin grabbed my collar and pulled me backward, through the curtain into the storage room. Her strength was terrifying. I felt like a child being dragged by a current.

"Medicine cabinet," she hissed. "Left side. Third shelf. Blue bottle."

I didn't ask questions. I found it—a small ceramic bottle with a cork stopper, no label, filled with something that smelled like burning flowers.

"Drink it."

"What is it?"

"Protection. Against his sight. He won't be able to see your soul."

I hesitated. The liquid was thick, almost syrupy. It glowed faintly in the dark.

"Xu Ming." Her voice cracked. "Please."

I drank.

The taste was ash and honey and something alive. It slid down my throat like a living thing, coiling in my stomach, and suddenly the world sharpened. I could see threads of light in the air—golden threads connecting Bai Suyin to everything around her. The shelves. The floor. Me.

"What did you just give me?"

"Eyes," she said. "Now you can see what I see."

Fahai stepped through the curtain. He didn't push it aside. He walked through it, and the fabric didn't even ripple.

The golden threads around Bai Suyin flared. They were connected to him too. A thick, pulsing cord of light that stretched from her chest to his palm. He was holding it. Pulling it taut.

"Eight hundred years," Fahai said softly. "How many times have we done this dance? You find a mortal. You hide. I find you. You run again."

"This time is different." Bai Suyin's voice was steady, but I could feel her trembling through the threads. "He drank the Sight. He knows what I am. There are no lies between us."

"Worse," Fahai said. "He knows what you are, and he stayed. That makes him a fool, not a protector."

I should have been afraid. I was afraid. But the potion had done something to my fear—pushed it to the side, made it manageable.

"What do you want?" I asked.

Fahai's golden eyes turned to me. The full weight of his attention hit like a physical force. I felt my knees buckle.

"I want what I have always wanted," he said. "Purity. Order. The boundary between species to remain unbroken. She is a snake who seeks to become human. That is an abomination."

"She's a person," I said.

"She is a spirit wearing a stolen face." He stepped closer. The golden cord between them tightened. "Do you know what happens when a snake succeeds in becoming human? She takes a life that was never meant for her. She occupies a space in the world that belongs to someone else. Every breath she takes as a human is a breath stolen from a soul that never got to be born."

"That's not—" I looked at Bai Suyin.

She wouldn't meet my eyes.

"Is that true?"

"I don't know," she whispered. "I've never made it that far. He always stops me first."

"But it could be true."

"It could be. Or it could be his lie. I don't know, Xu Ming. I've spent a thousand years trying to become something I wasn't born as. I've told myself it was beautiful. Righteous. But I've never asked what it costs."

Fahai smiled. It was the worst thing I'd seen on a human face.

"Finally," he said. "A crack in the illusion."

"Shut up." I stepped between them. "You don't get to use me as a weapon against her."

"Too late. You already are."

He raised his hand. The golden cord snapped taut, and Bai Suyin screamed.

It wasn't a human sound. It was high and keening, a frequency that made the bottles on my shelves shatter. Her form flickered—human, snake, human again—scales crawling up her neck like living tattoos.

"Stop!" I grabbed Fahai's arm.

He didn't move. He felt like stone wrapped in silk.

"You cannot touch me, pharmacist. I have purified my body for eight centuries. No mortal hand can—"

I bit him.

It was stupid. Desperate. But I sank my teeth into his wrist and tasted blood that burned like holy water.

He yanked his arm back, shock breaking his concentration. The golden cord went slack. Bai Suyin collapsed.

"You bit me." He stared at the wound. Blood dripped onto the floor, and where it landed, the wood blackened and smoked.

"You're bleeding," I said. "That means you're still human. Or close enough to be hurt."

He laughed. It was the worst sound I'd ever heard—joy and rage mixed into something inhuman.

"Eight hundred years," he said, "and no one has ever thought to bite me. You're either very brave or very stupid."

"Both," I said. "Now get out of my shop."

"I will leave," he said, "because I choose to. But understand this, Xu Ming. The potion she gave you—the Sight—it has a price. Every time you use it, you see more of the truth. And the truth will destroy you."

He turned to Bai Suyin. "I will give you three days. Three days to say goodbye to this life. Then I will find you, and I will finish what I started in the Tang Dynasty."

He walked out. This time, he used the door.

The moment he was gone, Bai Suyin collapsed fully. I caught her before she hit the ground. She was cold. Too cold. Her skin felt like scales.

"The potion," I said. "What did it really do?"

"It let you see." Her voice was barely a whisper. "But he's right. There's a price."

"What price?"

She looked up at me. Her eyes were fading—the snake colors bleeding out, leaving only human brown.

"You see the threads now. The connections between all things. But those connections go both ways. You can see me. Which means they can see you."

"Who?"

"The ones like me. The spirits. The old powers. Before tonight, you were invisible to them. A mortal in a mortal city. Now..." She touched my face. "Now you're marked. Every spirit within a hundred miles can feel you. They know something woke up tonight."

I helped her stand. The shop was a wreck. Broken glass everywhere. Medicine spilled across the floor. My small, safe life shattered around us.

"What do we do now?"

"We run," she said. "But first, I need to show you something."

She led me to the back room. Her bag was still there, the one she'd brought the first day. She opened it and pulled out a scroll—ancient yellow paper, tied with red silk.

"This is the truth," she said. "The reason Fahai has hunted me for so long. It's not because I'm a snake. It's because of what I carry."

She untied the silk.

The scroll unrolled by itself, floating in the air between us. Characters written in gold ink glowed in the dark. I couldn't read them, but I understood them. The potion translated meaning directly into my mind.

It was a map. A map of the world, but wrong. The continents were shifted. There were cities in places where oceans should be. And at the center, in the space where China should be, there was a wound. A bleeding gash in the fabric of reality.

"This is the world before the Veil," she said. "Before the spirits were pushed into the shadows. A thousand years ago, we walked openly. Humans knew us. Feared us. Loved us. Then something happened. Someone closed the door between worlds."

"Fahai?"

"No. He's just a guard. A gatekeeper. The one who closed the door was..." She hesitated. "It was me."

"You closed the door?"

"I was young. Angry. A human I loved had been killed by my own kind. In my grief, I made a deal with powers I didn't understand. I sealed the spirit world away from the human world. Trapped billions of beings on the other side. And I became the key."

"The key?"

She touched her chest. "I am the lock. The only way to reopen the door is through me. Through my blood. Through my death. Fahai doesn't want to kill me because I'm a snake. He wants to kill me because I'm a prison, and he wants to let the prisoners out."

The map rotated slowly. The wound at its center pulsed like a heartbeat.

"There are things on the other side," she whispered. "Things older than humanity. Things that remember when the world was theirs. If Fahai opens that door, everyone you love dies. Everyone I've ever tried to protect dies. The world burns."

"Why would he want that?"

"Because he believes it's the natural order. That humans stole the world from spirits, and he's here to give it back." She laughed bitterly. "He thinks he's the hero."

I looked at the map. At the wound. At her face, pale and exhausted and still somehow beautiful.

"Three days," I said. "He gave us three days."

"That's not a gift. It's a test. He wants to see what I'll do. Who I'll try to save. He's hunting my hope."

"Then we don't give him any."

She looked at me. For the first time since I'd met her, something other than fear or desperation flickered in her eyes.

"What are you saying?"

"I'm saying we fight. I'm saying we find another way. I'm saying you've been running for a thousand years, and I'm tired of watching you run."

"You're a pharmacist."

"I'm a man who bit a monk who walked through a wall. I think I've graduated from pharmacist."

She laughed. It was small and broken, but it was real.

"Your potion," I said. "Does it work both ways? Can you see me now?"

She nodded. "I've always been able to see you. From the moment you walked into that temple. You were the brightest thing I'd seen in centuries."

"What does that mean?"

"It means I didn't choose you randomly. The trial doesn't work that way. I saw you. Felt you. Knew that you were the only one who could help me." She took my hand. Her fingers were cold, but they interlaced with mine perfectly. "It means I've been in love with you since before we met. And I've been terrified of that love since the moment I recognized it."

The shop was destroyed. A monk with golden eyes was hunting us. The world was hanging by a thread.

And I was holding hands with a snake spirit who'd just told me she loved me.

"I need to check on something," I said. "A theory."

"What theory?"

"There's a text. In my family's collection. My grandfather left me a box of old books, said I'd know when to open them." I'd always thought it was a joke. My grandfather was a drunk who died when I was twelve. But now...

"Show me."

We went upstairs. My apartment was small, cluttered with the debris of a quiet life. The box was under my bed, dusty and forgotten.

title: The Serpent's Price
content:
The rain had stopped, but the air still trembled with the aftermath of Fahai's attack. Xu Ming stood in the wreckage of his shop, glass crunching beneath his shoes, trying to process what he had just witnessed.

Bai Suyin had vanished.

Not fled. *Vanished.* One moment she had been standing between him and Fahai, her scales shimmering in the broken light. The next, she had dissolved into mist that poured through the cracks in the floorboards like water seeking earth.

And Fahai had laughed.

"Clever," the monk had said, his golden eyes scanning the empty shop. "She always was good at running. But she cannot run forever."

Then he had turned and walked into the night, leaving Xu Ming alone with the smell of burnt incense and the echo of a thousand years of hatred.

That had been three hours ago.

Now Xu Ming sat in the back room, a cold cup of tea in his hands, waiting for something he couldn't name. His phone buzzed. Then again. Then a third time.

He picked it up.

*Xu Ming, it's Chen Wei. You didn't come to work today. Is everything okay?*

*Your uncle is worried. Call him.*

*I'm coming by after my shift.*

He typed a quick response: *I'm fine. Don't come.*

The lie tasted bitter on his tongue. He wasn't fine. His shop was destroyed. His life was unraveling. And the woman he was falling for was a snake spirit being hunted by a mad monk.

He should run. Call the police. Pretend none of this had happened.

But even as he thought it, he knew he wouldn't.

Because she had looked at him with those ancient eyes and said, *I need you to love me.*

And somewhere between the exploding door and the swirling mist, he had realized he already did.

The back door creaked.

Xu Ming's heart stopped. He reached for the mortar and pestle on the counter—the only weapon he had—and held it tight.

"Xu Ming."

Her voice. Weak. Broken.

He turned.

Bai Suyin stood in the doorway, but she was barely recognizable. Her dress was torn. Her face was pale. And her left arm—her left arm was gone.

Not bleeding. Not wounded. *Gone,* as if it had never existed, the sleeve of her dress hanging empty and flat against her side.

"What happened?" He rushed to her, dropping the mortar, reaching for her with trembling hands.

"Fahai's binding." She swayed, and he caught her before she could fall. "He touched me. Just a brush of his fingers against my shoulder. But his power... it's like acid. It burns through cultivation. I had to shed the arm to escape."

"Shed the arm." Xu Ming's voice cracked. "You mean you—"

"I mean I cut it off myself." Her smile was thin and terrible. "Do not look so horrified. It will grow back. In a month, perhaps two. But for now, I am diminished."

He helped her to the worn couch in the corner, the same couch where she had first told him her story. She collapsed onto it, her body curling into itself, her remaining hand pressed against her chest.

"I need yin energy," she whispered. "The pure yang of Fahai's binding is poisoning my core. If I do not balance it, I will lose more than an arm."

"Yin energy? What does that mean?"

She looked at him with eyes that held entire dynasties. "It means I need you to hold me. To breathe with me. To share your life force while I heal."

"That sounds like—"

"Like making love? Yes. It is similar. But not the same. I do not need your body, Xu Ming. I need your *essence.* The part of you that is soft and human and alive."

He should have felt afraid. Or disgusted. Or at least hesitant.

But the sight of her—broken, bleeding, diminished—stripped away all his defenses. She had been a thousand-year-old spirit, a creature of myth and power. And now she was curled on his couch, missing an arm, asking him to save her life.

"Tell me what to do."

She guided him through it. They sat facing each other, knees touching, hands pressed palm to palm. She closed her eyes and began to breathe in a rhythm that seemed to pull at something deep in his chest.

"Breathe with me," she murmured. "In for four. Hold for four. Out for four."

He followed her rhythm, and slowly, impossibly, he felt something moving between them. Not heat, exactly. More like a current, a flow of energy that started in his core and traveled up his arms, into her hands, through her body.

She gasped.

"Too much," she whispered. "You are giving too much."

"I don't know how to control it."

"Then stop. Slow down. I need only a thread, not a river."

He tried to pull back, but the current had its own will now. It surged through him, drawing from depths he hadn't known existed, pouring into her like water into parched earth.

"Xu Ming." Her voice was sharp now. "Stop."

"I can't."

Her eyes snapped open. For a moment, he saw her true form flickering behind her human face—the serpent, vast and ancient, coiled in the space between worlds.

"You are feeding me your life," she said. "Not your energy. Your *life.* If you continue, you will die."

"I don't care."

"You should."

"I don't."

He meant it. Every word. And the truth of it hit him like a physical blow.

He would die for her. He would let her drain him dry if it meant she could survive. And that was terrifying, not because he feared death, but because he had never loved anything enough to offer himself so completely.

The current slowed. Then stopped.

Bai Suyin pulled her hands away, her eyes wet with tears that looked like liquid silver.

"You love me," she said. Not a question. A statement.

"Yes."

"Truly?"

"More than I understand."

She closed her eyes, and a single silver tear traced down her cheek. "Then I am sorry."

"For what?"

"For what I must tell you."

She opened her eyes, and in them he saw the weight of centuries. "The heart trial is not about love. It is about *sacrifice.* For a serpent to become human, she must experience the full depth of mortal love—including its ending."

"What kind of ending?"

She looked away. "The only ending that matters."

A cold hand gripped Xu Ming's heart. "You mean death."

"I mean loss." Her voice was barely a whisper. "For the trial to complete, I must lose you. Not because I choose to. But because the universe demands balance. A snake cannot become human without paying the price of human grief."

"When?"

"When the trial is complete. When I am fully human. At the moment of my greatest happiness, you will be taken from me."

Xu Ming's hands trembled. "Taken how?"

"I do not know. The trial does not reveal its methods. But in every telling, in every legend, the mortal lover dies. It is the only way the serpent learns what it means to be human—to love so completely that loss becomes unbearable."

He wanted to scream. To throw something. To deny everything she had just said.

But the truth was written in her eyes, ancient and terrible and absolute.

"So this is doomed," he said. "From the beginning."

"All love is doomed, Xu Ming. Every human who loves knows they will eventually lose. That is the tragedy of mortality. That is what I have been trying to understand for a thousand years."

"Is that supposed to make me feel better?"

"No." She reached for him, her remaining hand cupping his cheek. "It is supposed to make you choose."

"Choose what?"

"Whether you would rather have me for a short time and lose me forever, or never have me at all."

The rain started again, a gentle patter against the broken window. Somewhere in the distance, a temple bell tolled midnight.

Xu Ming took her hand. Pressed it against his heart.

"I choose you," he said. "For whatever time we have."

Her smile was the most beautiful and heartbreaking thing he had ever seen.

"Then let us not waste it."

She leaned forward, and her lips met his.

The kiss tasted like rain and jasmine and a thousand years of longing. And as the world spun around them, Xu Ming felt something shift—not in the room, but in the fabric of reality itself.

A thread had been cut.

A new one had been woven.

And somewhere in the darkness, a monk with golden eyes stopped walking and smiled.

"Finally," Fahai whispered, raising his prayer beads to the moon. "She has bonded. Now she is vulnerable. Now she can die."

He turned and began walking back toward Xu Ming's shop, his footsteps silent on the wet stones.

The hunt was over.

The killing was about to begin.

title: The Scales of Betrayal
content:
The bullet hole in Xu Ming's shoulder had stopped bleeding, but the wound had turned an ugly purple around the edges. Snake venom did that—corrupted flesh faster than any infection he'd seen in his pharmacy. He'd treated snake bites before. Farmers from the outskirts, hikers who'd wandered into the wrong bamboo grove. Never a bite from a spirit.

Never a bite from the same woman whose blood he'd tasted an hour ago.

Bai Suyin knelt beside him in the basement of the abandoned textile factory, her hands glowing faintly as she pressed them against his shoulder. The warmth was pleasant. The venom dissolving under her touch was not.

"You're lucky," she said, not looking at him. "The Black Serpent's poison is meant to paralyze. Immobilize. Then consume."

"Feels lucky."

"Stay still."

He stayed still. Not because she told him to, but because moving sent spikes of fire through his chest. The basement smelled of mildew and old machine oil. Above them, the factory creaked and groaned, a skeleton of rusted steel waiting for the wrecking ball.

Zhang Linlin sat against the far wall, her knees drawn to her chest, watching them with hollow eyes. The Heishui agent had said nothing since they'd escaped through the subway tunnel. She'd just followed, silent as a ghost, her gun still in her hand.

"Who shot me?" Xu Ming asked.

"Does it matter?"

"It matters."

Bai Suyin's hands paused. Her eyes met his. "A hunter. One of Fahai's disciples who has abandoned the cloth for the gun. They call themselves the Scale-Breakers."

"Charming name."

"They are not charming men." She resumed her work. The glow intensified. The pain in his shoulder began to ebb, replaced by a deep, spreading numbness. "They believe that any spirit who seeks humanity is an abomination. That we must be kept in our true forms, crawling in the dirt where we belong."

"And Fahai?"

"Fahai is worse. He does not hate us. He pities us. He believes he is saving our souls by destroying our bodies."

Xu Ming closed his eyes. The factory groaned above them. Somewhere in the distance, a siren wailed and faded.

"How long?" he asked.

"Until what?"

"Until you're strong enough to fight him."

Bai Suyin was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was barely a whisper. "I should have told you the truth from the beginning."

"Which truth? There've been so many."

She flinched. It was subtle—a tightening around her eyes, a slight withdrawal of her hands—but he caught it. He caught everything now. The venom had sharpened his senses, made him aware of details he'd never noticed before. The way her pulse fluttered in her throat. The way her scent changed when she lied.

"The truth about my trial," she said. "About what it really means."

"I'm listening."

She stood. Walked to the basement's only window—a narrow slit near the ceiling that showed only darkness and rain. "Every spirit who seeks to become human must pass through three trials. The Heart Trial, which you know. The Body Trial, where we shed our scales and learn to walk on two legs. And the Soul Trial, where we give up our immortality."

"I thought you already gave that up."

"I gave up the *form* of immortality. The endless years. The ageless body. But the soul of a spirit is different from the soul of a human. It is... colder. More patient. More calculating."

She turned to face him. In the dim light, her eyes seemed to glow—not with power, but with something that looked almost like fear.

"To complete the Soul Trial, I must give up that coldness. I must learn to feel as humans feel. Fully. Completely. Without reservation."

"And?"

"And I cannot do that while I am still carrying the weight of my past." She took a breath. "Every spirit who completes the Soul Trial must first confront their greatest failure. The thing they regret most. The wound they have never allowed to heal."

Xu Ming's mouth went dry. "What's yours?"

"I told you I had a teacher. That Fahai killed her."

"Yes."

"I lied."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Zhang Linlin looked up, suddenly alert. Bai Suyin's face was unreadable.

"My teacher was not killed by Fahai," she said. "She was killed by me."

Xu Ming's shoulder throbbed. The numbness was spreading down his arm now, into his fingers. "Explain."

"Five hundred years ago, I was not as I am now. I was young. Reckless. I believed that power was the only path to freedom. My teacher tried to guide me, to show me that true strength came from understanding, not force." Her voice dropped. "I did not listen."

"Go on."

"There was a village. A small village in the mountains where humans and spirits lived in peace. My teacher had built that peace over centuries. But I was impatient. I wanted more. I wanted to prove that spirits were superior, that we should rule rather than coexist."

She closed her eyes. When she spoke again, her voice was raw.

"I challenged the village's guardian spirit. A dragon of the eastern mountains, ancient and wise. I thought I could defeat him. I was wrong."

"What happened?"

"He destroyed me. Not my body—I was too strong for that. But my cultivation. My progress. He stripped away five hundred years of growth in a single breath." She opened her eyes. "My teacher found me broken in the snow. She carried me back to our mountain. She spent three months healing me, feeding me her own essence, rebuilding what I had lost."

"And then?"

"Then Fahai came."

Xu Ming felt the cold before he heard the name. A chill that had nothing to do with the basement's damp air.

"He did not find us," Bai Suyin said. "I led him there. I was so desperate for revenge against the dragon that I made a deal with the Abbot. I told him where my teacher was hiding. I told him what she was. I traded her life for a chance to destroy my enemy."

"Bai Suyin."

"I watched him burn her. I watched her turn to ash while I stood beside him, and I did nothing." Her voice cracked. "I did everything. I held the torch."

The silence stretched. Above them, the factory groaned. Xu Ming's shoulder throbbed with a rhythm that matched his heartbeat.

"Why are you telling me this now?"

"Because Fahai knows." She turned to face him fully. "He has been holding this secret for five centuries. Waiting for the right moment. The right wound." She stepped closer. "He will use it to destroy us. He will tell you that I am incapable of love. That my soul is too cold. That I will betray you the moment it becomes convenient."

"Will you?"

"I don't know."

The answer was so honest it cut deeper than any lie.

"I don't know," she repeated. "I have been trying to become human for a thousand years. I have failed every time. Because every time, when the final trial comes, I choose myself. I choose survival. I choose the cold."

She reached out. Her hand hovered near his face, not quite touching.

"You deserve someone who can love you without reservation. Someone who has not already proven that they are capable of the worst betrayal."

Xu Ming caught her wrist. His grip was weak—the venom was still working through him—but it was enough.

"Your teacher," he said. "Did she forgive you?"

Bai Suyin's breath caught.

"Before she died. Did she forgive you?"

"She said..." Her voice broke. "She said that the only failure was refusing to try again. She said that becoming human was not about being perfect. It was about being willing to change."

"Then that's your answer."

He pulled her hand to his chest, pressed her palm against his heart.

"You failed five hundred years ago. You were young and stupid and cruel. But you are not that person anymore. I have seen you. The real you. The woman who brings herbs to sick children. The woman who gave up her immortality for a chance to be human."

"Xu Ming—"

"I don't know if I love you yet." He held her gaze. "But I know that I want to try. And I know that you are worth trying for."

Her eyes glistened. For a moment, she looked almost human. Fragile. Hopeful.

Then the factory above them exploded.

Concrete dust rained down. The basement shook. Xu Ming's shoulder screamed as he was thrown sideways, his healing wound tearing open again.

Bai Suyin was already moving. She was at the stairs before the dust settled, her body shifting, scales rippling across her skin.

"Stay here," she hissed.

"Like hell—"

"Xu Ming. Please."

The fear in her voice stopped him cold.

"I cannot fight him if I am worried about you. Stay here. Protect Linlin. If I do not return in ten minutes, take the tunnel beneath the basement floor. It leads to an old canal. Follow it south until you reach the lake."

"Bai Suyin—"

She kissed him. It was quick and fierce and tasted like copper and rain.

Then she was gone.

The basement fell silent. Xu Ming pressed his hand against his shoulder, feeling the blood seep through his fingers. Zhang Linlin stared at the stairs, her face pale.

"What was that?" she whispered.

"I don't know."

But he did know. He knew exactly what had just happened.

Fahai had found them. And Bai Suyin had gone to face him alone.

Xu Ming struggled to his feet. The world tilted. His vision blurred. But he forced himself to stand, to take one step, then another, toward the stairs.

"What are you doing?" Zhang Linlin asked.

"I'm going after her."

"She told you to stay."

"She also told me she loved me." He grabbed the railing, pulled himself up the first step. "And I'm not going to let her fight that monster alone."

"Xu Ming—"

The door at the top of the stairs burst open.

But it was not Fahai who stood there.

It was a woman. Young. Beautiful. Dressed in white silk that seemed to glow in the darkness. Her eyes were not human—they were slit-pupiled, golden, ancient.

"Hello, Xu Ming," she said.

Her voice was familiar. Too familiar.

His blood went cold.

"I am Bai Suyin's sister," the woman said. "And I have come to warn you."

The last thing he saw before everything went black was her smile—a predator's smile, full of teeth and hunger.

Behind it, Fahai's voice chanted sutras, and Bai Suyin screamed.

title: The Price of a Thousand Years
content:
The golden coins of Fahai's eyes swept across the shattered doorframe, and the rain followed him inside—sheets of water sliding off his shoulders like oil, never touching his skin. His robes were dry. His hands were still. His smile was the most patient thing Xu Ming had ever seen.

"Demon," Fahai repeated, tasting the word. "An interesting translation. The original text uses the character *yao*—monster. Beast. Something that should not exist in human form."

Bai Suyin moved before Xu Ming could blink. She stepped between them, her body blocking Fahai's line of sight, her spine straight as a blade. "You don't get to speak to him."

"The pharmacist?" Fahai's head tilted. "He is already marked. Your essence clings to his skin like perfume. He will carry your stench for the rest of his life, however short that may be."

"Enough." Her voice dropped to a register Xu Ming had never heard—something ancient, something that made the floorboards hum beneath his feet. "You want me. You have me. Leave him out of this."

"Touching." Fahai's prayer beads clicked once. "The serpent plays the martyr. But we both know why you chose him. The *xīn mó*—the heart trial. You need him to love you. Willingly. Purely." His smile sharpened. "Or did you think I didn't know?"

Xu Ming's blood went cold.

"I know everything, Bai Suyin. I know the names of every village you burned during the Song Dynasty. I know the face of every scholar you seduced and abandoned during the Yuan. I know the exact weight of karma pressing down on your immortal soul." Fahai stepped forward. The shattered wood crunched under his sandals. "And I know that if he stops loving you—if he dies, if he rejects you, if he simply wakes up one morning and decides you are not worth the danger—your cultivation collapses. You revert to base form. A snake. Stupid. Blind. Hunting rats in the mud."

Bai Suyin's hands curled into fists. "You've been chasing me for eight hundred years. You've never caught me. What makes tonight different?"

"Because tonight, you have something to lose."

Fahai's eyes flicked to Xu Ming.

And Xu Ming understood.

He wasn't a shield. He wasn't shelter. He was a hostage. A weakness that Fahai could exploit, break, or simply wait out. Bai Suyin had bound herself to a mortal, which meant she had tied her fate to something fragile. Something Fahai could shatter with a single well-aimed strike.

The realization hit him like a physical blow.

*She chose me because I was weak.*

He stumbled back. His hip hit the counter. Bottles rattled. The sound drew both their gazes—Bai Suyin's desperate, Fahai's amused.

"Ah." Fahai's voice softened. "He's beginning to understand."

"Don't listen to him." Bai Suyin's hand shot out, grabbed his wrist. Her fingers were cold. Shaking. "Xu Ming. Look at me. I chose you because you were *kind*. Because you helped a stranger without asking for payment. Because you looked at me like I was human."

"Were you human?" The question came out before he could stop it.

She flinched.

"Were you ever human, or was that just... performance?"

"I was trying." Her voice cracked. "I have been trying for a thousand years. Every life, every face, every name—I have been learning what it means to be human. And I am not finished yet. But you..." She squeezed his wrist so hard his fingers went numb. "You made me feel like I was already there."

Fahai laughed.

It was not a pleasant sound. It was the laugh of someone who had heard every excuse, every plea, every desperate confession across multiple lifetimes. "Beautiful. Genuinely beautiful. I almost feel guilty for what I'm about to do."

He raised his hand.

The prayer beads around his wrist flared gold.

And the world turned inside out.

Xu Ming felt it before he saw it—a pressure, like being dragged underwater, the air thickening to syrup. The shop's walls began to peel. Paint curled away from plaster. Photos of his grandfather, his father, the old formulations they'd perfected over decades, began to dissolve.

"NO!" Bai Suyin's scream was not human.

She lunged.

And she became something else.

Her body elongated mid-air, bones cracking, skin splitting, scales erupting from every pore. She was beautiful. She was monstrous. She was a river of white light coiling through his small shop, filling every corner, her body pressing against the walls, the ceiling, the floor.

Xu Ming saw her face one last time—her human face, twisted with rage and terror—before it dissolved into something with eyes like moons and a mouth that could swallow a man whole.

The shop exploded outward.

Wood shattered. Glass rained like diamonds. The roof tore open to reveal the black, rain-soaked sky. And towering above it all, filling the gap where his home had been, was Bai Suyin.

Her true form.

She was massive. Impossible. A serpent of pure white jade, scales gleaming with internal light, muscles rolling beneath her skin like mountains shifting. Her head alone was the size of a car. Her eyes were twin lanterns, burning with cold fire.

And Fahai stood beneath her, arms crossed, utterly calm.

"Finally," he said. "No more masks."

He raised his hand again.

The prayer beads detached from his wrist, floating upward, spinning, each bead glowing with the same terrible gold. They began to expand. To multiply. One bead became ten. Ten became a hundred. A hundred became a net of light spreading across the ruined space, catching Bai Suyin's massive form, burning into her scales.

She screamed.

The sound was a physical force. Xu Ming felt it in his chest, his bones, his teeth. The scream of a thousand years of pain, of every life she had lived and lost, of every friend she had buried, every home she had burned.

"YOU DON'T GET TO TAKE THIS FROM ME!" Her voice was thunder. "I HAVE EARNED THIS! I HAVE SUFFERED! I HAVE LOVED!"

"You have stolen," Fahai said quietly. "Every life you took was stolen. Every face you wore was stolen. Your existence is a debt that must be repaid."

The golden net tightened.

Bai Suyin's body convulsed. Scales fell like rain, sizzling where they touched the ground, turning to ash. Her massive head thrashed, knocking down what remained of the walls, sending debris flying.

Xu Ming watched from the floor.

He couldn't move. Couldn't speak. Couldn't think beyond the simple, staggering truth of what he was seeing.

*She was real. All of it. Real.*

And she was dying.

The thought hit him harder than any explosion. Harder than the collapse of his shop. Harder than the revelation that he was nothing but a tool in her ancient war.

*She is dying.*

He remembered her hand in his. The warmth of her skin. The way she had looked at him when she said his name—*Xu Ming*—like it meant something. Like he meant something.

Was it real?

He didn't know.

But he knew that the woman who had walked into his pharmacy three weeks ago, soaking wet, asking for herbs that hadn't been used in a thousand years, had made him feel alive for the first time in his quiet, safe, predictable life.

"Bai Suyin!" His voice was a rasp. A whisper. Lost in the chaos.

But she heard him.

Her massive head turned. Those moon-like eyes found him among the ruins. And in them, he saw something that made his heart stop.

She was afraid.

Not of Fahai. Not of death. She was afraid of what he thought of her. Of whether he would hate her now that he had seen her true face.

"Xu Ming." Her voice was small. Human. "I'm sorry."

"For what?"

"For not telling you everything." A tear slid down her scaled cheek. It was not water. It was light. "For hoping you would never have to see this. For wanting to be human so badly that I convinced myself I already was."

"You are human." The words came from somewhere deep. Somewhere he didn't know he had. "You are the most human person I have ever met."

Her eyes widened.

The golden net tightened.

And Xu Ming made a decision.

He stood up.

His legs were shaking. His hands were empty. He had no weapons, no magic, no power. He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs for coughs and fevers. His life was small and safe and predictable.

But Bai Suyin had chosen him.

And he would not let her die alone.

"Fahai." He said the name like a curse. "Let her go."

The monk turned. His golden eyes studied Xu Ming with something that might have been curiosity. "Or what, pharmacist? What could you possibly do to stop me?"

Xu Ming reached into his pocket.

His fingers closed around the small vial he had prepared that morning. The herb combination he had been testing for weeks. The one that, according to his family's old texts, could paralyze a man for hours with a single drop.

He had no idea if it would work on a monk with golden eyes and a thousand years of power.

But he was going to find out.

"Call it a pharmaceutical opinion," Xu Ming said, and threw the vial directly at Fahai's face.

The monk caught it without looking.

His fingers closed around the glass. His smile never wavered.

"Amateur."

He crushed the vial.

The liquid splashed across his palm.

And nothing happened.

Fahai laughed. "Did you think—"

His voice cut off.

His eyes went wide.

His hand began to smoke.

"What—" He stared at his palm, where the liquid was eating through his skin, burning gold into black. "What is this?"

Herb. Not the paralytic. A different bottle. One Xu Ming had hidden in his sleeve, prepared weeks ago when he had first suspected that Bai Suyin was not entirely human.

A mixture of *bái jiàng* and *shé tuì*—white arsenic and snake shed.

A poison that, according to the oldest texts in his family's library, was used during the Tang Dynasty to hunt spirits.

Xu Ming had never tested it.

He had never believed it would work.

But Fahai was screaming now, clutching his hand, the golden net flickering, dissolving, falling apart around Bai Suyin's massive body.

"YOU—" Fahai's voice cracked. "HOW DO YOU KNOW THIS?"

"My family has been healing spirits for four hundred years," Xu Ming said quietly. "We just didn't talk about it."

Bai Suyin's body began to shrink. To contract. To coil back into human form.

She landed on the broken floor, naked, shivering, her skin covered in fresh burns and bleeding wounds.

Xu Ming ran to her.

He grabbed her shoulders. Pulled her close.

"I need time," she whispered.

"We don't have time."

"Then we run."

Fahai was still screaming, but his voice was changing. Deepening. Becoming something older, angrier, more terrible.

"You have made a mistake, pharmacist," he said, his voice no longer human. "You have marked yourself. My order will find you. My brothers will hunt you. You will never know peace again."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin.

She looked back.

And for the first time

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Price of Scales

The tea had gone cold in Xu Ming's hands. Across the table, Bai Suyin watched him with eyes that held centuries of waiting. The morning light filtered through the blinds, striping her face like bars.

"You're sure?" he asked.

"It's the only way." She set down her cup. "Fahai knows every hiding place. Every ally I've ever had is either dead or too afraid to help. There's nowhere left to run."

"Then we fight."

"We tried that. Remember the warehouse?" She touched her ribs where the burns had been. "I'm still healing. He's only getting stronger."

Xu Ming had watched the news that morning. Three men found dead in a temple outside Suzhou. Their throats had been torn out. The police called it an animal attack. Xu Ming knew better. Fahai was sending a message.

"So what's your plan?" he asked.

"I complete the trial."

"The trial requires you to fall in love."

"Yes."

"And you have to..."

"Consummate it." She said it without flinching. "The final transformation requires the convergence of three elements: a sincere heart, a willing sacrifice, and the union of yin and yang. My teacher wrote it in her final journal before Fahai burned her alive."

Xu Ming's throat tightened. "You're talking about sex."

"I'm talking about alchemy." She leaned forward. "My body has cultivated for a thousand years. Your body is mortal. When they join, the spiritual energy passes through you like lightning through a rod. You will be changed. I will be changed. And if it works, I will become fully human."

"And if it doesn't work?"

She looked away. "Then the energy backlash will destroy us both. Or Fahai will find us before we recover. Or a hundred other things could go wrong."

Xu Ming stood up. Walked to the window. Outside, the street was quiet. An old woman swept her doorstep. A delivery truck rumbled past. Normal life, happening right outside his door, completely unaware that a thousand-year war was about to reach its final battle in a rented apartment above a pharmacy.

"When?" he asked.

"Tonight. The energy is strongest during the Hour of the Rat."

"Midnight."

"Yes."

He turned to face her. "And if I say no?"

"Then I die." She said it simply, without drama. "Fahai will find me within the week. He'll cut out my core and add it to his collection. He has thirty-seven of them now. Spirits he's destroyed. Each one screams inside his prayer beads forever."

"That's not a choice."

"No. It's not." She stood, walked to him, placed her hand on his chest. "I didn't come to you because I wanted to use you, Xu Ming. I came because I sensed something in you. A purity I haven't seen in centuries. You're not like other mortals. You see the world as it could be, not as it is."

"You barely know me."

"I've known you for a thousand years." She smiled, and for a moment, she looked almost young. "I dreamed of you before I met you. The immortals showed me pieces of my future. I saw your face in the clouds above Mount Emei. I heard your voice in the wind through the bamboo groves. When you were born, thirty years ago, I felt it. A shift. Like a door opening."

Xu Ming didn't know what to say. His hands found hers. They were warm. Human warm. He couldn't feel the scales anymore.

"I'm scared," he admitted.

"So am I." She squeezed his fingers. "I've never done this before. A thousand years, and I've never..." She paused. "I wanted to. Many times. But I knew it had to be right. It had to be you."

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who ever looked at me and didn't see a monster." Her eyes glistened. "Even before you knew what I was. You just saw a woman who needed medicine."

Xu Ming pulled her close. She fit against him perfectly, her head tucked under his chin, her breath warm on his neck. He could feel her heartbeat. Steady. Strong. Determined.

"Tell me what to do," he said.

---

The preparation took hours.

Bai Suyin drew formations on the floor with crushed cinnabar and powdered pearl. She lit seven candles in a specific pattern. She placed a mirror on the east wall, facing west, to catch the moon's reflection.

"This will contain the energy," she explained, "so it doesn't burn through the building."

"Comforting."

She laughed. It was a real laugh, not the careful one she used with customers. "I've done this before. Sort of. The theory is sound."

"You've never actually done it?"

"I've never had a partner worth risking." She looked at him, and her expression softened. "This is different."

Xu Ming helped her arrange the last of the materials. His hands were steady now. The fear had settled into something quieter, more like acceptance. Whatever happened tonight, at least he would know. At least he would try.

As dusk fell, Bai Suyin changed into a white dress. Simple. Flowing. She'd bought it that afternoon from a shop down the street, paying with cash from a worn leather purse.

"I want to do this as a woman," she said, catching his look. "Not a spirit. Not a snake. A woman."

"You look beautiful."

"Thank you." She touched his cheek. "You should rest. I'll wake you when it's time."

"I won't be able to sleep."

"Then sit with me."

They sat on the floor, surrounded by the candles and the formations. She leaned against him, and he wrapped his arm around her. Outside, the city hummed with night life. Cars. Music. Laughter. None of them knew what was about to happen in this small room.

"Tell me about your teacher," Xu Ming said.

Bai Suyin was quiet for a long moment. "Her name was Qingzhu. Green Bamboo. She was a thousand years older than me. She found me when I was just a snake, barely able to think. She taught me everything. How to meditate. How to draw power from the moon. How to take human form." Her voice cracked. "She was the closest thing I had to a mother."

"What happened?"

"Fahai came to our mountain. He said we were abominations. That we had to be cleansed." She pulled her knees up. "Qingzhu fought him for three days. She was powerful. Almost as powerful as him. But he had relics. Weapons blessed by ancient monks. He trapped her in a circle of fire and..." She stopped.

"You don't have to tell me."

"I want you to know." She took a shaky breath. "He didn't just kill her. He took her core while she was still alive. I watched her scream for hours. He held it up to the moon and laughed. Then he ground it into powder and scattered it in the river."

Xu Ming held her tighter. "I'm sorry."

"I've carried that memory for eight hundred years. Every time I close my eyes, I see it. Every time I dream, I hear her calling for me." She turned to face him. "That's why I can't run anymore. If I run, her death means nothing. If I run, he wins."

"Tonight, we make sure he doesn't."

She kissed him. Soft. Gentle. A promise.

---

The clock struck midnight.

The candles flickered, then burned steady. The moon rose high enough to catch the mirror, and the room filled with silver light.

Bai Suyin stood in the center of the formation. Her white dress glowed. Her eyes reflected the moonlight.

"Are you ready?" she asked.

Xu Ming stepped into the circle with her. "Yes."

"Whatever happens, don't let go of me. The energy will try to push us apart. It will try to make me transform. You have to hold me here. Hold me as human."

"I will."

She reached up and touched his face. Her fingers were cool. Her palm pressed against his cheek.

"I love you, Xu Ming." She said it like it was simple. Like it was the easiest truth in the world. "I've loved you before I knew what love was. I've loved you in every life I've lived. And in this one, I choose you."

"I love you too."

She kissed him.

And the world exploded.

---

The energy hit him like a wave of fire and ice. It poured through her lips, into his mouth, down his throat, filling his lungs. He couldn't breathe. He couldn't think. He could only feel her, pressing against him, her body changing, shifting, scales rippling beneath the dress.

But he held on.

He wrapped his arms around her and held tight.

The formation on the floor blazed with light. The mirror cracked. The candles went out, then reignited with white flames.

Bai Suyin's body arched against him. She cried out, but it wasn't pain. It was release. A thousand years of cultivation, a thousand years of waiting, all pouring out in a single moment.

And Xu Ming took it. All of it.

He felt her memories flash through him. Her childhood as a snake in the bamboo forests. Her first transformation. Her teacher's face. Fahai's burning eyes. The centuries of hiding, of running, of hoping.

Then he felt her heart. True and pure and desperate.

And he loved her.

He loved her with everything he had.

The light peaked. The room shook. The building groaned.

And then, silence.

---

Xu Ming opened his eyes.

He was lying on the floor. The candles had burned down to nothing. The mirror was shattered. The formations were smeared, broken.

And Bai Suyin was gone.

In her place, coiled on the floor, was a snake.

White. Massive. Its scales gleamed like pearls. Its eyes were golden, pupil-less, ancient. It lifted its head and looked at him.

"Bai Suyin?" he whispered.

The snake opened its mouth. No words came out. Just a soft, mournful hiss.

Xu Ming's blood ran cold.

The transformation hadn't worked.

She was trapped.

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The golden glow faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. Too ordinary. The kind of eyes that could watch a man die and feel nothing.

"You're wrong," Xu Ming said, stepping between them. His voice cracked but held. "She's not a demon."

"She is a serpent that has stolen a human shape." Fahai didn't move from the shattered doorway. Rain swept in behind him, darkening his robes. "The distinction is meaningless to the dead."

Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's wrist. Her fingers were ice.

"Don't," she whispered.

"I won't let him—"

"You cannot stop him. Not yet." Her grip tightened. "But you can slow him down."

Fahai laughed. It was a dry sound, like leaves scraping stone. "The pharmacist? He mixes herbs for old women with back pain. What could he possibly do?"

Xu Ming's fear crystallized into something harder. Anger.

"I know what you are," he said. "I've been reading."

Fahai's smile faltered.

"The legends," Xu Ming continued. "The records in the temple archives. You're not a monk. You're a punishment. A curse given flesh. Every hundred years, you hunt one of her kind. And every hundred years, you fail."

The prayer beads in Fahai's hand stopped clicking.

"Who told you this?"

"Old Master Chen. Before he died." Xu Ming felt Bai Suyin's fingers tighten. "He kept journals. Three generations of Fahai hunters. They all ended the same way—alone, forgotten, their obsession consuming them from inside."

Fahai's face went very still. Very quiet.

Then he moved.

Not fast. Not violent. He simply walked forward, and the air around him rippled like heat haze. The bottles on Xu Ming's shelves began to vibrate. A low hum filled the room.

"Old Master Chen," Fahai said, stopping three feet away. "I remember him. He came to my temple in 1967, during the chaos. He begged me to stop hunting."

"What did you do?"

"I showed him mercy." Fahai's hand shot out, grabbing Xu Ming's throat. "I let him live."

Bai Suyin snarled. Her form blurred, scales rippling across her skin, and she struck Fahai's arm with enough force to shatter bone.

Fahai didn't even flinch.

"I have been purifying my body for eight centuries," he said, not looking at her. "Your venom cannot touch me. Your strength cannot move me. You are a child playing at power."

He released Xu Ming, who crumpled to his knees, gasping.

"Run," Bai Suyin hissed. "Now."

"I won't leave you—"

"You will. Or we both die." Her eyes met his. They were no longer human. Vertical pupils, gold and green, filled with a depth that made his soul tremble. "Trust me."

Xu Ming's legs wouldn't move. But something in her gaze pushed him—a command wrapped in desperation. He scrambled backward, through the broken door, into the rain.

The last thing he saw before the shop collapsed was Bai Suyin's body twisting, expanding, becoming something that had no place in the mortal world.

---

The temple rose from the mist like a bone breaking through skin.

Xu Ming had never been inside. He'd passed it a thousand times, a tourist attraction, a place for old women to burn incense and pray for grandchildren. Now it looked different. Hungry.

He burst through the main gate, soaking wet, lungs burning.

"Help!" he screamed. "Someone help!"

A young monk appeared, face concerned. "Brother, what's wrong?"

"Fahai—he's attacking—there's a woman—"

The monk's expression shifted. Not to alarm. To recognition.

"Ah. The serpent's guardian has come." He stepped aside, revealing a path deeper into the temple. "The abbot expected you."

Xu Ming's blood went cold. "Expected me?"

"The snake always runs to its mate. It is the oldest pattern." The monk's voice was gentle, almost kind. "Please. Come inside. The abbot wishes to speak with you before the final confrontation."

"I'm not going anywhere with you."

"You misunderstand, Brother." The monk's hand shot out, gripping Xu Ming's wrist with impossible strength. "This is not an invitation."

---

The abbot's quarters smelled of sandalwood and blood.

Xu Ming was pushed to his knees on a woven mat. The room was sparse: a low table, a single candle, and Fahai sitting cross-legged, his robes now dry, his hands folded in his lap.

"You have courage," Fahai said. "Stupid courage, but courage nonetheless."

"Where is she?"

"Fighting a distraction I created. A lesser demon I have kept chained in the basement for thirty years." Fahai's lips curled. "It will occupy her for an hour. Perhaps two. By then, this will be finished."

Xu Ming's hands clenched into fists. "What do you want?"

"To offer you a choice." Fahai leaned forward. The candle flame bent toward him, as if bowing. "You love the serpent. I can see it in your eyes, smell it on your skin. That love makes you dangerous. But it also makes you useful."

"I'm not helping you kill her."

"Kill her?" Fahai laughed. "Pharmacist, I have tried to kill that creature for eight hundred years. I have drowned her. Burned her. Buried her under mountains. She always returns. Always finds a new body, a new name, a new fool to love her."

He stood, pacing to the window. Rain streaked the glass, distorting the city lights beyond.

"I have realized that death is not the answer. She must be transformed. Purified. Stripped of her serpent nature so completely that she becomes human in truth—not just in form, but in soul."

"How is that different from killing her?"

"Because she will live. And you will live with her." Fahai turned. His eyes were gold again. "I can perform the Purification Rite. It will burn away her demonic essence, leave only the mortal woman beneath. She will lose her powers. Her memories. Her immortality."

"But she'll be human."

"Yes." Fahai's smile was terrible. "And she will remember nothing. Not you. Not her cultivation. Not the thousand years she spent becoming. She will be a blank slate, born anew at twenty-five years old, with no knowledge of what she was."

Xu Ming's heart stopped.

"She won't know me."

"No. But you will know her. And you can build a new life together. A human life. Free from hunting, from running, from the weight of scales and centuries."

The silence stretched.

"That's not love," Xu Ming said finally. "That's murder dressed in mercy."

"Is it?" Fahai knelt before him, close enough that Xu Ming could smell the incense on his breath. "Consider the alternative. I hunt her for another thousand years. She accumulates more enemies, more debts, more blood on her hands. Eventually, a more powerful demon will destroy her. Or a heavenly tribulation will strike her down. Or she will simply lose the will to continue and let herself fade."

He paused.

"Or, worst of all, she will succeed. She will become fully human through the Heart Trial—through your love. And then she will watch you age and die while she remains unchanged, condemned to love you across a hundred lifetimes, each one ending in your death and her grief."

Xu Ming's vision blurred.

"You don't know that."

"I have seen it happen. Twelve times. Twelve mortal lovers. Twelve broken hearts that shattered her a little more each time." Fahai's voice dropped to a whisper. "I am not her enemy, pharmacist. I am the only one who can end her suffering."

The door slid open.

Bai Suyin stood in the frame, her dress torn, blood dripping from a cut above her eye. Her chest heaved. Her eyes burned.

"Liar," she said.

Fahai didn't turn. "You killed the distraction faster than I expected."

"I smelled what you were doing." Her gaze locked on Xu Ming. "Don't listen to him. He's扭曲 everything."

"Then tell me the truth," Xu Ming said. His voice was raw. "Did you have twelve lovers before me?"

The silence was answer enough.

"Did they all die?"

"Not all." Bai Suyin's jaw tightened. "Some left. Some went mad. Some I had to let go because the hunt became too dangerous for them to survive."

"And the ones who stayed? Who loved you until the end?"

"Xu Ming—"

"How many, Bai Suyin?"

Her eyes glistened. "Three."

Fahai laughed. "Four, if you count the one who threw himself off a cliff rather than watch you age while he grew old."

"That was not my fault!"

"Wasn't it?" Fahai stood, spreading his arms. "You chose them. You loved them. And your nature destroyed them, every time. You are a poison, serpent. Everything you touch withers."

Bai Suyin's form flickered. Scales appeared on her arms, then faded. Her breath came in ragged gasps.

"I am trying to become human."

"Then prove it." Fahai's voice softened. "Let me perform the Purification. Shed your power. Shed your memories. Shed everything that makes you a demon. Become truly human, and live a single, mortal life with the man you love."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you prove that your power matters more to you than your love. And the pharmacist will know the truth of your heart."

Bai Suyin turned to Xu Ming.

Her face was a mask of anguish.

"He's right," she whispered. "About some of it. I have hurt people. I have left destruction in my wake. But I have also loved, truly, deeply, with every piece of my soul."

She stepped forward, taking his hands.

"I love you, Xu Ming. Not because of the trial. Not because I need protection. Because when I look at you, I see a man who gave me medicine without asking questions. Who walked with me by the lake and didn't run when I told him the truth. Who stood between me and a hunter with golden eyes."

Her hands were shaking.

"I want to be human. For you. But if I let him take my memories, I won't remember why. I won't remember you. I'll be a stranger wearing my face."

Xu Ming looked at Fahai.

Then back at Bai Suyin.

"If I let him do it," he said slowly, "and she forgets me... can I make her love me again?"

Fahai's eyebrows rose. "You would start from nothing?"

"I would start from scratch. I would court her. Win her trust. Make her fall in love with me all over again." Xu Ming's voice hardened. "If she's truly human, she has free will. She can choose me or not. But at least she'll have a choice."

Bai Suyin stared at him.

"You would do that?"

"I would do anything to keep you alive."

The tears broke free, streaming down her face.

"You stupid, wonderful man."

Fahai watched them, his expression unreadable.

"Then it is decided," he said. "The Purification will begin at dawn. You will have one night to say goodbye to the woman you love."
