He turned and walked into the shadows.

Bai Suyin collapsed into Xu Ming's arms, sobbing.

"I don't want to forget you."

"Then don't." He held her tight

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes swept across the shattered back door, casting long shadows that danced like living things across Xu Ming's pharmacy. His prayer beads clicked with each step forward, a sound that seemed to echo from the walls themselves.

Bai Suyin moved before Xu Ming could blink. She stepped between him and the monk, her wet dress still dripping onto the broken glass. Her spine straightened. The trembling was gone.

"Fahai." She spoke the name like it was poison on her tongue. "Eight hundred years, and you still chase shadows."

"You are no shadow, 白素贞." Fahai's robes settled around him as he stopped in the center of the room. His shaved head gleamed under the fluorescent lights. "You are a serpent who has shed too many skins. Each time you rise, you leave a trail of broken mortals behind you."

"I have never broken a mortal who did not deserve it."

"Liar." His voice cracked like thunder. The bottles on Xu Ming's shelves rattled. "What of the scholar in Suzhou? The merchant in Nanjing? The soldier who threw himself from the city wall because you would not love him back?"

Bai Suyin's jaw tightened. "Those were accidents of my nature. I did not intend—"

"You intended to become human." Fahai's prayer beads stopped moving. "That is the sin. You wish to ascend beyond your station. To wear a face that does not belong to you."

Xu Ming found his voice. "She's standing right here. You can talk to her like a person."

Fahai's golden eyes shifted to him. The weight of that gaze pressed against Xu Ming's chest like a physical force. "She is not a person, pharmacist. She is a parasite wearing a corpse of flesh she stole from the cycle of rebirth. Every breath she takes as a human is a theft from someone who actually died."

"That's not true." Xu Ming stepped forward, ignoring Bai Suyin's hand reaching to stop him. "I've seen her. Talked to her. She's more human than most people I know."

"You know nothing." Fahai raised his hand. Fire flickered between his fingers, green and hungry. "But you will learn."

Bai Suyin's arm shot out, forming a wall of white light that shattered the fire before it could reach Xu Ming. The impact threw her backward, her feet sliding across the wet floor. She caught herself against the counter, breathing hard.

"Run," she said to Xu Ming. "Now."

"No."

"Xu Ming, I am trying to protect you—"

"Then stop treating me like a child." He grabbed a bottle from the shelf behind him, trying to remember what herbs could cause paralysis if inhaled. Not that he had any intention of using them. But the weight of the glass in his hand steadied him. "I'm not going anywhere."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "The mortal has courage. I will grant him that much before I burn this place to ash."

"You will not touch him." Bai Suyin straightened, and something changed in the air. The temperature dropped. Moisture condensed on every surface. The rain outside seemed to grow louder, as if answering a call. "I have spent a thousand years cultivating. I have faced you across a dozen lifetimes. I am tired of running."

"Then stop." Fahai spread his arms. "Face me. End this. Become the demon I have always known you to be."

"Demon?" Her voice cracked. "I have saved more lives than you have prayed. I have healed the sick, protected the weak, guided the lost. What have you done, Fahai, besides destroy?"

"I have maintained the balance." His golden eyes flared. "I have ensured that the boundary between human and spirit remains unbroken. You would shatter it for your own selfish desire to feel what humans feel. To love. To be loved." He spat the words like curses. "You are not worthy of love. You are a snake. A thing of scales and hunger. No amount of cultivation can change what you are."

Xu Ming saw it happen. Saw the crack in Bai Suyin's composure. The way her shoulders dropped, just slightly. The way her breath caught.

He had seen that look before. In cancer patients who received their diagnosis. In families who learned their loved one had not survived surgery. It was the look of someone hearing a truth they had always feared.

"Bai Suyin." He said her name softly. "Don't listen to him."

"He's right." Her voice was barely a whisper. "I am a snake. I have killed. I have deceived. I have worn the faces of women who died, taken their names, lived their lives. What right do I have to become human?"

"The right of choice." Xu Ming stepped to her side. "The right of change. You're not the same being who made those choices a thousand years ago. You've grown. You've learned." He looked at Fahai. "That's something this monk will never understand."

Fahai's face twisted. "愚昧无知." Foolish ignorance. "You speak of growth, but you have only known her for days. I have watched her for centuries. I have seen every mask she wears, every lie she tells, every heart she breaks in her quest for transcendence. She will break yours too, pharmacist. It is only a matter of time."

"Then let her break it." Xu Ming felt the words leave his mouth before he fully understood them. "Let her try. Let her fail. Let her succeed. Whatever happens, it's my choice to make, not yours."

Bai Suyin stared at him. Her eyes were wet, but she was not crying. Not yet.

Fahai's prayer beads began to glow. "You have chosen. And now you will both face the consequences."

He threw the beads.

They did not fly like string. They expanded, each bead growing to the size of a fist, spinning in the air, forming a cage of light that descended toward Bai Suyin. She raised her hands, white energy flaring, but the beads passed through her defense like it was smoke.

"These are not physical," Fahai said. "They are dharma. The weight of every law she has broken. The karma of every life she has taken."

The beads struck Bai Suyin's body, and she screamed.

It was not a human sound. It was the cry of something ancient and wounded, something that had been running for so long it had forgotten how to stop. She fell to her knees, her hands clutching her chest, her skin flickering between human and scale.

"Stop!" Xu Ming lunged at Fahai.

The monk barely glanced at him. A wave of force sent Xu Ming crashing into the shelves, bottles shattering around him, herbs and glass raining down. He hit the floor hard, the breath driven from his lungs.

"Xu Ming!" Bai Suyin tried to rise, but the beads held her down, pressing her into the floorboards.

"Watch, mortal." Fahai walked toward her, his footsteps deliberate. "Watch what she truly is."

He reached down and grabbed her hair, pulling her head back. Her face was half-transformed now, scales spreading across her cheeks, her eyes gone golden and vertical-pupiled. She bared her teeth, and they were fangs.

"This is the truth beneath the skin." Fahai's voice was almost gentle. "This is the serpent that has haunted humanity since the beginning. You think you can love this? You think this thing can love you back?"

Xu Ming forced himself to his knees. Blood ran from a cut on his forehead, mixing with the rain that poured through the broken door. "Let her go."

"Or what? You will poison me with your herbs?" Fahai laughed. "You are a gnat. A mayfly. You live for a blink and think you understand eternity."

"Let her go."

"Or what, mortal?"

Xu Ming reached into his pocket. His fingers closed around the charm Bai Suyin had given him on their first walk by the lake. The white jade piece she had worn around her neck for centuries. She had pressed it into his palm and said, "If you ever need me, hold this and call my name."

He had thought it was a metaphor.

He pulled out the charm. It was warm. It pulsed with a light that matched the flickering in Bai Suyin's eyes.

"Bai Suyin," he said. "I call you."

The world stopped.

The rain froze in midair. The light from the beads went dark. Fahai's eyes widened.

And Bai Suyin exploded.

White light erupted from her body, so bright it burned shadows into the walls. The beads shattered, their fragments scattering like broken prayers. Fahai stumbled backward, his arms raised to shield his face.

When the light faded, Bai Suyin stood.

She was not human.

Her skin was covered in scales, white and gleaming like polished moonlight. Her eyes were burning gold. Her hair had turned silver-white, flowing around her like water. And behind her, filling the pharmacy, coiling through the shelves and out through the broken door, was her true form.

A serpent of impossible size, made of light and shadow, her scales shimmering with the weight of a thousand years.

"You should not have made him choose," she said, and her voice was a thousand voices layered together. "You should not have touched him."

Fahai's face had gone pale, but he did not retreat. "Finally. The serpent shows her true face. Now the world will see you for what you are."

"The world will see nothing." Bai Suyin raised her hand. The serpent behind her moved, its head descending until it hovered inches from Fahai's face. "I will take you to the boundary between worlds. I will seal you there for a thousand years. And when you return, I will be human. And you will be nothing but a forgotten name."

"You cannot seal me." Fahai's golden eyes blazed. "I am the Abbot of Jinshan. I am the hand of heaven. I am—"

"You are a man who has wasted centuries chasing a ghost." Bai Suyin's hand closed into a fist. "And I am done running."

The serpent's mouth opened.

And the world went white.

title: The Scale Beneath the Skin
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the threat remained, hanging in the air like smoke from a snuffed candle.

Xu Ming stepped forward, placing himself between Bai Suyin and the abbot. His heart hammered against his ribs, but his voice came out steady. "This is my shop. You broke my door. I want you to leave."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "Brave pharmacist. Do you know what you're protecting?"

"Someone who needs help."

"She needs help the way a cobra needs help drinking from a cup." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he moved them through his fingers. "She has been hunting you since the moment she walked through your door. Every glance, every touch, every whispered word — all calculated. All part of the hunt."

"That's not true." But Xu Ming felt the words land like stones in his stomach. He remembered how she'd appeared that first day, asking for herbs no modern pharmacist should know. How she'd known his name before he introduced himself. How she'd found him at the lake.

"I have tracked this serpent across eight centuries," Fahai continued, stepping over the splintered wood. "I have seen her destroy families. Corrupt villages. Drive men to madness and suicide. She wears beauty like a trap, and you, Xu Ming, have already stepped into it."

"She killed my teacher," Xu Ming said, repeating Bai Suyin's words. "She told me herself."

"Did she tell you why?"

Xu Ming turned to look at Bai Suyin. She stood frozen, her face pale, her hands clenched at her sides. She said nothing.

"The serpent's teacher was a Taoist immortal named Lü Dongbin," Fahai said. "He taught her cultivation. He protected her. Raised her like a daughter. And when she had learned everything he could teach, she consumed his essence to accelerate her transformation."

"That's a lie." Bai Suyin's voice cracked like ice breaking. "Lü Dongbin was dying. The Heavenly Court had sentenced him to dissolution. He gave me his essence willingly, to preserve his teachings."

"And you believed that?" Fahai's smile widened. "You have been alive a thousand years, serpent, and you still do not understand how mortals lie to protect themselves. Lü Dongbin was not sentenced to dissolution. He was sentenced to reincarnation — to live a hundred lives as a mortal, stripped of cultivation. He gave you his essence because he would rather die than face that fate. You did not inherit his teachings. You inherited his cowardice."

Bai Suyin staggered as if struck. Her hand went to her chest, fingers pressing against her heart. "You're lying."

"I have never lied. It is the only weapon I do not possess." Fahai turned back to Xu Ming. "She told you she needs your love to complete her trial. This is true. But she did not tell you what happens when she succeeds."

Xu Ming's mouth was dry. "What happens?"

"If she completes the heart trial, she becomes fully human. Her spirit enters the cycle of reincarnation. She can live, die, and be reborn like any mortal soul. But the power she cultivated for a thousand years — the essence of Lü Dongbin, the techniques she stole from dead masters — that power must go somewhere."

The shop felt smaller. The walls seemed to press inward.

"It goes to her partner." Fahai's voice dropped to a whisper. "To the man who loved her. You will inherit a thousand years of cultivation, Xu Ming. You will become a god-king among mortals. Or you will burn from the inside out when the power finds nowhere to go."

"That's—" Xu Ming couldn't finish the sentence.

"I am not threatening you. I am warning you. If she succeeds, you die. If she fails, she remains a spirit, and I will seal her beneath the Leifeng Pagoda for another hundred years. There is no happy ending here."

Bai Suyin moved. Not toward Fahai, but toward Xu Ming. She took his hands in hers, and he felt her trembling.

"I did not know," she said. "About Lü Dongbin. About what happens when the trial completes. I swear to you, on everything I have ever loved, I did not know."

"Would it have changed anything?" Xu Ming asked. "Would you have stopped?"

Her silence was answer enough.

"I need to think," he said, pulling his hands away. "Both of you. Get out of my shop."

"Xu Ming—"

"OUT."

Bai Suyin's eyes shimmered with tears, but she turned and walked through the broken door into the rain. Fahai followed, pausing at the threshold.

"You have until dawn, pharmacist. Then I will burn this city to find her if I must."

The door frame was empty. Rain poured through the gap, soaking the floor where Bai Suyin had stood.

Xu Ming sat down in the middle of the wreckage. His hands wouldn't stop shaking. He'd spent his whole life avoiding conflict, avoiding risk, avoiding anything that might disturb the careful balance of his small existence. And now he was caught between a thousand-year-old snake spirit and a monk with golden eyes, both of whom wanted something from him he couldn't give.

He didn't know how long he sat there. Minutes. Hours. The rain stopped. The streetlights flickered on. His phone buzzed with messages from his mother, asking why he hadn't come for dinner.

He didn't answer.

At midnight, he heard footsteps on the wet pavement outside. He looked up, expecting Fahai or Bai Suyin, ready for another confrontation.

Instead, a woman in a business suit stood in the doorway. She was young, early thirties, with sharp features and hair pulled back in a tight ponytail. She held an umbrella, but she wasn't using it. Rain plastered her clothes to her skin.

"Xu Ming?" she asked.

"Yes."

"I'm Agent Chen Wei, Ministry of State Security." She flashed a badge too quickly for him to read. "We need to talk about the woman you've been seeing."

"Bai Suyin?"

"No." Chen Wei stepped into the shop, closing her umbrella. "The other one."

Xu Ming's blood went cold. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"I think you do." She pulled out her phone, tapped the screen, and held it up. It was a photo taken from across the street — Xu Ming walking with a woman he'd never seen before. She had short black hair, a red jacket, and a knowing smile.

"I've never met her."

"This photo was taken yesterday. Three blocks from here. You were holding her hand."

Xu Ming stared at the image. The man was him. The clothes were his. But he had no memory of this woman, this moment, this day.

"I don't—" His voice cracked. "I don't remember this."

"Memory loss. Convenient." Chen Wei put the phone away. "Let me tell you what I know, Xu Ming. Bai Suyin is not the only snake spirit in Hangzhou. There's another. Younger. More dangerous. She calls herself Xiao Qing, and she has been following you for weeks."

"Why?"

"Because she wants what Bai Suyin wants. A mortal partner. A way to complete the heart trial. But Xiao Qing is impatient. She doesn't want to wait for you to fall in love. She's been using memory magic to insert herself into your life, hoping to replace Bai Suyin in your heart."

Xu Ming's head spun. Another snake spirit. Another woman who wanted something from him. He felt like a hunted animal, trapped in a cage he couldn't see.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because the Ministry has been tracking spirit activity in Hangzhou for decades. We know about Fahai. We know about the trial. And we know that if any of these spirits complete their transformation, the energy release will be catastrophic." Chen Wei stepped closer. "You're not just a pharmacist, Xu Ming. You're a linchpin. The entire balance of power in this city depends on which snake you choose."

"I don't want to choose anyone."

"Too late." She handed him a card with a phone number. "Call me when you remember the woman in the red jacket. I promise you, she's not as gentle as Bai Suyin."

Chen Wei left without another word. Xu Ming stood in the wreckage of his shop, holding the card, feeling the weight of the knife pressed against his throat.

He didn't remember the woman in red. He didn't remember holding her hand. But as he looked down at his palm, he saw something that made his stomach drop.

A single green scale, caught in the lines of his skin.

He hadn't been holding a woman's hand.

He'd been holding a snake's.

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming, and he felt something cold crawl down his spine—not fear, but recognition. He had seen those eyes before. In dreams he'd forgotten upon waking. In the reflection of dark water. In every story his grandmother had told him as a child, warning him away from the lakes after sunset.

"I am not harboring anyone," Xu Ming said, stepping forward. His voice cracked, but he forced it steady. "This is my shop. She is my customer. You have no right to break my door."

Fahai's smile did not waver. "Your loyalty is admirable. Misplaced, but admirable." He stepped fully into the room, and the shadows seemed to bend around him, clinging to his robes like living things. Rain dripped from his shaved head, ran down his face, but he did not blink. "She has woven her poison into your mind. Made you believe you chose her. But snakes do not love, pharmacist. They consume."

"Then why does she need my permission?"

The question hung in the air. Fahai's smile flickered.

"You said she's a demon," Xu Ming continued, finding his voice growing stronger. "But demons don't ask. They take." He turned to Bai Suyin, who stood frozen behind the counter. Her face was pale, her hands gripping the wooden edge so hard her knuckles had gone white. "You asked me if I would help you. You asked me to choose. That's not what a demon does."

"Clever boy," Fahai said softly. "But you are playing a game you do not understand. The heart trial requires consent. That is the rule. She needs you to give yourself freely because the Celestial Bureaucracy demands it. But once she has completed her transformation, what do you think happens to the mortal who served as her vessel?"

Bai Suyin's head snapped up. "That is not—"

"A mortal who has been touched by spirit essence," Fahai continued, his voice dropping to a whisper, "becomes a bridge. A door. The snake does not need to love you after her transformation is complete. She only needs to have *been loved* by you. And once she ascends, the connection remains. She can draw upon your life force whenever she wishes. Feed on your years. Your children. Your children's children."

"That's enough." Bai Suyin's voice cut through the shop like a blade. She stepped around the counter, and Xu Ming saw that her eyes had begun to change—the brown deepening to gold, the pupils thinning to vertical slits. "You twist everything, Fahai. You always have."

"Do I?" Fahai reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age, tied with red silk. "This is the registry of the Snake Clan's heart trials for the past six hundred years. Forty-seven mortals chosen by your kind. Forty-seven families marked with spirit debt." He tossed the scroll onto the floor, where it unrolled to reveal row after row of names. Some were written in characters so old Xu Ming could barely read them. Others were newer, the ink still dark. "Show him the names, white snake. Show him what happens to those who love you."

Bai Suyin's claws had extended. Her voice came out as a hiss. "Those names are not mine."

"No. They belong to your mother. Your aunts. Your sisters." Fahai's golden eyes never left her face. "But the blood is the same. The nature is the same. You cannot escape what you are."

Xu Ming looked at the scroll. At the names stretching back centuries. At the notes beside each one—dates of death, causes, remaining family. He saw patterns. Husbands who died young. Children who never reached adulthood. Entire bloodlines that had simply... stopped.

He looked at Bai Suyin.

Her face was a mask of rage and grief. But she did not deny it.

"I did not know," she whispered. "I swear to you, Xu Ming. I did not know about the others. My mother never told me. My sisters never spoke of it."

"But you knew it was possible." The words left his mouth before he could stop them. "You knew what heart trials can become. You told me yourself—the snake who fails the trial dies. You never told me what happens to the human who succeeds."

She flinched as if he had struck her.

"Because I would never let it happen!" Her voice broke. "I am not my mother. I am not my sisters. I have spent a thousand years cultivating to become something different, something better, and if you cannot see that—"

"What I see," Fahai interrupted, "is a snake weaving her web." He raised his hand, and the prayer beads around his wrist began to glow—a harsh, golden light that made Bai Suyin stumble backward. "I see a mortal caught in silk, believing he has found love when he has only found prey."

"Then kill me." Xu Ming said it quietly. Simply.

Both of them stared at him.

"If I am prey," he continued, "if I am just food for her transformation, then I am already dead. She could have taken everything from me the first night she came to my shop. She didn't." He looked at Fahai steadily. "But you broke down my door. You threatened her in my home. And you have been following her for eight hundred years, which means either you are very dedicated to justice, or you have made this personal."

Fahai's eyes narrowed. The golden light flickered.

"Tell me," Xu Ming said. "What did she do to you? What did any snake spirit do to you, that you cannot let this go?"

Silence.

The rain hammered against the roof. Somewhere in the distance, thunder rolled.

Fahai's face changed. For one brief moment, the golden mask slipped, and Xu Ming saw something underneath—not anger, not righteousness. Grief. Old and deep and rotting.

"My sister," Fahai said. The words came out like stones. "She was chosen for a heart trial. Three hundred years ago. She believed she was loved. She gave everything to help the snake complete her transformation." His hands began to shake. "And when it was done, the snake ascended. Left my sister with nothing but a debt that consumed her bloodline. I watched my entire family wither. One by one. Until only I remained."

He stepped forward, and the prayer beads blazed.

"I am not a monster, pharmacist. I am the last survivor of a massacre that the Celestial Bureaucracy refuses to acknowledge. And I have sworn that no other family will suffer as mine did."

Bai Suyin's claws retracted. Her eyes returned to human. She looked at Fahai—truly looked at him—and Xu Ming saw something he had never expected to see on her face.

Pity.

"Your sister," Bai Suyin said softly. "Was her name Lin? Lin Yue?"

Fahai went still.

"She was my teacher's lover." Bai Suyin's voice was gentle. "Before you came for us. Before the Mountain of a Thousand Blossoms burned. She was a kind woman. She gave my teacher hope that a snake could be worthy of human love."

"You lie." Fahai's voice cracked. "You lie to save yourself."

"I do not. My teacher's name was Bai Qing. She was the one who completed the heart trial with Lin Yue. And when Lin Yue died—when your family died—my teacher did not ascend. She stayed. She waited. She spent two hundred years trying to find a way to undo the debt, to free Lin Yue's bloodline from the curse." Bai Suyin's eyes glistened. "She was still searching when you found her. When you burned the mountain. When you killed her."

Fahai's face had gone white.

"You killed the only snake who was trying to fix what had been done to your family."

The prayer beads stopped glowing. They hung dark and dead from his wrist.

Xu Ming stood in the center of the storm—between a hunter who had destroyed the one person who could have saved him, and a spirit carrying the weight of her teacher's unfinished work.

"Do you see now?" Bai Suyin whispered. "We are both cursed by the same story. We just read different versions."

Fahai did not answer. His hands hung at his sides. His golden eyes had dimmed to something almost human.

"I cannot stop," he said finally. "Even if what you say is true. I have spent too long hunting. I have done too much. There is no path back for me."

"There is always a path back." Bai Suyin took a step toward him. "You just have to choose it."

For a long moment, the three of them stood in the ruined shop. Rain poured through the broken door. The scroll lay unrolled on the floor, its names slowly blurring as water soaked into the ancient paper.

Then Fahai laughed.

It was not a happy sound. It was the laugh of a man who had been running so long he had forgotten what stillness felt like.

"You are a fool, white snake. Or a genius. I cannot decide which." He looked at Xu Ming. "She has told you a different version of the story. Perhaps it is true. Perhaps it is not. But I will make you a bargain, pharmacist."

"I don't want your bargains."

"Listen first." Fahai's eyes hardened. "I will leave this city. I will not hunt Bai Suyin for one month. Use that time to discover the truth. Learn the history of the Snake Clan. Find out what happened to Lin Yue's bloodline." He paused. "But if you discover that she has lied to you—if you find even one thread of deception—you will come to me. And you will help me finish what I started."

Bai Suyin's breath caught.

"Xu Ming, no. He will twist everything. He will make you see lies where there is only truth."

"Then prove her wrong," Fahai said. "Prove that a snake can love without consuming. Prove that your teacher's dream was not a fool's hope." He pulled a jade token from his robe and tossed it to Xu Ming. "When you have your answer, break this. I will find you."

He turned and walked into the rain. His robes did not get wet. The shadows did not cling to him. He simply dissolved into the storm, leaving nothing behind but broken wood and the weight of old grief.

Xu Ming looked at the jade token in his hand. It was warm. Almost alive.

"What have you done?" Bai Suyin whispered.

"I've bought us time."

"You have given him a leash to pull you with." She grabbed his shoulders, her grip fierce. "Xu Ming, he will not wait a month. He will watch. He will test. And if he sees even a moment of doubt, he will use it."

"Then I won't doubt."

She stared at him. Her eyes searched his face, looking for something—fear, hesitation, the beginning of betrayal.

Instead, she found only exhaustion.

"I need to know the truth," Xu Ming said. "Not his truth. Not yours. The truth." He held up the jade token. "And I need you to help me find it."

Bai Suyin's hands slid from his shoulders. She looked smaller than he had ever seen her—not like a thousand-year-old spirit, but like a woman standing in the ruins of her own hope.

"If I show you the truth," she said slowly, "you may not like what you find."

"I know."

"You may decide he was right about me."

"I know."

"And if that happens—" Her voice broke. "If that happens, I

title: The Price of a Thousand Years
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, but the afterimage burned in Xu Ming's vision like a camera flash. He blinked, trying to clear his sight, and when his vision returned, the shop had changed.

The shattered door still lay in pieces. Rain still hammered the windows. But between them and the abbot stood Bai Suyin, her arms spread wide, her body curved like a bow ready to release.

"You will not touch him," she said.

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. It was the laugh of someone who had heard the same words a hundred times and found them equally amusing each occasion. "Touch him? I have no interest in the pharmacist. He is merely a vessel you have chosen to fill with your poison." He stepped over the broken doorframe, his wet robes trailing across the splintered wood. "Step aside, serpent, and I will let him live."

"His life was never yours to grant or take."

"Nor yours to protect." Fahai's hand moved to his prayer beads, each bead the size of a child's fist, dark wood carved with sutras that seemed to shift when Xu Ming tried to read them. "You have broken the covenant. You have revealed yourself to a mortal. You have attempted to complete the heart trial through deception rather than truth."

"I told him everything."

"You told him a story. A convenient narrative that paints you as victim and me as monster." Fahai's eyes found Xu Ming, and the gold flickered back to life. "Did she tell you about the village? The one she drowned three hundred years ago?"

Bai Suyin went still.

"Three hundred years ago," Fahai continued, his voice taking on the rhythm of a sermon, "a white snake spirit lived in the mountains of Sichuan. She had cultivated for seven hundred years and believed herself ready for tribulation. But the tribulation did not come. Instead, a hunting party found her lair. Mortal men, armed with torches and spears, who had heard stories of a serpent that stole children in the night."

"That is not what happened," Bai Suyin whispered.

"They burned her cave. They killed the smaller snakes she had taken as students. And in her rage, she called the rain. Not gentle rain like this." Fahai gestured at the storm outside. "She called a flood that washed the village from the mountain. Three hundred souls. Men, women, children. Drowned in water turned red with their own blood."

Xu Ming's throat tightened. He looked at Bai Suyin, and for the first time, he saw her not as the woman who had pressed his hand to her heart, but as something older. Something capable of terrible things.

"The children," Xu Ming said, his voice barely audible. "Did you...?"

She turned to face him, and the anguish in her eyes was real. It was the kind of pain that had no bottom. "I was mad with grief. My teacher had just been killed by Fahai. My students were ash. I wanted to make them feel what I felt." A single tear escaped down her cheek. "I have regretted it every day since. Every single day."

"Regret does not resurrect the dead," Fahai said. "And repentance does not erase three hundred screams."

"Neither does vengeance," Bai Suyin snapped, turning back to face him. "But you have never understood that, have you? Every life I have taken, you have matched. Every atrocity I committed, you have exceeded. We are the same, Fahai. The only difference is that you call your slaughter holy."

Fahai's composure cracked. Just a hairline fracture, visible only in the tightening of his jaw. "I have never—"

"The monastery in Tibet." Bai Suyin's voice rose. "The one you burned because they sheltered spirits. The scholars you executed because they studied forbidden texts. The children you drowned because they were born with serpent blood in their veins." She took a step toward him. "Shall I continue? I have eight hundred years of your sins catalogued in my memory."

"Those were necessary."

"Necessary." She laughed, and the sound was hollow. "You call your atrocities necessary and mine evil. That is the convenience of being the hunter rather than the hunted. You get to write the history."

Xu Ming's mind raced. He had studied history in university, read accounts of massacres and wars and religious persecution. But those had been words on a page. Distant. Abstract. Now he was standing between two beings who had lived through centuries of blood, and the weight of it pressed down on him like a physical force.

"How many?" he asked.

Both of them turned to look at him.

"How many people have you killed?" Xu Ming directed the question at Bai Suyin. "Not in self-defense. Not in grief. How many?"

She was silent for a long moment. Rain drummed against the windows. Somewhere in the distance, a car horn blared.

"More than I can count," she finally said. "I have lived a thousand years. For most of them, I was not the woman you see now. I was a creature of instinct and hunger. I killed because I was hungry. I killed because I was afraid. I killed because I was angry." She met his eyes. "I cannot undo that. I can only be better now."

"Words," Fahai said. "Words from a serpent's tongue."

"There is a difference between her and you," Xu Ming said, and both of them froze. "You're still doing it. You're still killing. She's trying to stop."

Fahai's eyes widened. Just slightly. Just enough for Xu Ming to see that he had struck something true.

"You defend a demon," Fahai said.

"I defend a woman who came to me for help." Xu Ming stepped forward, placing himself beside Bai Suyin. His legs were shaking. His heart was pounding. But he had spent his life in the background, watching others make decisions, never taking a stand. If he was going to die, he would die standing.

"Xu Ming," Bai Suyin said, her voice soft. "You don't have to—"

"Yes, I do." He took her hand. Her fingers were cold, but they curled around his with desperate strength. "I don't know if I can love you. I don't know if what I feel is real or just the result of whatever magic you've woven around me. But I know that the man standing across from us wants to kill you, and I know that's wrong."

"The heart trial," Fahai said, and now there was something new in his voice. Something that sounded almost like fear. "It's already begun."

"Then stop it," Xu Ming said. "Whatever this is, end it. Let her go."

"I cannot." Fahai raised his hand, and the prayer beads began to glow. "The trial, once initiated, cannot be undone. If she completes it, she becomes human. If she fails, she dies. And if I kill her before the trial concludes, the backlash will destroy everything within a mile of this place." His smile returned, cold and terrible. "Including you, pharmacist."

"Then why haven't you killed her already?"

"Because I wanted to see if she would corrupt you first. If she would drag another mortal into her web." Fahai lowered his hand, and the glow faded. "She has. And now you are part of this whether you wish it or not."

Bai Suyin's grip tightened on Xu Ming's hand. "What are you saying?"

"I am saying that the trial has expanded." Fahai reached into his robes and withdrew a scroll, yellowed with age, sealed with red wax. "I have spent eight hundred years studying the heart trial. Its rules. Its loopholes. And I have found one." He broke the seal and unrolled the scroll, revealing characters that seemed to writhe on the parchment. "If a mortal willingly binds himself to a spirit's trial, knowing the spirit's true nature, the trial becomes mutual."

"What?" Bai Suyin's voice cracked.

"He cannot complete your trial for you. But he can be bound to its consequences." Fahai's eyes locked onto Xu Ming. "If you die, she dies. If she dies, you die. And if she fails to complete the trial by the end of the seventh day..." He let the scroll roll back up with a snap. "You both perish."

Xu Ming's blood turned to ice. "You're lying."

"I never lie." Fahai tucked the scroll back into his robes. "It is against my vows."

"Then why tell us?" Bai Suyin demanded. "Why give us warning?"

"Because I want you to suffer." The words were simple. Honest. More terrifying than any threat. "I want you to know that the man who chose to stand beside you will die because of that choice. I want you to watch the hope drain from his eyes as the seventh day approaches. And I want you to feel, in your final moments, the weight of every life you have taken, pressing down on you until you cannot breathe."

He turned and walked back toward the broken door.

"Seven days," he said without looking back. "The clock begins now."

The rain swallowed him as he stepped outside. One moment he was there, a dark silhouette against the storm. The next, he was gone, as if he had never existed at all.

Xu Ming stood in the ruined shop, holding Bai Suyin's hand, listening to the rain and the distant thunder and the sound of his own heart beating far too fast.

"We can run," he said.

"He will find us."

"We can fight."

"His cultivation is deeper than mine. He has spent centuries hunting spirits. He knows every trick, every technique, every weakness." She turned to face him, and her eyes were wet. "I have doomed you."

"You asked me to love you."

"I asked you to save me."

"Same thing." Xu Ming lifted his free hand and touched her face. Her skin was cold. Cold as snow. Cold as death. But beneath it, he could feel the warmth of her blood, the pulse of her heart, the desperate life that had survived a thousand years and refused to surrender.

"There is another way," she said.

"What?"

She looked at the shattered door. At the rain. At the street where Fahai had disappeared. "I can sever the trial. Break the connection between us. It will kill me, but you will survive."

"No."

"Xu Ming—"

"No." He pulled her close, and she was stiff in his arms, a creature unaccustomed to embrace. "I didn't choose to be part of this. But I'm in it now. And I'm not going to let you sacrifice yourself so I can go back to my quiet little life." He pressed his lips to her hair. "I have seven days. Seven days to figure out how to love you, or how to save you, or how to do both."

"Seven days is not enough."

"Then we'll make it enough." He pulled back and looked into her eyes. "Tell me everything. Not the story you told me before. Everything. Every sin. Every regret. Every moment of your thousand years. I need to know who I'm dying for."

She stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed, and the sound was broken and beautiful and terrified.

"I have lived a thousand years," she said. "It will take more than seven days to tell you everything."

"We have seven days."

"Seven days to live. Seven days to love. Seven days to become human." She reached up and touched his face, and her fingers were warm now. Human warm. "What do you want first?"

Xu Ming looked at the ruined shop. At the rain. At the woman who had

title: The Price of Shelter
content:
The rain hammered against the shattered doorframe, mixing with the splinters of wood scattered across Xu Ming's floor. Fahai stood in the opening, his golden robes darkened by water, his prayer beads clicking with mechanical precision.

Xu Ming's body moved before his mind caught up. He stepped between the monk and Bai Suyin.

"Get out of my shop."

Fahai's smile didn't waver. "You shelter a snake, pharmacist. Do you know what she is? What she has done?"

"I know she's bleeding in my back room, and you just destroyed my door."

"Your door." Fahai laughed—a sound like stones grinding together. "I have burned temples to kill her kind. I have drowned villages. I have watched her crawl out of the wreckage of a dozen lives, each time more human than the last, each time more dangerous." He took a step forward. His bare feet left wet prints on the floor. "You think a wooden door matters to me?"

Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's shoulder. Her fingers were cold, trembling.

"Don't," she whispered. "He wants you to fight. He wants a reason."

"Too late for that." Fahai raised his hand, and the prayer beads glowed—not with light, but with *absence*, a darkness that drank the room's warmth. "You have already chosen, pharmacist. You touched her. You let her into your home. You are *marked*."

Xu Ming's skin prickled. He looked down at his hands. Nothing visible. But he felt it—something clinging to him like cobwebs, thin and cold and hungry.

"A tracking charm," Bai Suyin said. "Every spirit he kills leaves a residue. Every mortal who helps one carries the stain." Her voice cracked. "I didn't tell you. I should have told you."

"You didn't tell me a lot of things."

"I was afraid you would run."

"I'm still thinking about it."

Fahai laughed again. "Run, then. Run far. The stain follows. I will find you in a year, or ten years, or at the deathbed of your grandchildren. It does not matter. I am patient."

Xu Ming's pharmacy was small. Twenty feet wide, thirty feet deep. Shelves of glass jars filled with herbs—chrysanthemum, ginseng, licorice root. A counter worn smooth by decades of elbows. A back room with a cot and a kettle.

This was his world. Measured. Controlled. Safe.

And now a thousand-year-old snake spirit was trembling behind him, and a monk with golden eyes was promising to hunt him across generations.

"Let her go," Xu Ming said.

Fahai tilted his head. "Excuse me?"

"Let her go. Let her leave. I'll take the stain. Whatever it costs. Just let her walk out that door."

"Xu Ming, no—"

He shook Bai Suyin off. "You came to me for protection. This is me protecting you. Go. Find another mortal. Find another city. Live."

Bai Suyin's eyes were wet. "You don't understand. He will not stop. He will never stop. I have run for eight hundred years. I am *tired* of running."

"Then fight."

Fahai's smile vanished. For the first time, something like interest flickered across his face. "You wish to fight me, pharmacist? You, who mix herbs for old women's coughs?"

"I wish to understand why you hate her so much."

"Hate?" Fahai's voice dropped. "I do not hate snakes. I do not hate spirits. I hate *lies*. She wears human skin. She speaks human words. She claims to love human hearts. But she is a serpent. A predator. Her love is hunger. Her kindness is patience. Given a thousand years, she will consume everything you are and grow stronger for it."

"You don't know that."

"I have seen it." Fahai's eyes burned brighter. "I have seen her sisters. I have seen what they become when the mortal they love grows old and dies, and they are left alone again. They do not mourn. They *hunt*. They find new faces, new names, new hearts to break. It is their nature."

Bai Suyin's voice cut through the room like a blade. "You speak of nature, Fahai. But you have never once asked me what I *want*."

"I know what you want. You want to be human."

"Yes." She stepped past Xu Ming, standing in the open, rain streaming down her face. "I want to be human. I want to grow old. I want to die. I want to feel pain that ends, and joy that matters because it *ends*, and love that is precious because I only get one lifetime to experience it."

"You are lying."

"I am *not*." Her voice broke. "You think cultivation is a gift? A thousand years of watching everyone you love turn to dust? A thousand years of being alone, always alone, because the only beings who can understand you are monsters like yourself?"

She grabbed the front of Fahai's robes. He did not move.

"You have never been lonely," she said. "You have never been so lonely that you would trade immortality for a single human heartbeat. So do not speak to me of nature. You do not know what I am."

Fahai looked down at her hand. Then he looked at Xu Ming.

"Interesting," he said. "She has never shown emotion before. In any of her lives. She has always been cold. Calculating. A serpent's patience." He smiled. "You have changed her, pharmacist. She *feels* now. Which means she can be hurt."

He moved faster than Xu Ming could track. One moment he was standing still. The next, his hand was around Bai Suyin's throat, lifting her off the ground.

She did not struggle. Her body went limp, her eyes closing.

"Beautiful," Fahai said. "The perfect vessel. Do you know how many years I have waited for a serpent to love? To care? To have something she would die to protect?"

"Put her down." Xu Ming grabbed a mortar from the counter. It was heavy. Stone. He hefted it in both hands.

Fahai laughed. "You will throw a bowl at me? I have faced down dragons, pharmacist. I have shattered mountains. I have—"

Xu Ming threw the mortar.

It hit Fahai square in the face.

The monk staggered. His grip loosened. Bai Suyin dropped, gasping, her hands clawing at her throat.

Fahai touched his nose. Blood. Red and human and real.

"You—" His voice was flat. Shocked. "You *bled* me."

"Get out of my shop."

"You hit me with a *bowl*."

"It was a mortar. They're heavier. Get out."

Fahai's face twisted. For a moment, he looked less like a monk and more like a child who had been denied a toy. Then his expression smoothed, and he laughed—a genuine laugh, warm and surprised.

"Wonderful," he said. "Absolutely wonderful. A mortal who fights with cookware. I have not been surprised in three hundred years." He bowed, low and mocking. "You have earned a reprieve, pharmacist. One night. Tomorrow, I will return. And I will bring my true tools."

He stepped backward through the door, into the rain, his golden eyes never leaving Xu Ming's.

"Sleep well," he said. "Dream of snakes."

Then he was gone.

The rain filled the silence. Xu Ming stood in the wreckage of his door, breathing hard, his hands shaking.

Bai Suyin was on her knees, her head bowed. Water pooled around her. Blood too—the wound in her side had reopened.

"I'm sorry," she whispered.

"Stop apologizing."

"I should not have come here. I should not have dragged you into—"

"Stop." He knelt beside her. His hands were still shaking. He pressed them against her wound, feeling the slick warmth of her blood. "I hit a monk with a bowl. A monk who hunts snake spirits. A monk who has been alive for at least three hundred years."

"More. He is older than me."

"Great. Wonderful." Xu Ming's voice cracked. "I'm a pharmacist. I make tea for old ladies. I read medical journals. I have a cat."

"You have a cat?"

"Her name is Mochi. She sleeps in the herb cabinet."

Bai Suyin laughed—a broken, wet sound. "You are the strangest mortal I have ever met."

"And you're the only snake spirit I've ever met. So we're even."

He helped her stand. She leaned on him, heavy and warm and alive. Together, they limped to the back room. Xu Ming laid her on the cot, fetched clean bandages, boiled water.

Outside, the rain continued to fall.

Inside, something had changed.

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice was soft. "Why did you hit him? You could have let him take me. He might have left you alone."

"I don't know." He pressed a cloth to her wound. "Maybe because he broke my door. That door was custom-made."

"That is not the reason."

"No." He looked at her. Her face was pale. Her eyes were dark and deep and ancient. "I don't like people who threaten my customers."

"I am not a customer. I am a demon."

"You're a person who needs help. That's enough."

She stared at him. Then, slowly, she reached up and touched his face. Her fingers were cold. Her touch was gentle.

"You have a good heart," she said. "That is why I chose you. Not because you were weak. Not because you were easy. Because you were *good*."

Xu Ming's throat tightened. "I'm not good. I'm just tired."

"Tired is good. Tired means you have been fighting. Tired means you have not given up." She smiled. "Rest now. I will keep watch."

"You're bleeding."

"I have bled for a thousand years. A few more hours will not kill me."

He should have argued. He should have insisted on treating her wound properly. But the adrenaline was fading, and exhaustion was crashing over him like a wave.

He lay down on the floor, his back against the wall, his eyes on the door.

"Xu Ming."

"Mm."

"Thank you. For not running."

He closed his eyes. "Ask me again tomorrow."

The rain stopped sometime before dawn. Xu Ming woke to silence and the smell of herbs and the weight of a blanket that had not been there when he fell asleep.

Bai Suyin was gone.

The cot was empty. The bandages were folded neatly on the pillow. A note lay on top of them, written in careful, elegant characters.

*I have gone to face him alone. It is the only way to keep you safe. If I do not return, know that I loved you. Not because of the trial. Because you were the first person in a thousand years who saw me and did not flinch.*

*— Bai Suyin*

Xu Ming read the note three times.

Then he stood up, put on his coat, and walked out the door.

Somewhere in Hangzhou, a monk with golden eyes was hunting a white snake. And a pharmacist with a broken door was going to find them both.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: THE PRICE OF SHELTER

Fahai took another step into the shop. His robes were black silk, not the rough gray of temple monks. A gold thread ran along the collar—scripture, perhaps, or something older. His prayer beads clicked as he moved, each bead the size of a child's knuckle, carved from bone.

"Demon," Xu Ming repeated. The word felt wrong in his mouth. "She's not—"

"She is a snake that has worn human skin for too long." Fahai's golden eyes never left Bai Suyin. "Ask her how many she has killed. Ask her about the scholar in Suzhou, the merchant in Nanjing, the fisherman who pulled her from a net three hundred years ago and was found the next morning with his throat—"

"Enough." Bai Suyin's voice cracked like ice. "You twist every story. You always have."

"Then correct me." Fahai spread his hands. "Tell the pharmacist the truth. Tell him about your teacher, who taught you to consume human essence to accelerate cultivation. Tell him about the twenty-three students at White Cloud Academy who died of 'plague' the winter you stayed there."

Xu Ming's grip tightened on the counter. "Is this true?"

"No." Bai Suyin's eyes blazed. "Not like that. My teacher—she taught me to survive. The world does not welcome spirits, Xu Ming. We are hunted. Poisoned. Burned. If I had not learned to defend myself, I would have been killed before my first century."

"Defense." Fahai laughed. The sound had no warmth. "You call it defense when you drain a man's life force through his lips?"

"I have never killed an innocent."

"The fisherman—"

"He was a poacher." Bai Suyin's jaw set. "He trapped my sister spirits in nets, sold their scales to alchemists, their venom to assassins. When I freed them, he came at me with a harpoon tipped in silver. I defended myself."

"And the scholar in Suzhou?"

"Deserved what he found."

Fahai's smile thinned. "Convenient. Every death justified. Every life taken a necessary sacrifice." He turned to Xu Ming. "Do you see now? She will tell you what you want to hear. She will make you believe you are special. But you are not the first, pharmacist. You are not even the first this century."

"I know." The words came out before Xu Ming could stop them.

Both of them stared at him. Bai Suyin's expression flickered—confusion, then something sharper.

"Excuse me?" Fahai said.

"I know I'm not the first." Xu Ming stepped out from behind the counter. His legs felt like rubber, but he kept moving. "She told me about the heart trial. About the mortals before me. She didn't hide it."

"Did she tell you they all died?"

The room went cold.

"Fahai," Bai Suyin warned.

"Did she tell you that the heart trial has never succeeded? That every mortal who attempted it either went mad, took their own life, or was killed by her enemies?" Fahai's beads clicked faster. "Did she tell you that you are walking into a trap that has already snapped shut on a dozen men before you?"

Xu Ming kept his voice level. "She told me the trial was dangerous."

"Dangerous." Fahai laughed again, harder this time. "She has been cultivating for a thousand years. She has survived dynasties, wars, famines. And now, at the final gate, she needs a mortal's love to complete her transformation. Not because she loves you. Because the heavens demand a sacrifice of the heart. She needs to *feel* love, true love, to shed her serpent nature forever."

"And if she succeeds?"

"Then she becomes fully human. Immortality. Freedom. No more hiding." Fahai's eyes narrowed. "And you become nothing. The trial consumes the mortal, Xu Ming. It takes your love, your life force, your very soul as fuel for her ascension. She will walk away human, and you will be a husk."

"That's not—" Bai Suyin started.

"Is that the truth?" Xu Ming cut her off. "Will I die?"

Silence stretched between them. Rain hammered the roof. Water dripped through the broken door, pooling on the tiles.

"Yes." Bai Suyin's voice was barely a whisper. "If the trial completes, you will die."

The words hit him like a physical blow. He had known it was dangerous. He had not known it was fatal.

"But," she continued, "if we stop before completion—if I break the trial early—you survive. I lose everything. My cultivation, my humanity, a thousand years of work. But you live."

"Liar," Fahai hissed. "You would never—"

"I would." She turned to face him fully. "You think you know me, Fahai. You have hunted me across eight centuries, but you have never understood me. I am tired. Tired of running. Tired of killing. Tired of wearing a face that is not truly mine." She looked at Xu Ming. "I came to you because your aura was kind. I stayed because you treated me like a person, not a monster. If you choose to help me, we can fight him. But if you choose to walk away, I will go. And I will not force you."

Fahai's golden eyes flickered. For a moment, something like uncertainty crossed his face. Then it hardened.

"Pretty words. But I have seen your true nature, serpent." He reached into his robe and pulled out a bronze mirror. The surface was dark, almost black, etched with characters Xu Ming couldn't read. "Do you know what this is?"

Bai Suyin recoiled. "No. You wouldn't—"

"The Mirror of True Form. Forged by a Tang dynasty exorcist from the scales of a thousand-year dragon. It strips away all illusion, all glamour, all lies." Fahai held it up. "Shall we show the pharmacist what you really look like?"

"Stop." Her voice cracked.

"Too late."

Fahai angled the mirror toward her. Light—not sunlight, but something older, something that seemed to come from inside the bronze—caught Bai Suyin's reflection.

She screamed.

Not a human scream. Something deeper, wilder, that shook the walls and shattered the remaining windows. Her body twisted, bent, reshaped itself in ways that should have broken bones. Her skin rippled, scales pushing through, then retreating, then pushing again.

"Stop it!" Xu Ming lunged forward, but Fahai shoved him back with one hand. The monk's strength was impossible—Xu Ming flew across the room, slammed into the medicine cabinets, and collapsed among shattered jars of herbs.

"The truth," Fahai said, his voice calm over Bai Suyin's agonized cries. "You wanted to protect her. You wanted to love her. But you cannot love what you cannot see."

The mirror flared brighter.

Bai Suyin's form dissolved.

For a moment, Xu Ming saw it—the thing beneath the woman. A serpent of impossible size, coiled in a space that couldn't hold it, scales white as winter moonlight, eyes like burning stars. It was beautiful. It was terrifying. It was *wrong*, a creature that had no place in the human world.

Then the light faded.

Bai Suyin collapsed to her knees, human again, gasping. Her dress was torn. Her face was wet with tears and blood—blood from her nose, her ears, her eyes.

"Now you see," Fahai said. "Now you understand."

Xu Ming pushed himself up. His ribs ached. His vision swam. But he looked at Bai Suyin—shaking, bleeding, broken—and felt something twist in his chest.

"It doesn't matter."

Fahai's smile froze. "What?"

"It doesn't matter what she is." Xu Ming limped toward Bai Suyin. "You showed me a monster. But monsters don't cry. Monsters don't bleed. Monsters don't beg for mercy."

He reached her side and knelt, putting himself between her and the mirror.

"She came to me for help. She told me the truth—most of it, maybe all of it. She warned me I might die." He looked up at Fahai. "And you burned down a mountain. Killed her teacher. Hunted her for eight hundred years. Tell me, Abbot—which one of you is the demon?"

Fahai's composure cracked. The golden eyes blazed. "You fool. You absolute fool. She has already ensnared you."

"No." Xu Ming took Bai Suyin's hand. Her fingers were cold, trembling. "I'm just choosing who to trust."

"Then you choose death."

The prayer beads snapped.

They didn't just break—they exploded, each bead flying outward like bullets. One hit the wall, embedding itself in the brick. Another shattered a display case. A third struck Xu Ming's shoulder, spinning him sideways.

Bai Suyin moved.

One moment she was on her knees, broken. The next she was in front of Fahai, her hand wrapped around his throat, her nails digging into his skin.

"Leave," she said. "Now. Or I will end this."

"You cannot." Fahai's voice was strained but unafraid. "Kill me, and the monastery sends ten more. A hundred more. They will hunt you across every province, every country, every life you try to build. I am the first stone, serpent. There are always more."

"Then I will deal with them when they come."

"Will you?" He reached up and grabbed her wrist. His hand glowed—faint gold light, like the mirror. "You forget. I am not merely a hunter. I am also a priest."

He spoke a single word.

Xu Ming didn't recognize the language. It wasn't Chinese, wasn't anything he'd ever heard. But the effect was immediate.

Bai Suyin screamed again. Her hand released Fahai's throat. She stumbled backward, clutching her chest, and Xu Ming saw it—a mark, burning through her clothes, searing into her skin.

A character. 封. *Seal.*

"What did you do?" Xu Ming shouted.

"Merely reminded her of her place." Fahai straightened his robes. "The seal will suppress her cultivation for three days. No transformation. No magic. No serpent tricks." He smiled. "Just a woman. Weak. Vulnerable."

Bai Suyin collapsed. Xu Ming caught her before she hit the ground.

"The monastery will arrive at dawn," Fahai said. "You have until then to reconsider your choices, pharmacist. Harboring a demon carries the same punishment as being one." He turned and walked through the broken door, into the rain. "Choose wisely."

He vanished into the darkness.

Xu Ming held Bai Suyin's shivering body, feeling her heartbeat against his palm. Slow. Weak. Human.

"What do I do?" he whispered.

She didn't answer. She couldn't.

And outside, through the rain, he heard it—the distant sound of temple bells.

They were coming.

---

Xu Ming carried Bai Suyin upstairs to his apartment. She weighed almost nothing, her breathing shallow, the seal on her chest still glowing faintly. He laid her on his bed, covered her with his blanket, and stood there, shaking.

Three days. The monastery at dawn.

He had no weapons. No training. No idea how to fight spirits or monks or whatever Fahai was.

But he had a pharmacy downstairs. Herbs. Poisons. A thousand years of Chinese medicine, locked in jars

title: The Price of Shelter
content:
The golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming with the weight of centuries. Fahai stepped fully into the shop, his wet robes leaving trails across the broken wood. Each prayer bead in his hand was carved from bone—human bone, Xu Ming realized with horror as the light caught the telltale yellowing.

"Give me the serpent," Fahai said. "And I will let you live."

Bai Suyin moved before Xu Ming could respond. She stepped between them, her body angled, protective. Her wet hair dripped onto the floor, and Xu Ming noticed that where the drops landed, tiny flowers bloomed and died in the space of a breath.

"She is under my roof," Xu Ming said. His voice cracked, but he forced the words out. "In my city, that means something."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound—more like stones grinding together. "Your city? Boy, I walked these streets when they were mud paths between rice paddies. I saw the first stones of the Leifeng Pagoda laid by hand. Your laws are less than a whisper to me."

"Then why haven't you taken her?"

The question hung in the air. Fahai's smile flickered, and Xu Ming saw it—a hesitation, barely visible, but real.

"Because he cannot," Bai Suyin said softly. "Not here. Not in this place."

She turned to Xu Ming, and her eyes held something he hadn't seen before. Fear. Not for herself—for him.

"The trial is incomplete," she continued. "Until I either succeed or fail, I am bound to you. If he kills me now, the backlash would destroy everything within a li. He doesn't care about that. But if he kills you—" She swallowed. "The trial would be void. I would be cast back a thousand years of cultivation. He wants me to suffer first."

"Clever snake." Fahai circled them slowly, his prayer beads clicking. "But you miss one detail. I don't need to kill him. I only need to make him reject you."

Xu Ming felt the temperature drop. Frost crept across the broken windowpanes.

"Every day you stay with him, every night you spend in his presence, you bind yourself tighter to his soul. When the trial ends, if he does not love you, that bond becomes a chain. You will be dragged back to the spirit world, your humanity stripped away, your memories scattered like ash." Fahai's golden eyes gleamed. "But if I can convince him to renounce you willingly, the chain breaks early. You fall before you can rise."

"He's lying," Bai Suyin said quickly. Too quickly.

"Is he?"

Xu Ming looked at her. Her hands were shaking. For all her power, for all her thousand years, she was terrified. Not of Fahai's violence—but of Xu Ming's choice.

"I need time," Xu Ming said again.

"You have until dawn," Fahai replied. "Then I will return with the full authority of the Buddhist council. They will seal this shop. They will bind your soul to theirs for interfering with divine law." He turned to leave, then paused at the broken door. "One more thing, pharmacist. Ask her about the lake. Ask her what happened the last time she loved a mortal."

He vanished into the rain.

The silence after his departure was worse than the violence. Xu Ming stood among the wreckage, his hands still shaking, his mind racing.

"What happened at the lake?"

Bai Suyin closed her eyes. When she opened them, they were wet with unshed tears.

"His name was Xu Xian."

The name hit Xu Ming like a physical blow. "That's—that's the name from the story. The legend. The one grandmothers tell children."

"It is not a legend." Her voice was barely a whisper. "It is my history. Xu Xian was a pharmacist, like you. He was gentle, kind, trusting. I loved him. Truly. And Fahai destroyed him to punish me."

"The story says you were trapped under the pagoda."

"The story simplifies." She moved to the counter, her fingers tracing the scattered herbs. "When the trial failed—when Xu Xian died—I went mad. I flooded the city. Thousands drowned. I would have killed everyone in Hangzhou if Fahai hadn't stopped me." Her voice broke. "I was a monster. I became exactly what he always said I was."

Xu Ming felt the floor shift beneath him. "You killed thousands of people."

"I drowned an entire district." She turned to face him, and her beauty seemed terrible now, edged with something ancient and unforgiving. "I was a hundred feet of white serpent, and I did not care who I destroyed. The monks spent three days fishing bodies out of the water."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you deserve to know what you are protecting. I am not a damsel in distress. I am not a gentle spirit seeking love. I am a killer who has spent eight hundred years trying to earn back the humanity I threw away." She stepped closer, and he did not back away. "Fahai is not wrong to hunt me. He is wrong in his methods. Wrong in his cruelty. But his cause is just."

"Then why should I help you?"

"Because I am trying to be better." Her hand reached out, hovered near his cheek, not quite touching. "Because I have paid for my sins in suffering. Because if you reject me now, I will lose everything I have built. Not my power—I would welcome that loss. But my chance to be human. To feel the sun without burning. To taste food without it turning to ash in my mouth. To live a mortal life, brief and precious, and die a mortal death."

Xu Ming looked at her hand. The skin was perfect, unblemished, but he knew what lay beneath. Scales. Coils. A thousand years of hunger and rage and sorrow.

"If I agree to help you," he said slowly, "what happens to me?"

"You become my anchor. My heart." She finally touched his face, and her fingers were cold, like river water in winter. "If the trial succeeds, you will live a normal life with me. We will grow old together. You will die, and I will follow you into death shortly after."

"And if it fails?"

"Then I will try to protect you. And if I cannot—" She looked away. "You will die. But I will die with you, one way or another. I will not survive another loss."

It was not a comforting promise. It was a terrifying one. And somehow, that made it more real.

"Show me," Xu Ming said.

"Show you what?"

"Your true form. The one that filled the lake. The one that drowned a district."

Bai Suyin's eyes widened. "That would—"

"I need to see what I'm agreeing to protect. I need to understand." He took her hand, feeling the cold spread up his arm. "I cannot love a shadow. I cannot trust a story. Show me the monster, Bai Suyin. Then I will decide."

She stared at him for a long moment. Then she nodded.

"Stand back."

He moved to the far wall. Bai Suyin walked to the center of the room, her bare feet on the broken wood. She closed her eyes. Her breathing changed—slower, deeper, like something vast waking beneath the earth.

"I have not done this in three hundred years," she said. "It will not be gentle."

The air thickened. The lights flickered and died. In the darkness, Xu Ming heard something shift—scales against wood, a whisper of immense weight moving through impossible spaces.

Then he saw her.

Not fully. The shop was too small for her true size. But he saw the suggestion of her—a shadow that filled every corner, a presence that pressed against his chest, his throat, his skull. Her eyes opened in the dark, and they were not human. They were vertical slits of burning silver, ancient and cold and endless.

"I am what you asked to see," her voice came from everywhere, from inside his own mind. "I am the serpent who drank the Yellow River dry. I am the storm that sank a thousand ships. I am the hunger that devours mountains and dreams and gods."

A coil appeared in the darkness—thicker than his body, gleaming white, moving with terrible grace. It wrapped around the shop's support beam, and the beam groaned in protest.

"I am also the woman who cried for a hundred years after Xu Xian died. I am the spirit who collected every flower he ever touched and pressed them into a book I still carry. I am the monster who learned to pray."

The vision faded. The lights returned. Bai Suyin stood before him, human again, trembling.

Xu Ming's legs gave out. He sat down hard on the floor, his heart pounding, his mind reeling.

"Now you know," she said softly.

He looked up at her. The same face. The same eyes. But he saw the shadows beneath them now, the weight she carried.

"Okay," he said.

"Okay what?"

"I'll help you." The words came out before he could think them through. "I don't know if I can love you. I don't know if I can trust you. But I know what it means to carry guilt. And I know that Fahai is not a savior." He pushed himself to his feet. "I will be your anchor. I will be your shelter. And when dawn comes, I will stand with you."

Bai Suyin's face crumpled. She didn't cry—she was too old for tears—but something broke in her expression, some wall she had built over centuries.

"You do not understand what you have offered," she whispered.

"Then explain it to me."

"An anchor is not just protection. It is a bond. If Fahai kills you, I will feel it. If he tortures you, I will feel it. And if you betray me—" Her voice dropped to nothing. "The trial will consume us both."

The back door, still hanging from its hinges, creaked in the wind.

And from outside, Xu Ming heard footsteps. Many footsteps. Coming closer.

"He said dawn," Xu Ming said.

"He lied." Bai Suyin's face hardened. "He never intended to give us until dawn. He wanted me to show you my true form. He knew it would either drive you away or bind you to me. Either way, he wins."

"Then what do we do?"

She grabbed his wrist. Her grip was iron.

"We run."

The shop's front door exploded inward, and the room filled with monks—not the peaceful kind from the temple, but warriors in grey robes, their heads shaved, their hands wielding iron staffs. At their center stood Fahai, his golden eyes burning brighter than before.

"The Council has voted," he said. "The serpent is to be exterminated. The mortal is to be cleansed."

"Cleansed?" Xu Ming's voice rose. "I'm not the one who—"

"You have been tainted by her presence. Your soul carries her scent. It must be purified." Fahai raised his hand. "By fire, if necessary."

Bai Suyin moved. She was faster than Xu Ming's eyes could follow—one moment beside him, the next in front of Fahai, her hand wrapped around his throat.

"No one burns him," she snarled.

Fahai smiled. "Then you will burn instead."

The monks surged forward. Bai Suyin's form flickered—human, then serpent, then human again. Xu Ming grabbed a bottle from the shattered shelves, hurled it at the nearest monk. It shattered against his staff, spraying bitter herbs everywhere.

"Run

title: The Third Path
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown. But the weight of his presence did not diminish. He stood in the shattered doorway, water pooling at his feet, prayer beads still clicking with mechanical precision.

"A demon," he repeated, savoring the word. "Do you know what happens to those who shelter demons, pharmacist?"

Xu Ming's throat had closed. He couldn't speak. His mind was still processing the explosion, the splintered wood, the impossible eyes.

"He burns them," Bai Suyin said quietly. She had not moved from behind the counter. Her voice was steady, but Xu Ming saw her hands gripping the edge of the wood, knuckles white. "He burns them alive. Or worse. Sometimes he leaves them alive and burns everyone they love first."

"Exaggeration." Fahai stepped into the shop, his wet robes dragging across the floor. "I only burn those who refuse to repent. Those who knowingly harbor evil." He stopped, tilting his head like a bird examining prey. "But you, Xu Ming, are innocent. You did not know what she was. You were deceived. Seduced."

"We've done nothing," Xu Ming managed.

"Nothing?" Fahai laughed, and the sound was wrong — too many echoes, as if two people were laughing at slightly different times. "She has marked you. I can see it. The scent of her spirit clings to your skin like oil. She has been feeding you her essence, strengthening your connection, preparing you for the heart trial."

"I told him the truth," Bai Suyin said. "Everything. The trial. My nature. You. He chose to help me anyway."

"And that makes it better? He knows what you are and still shields you?" Fahai's prayer beads stopped. "That makes him complicit."

Xu Ming's fear had calcified into something harder. "Complicit in what? She hasn't hurt anyone. She came to me for medicine. For help. You're the one who broke down my door."

"I am the one who has spent eight centuries cleaning up the messes of spirits who think they can become human." Fahai's voice dropped, losing its theatrical edge. "Do you know why it's forbidden? Do you know what happens when a snake sheds its last skin and stands on two legs?"

"Tell me."

"The first one was called Bai Suzhen." Fahai's eyes flickered to Bai Suyin. "Your mother. Or your previous incarnation. Or the source you were split from — the records are unclear. What I know is this: she completed the heart trial. She loved a mortal, and he loved her. She became human. And within three years, she had destroyed him."

Xu Ming felt the words like a physical blow. He looked at Bai Suyin, but her face was unreadable.

"She did not destroy him," Bai Suyin said. "He died. There is a difference."

"He died because she could not control her nature. Because the human body she took was a cage, and cages break. She transformed during an argument. Her true form crushed him. Pulverized him. They found pieces of his body scattered across three city blocks." Fahai's voice was flat, clinical. "The monks of that era covered it up. Called it a gas explosion. But I was there. I saw."

"That was eight hundred years ago," Bai Suyin said. "I am not her."

"You are her blood. Her spirit. Her ambition." Fahai stepped closer. "The snake always returns to its nature. You can cultivate for a thousand years, learn compassion, learn love, but in the end, you are still a predator wearing a woman's face."

Something in Bai Suyin's expression broke. Just a crack. Just for a moment. But Xu Ming saw it.

"That's not true," he said.

Both of them turned to look at him.

"I've been studying traditional medicine for ten years," Xu Ming continued. "I've seen snakes. I've handled them. They don't feel compassion or malice. They just are. They eat, they sleep, they survive. But Bai Suyin—" He stopped, searching for words. "She came to my shop with a cough. A fake cough, I know that now. But she stayed because she wanted to understand humans. She asked me about sadness. About why people cry. A real predator wouldn't care."

"Sentiment," Fahai said. "You've been enchanted."

"Have I?" Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. "Show me your true form."

She flinched. "No."

"Show me. Now. If I'm enchanted, I should be terrified. If I'm not, I should see something true." He held her gaze. "I need to know what I'm protecting."

"You don't understand what you're asking."

"Then make me understand."

The air in the shop changed. The temperature dropped. Xu Ming's breath began to mist.

Bai Suyin's eyes closed. When they opened again, they were no longer human — vertical pupils, gold irises, the eyes of something that had watched civilizations rise and fall from the shadows of mountains.

Her skin began to shift.

Not the full transformation she had shown him before. Something slower. More deliberate. Scales appeared along her jawline, small and iridescent, catching the dim light like wet jewels. Her hair moved, though there was no wind — it writhed, separate from her body, strands reaching toward the ceiling like curious serpents.

She raised her hand, and her fingers elongated. Nails became claws. Her palm flattened, widened, the bones rearranging themselves beneath the skin.

"The first stage," she said, and her voice had harmonics now, a lower register underneath that vibrated through Xu Ming's chest. "What I look like when I stop pretending to be human but haven't yet become a monster."

She looked wrong. Beautiful and wrong, like a painting that had been slightly altered — the proportions off, the shadows falling in impossible directions. Human enough to be familiar. Snake enough to be terrifying.

Xu Ming's heart hammered. His instincts screamed at him to run. But he stayed.

"Does this frighten you?" Bai Suyin asked.

"Yes."

"Good. It should." The scales began to fade. Her eyes shifted back to brown. Her hand returned to normal. "Because this is the least frightening thing about me."

Fahai had watched the entire display without moving. Now he spoke. "She is showing off. Proving her control. But control is an illusion, pharmacist. The nature always wins. Ask yourself: when she transforms in rage, when her blood is hot and her mind is clouded, will she remember to be gentle? Or will she revert to what she was before she learned to walk on two legs?"

Xu Ming's throat was dry. "You said there was a first one. Bai Suzhen. But she's still here. She's the same spirit, isn't she?"

"No." Fahai's voice hardened. "Bai Suzhen was destroyed. I burned her myself. But her essence — her core — it was too strong to be erased entirely. It reformed. Regrew. Like a lizard's tail." He gestured at Bai Suyin. "This is the fifth incarnation. The fifth time she has crawled out of death and tried again."

Bai Suyin's face was stone. "You killed me four times. You will not kill me a fifth."

"Then prove it." Fahai reached into his robes and withdrew something that made Bai Suyin step backward — a small bronze bell, ancient and pitted, tied with a red ribbon. "This bell was forged from the metal of the temple where Bai Suzhen was destroyed. It has been blessed by thirteen generations of monks. When I ring it, your true form will be forced out. Every scale. Every coil. Every inch of the serpent you really are."

He held it up.

"Shall we test your control?"

"Don't," Bai Suyin said. Her voice cracked. "Xiu Ming is still here. If you force my form, I cannot guarantee—"

"You cannot guarantee you won't kill him." Fahai smiled. "Exactly my point."

Xu Ming's brain was racing. He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs. He did not fight immortal monks or snake spirits. But he was also the only person in this room who wasn't either a hunter or prey.

"Wait," he said. "Both of you. Wait."

Fahai's hand paused on the bell. Bai Suyin's eyes, terrified, met his.

"You want to test her control," Xu Ming said to Fahai. "Fine. But not with the bell. Not with force." He looked at Bai Suyin. "You said you need me to love you. For the trial. For your power."

She nodded slowly.

"Then love is the test." He turned to Fahai. "If she's really a predator wearing a human face, she can't love. She can only consume. So give me a week. Seven days. I'll stay with her. Live with her. If she hurts me or tries to control me, you can do whatever you want."

Fahai's expression flickered. Interest. Calculation. "You would risk your life for a demon?"

"I would risk my life for someone who came to me for help." Xu Ming's voice was steadier than he felt. "And I would rather find out the truth through living it than through violence."

A long silence.

Then Fahai lowered the bell. "Seven days. But I will be watching. Every moment. If I sense even a hint of her nature slipping, I will intervene." He looked at Bai Suyin with cold satisfaction. "And I will make sure you watch me burn her."

He turned and walked out into the rain, leaving the shattered door hanging open.

The moment he was gone, Bai Suyin collapsed. Her legs gave out. She fell to her knees, gasping, her whole body trembling.

Xu Ming moved to her side, reaching out—

"Don't touch me." Her voice was ragged. "Not yet. Give me a moment."

He pulled his hand back. "What just happened? You were so strong before. Confident. What did he do to you?"

"He reminded me." She looked up, and there were tears in her eyes — real tears, human tears. "Every time I die, I remember. The burning. The drowning. The cutting. He has killed me in four different ways, Xu Ming. And each time, I wake up alone, in a new body, with nothing but the memory of how it felt to die."

"I promised you seven days," he said. "I meant it."
