"Seven days is not enough. Seven years would not be enough." She laughed bitterly. "I have been running for eight hundred years. I have loved mortals before, and they have all died — some by my hand, some by Fahai's, some by simple age. I am a curse wrapped in a woman's skin."

"Then why do you keep trying?"

She was silent for a long moment. Rain dripped through the broken door. The shop smelled of wet wood and crushed herbs.

"Because once," she said finally, "before Fahai, before the burnings, before I knew what I was — I was happy. Truly happy. I lived in a mountain temple with a teacher who loved me. I learned to read. To write poetry. To laugh." She wiped her eyes. "I want that again. I want to be human enough to feel joy without knowing it will end in fire."

Xu Ming didn't know what to say. He had no wisdom for this. He was a pharmacist who had never left Hangzhou, never fought for anything, never loved anyone deeply enough to risk death.

But he had promised seven days.

"Stand up

title: The Debt of Scales
content:
Xu Ming's heart hammered against his ribs as Abbot Fahai's golden eyes swept across the ruined doorway. The rain had turned his robes to heavy black curtains, and the prayer beads in his hand clicked with each step forward—a sound like bones breaking.

"You made a mistake coming here," Bai Suyin said. Her voice had changed. It carried echoes now, layers of sound that vibrated in Xu Ming's chest like a second heartbeat.

"You made yours eight hundred years ago." Fahai raised his hand. The prayer beads unwound, each bead glowing with symbols that burned like hot iron. "I offered you mercy then. Transcendence. A clean death that would release you from your serpent nature."

"I offered you a chance to understand." Bai Suyin stepped forward, positioning herself between Fahai and Xu Ming. "You refused to see that we are not so different."

"We are nothing alike." Fahai's hand snapped forward. The beads shot through the air like bullets, each one finding a target—the shelves, the walls, the floor around Bai Suyin's feet. Where they struck, wood blackened and cracked. Glass melted.

Bai Suyin didn't flinch. She raised her own hand, palm open, and the beads that had landed around her began to tremble. Then they flew upward, circling her wrist, forming a living bracelet that glowed with trapped light.

"I have been collecting your prayers for centuries," she said softly. "Every bead you have thrown at me. Every curse you have woven. I have worn them all."

Fahai's composure cracked. Just a flicker. But Xu Ming saw it—the shock in those golden eyes.

The beads around Bai Suyin's wrist shattered. The light that burst from them filled the shop with blinding white. Xu Ming threw up his arms, felt the heat wash over him like standing too close to a furnace.

When the light faded, Bai Suyin had changed.

Her dress was gone. In its place, a gown of white scales that caught the dim light like oil on water. Her hair had grown, falling past her waist, and her eyes—her eyes were no longer human. They were vertical slits of pure black, ringed with gold.

"I have lived a thousand years, Fahai." Her voice was the whisper of wind through bamboo. "You have hunted me for eight hundred. In that time, I have learned every spell you use. Every weapon you carry. Every prayer you recite before sleep."

She took a step toward him. The floorboards beneath her feet whitened with frost.

"You are predictable, monk. And predictable things die."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound—it was the scrape of stone on stone, the grinding of a mill that had been turning too long.

"You think I have not changed in eight hundred years?" He reached into his robes and drew out a scroll. Yellowed parchment, tied with red silk. "You think I have not learned from my failures?"

He threw the scroll into the air. It unrolled with a snap that broke two windows. And inside it, Xu Ming saw writing. Thousands of characters, each one carved into the parchment as if burned with a red-hot needle.

Bai Suyin recoiled. The frost on the floor retreated.

"What is that?"

"The story of every life you have lived." Fahai's smile returned. "Your first incarnation as a serpent in the mountains of Shu. Your second as a concubine in the Tang court. Your third as a river goddess in Fujian. All of them. Every name you have worn. Every face you have shown the world."

He pointed at the scroll. The characters began to glow.

"And now I know every name that will kill you."

Bai Suyin's hands clenched into fists. The scales on her arms rippled, and for a moment, Xu Ming saw something beneath her skin—a shadow of massive coils, of a body that had no end.

"You cannot kill me with names."

"I can kill you with truth." Fahai began to chant. The words were old. Older than Mandarin. Older than the language of the Han. They were the sounds of wind over stone, of water carving canyons, of the earth remembering what it had buried.

Bai Suyin screamed.

It was not a sound that should have come from a human throat. It was high and thin, the cry of a creature being pulled apart. She fell to her knees, her hands pressed to her ears, blood seeping between her fingers.

Xu Ming moved before he thought.

He grabbed the nearest thing—a mortar, heavy stone, used for grinding herbs—and threw it at Fahai's head. The monk didn't even look. He raised one hand, and the mortar stopped in midair, hovering as if caught by an invisible thread.

"Your loyalty is admirable, pharmacist." Fahai's chanting paused. "Your timing is not."

The mortar reversed direction. It struck Xu Ming in the chest, sent him crashing through the wooden counter. Splinters tore his arms. His ribs screamed. He tasted blood.

"Stop!" Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "He is mortal. He has nothing to do with this."

"He has everything to do with it." Fahai turned to face her fully. The scroll hovered beside him, its characters blazing. "You chose him as your heart trial. That makes him part of your debt. And debts must be paid."

He resumed chanting. The words grew louder. The scroll's light intensified.

Bai Suyin's transformation accelerated. Her jaw unhinged, stretching wider than any human jaw should. Scales crawled up her neck, over her cheeks, across her eyelids. Her fingers fused together, becoming something else, something with claws that scraped against the floor.

"Xu Ming." Her voice was a whisper now, barely audible beneath Fahai's chanting. "Run."

He couldn't. His legs wouldn't move. Not from fear—from something else. Something in the air that pressed down on him, that made the world feel slow and thick.

Fahai's chanting reached a crescendo. The scroll burst into flame. And from the flame, a figure emerged.

It was a woman. Tall. Dressed in white. Her face was blank, expressionless, like a mask that had never learned to smile. She walked toward Bai Suyin, and with each step, the temperature dropped.

"Sister," the figure said. Her voice was Bai Suyin's voice. Her face was Bai Suyin's face. But there was nothing behind the eyes. Nothing at all.

Bai Suyin stared at her double. The transformation halted. Her jaw snapped back into place. The scales retreated.

"Who is that?" Xu Ming forced the words out.

Fahai answered. "Her first name. The one she was born with, before she learned to cultivate. Before she learned to love. Before she became anything other than a serpent."

The double reached for Bai Suyin. Its fingers touched her cheek.

And Bai Suyin began to change again—but this time, not into something with scales and claws. This time, she shrank. Her gown hung loose. Her face grew younger. Her eyes widened with something Xu Ming had never seen in her before.

Fear.

"No," she whispered. "I am not that creature anymore. I left her behind."

"You cannot leave behind what you are." Fahai's voice was gentle now. Almost kind. "You can only pretend. And pretense is a fragile thing."

The double wrapped its arms around Bai Suyin. She didn't fight. She stood there, trembling, as the figure held her like a child holding a doll.

Xu Ming forced his legs to move. One step. Two. The pressure in the air pushed against him, but he pushed back. Pain flared in his chest, his arms, his cracked ribs.

"Let her go."

Fahai didn't even look at him. "You cannot save her, pharmacist. You cannot love her. You cannot protect her. She is a serpent. And serpents return to the earth."

"I said let her go."

Xu Ming grabbed the scroll. The parchment was still hot, still burning with that yellow flame. It seared his palms, and he smelled his own flesh cooking. But he didn't drop it.

He ripped it in half.

The characters screamed. Not a sound—a sensation. The air itself cried out as the scroll tore, as the writing scattered into fragments of light that spun through the shop like dying fireflies.

Fahai's chanting stopped.

The double dissolved.

Bai Suyin collapsed.

And Fahai turned to look at Xu Ming with eyes that were no longer gold. They were white. Pure white, like the heart of a flame that had burned too long.

"Do you know what you have done?"

Xu Ming's hands were shaking. The pain was unbearable. But he held the torn scroll, the burning parchment, and he didn't let go.

"I saved her."

"You have condemned yourself." Fahai walked toward him. Each step was measured, deliberate, the gait of a man who had all the time in the world. "The scroll was bound to me. By breaking it, you have inherited its debt. Every name she has worn. Every life she has lived. Every sin she has committed."

He stopped in front of Xu Ming. Reached out. Touched his forehead with one finger.

"Now you carry them too."

Xu Ming's vision went white.

He saw mountains. Ancient forests. A serpent coiled around a stone, watching the stars. He saw a palace, silk robes, a woman laughing in a garden. He saw a river, flooded banks, villagers praying to a goddess made of water and mercy.

He saw Bai Suyin. A hundred Bai Suyins. A thousand. Each one different. Each one the same.

And in every one, he saw himself.

"You will dream her memories," Fahai said. "You will feel her pains. You will carry her burdens. And when she falls, you will fall with her."

The monk stepped back. His eyes returned to gold. His smile returned.

"This is my gift to you, pharmacist. The gift of understanding. You wanted to save her. Now you will know what it costs."

He turned and walked through the ruined door. The rain swallowed him. The darkness closed behind him like a curtain.

Xu Ming fell to his knees. His hands were still burning. His head was full of voices—her voices, all of them, speaking in languages he had never heard, telling stories he had never lived.

Bai Suyin crawled toward him. Her face was pale, her eyes human again, her body small and fragile in the ruins of his shop.

"Xu Ming." Her hand touched his cheek. Her fingers were cold. "What have you done?"

He looked at her. And for the first time, he saw her clearly. Not as a woman. Not as a spirit. Not as a snake.

As a prisoner.

"I broke the scroll," he said.

She shook her head. Tears fell, mixing with the rain, running down her cheeks like rivers through old stone.

"It was the only thing that could save me," she whispered. "The names. If he gathered all of them, he could release me. End the cycle. Let me die."

"He was trying to kill you."

"He was trying to free me." Her voice broke. "And now you have bound yourself to my curse. You will live as long as I live. You will suffer as I suffer. You will never be free."

Xu Ming looked down at his hands. The burns were healing. Too fast. Far too fast.

"The pain will pass," he said.

"No. It will never pass." She pressed her forehead to his. "You have tied yourself to a thousand years of sorrow, Xu Ming. And I cannot untie you."

Outside, the rain stopped.

The silence that followed was worse than the

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the threat remained, hanging in the air like smoke from an extinguished candle.

"I see you've chosen," Fahai said, stepping fully into the shop. His robes were simple gray cotton, soaked through from the rain, but he moved with the grace of someone who had never known hesitation. "The pharmacist over the cultivator. The mortal over the immortal."

Bai Suyin positioned herself between them. Her dress still clung to her skin, wet from the lake, but her posture had shifted — shoulders back, chin raised, the posture of a warrior rather than a woman seeking refuge.

"Fahai," she said, her voice cold as the rain outside. "Eight hundred years, and you still haven't learned when to stop."

"Eight hundred years," he repeated, "and you still haven't learned that some boundaries cannot be crossed."

Xu Ming's mind raced. His shop was destroyed. The back door hung in splinters. Herbs scattered across the floor, their medicinal scents mixing with the smell of wet wood and something else — something sharp and metallic, like old coins left in the sun.

"This is my home," Xu Ming said, stepping forward despite every instinct screaming at him to run. "You can't just—"

"I can do whatever is necessary." Fahai didn't look at him. His eyes remained fixed on Bai Suyin. "The girl you knew as Qingqing — she was your teacher's daughter, was she not? The one who died in the mountain fire."

Bai Suyin flinched. It was barely visible, a微小 twitch at the corner of her mouth, but Xu Ming caught it.

"Her mother," Fahai continued, "was a three-hundred-year-old green snake who thought love would save her. She burned beside the mountain. I remember her screams. They sounded almost human."

"Say another word," Bai Suyin whispered, "and I will show you what a thousand years of cultivation looks like when it stops pretending."

"Please." Fahai spread his arms, smiling. "Show me. Give me the excuse I need."

The air thickened. The temperature dropped. Xu Ming's breath fogged in front of his face, and the rain outside seemed to slow, each drop hanging in the air for a fraction of a second longer than physics allowed.

Bai Suyin's eyes began to change. The brown irises shifted, pupils becoming vertical slits. Her skin took on a pale luminescence, scales rippling beneath the surface like water over stones.

"No," Xu Ming said.

He didn't know where the word came from. It wasn't courage — courage required a choice, and this felt like something else. Instinct. The same force that made him reach for falling objects before his brain registered they were falling.

He stepped past Bai Suyin, placing himself between her and Fahai.

"If you're going to fight," he said, his voice steadier than he felt, "do it somewhere else. This is a pharmacy. People come here to heal."

Fahai's smile didn't waver, but something shifted in his eyes. Interest. Curiosity. The look of a man who had just noticed an insect performing an unexpected trick.

"You would protect a demon?"

"She's not a demon."

"She is a snake who has stolen a human form. She has consumed the essence of a thousand lives to maintain that shape. Every breath she takes is theft from the natural order."

"That's not true." Bai Suyin's voice came from behind him, strained. "I have never taken a life. Every year of my cultivation came from my own effort. The night sky. The mountain winds. The—"

"The moon," Fahai finished. "Yes, I know the fairy tale. But I have seen what lies beneath the scales. I have watched your kind feast on human hearts and call it love."

"I am not your enemy," she said, stepping beside Xu Ming. Her hand found his, fingers interlacing. Her skin was cold. Cold as the lake water that had soaked through her dress. "I never was. You made me your enemy when you burned my teacher. When you killed everyone I loved. When you decided that my existence was a crime."

Fahai's hand moved to his prayer beads. They were not the wooden beads Xu Ming had seen monks carry. These were carved from bone. Human bone, he realized with a jolt. Finger joints, polished smooth and strung on red silk.

"The Lotus Sutra teaches that all beings possess Buddha-nature," Fahai said. "But some beings choose to reject that nature. To wallow in the mud of delusion. To cling to the illusion of self when they should be seeking liberation."

"I am seeking liberation," Bai Suyin said. "That's what the heart trial is. You know this. You did it yourself."

The words hung in the air.

Xu Ming turned to look at her. "What?"

Fahai's expression flickered. For the first time, something real showed through — not anger, not malice, but something older. Something that looked almost like grief.

"You never told him," Fahai said softly. "You never told him what I am."

"He is a hunter," Bai Suyin said. "A monk who—"

"I am a snake," Fahai interrupted.

The rain stopped. Not gradually. Instantly. As if the sky itself had paused to listen.

Xu Ming stared at the man in the gray robes. The prayer beads. The golden eyes. The perfect, terrible smile.

"That's impossible," he said. "You— you hunt spirits. You—"

"I hunt those who fail." Fahai's voice dropped, losing its theatrical edge. "I hunt those who lose themselves in the flesh, who forget what they are trying to become. I was a serpent once. A black snake, coiled in the roots of a mountain, dreaming of the sky. I cultivated for six hundred years. I completed the heart trial. I became human."

Bai Suyin's grip tightened on Xu Ming's hand.

"And then?" Xu Ming asked.

"And then I saw what my kind become when they succeed. When they forget the suffering that shaped them. When they take human form and decide that human rules no longer apply." Fahai's jaw tightened. "I saw a white snake devour an entire village because a hunter had killed her mate. I saw a green snake seduce a hundred men and leave them hollow shells. I saw what power does to those who have never learned to be powerless."

"So you became a hunter."

"I became a jailer. I guard the boundary between what was and what could be. I do not kill spirits — I send them back. Back to the mountain. Back to the mud. Back to the beginning, where they can try again, if they have the wisdom to learn."

"And me?" Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "I have never killed. I have never taken a life. I have spent a thousand years trying to be worthy of this form, and you—"

"You are too powerful." Fahai's eyes met hers, and for the first time, Xu Ming saw something like pity in them. "You have cultivated for a millennium. Your true form could level this city. If you complete the heart trial, if you become fully human, you will be the most powerful mortal to walk the earth in five hundred years."

"That's not—"

"Your teacher knew. That's why she died protecting you. She knew what you would become, and she knew that the world was not ready." Fahai stepped closer, his wet robes brushing against the scattered herbs. "I am not here to destroy you, Bai Suyin. I am here to offer you a choice."

"What choice?"

"Come with me. Return to the mountain. Spend another thousand years in contemplation. When you emerge, I will be gone. The laws will have changed. Perhaps the world will be ready for you then."

"And if I refuse?"

Fahai's hand went to his prayer beads. "Then I will send you back myself."

Xu Ming's mind was spinning. He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs for coughs and fevers. His life was small and safe and predictable. And now a thousand-year-old snake was asking him to be her shelter, while another thousand-year-old snake was asking him to step aside.

"Why now?" Xu Ming asked.

Fahai turned to him. "What?"

"You said she's been cultivating for a thousand years. You've been hunting her for eight hundred. Why is tonight different? Why break down my door tonight?"

Fahai was silent for a long moment.

"Because tonight," he said slowly, "she is with child."

The words hit Xu Ming like a physical blow. He turned to Bai Suyin. Her face was pale, her eyes wide, her hand pressed against her stomach.

"What?" he whispered.

"I didn't know how to tell you," she said. "I only realized this morning. When we were at the lake. The energy—"

"The heart trial," Fahai interrupted. "When a spirit completes the heart trial, the transformation is not just spiritual. It is physical. The body reshapes itself. And when the transformation is incomplete, when the heart trial is still in progress..." He paused. "Life finds a way."

Xu Ming's hand went to Bai Suyin's stomach. Beneath the cold fabric of her dress, he felt warmth. A faint pulse, separate from her heartbeat.

"Is it..." He couldn't finish the sentence.

"Human," Bai Suyin said softly. "Mostly. It will be born human. But it will carry my essence. My power. My thousand years of cultivation, compressed into a mortal vessel."

"A child that should not exist," Fahai said. "A hybrid of spirit and human. An aberration that will grow more powerful with each passing year, until it becomes a threat to everything I have spent eight centuries protecting."

"Then we have a third option," Xu Ming said.

Fahai raised an eyebrow.

"Protection," Xu Ming continued. "If she completes the heart trial, she becomes human. The child becomes human. The power fades into mortality, diluted across generations until it's nothing but stories."

"That would take—"

"Nine months," Bai Suyin whispered. "The child will be born in nine months. If I complete the trial before then, if I become fully human, the power will transfer to the child instead of manifesting in me."

"And the child?"

"Will be mortal. Powerful, yes. Gifted, yes. But mortal. Bound by human laws, human limits, human time."

Fahai's golden eyes studied them both. The rain began again, soft at first, then harder, pounding against the broken door, seeping into the ruined shop.

"You have nine months," he said finally. "Nine months to do what no spirit has done in a thousand years. Nine months to love a mortal more than you love your own power."

"And if we succeed?"

"Then I will leave you in peace. I will find another mountain to guard, another boundary to patrol. I will forget your name."

"And if we fail?"

Fahai's hand tightened on his prayer beads. "Then I will send all three of you back to the beginning."

He turned and walked into the rain. The darkness swallowed him, and within seconds, he was gone, as if he had never been there at all.

Xu Ming stood in the ruined shop, holding a thousand-year-old snake spirit who was carrying his child, and for the first time in his quiet, safe, predictable life, he understood what it meant to have something worth fighting for.

"We need to leave," Bai Suyin said. "He will not wait nine months. He will watch. He will test us. He will find every crack in our armor and pry it open."

Xu Ming looked at the shattered door, the scattered

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The apartment was silent except for the rain.

Xu Ming sat on the edge of his bed, the shotgun across his knees. It was a double-barreled antique that had belonged to his grandfather, kept in the back of a closet for thirty years. He'd loaded it with rock salt and rusted nails—the Internet's recommendation for supernatural threats.

It felt pathetic.

Bai Suyin stood by the window, watching the street below. She had changed into dry clothes—his clothes—a gray sweater that hung loose on her frame. Her wet hair left dark patches on the shoulders. She hadn't moved in twenty minutes.

"You should sleep," she said without turning.

"I should stay awake."

"He won't come tonight. Not after how we left things."

Xu Ming's hands tightened on the shotgun. "How we left things? He blew a hole in my shop. He tried to—" He stopped. The memory was still too raw. Fahai's golden eyes. The way the air had turned solid when he raised his hand. The chanting that made Xu Ming's teeth ache in their sockets.

And then the bell.

Bai Suyin had produced a small bronze bell from nowhere, and when she rang it, the sound had cracked the windows and sent Fahai stumbling back through the hole in the wall. They'd run. Three blocks through the rain, her hand locked around his wrist, pulling him faster than he'd ever moved.

Now they were here. His apartment. His sanctuary.

It felt like a cage.

"I need to understand something," Xu Ming said.

Bai Suyin turned. Her face was pale in the dim light. "What?"

"Fahai said you killed your teacher. He said you—" He swallowed. "He said you devoured her cultivation."

Something flickered in her eyes. Pain. Rage. Guilt. She looked away.

"Is it true?"

"Yes."

The word hit him like a stone.

"I was young," she said. "By human standards, I was a child. But by snake standards, I was already ancient. I had been cultivating for three hundred years when I met my teacher. She was a white snake too. A true immortal. She had achieved human form five hundred years before I was born."

Xu Ming didn't move. Didn't speak.

"She took me in. Taught me to read the stars. To draw qi from the earth. To shape my energy into human form." Bai Suyin's voice dropped. "She loved me like a daughter."

"What happened?"

"Fahai happened." Her jaw tightened. "He found her first. Tortured her for information about other spirits. When she wouldn't talk, he—" She stopped. Her hands were shaking. "He sealed her cultivation in a pearl and left her to die as a common snake. I found her three days later. She was barely alive. She begged me to take the pearl."

"To take her cultivation."

"Yes."

Xu Ming felt sick. "Why would she ask that?"

"Because she knew what I would face. She knew Fahai would hunt me the same way. She wanted me to be strong enough to survive." Bai Suyin's eyes met his. "I refused. I told her I would find another way. But she was dying, Xu Ming. She was suffering. And I—" Her voice cracked. "I was weak. I was afraid. So I took the pearl. I absorbed her power. And I became what I am."

The rain filled the silence between them.

"That's why Fahai calls you a demon," Xu Ming said slowly.

"It's why he's not wrong."

He stood up. The shotgun felt heavy in his hands. "You were a child. You were desperate. You were trying to survive."

"Does that change what I did?"

"No." He walked toward her. "But it changes what you are."

Bai Suyin's breath caught. "Xu Ming—"

"There's a difference between someone who kills because they want to and someone who kills because they have no choice." He stopped in front of her, close enough to see the faint scales flickering at the edges of her eyes. "You're not a demon, Bai Suyin. You're a survivor."

She stared at him. Her lips parted. For a moment, she looked like she might cry.

Then the window shattered.

Xu Ming threw himself in front of her as glass exploded inward. He raised the shotgun, fired blindly—the blast deafening in the small room. The rock salt and nails tore through the air.

And hit nothing.

A paper talisman was stuck to the wall where the shot had passed. It was covered in red characters that glowed like embers.

"Clever," said a voice from outside. "But ineffective."

Fahai stepped through the broken window as if the third-floor drop meant nothing. His robes were dry. His face was calm. His golden eyes fixed on Bai Suyin with the patience of a hunter who had tracked his prey across centuries.

"I told you," he said. "You cannot run from karma."

Bai Suyin stepped in front of Xu Ming. Her hands were empty, but the air around her began to shimmer. "Stay behind me."

"白素贞," Fahai said, almost gently. "You have evaded me for eight hundred years. You have hidden in cities and mountains, in temples and brothels. You have worn human faces and spoken human words. But you cannot hide any longer."

"I'm not hiding anymore."

"No?" He smiled. "Then why do you cling to this mortal? Why do you hide behind his love like a child clutching a toy?"

"Because he's not a toy." Her voice was steel. "He's my choice."

Fahai's smile vanished. "Choice. You think you have a choice. You think the universe allows snakes to choose their fate." He stepped forward. The talisman on the wall burned brighter. "The Celestial Court has decreed that you must be purified. I am the instrument of that decree."

"The Celestial Court has been dead for three thousand years."

"Dead. Sleeping. It makes no difference. Their laws remain."

Xu Ming stepped around Bai Suyin. The shotgun was empty, but he held it like a club. "Get out of my apartment."

Fahai's eyes shifted to him. "You. The pharmacist. You have no idea what you're involved in."

"I know exactly what I'm involved in. I'm involved in her."

"Foolish. She will consume you. She will take your cultivation, your life, your soul, and leave your husk to rot."

"She hasn't."

"Not yet." Fahai's hand moved, and another talisman appeared between his fingers. "But she will. It's what snakes do."

Bai Suyin moved.

She was faster than anything Xu Ming had ever seen. One moment she was beside him, the next she was in front of Fahai, her hand reaching for his throat. But Fahai was faster. He caught her wrist, twisted, and slammed her into the wall.

The building shook.

Bai Suyin's body flickered. For a split second, Xu Ming saw something else—a massive shape, white scales, eyes like burning moons. Then she was human again, gasping, pinned by Fahai's grip.

"Your true form is weak," Fahai said. "You have been mortal too long. You have forgotten what it means to be a spirit."

"I haven't forgotten anything."

"Then show me." He released her, stepping back. "Show me the monster that devoured its own teacher."

Bai Suyin stayed where she was, pressed against the cracked wall. Her face was pale. Her hands were trembling.

Xu Ming saw it then. The fear. Not of Fahai, but of herself. She was afraid of what she would become if she let go.

"Don't," he said.

She looked at him.

"Don't give him what he wants."

"He wants me dead," she said.

"No. He wants you to prove him right."

Fahai laughed. "Clever mortal. But wisdom will not save you." He raised his hand. The talismans on the wall began to glow in unison, three of them now, forming a triangle around the room. "I have already sealed this space. You cannot escape. You cannot summon help. And you, snake, cannot transform without destroying the man you claim to love."

The temperature dropped.

Xu Ming's breath fogged in the air. Frost crept across the floor. The talismans hummed with power, and the pressure in the room grew until his ears popped.

Bai Suyin straightened. Her eyes were dark, empty. "If I die, I want you to know something."

Xu Ming's heart clenched. "Don't talk like that."

"The heart trial," she continued, ignoring him. "It's real. What I feel for you—it's real. I didn't plan it. I didn't expect it." She looked at Fahai. "But you would never understand that. You've never loved anything except your own righteousness."

Fahai's face twisted. "I loved the law. I loved order. I loved a world where demons did not wear human skin."

"Then you loved nothing."

She moved again.

This time, she didn't try to attack. She dove for Xu Ming, wrapped her arms around him, and the world exploded into white.

—

Xu Ming opened his eyes to an empty void.

He was standing on nothing, surrounded by nothing. The apartment was gone. Fahai was gone. Even Bai Suyin was gone.

Then he heard her voice.

*Xu Ming.*

"Where are you?"

*Inside. I brought you inside my barrier. We have a few minutes before he breaks through.*

He turned, but there was nothing to see. Just white. Infinite white.

*I need to tell you something.*

"Tell me when we're not about to die."

*This is the only time. Listen.* Her voice was urgent, desperate. *The heart trial requires completion. It requires a choice. Your choice.*

"I know. You told me."

*No. I didn't tell you everything.* A pause. *There are three stages to the trial. The first is attraction. The second is trust. The third is sacrifice.*

"What kind of sacrifice?"

*To complete the trial, one of us must give up something irreplaceable. For me, it would be my cultivation. My power. My true form. I would become fully human, with all the weakness that entails.*

"And for me?"

Silence.

"If I complete the trial," he said slowly, "what do I give up?"

*Your mortality,* she whispered. *If you choose to complete the trial, you will become like me. A spirit. Immortal. Bound to the serpent path forever.*

The void spun around him.

"You're saying I would become a snake?"

*No. You would become something between. Human and spirit. Mortal and immortal. You would never die, but you would never truly live either. You would exist in the space between worlds, hunted by those like Fahai, feared by those like your own kind.*

"Why didn't you tell me this before?"

*Because I was selfish.* Her voice broke. *Because I wanted you to love me before you knew the cost. Because I thought—* She stopped. *I thought if you knew, you would run.*

"Would you have blamed me?"

*No.*

The white around him began to crack. Golden light bled through the seams.

*He's almost through,* she said. *I need your answer, Xu Ming. Do you choose the trial? Do you choose me?*

The void shattered.

Fahai stood before them, robes burning with golden fire. His face was no longer calm. It was a mask of righteous fury, and behind him, the talismans had multiplied into

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Bridge Between Hearts

The hospital room smelled of antiseptic and fear. Xu Ming sat in a plastic chair beside Lin Feng's bed, watching his friend's chest rise and fall beneath white sheets. Machines beeped. IV drips counted seconds. The body in the bed was Lin Feng's, but the spirit inside was something else entirely.

Three days since the temple. Three days since Fahai's golden eyes had pinned him like a butterfly to a board. Three days since Bai Suyin had vanished into smoke and thunder, leaving him alone with the abbot's final words: *You will bring her to me, or your friend will suffer the consequences.*

Xu Ming had agreed. What choice did he have?

But now, watching Lin Feng's face twitch in unnatural rhythms—a smile where there should be pain, a frown during laughter—he understood the true cost of that agreement.

"She's fighting," a voice said from the doorway.

Xu Ming didn't turn. He knew that voice. Low, musical, carrying the weight of centuries. "You shouldn't be here."

"And yet here I am." Bai Suyin stepped into the room, her movements silent as silk. She wore hospital scrubs she'd stolen from a laundry cart, her hair pulled back in a practical bun. Without her usual elegance, she looked almost human. Almost vulnerable. "I've been watching the hospital for two days. Fahai's monks come and go. They check the morgue, the ICU, the psychiatric ward. They're looking for me."

"Then leave."

"I can't."

She stopped beside him, close enough that he could smell the rain on her skin. Or was that her natural scent? He no longer knew where the woman ended and the serpent began.

"Lin Feng is dying," she said quietly. "Not his body. His soul. Fahai has bound a fragment of his own consciousness inside your friend's mind. Every hour, that fragment grows. It consumes Lin Feng's memories, his personality, his very self. In three days, there will be nothing left of the man you knew."

Xu Ming's hands clenched into fists. "You told me you could save him."

"I said I could try." She knelt beside his chair, her face level with his. "There is a way. But it will require something from you that you may not be willing to give."

"Name it."

She hesitated. For the first time since he'd met her, Bai Suyin looked uncertain. Afraid, even. "In my true form, I can enter Lin Feng's consciousness. I can hunt Fahai's fragment and destroy it. But to maintain my human shape inside his mind, I need an anchor. Someone in the physical world who holds my essence steady."

"You need me to hold your hand?"

"I need you to hold my heart." She pressed her palm to his chest. "Literally. While I travel through Lin Feng's memories, you must keep my physical form alive. My heart will stop beating. My lungs will stop breathing. I will be dead to the world. But if you hold my heart—my real heart, not this human imitation—you can keep the connection open."

Xu Ming's blood went cold. "Your real heart."

"The one I've been cultivating for a thousand years." She pulled open the neck of her scrubs, revealing a faint glow beneath her sternum. "It's not flesh. It's crystallized essence. A pearl of pure energy. If you hold it in your hands, you will feel everything I feel. Every battle. Every pain. Every moment of the hunt."

"And if I let go?"

"Then I will be trapped inside Lin Feng's dying mind forever." She smiled, but there was no humor in it. "Two souls lost instead of one. Fahai's favorite outcome."

Xu Ming stared at the glow beneath her skin. The pearl. The heart of a thousand-year-old snake spirit. He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs. He bandaged wounds. He was not meant to hold the life force of a mythical creature in his bare hands.

"How do I get it out?"

"You cut me open."

The words hung in the sterile air. Machines beeped. Lin Feng's face twitched. And Bai Suyin looked at him with absolute, terrifying trust.

"I'll show you where to cut," she continued. "The incision will seal behind your blade. I won't bleed. But the pain will be real. I will feel every inch of steel parting my flesh. And you will feel it too, through the pearl."

"Then I'll hurt you."

"Yes." She took his hand, guided it to her chest. "But you will also save me. And Lin Feng. And yourself."

"Why does it have to be this way?"

"Because Fahai knows my tricks. He's hunted me across centuries. He's seen every spell, every technique, every strategy I've ever used. But he has never seen me die. He has never seen me give my heart to a mortal." Her eyes searched his. "He doesn't understand love. He thinks it's weakness. But love is the one thing he cannot counter."

Xu Ming looked at Lin Feng, whose face had frozen in an expression of pure terror. He looked at Bai Suyin, who was offering him her very essence. And he thought of his small shop, his quiet life, his predictable days of grinding herbs and measuring doses.

That life was gone. It had been gone since the moment she walked through his door.

"Tell me what to do."

---

The bathroom was small, tiled, lit by fluorescent lights that hummed with ancient energy. Bai Suyin sat on the closed toilet lid, her scrubs removed, her torso bare. The glow beneath her sternum pulsed like a second heartbeat.

Xu Ming held a scalpel. His hands were steady. His heart was not.

"You'll feel a pulling sensation," she said. "Don't fight it. Let the connection flow through you. When I enter Lin Feng's mind, you'll see what I see. Hear what I hear. But you must not speak. You must not react. Fahai's fragment will be listening for any disturbance."

"What if something goes wrong?"

"Then you let go." She met his eyes. "If the pain becomes too much, if you feel yourself losing control, release the pearl. Let me die inside him. It will be quick. I've died before."

"Bai Suyin—"

"I've lived a thousand years, Xu Ming. I've loved and lost and loved again. I've been burned, drowned, buried alive. Death does not frighten me." She reached up, cupped his cheek. "But losing you before I've truly had you? That terrifies me."

He leaned into her touch. The scalpel felt heavy in his hand.

"I need you to promise me something," she said. "After this is done—if I survive, if Lin Feng survives—I need you to decide. Not because of the trial. Not because Fahai is hunting us. Because you want to. You, Xu Ming, the quiet pharmacist who gave me herbs without asking questions. I need you to choose me."

"You're asking me to love you."

"I'm asking you to try." She released his face, lay back against the toilet tank. "Now cut. Just above the sternum. Straight down. Don't hesitate."

He didn't.

The blade sank through her skin like butter. No blood. Just light. Golden light that spilled from the wound and filled the bathroom with warmth. Bai Suyin gasped, her back arching, her fingers digging into the toilet seat.

"Keep going," she hissed. "Deeper. You'll feel it."

His hand moved on its own, guided by instinct or by her—he couldn't tell. The wound opened wider, and there it was: a pearl the size of his fist, pulsing with inner fire. It floated in the cavity of her chest, connected to nothing, sustained by nothing but its own impossible existence.

"Take it."

He reached in. His fingers closed around the pearl. And the world exploded.

---

He was Lin Feng.

He was eight years old, crying in a schoolyard while older boys stole his lunch money.

He was fifteen, holding his father's hand in a hospital room as cancer ate him from inside.

He was twenty-two, graduating pharmacy school, the first in his family to get a degree.

He was twenty-eight, meeting Xu Ming for the first time, thinking: *This man looks as lonely as I am.*

Then the memories tore apart.

A golden thread wove through them, darkening every joy, poisoning every hope. The thread had a voice. Deep. Ancient. Hungry.

*You cannot hide from me, serpent. I have found you in a hundred bodies, a thousand lives. I will find you again.*

Bai Suyin's consciousness slammed into Lin Feng's mind like a comet.

She was no longer human. She was white scales and ancient power, a river of muscle and magic that coiled through the landscape of memory. Her voice, when she spoke, was the sound of mountains cracking.

*Show yourself, monk. Stop hiding behind a dying man's flesh.*

The golden thread thickened, twisted, became a figure. Fahai stood in the center of Lin Feng's childhood bedroom, his robes immaculate, his eyes burning coins.

*You brought a mortal into this,* he said, his lips curling with disgust. *You gave him your heart.*

*I gave him my trust. Something you will never understand.*

*Trust is weakness. Love is poison. I have spent eight hundred years proving this.*

*Then prove it now.*

They collided.

Xu Ming felt it all. Every strike. Every scream. Every moment of Bai Suyin's body being torn apart and reforming. The pearl in his hands grew hot, then cold, then hot again. He clutched it to his chest, feeling her pain as his own, her desperation as his own.

In Lin Feng's mind, the battle raged across decades. They fought in the pharmacy where Lin Feng had worked. They fought in the temple where Fahai had first learned to hunt. They fought in Bai Suyin's oldest memory—a mountain forest, green and ancient, where a white snake had first opened her eyes to consciousness.

*You cannot win,* Fahai roared. *I have killed you before. I will kill you again.*

*Then kill me,* Bai Suyin replied. *But know this: every death makes me stronger. Every life teaches me to love more deeply. You cannot destroy what you refuse to understand.*

The golden thread snapped.

Lin Feng's body convulsed. Machines screamed. Nurses rushed into the room, but Xu Ming couldn't see them, couldn't hear them. He was still holding the pearl, still connected to Bai Suyin, still feeling her fade.

*Xu Ming.*

Her voice was a whisper. A breath. A thousand-year-old prayer.

*I'm here.*

*I love you. I have loved you since the moment I chose you. Not because of the trial. Because you looked at me and saw a woman, not a monster.*

*Don't talk like that. Come back.*

*I'm trying. But the fragment—*

She screamed.

Xu Ming's eyes flew open. The bathroom was gone. The hospital was gone. He was standing in a field of white, endless and empty, and Bai Suyin lay at his feet, her body broken, her scales cracked.

Behind her stood Fahai.

But it wasn't the Fahai from the temple. This was something older. Something vast. A thousand arms extended from his body, each one holding a weapon, each one aimed at Bai Suyin's heart.

*You have done well, pharmacist,* the thousand-armed god said. *You brought her to me. You held her heart while I destroyed her.*

"You tricked me."

*I used you. There is a difference.*

Xu Ming looked down at Bai Suyin.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: THE PRICE OF IMMORTALITY

The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, but the pressure in the room did not diminish. He stood in the shattered doorway, water pooling around his sandaled feet, prayer beads clicking with a rhythm that seemed to match Xu Ming's own heartbeat.

"Demon," Xu Ming repeated. The word felt foreign in his mouth. "She's not a demon."

Fahai's smile did not waver. "She has consumed the essence of seventeen monks across three dynasties. She has drowned a village in Jiangxi when they refused her shelter. She has killed every teacher I have sent against her for eight hundred years." He stepped forward. "What would you call that, pharmacist?"

"Lies." Bai Suyin's voice cracked like ice. "You burned that village yourself, Fahai. You killed those monks when they refused to join your crusade. You—"

"Enough." Fahai raised one hand, and the air around Bai Suyin compressed. She gasped, her body folding inward as if crushed by invisible walls. Xu Ming watched her knees buckle, her hands clawing at nothing.

"Stop!" He moved between them. "This is my shop. My home. You have no right—"

"I have every right." Fahai's voice dropped to a whisper that somehow filled the entire space. "I am sanctioned by the Heavenly Court. I carry the mandate of the Jade Emperor himself." He reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll sealed with golden characters that seemed to writhe like living things. "This is a writ of exorcism. It grants me authority over any spirit, demon, or immortal who threatens the mortal realm."

Bai Suyin's eyes went wide. "That's not possible. The Heavenly Court disbanded after the War of Seals."

"Did they?" Fahai unfurled the scroll. The characters blazed with light, and Xu Ming felt his skin prickle as if struck by static. "Or did they simply become more selective about who they revealed themselves to?"

For the first time, Xu Ming saw genuine fear in Bai Suyin's face. Not the fear of death—he'd seen that in patients before, the quiet acceptance of the inevitable. This was something deeper. The fear of being wrong about everything.

"She came to me for help," Xu Ming said, his voice steadier than he felt. "She told me about the heart trial. About becoming human. About you hunting her across eight centuries."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "The heart trial. Yes. A beautiful lie, isn't it? The promise that a demon can become human through love." He shook his head. "It has never worked. It will never work. The trial is a trap set by the Snake Queen herself to ensnare mortal hearts and consume their life force."

"That's not true." But Xu Ming's voice wavered. He looked at Bai Suyin, still crumpled on the floor, gasping for breath. "Tell me it's not true."

"It's... complicated." She forced the words out between ragged breaths. "The trial is real. But Fahai is right about one thing—it has never succeeded. Every spirit who attempted it was destroyed by the backlash."

"Why?"

"Because love requires sacrifice." She met his eyes. "And spirits... we are not good at sacrifice. We take. We consume. We hoard power like dragons hoard gold. To truly love means to give something away. To become less so that another can become more."

Fahai's prayer beads stopped clicking. "She speaks truth. For once." He tilted his head. "But she omits the critical detail. The sacrifice is not symbolic, pharmacist. It is literal. For her to complete the heart trial, she must give up her cultivation. All of it. A thousand years of accumulated power, gone in an instant."

Bai Suyin's silence was louder than any confession.

"She would become mortal," Fahai continued. "Weak. Fragile. Aged by the years she has lived. She would feel hunger, pain, disease—all the things she has avoided for millennia." He stepped closer to Xu Ming. "And you, pharmacist, would absorb her cultivation. Become an immortal yourself. Live for centuries while she withers beside you."

Xu Ming's blood ran cold. "That's not—"

"It is the mechanism of the trial." Fahai unrolled the scroll further. "Written here in the original celestial script. The heart trial is a transference ritual disguised as romance. The mortal gains power. The spirit loses everything." He looked at Bai Suyin with something that might have been pity. "She did not tell you this, did she?"

"Because it doesn't matter!" Bai Suyin struggled to her feet. "I would give it all up willingly. Every year of cultivation. Every ounce of power. I would rather live one human lifetime with you than a thousand years as a spirit."

"Pretty words." Fahai's voice dripped with contempt. "But you have said them before, have you not? To the scholar in the Tang Dynasty. The general in the Song. The poet in the Ming." He counted on his fingers. "Each one you loved. Each one you drained of their life force when the trial failed."

"That's not true!" But her voice cracked.

"Is it not?" Fahai produced another scroll, this one yellowed with age. "Shall I read the testimony of the scholar's family? How he wasted away after meeting a beautiful woman by West Lake? How his hair turned white in a single month? How he died calling her name?"

Xu Ming felt the floor shift beneath him. "Bai Suyin. Look at me."

She turned, and he saw it now—the guilt hiding behind her eyes. The shadows she had always carried. The way she never spoke of her past in specifics, only in sweeping gestures and emotional truths.

"How many?" His voice was barely a whisper.

"I don't know." Tears streamed down her face. "I stopped counting after the first century. Every time, I believed it would work. Every time, I convinced myself this was the one. The mortal who could love me enough to complete the trial." She reached for him. "But you're different, Xu Ming. I can feel it. The connection between us—it's deeper than before."

"Before." The word hit him like a physical blow. "You've done this before. Multiple times. With multiple men."

"Yes." She did not look away. "And each time, I failed. Each time, I destroyed them." Her hand trembled. "But I learned. I grew. I understand now what I did wrong. I was too attached to my power. I held back pieces of myself, thinking I could complete the trial without full sacrifice."

"And now?"

"Now I am ready to give everything." She pressed his palm against her chest again. "Feel my heart, Xu Ming. Feel how it beats for you, not for power. Not for the trial. For you."

Her heart was racing, fluttering like a bird trapped in a cage. But beneath that, Xu Ming felt something else—a slow, deep pulse that did not belong to any human. The heartbeat of something ancient and patient.

Fahai watched them with cold amusement. "The dance continues. Every thousand years, the same steps. The spirit offers love. The mortal offers trust. And in the end, one of them dies." He tucked the scrolls back into his robe. "I have seen this play out forty-seven times. I have never seen a happy ending."

"Then why not just kill me now?" Bai Suyin's voice was bitter. "Why the speeches? Why the performance?"

"Because I want you to understand." Fahai stepped closer, and Xu Ming saw something in his golden eyes—not hatred, but exhaustion. "I want you to know that your suffering is not punishment. It is consequence. Every life you destroyed, every heart you broke, every family you shattered—they are all part of the weight you carry." He touched his chest. "I have carried that weight for eight hundred years. Watching you. Hunting you. Cleaning up your messes."

"I never asked you to."

"No. You never did." Fahai's expression softened. "But someone had to. The mortal world cannot protect itself from beings like you. So I became the wall. The barrier. The hand that reaches down from heaven to correct the balance."

Xu Ming stepped forward. "You said she killed seventeen monks. Drowned a village. Destroyed her previous lovers." He held Fahai's gaze. "But she also said you burned that village. Killed those monks. Do you deny it?"

Fahai's jaw tightened. "I did what was necessary."

"Necessary." Xu Ming tasted the word like poison. "That's what every tyrant says. Every murderer. Every monster who convinces themselves they're the hero."

"The village was infected with demonic energy. The monks had been corrupted by a hundred-year-old serpent they tried to exorcise." Fahai's voice was flat. "I did not kill innocents. I purified a plague."

"And the monks who refused to join you?"

"A complication." Fahai's eyes flickered. "They chose to protect the demon rather than destroy it. They made themselves enemies of heaven."

Xu Ming turned to Bai Suyin. "Is this true? About the village?"

"Yes." Her voice was small. "But not the way he tells it. The village was infected because he drove the serpent into it. The monks were killed because they tried to protect the villagers, not the demon." She looked at Fahai with ancient hatred. "He has been rewriting history for centuries. Making himself the righteous hunter while painting me as the monster."

"Does it matter?" Fahai spread his hands. "You are a monster. I am a hunter. The roles are fixed." He looked at Xu Ming. "And you, pharmacist, must choose. Do you side with the demon who has deceived you, or the monk who has spent eight hundred years protecting humanity from her kind?"

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. At her tear-streaked face. At the guilt that radiated from her like heat from a dying fire. He looked at Fahai. At his golden eyes. At the exhaustion that lurked behind his righteous fury.

"I choose neither." Xu Ming's voice was steady. "I choose the truth. And the truth is that you're both so trapped in your ancient war that you've forgotten what you're fighting for."

Fahai raised an eyebrow. "And what are we fighting for?"

"Love." Xu Ming took Bai Suyin's hand. "She's fighting for a chance to love without destroying. And you're fighting to protect love from being destroyed." He squeezed her fingers. "But in your war, you've both become the thing you hate."

Bai Suyin's breath caught. Fahai's expression flickered—a crack in the mask of righteous fury.

"You are more perceptive than most mortals," Fahai said quietly. "But perception does not change reality. The trial will fail. She will drain you. And I will be here to clean up the remains."

"Then let it fail." Xu Ming stepped closer to Bai Suyin. "Let her drain me. Let me die trying to love her." He looked into her eyes. "Because a life without trying to love—truly love—is not a life worth living."

Bai Suyin's tears fell freely now. "You don't understand. When the trial fails, it doesn't just kill you. It consumes your soul. You cease to exist. No reincarnation. No afterlife. You become power, absorbed into me."

"I know."

"And you still choose this?"

Xu Ming kissed her forehead. "I choose you. Every version of you.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Fifth Incarnation

The wound should have healed by now.

Xu Ming pressed the cold compress against the gash on his forearm—a parting gift from a shard of Fahai's shattered prayer beads three days ago—and watched the skin refuse to knit itself back together. The edges were red. Angry. Infected in a way that made his pharmacist's instincts scream tetanus, sepsis, death.

"You're staring at it again."

Bai Suyin stood in the doorway of his bedroom, wearing one of his old shirts and a towel wrapped around her wet hair. Behind her, the shop was a wreck of boarded windows, scattered herbs, and the lingering smell of burnt incense that never quite faded.

"The bleeding stopped," she said. "That's enough."

"It's not healing."

"Because it was made by a weapon consecrated against my kind." She crossed the room, took his wrist, examined the wound with clinical detachment. "The damage transfers. What would be a scratch on me becomes a crippling wound on you. I'm sorry."

"Don't apologize." He pulled his arm back. "Just... tell me what to do."

"Nothing. Wait for it to scar." She sat on the edge of his bed, and for a moment she looked almost human—tired, fragile, uncertain. "Fahai will not attack again tonight. He's wounded too. I saw him limping."

"You saw him limping and didn't finish him?"

"He's not that easy to kill." Her voice dropped. "I've tried. Eight times across five incarnations. I know his patterns. He will rest for three days, then come again with something worse than prayer beads. Perhaps the Golden Bowl. Perhaps the Dragon Staff. He has many toys."

Xu Ming leaned against the wall. His legs ached. His head throbbed. Every bone in his body felt like it had been rattled loose and jammed back in wrong.

"The police came this morning," he said. "Neighbors reported an explosion. I told them I had a gas leak."

"And they believed you?"

"Old Mr. Zheng from the noodle shop vouched for me. Said he saw me running out with a fire extinguisher." He laughed, but there was no humor in it. "The entire city is built on lies small enough to swallow. One more doesn't change anything."

Bai Suyin looked at him with something that might have been admiration. Or pity. He couldn't tell anymore.

"You've adapted quickly," she said.

"Or I'm in shock."

"Both are possible."

They sat in silence for a long moment. The rain had stopped sometime in the night, leaving Hangzhou slick and glistening under a bruised sky. Xu Ming could hear water dripping from the eaves, cars splashing through puddles, the distant hum of a city that had no idea a thousand-year war was being fought in a back-alley pharmacy.

"Tell me about the others," he said.

"What others?"

"The other incarnations. You said you tried to kill him eight times across five lives." He met her eyes. "Tell me about them."

Bai Suyin's face went still. For a moment, he thought she would refuse. Then she looked down at her hands—her beautiful, slender, human hands—and began to speak.

"The first time, I was still learning. I had just taken human form. My cultivation was young, my heart was weak, and I believed that if I simply explained myself, he would understand." She smiled, bitter and cold. "I was wrong. He struck me down in the streets of Lin'an. Burned my body. Scattered my ashes across the West Lake. It took me sixty years to reform."

Xu Ming felt his stomach turn. "He killed you."

"Many times. The second incarnation, I tried to run. I fled to the mountains, buried myself in meditation, thought I could hide until he forgot me. He found me in a cave and sealed me beneath a pagoda for three hundred years." She touched her chest. "I can still feel the weight of the stone. The darkness. The silence."

"Why didn't you just give up?"

"Because giving up is death." Her voice was flat. Final. "When a spirit abandons the path to humanity, she does not become an animal again. She dissolves. Ceases to exist. Every moment I spend running, I am still fighting to survive."

"Third incarnation?"

"Love." She said the word like it was a wound. "I found a man in a village near Suzhou. A fisherman. Kind. Simple. He did not know what I was. I told myself I could complete the trial with him, that the heart trial was never about power but about connection." Her jaw tightened. "Fahai killed him. Drowned him in the lake where we first met. Made me watch."

Xu Ming said nothing. There was nothing to say.

"Fourth incarnation, I became a weapon. I spent a hundred years learning the art of war from a dragon spirit in the Eastern Sea. I gathered allies. Built an army of lesser spirits. I marched on Fahai's temple with three hundred warriors at my back." She looked at him. "He killed them all. Every single one. And when I lay bleeding in the ruins of his monastery, he stood over me and said, 'You cannot defeat me, Bai Suyin. You can only survive me.'"

"And this time?"

"This time I tried something different." She reached out, touched his face. Her fingers were cold. "I found a man who did not know what I was. A quiet man. A gentle man. A man who would choose to help a stranger because it was the right thing to do." Her thumb traced his cheekbone. "I told myself that if I could make him love me without my power, without my beauty, without my cultivation—if he could love the woman beneath the spirit—then the trial would complete. And I would become human."

Xu Ming caught her wrist. Held it. Felt her pulse—slow, steady, inhumanly calm.

"And now?"

"Now Fahai has found me again. And you are caught in the middle." She pulled her hand away. "I should leave."

"No."

"Xu Ming—"

"No." He stood up, ignoring the fire in his arm. "You came to me. You chose me. You dragged me into this world of snake spirits and murderous monks and a thousand years of hatred. You don't get to run now."

"I am trying to protect you."

"You're trying to protect yourself." He stepped closer. "You've been running for a thousand years, and every time you run, you lose. You lose your home. Your love. Your army. Yourself." His voice was shaking, but he didn't care. "What if this time you stayed? What if this time you fought?"

"I cannot win."

"Then at least die standing."

The words hung between them. Bai Suyin stared at him, her eyes searching his face for something he couldn't name. Then, slowly, she smiled. It was not a happy smile. It was the smile of someone who had forgotten how to hope and was remembering against her will.

"You sound like my teacher," she said.

"Was he smart?"

"He was dead." She stood up, walked to the window, looked out at the wet streets. "But he taught me one thing worth remembering. 'A spirit who seeks humanity must first learn to be human. And to be human is to choose.'"

"I'm choosing," Xu Ming said. "I'm choosing to stay."

She turned. Her eyes were wet. "Then I will choose to fight."

The shop's front bell rang.

They both froze.

"That's not the wind," Bai Suyin whispered. "The wind doesn't ring bells."

Xu Ming moved toward the door. She grabbed his arm.

"Don't."

"It could be a customer."

"It's midnight."

They stood in the darkness of his bedroom, listening. The bell rang again. Then footsteps. Slow. Deliberate. The sound of wet shoes on wooden floorboards.

"I told you," a voice said. Not Fahai. Higher. Younger. Almost playful. "I told you he would find her, but no. You never listen, do you?"

Bai Suyin's face went pale. Not fear. Recognition.

"Who is that?" Xu Ming whispered.

She didn't answer. She walked past him, through the shattered doorway, into the shop. Xu Ming followed.

A young woman stood in the center of the room. She wore a red dress that clung to her body like wet paint. Her hair was short, dyed black with streaks of silver. Her eyes were the color of amber, and they were fixed on Bai Suyin with an intensity that made Xu Ming's skin crawl.

"Xiao Qing," Bai Suyin breathed.

"Hello, sister." The woman smiled. "Long time no see."

"Where have you been?"

"Here. There. Everywhere." Xiao Qing circled the room, trailing her fingers over the shattered shelves, the scattered herbs. "I've been watching you. Waiting. Wondering when you'd finally realize that you can't do this alone."

"Xiao Qing." Bai Suyin's voice was hard. "Why are you here?"

"Because Fahai isn't the only one hunting you." Xiao Qing stopped, turned, met her eyes. "The Dragon King of the West Sea has placed a bounty on your head. Ten thousand years of cultivation for whoever delivers you alive."

The room went cold.

"Why?"

Xiao Qing's smile widened. "Because you stole something from him. Something he wants back." She looked at Xu Ming. "Or rather, someone."

Xu Ming felt the floor drop out from under him.

"What is she talking about?" he asked.

Bai Suyin didn't answer. She was staring at Xiao Qing with an expression he had never seen on her face before.

Fear.

"Tell him," Xiao Qing said. "Tell him who he really is. Or I will."

"Don't."

"Tell him, Bai Suyin. Tell him that you didn't choose him by accident." Xiao Qing's voice dropped to a whisper. "Tell him that he's the reincarnation of the fisherman you loved in Suzhou. The one Fahai drowned. Tell him that you've been hunting him across lifetimes, waiting for him to be reborn, so you could finish what you started."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin.

"Is it true?"

She wouldn't meet his eyes.

"Bai Suyin. Look at me. Is it true?"

Slowly, painfully, she raised her head.

"Yes."

The word hit him like a blade.

"You didn't choose me," he said. "You chose a ghost."

"I chose you." Her voice cracked. "You are him. You have always been him. Your soul carries the same light, the same kindness, the same—"

"Stop." He stepped back. His hand went to his chest, where his heart was beating too fast, too loud. "You've been lying to me since the beginning."

"I was trying to protect you."

"From what? The truth?" He laughed, but it was hollow. "You're no better than Fahai. You're both using me for your own war."

"That's not—"

The front door exploded inward.

Fahai stood in the opening, golden staff in hand, robes billowing in a wind that came from nowhere. Behind him, the street was empty. The moon was red.

"白素贞," he said. "I've brought company."

He stepped aside.

And the creature that walked through the door was not human. It was twelve feet tall, scaled, serpentine, with eyes like molten gold and a mouth full of needle-teeth. It wore no clothes. It needed none.

The Dragon King of the West Sea had arrived.

And he was looking directly at Xu Ming.

"

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the threat remained, coiled in his posture, in the way his wet robes hung like cerements around his frame.

"Three days," he said, and his voice carried the weight of mountains. "You have three days to surrender the serpent, or I will burn this entire district to find her."

He turned and walked back into the rain, his footsteps leaving no prints on the wet floor. The door hung broken, swinging in the wind, and through it Xu Ming could see the monk's silhouette dissolve into the storm like smoke.

Bai Suyin collapsed.

Her legs gave out. She caught herself against the counter, sending bottles clattering to the floor. Her breath came in ragged gasps, and for the first time since he'd met her, she looked genuinely afraid.

"He's stronger than I thought," she whispered. "Eight hundred years of hunting. Eight hundred years of absorbing the power of every spirit he's killed." She looked up at Xu Ming, and her eyes were wet with something that might have been tears. "I cannot fight him alone."

Xu Ming's hands were still shaking. He picked up a fallen bottle of ginseng root, set it back on the shelf. The motion was automatic, a pharmacist's reflex, but it steadied him.

"You said you needed me to love you."

"Yes."

"Is that enough? Love against eight hundred years of power?"

Bai Suyin laughed, but it was bitter, hollow. "Love is the one thing he cannot understand. He has spent centuries cutting himself from human emotion, believing it makes him pure. But purity without compassion is just another form of cruelty." She pressed her palm against her chest. "If you love me, truly, I can ascend. I can shed my serpent nature completely and become human. And as a human, I cannot be killed by his methods. They work only on spirits."

"Three days," Xu Ming repeated. "That's not enough time."

"It is enough time to fall in love if you choose to." She met his gaze, and there was something raw in her expression, something unguarded. "I am not asking you to pretend. I am asking you to look at me—at what I truly am—and decide if you can accept it."

Xu Ming thought of her scales. The pressure in the room. The massive shape that had loomed behind her, barely contained by the laws of physics. A thousand years of cultivation. A creature of legend standing in his pharmacy, asking for his heart.

"I need to see you," he said. "All of you."

She went still. "That is dangerous. My true form could crush you without meaning to."

"Then show me somewhere else. Somewhere empty."

Bai Suyin was silent for a long moment. Then she nodded slowly. "There is a place. An abandoned temple in the hills outside the city. I have used it before, in other lifetimes, when I needed to be myself without walls."

She extended her hand. Xu Ming took it.

The world inverted.

One moment they were standing in his shop, surrounded by broken glass and the smell of rain. The next, they were somewhere else entirely—a dark space filled with the scent of old incense and rotting wood. Moonlight streamed through holes in the roof, illuminating broken Buddha statues and faded murals of dragons.

"This temple was built during the Tang Dynasty," Bai Suyin said, releasing his hand. "It was dedicated to a serpent goddess who protected the local villages from flood. But when the monks came, they tore down her shrines and built their own temple on the ruins. They said her power was demonic."

She walked to the center of the space, where moonlight pooled like water on the cracked stone floor. "I was that goddess. Before I chose to cultivate human form."

Xu Ming's breath caught. "You were worshipped?"

"For three hundred years. Then forgotten. Then hunted." She turned to face him, and her eyes began to change—the pupils elongating, turning vertical, the irises shifting from brown to pale gold. "You wanted to see me. Are you certain?"

"Yes."

The transformation began.

It was nothing like the partial shift in his shop. That had been a warning, a glimpse. This was a revelation.

Her skin rippled, scales emerging like armor unfolding. Her body elongated, bones cracking and reforming, limbs shifting into something that was not quite human. She grew taller, then taller still, her head rising above the broken roof beams, her neck thickening, her shoulders broadening until she filled the temple space.

And behind her, visible now in the moonlight, was the tail.

It coiled and coiled, filling the temple, wrapping around fallen pillars, extending through broken walls into the night beyond. Scales the size of dinner plates caught the moon's glow and threw it back in waves of white and silver. Her face—still recognizably beautiful, still her—was now framed by a crest of translucent scales that rose like a crown.

She was magnificent. She was terrifying. She was a thousand years of power given form.

Xu Ming stood at her feet, and he was smaller than one of her scales.

"Is this enough?" Her voice was deeper now, resonant, vibrating through the stone. "Do you see what I am?"

Xu Ming's heart hammered against his ribs. Every instinct screamed at him to run. This was not a woman. This was a force of nature, an apex predator, a creature that had eaten storms and drunk from rivers and lived through dynasties.

But he didn't run.

He looked at her eyes—those great, golden, vertical pupils—and he saw something in them that surprised him.

Fear.

She was afraid of his answer. Afraid that after all of this, after showing him everything, he would turn away. And if he did, she would lose. Not just the trial, but everything. A thousand years of cultivation, of sacrifice, of becoming—all of it would mean nothing if the mortal she chose could not love her.

"Bai Suyin," he said, and his voice was barely a whisper. "You're beautiful."

The scales rippled. A sound escaped her throat, something between a hiss and a sob. "You do not have to lie to me."

"I'm not lying." He stepped forward, reached out, pressed his palm against the smooth curve of her jaw. The scales were cool, hard, impossibly strong. But beneath them, he could feel her pulse. "You're the most beautiful thing I've ever seen."

She lowered her head, bringing her face close to his. Her breath was warm, scented of rain and something ancient, like the air after a storm. "If you accept me like this," she said, "then the trial is almost complete. One more step. One more truth."

"Tell me."

"The trial requires me to give you something in return. A gift of equal value to the love you offer." Her eyes searched his. "I can give you immortality. Not as a spirit, but as a human who will never age, never sicken, never die. I can give you power—the ability to command the elements, to speak to animals, to heal with a touch. I can give you knowledge—every secret I have learned in a thousand years, every mystery of cultivation and transformation."

She paused. "Or I can give you the truth."

"What truth?"

"The truth about why I chose you. Not a random mortal. Not just anyone who would serve the trial. You. Specifically. Xu Ming, the pharmacist who works alone, who reads old books, who dreams of mountains he has never seen."

Xu Ming's blood went cold. "What do you mean?"

"I knew you before this life." Her voice dropped even lower. "Three hundred years ago, when I was still a goddess, there was a monk who tended my shrine. He was the only one who defended me when the new temple was built. He was killed trying to protect my altar."

She pressed her forehead against his, scale against skin. "You were that monk, Xu Ming. Your soul recognized me the moment you saw me. That is why you felt drawn to me. That is why you accepted what I am. Because three hundred years ago, you loved me enough to die for me."

The world tilted. Xu Ming's knees buckled, and he caught himself against her scales, his hands sliding on their smooth surface.

"I don't remember," he whispered.

"Of course not. You died. You reincarnated. But I never forgot." Her voice cracked. "I have searched for you through every lifetime. Watched you be born, live, die, be born again. And in this life, finally, you were old enough, close enough, ready enough for me to approach."

"Why didn't you tell me?"

"Because you needed to choose me freely. Not because of a debt, not because of a past life, but because in this moment, in this body, you see me and you want me." She pulled back, her great form beginning to shrink, scales folding into skin, bones compressing, until she stood before him again as a woman in a wet dress. "But you asked for the truth. And so I give it."

Xu Ming stared at her. His mind was a storm of memories that weren't his, feelings that belonged to someone else, a love that had crossed three centuries to find him again.

"I need time," he said again.

"We have three days."

"That's not—" He stopped. Something cold touched his neck. He reached up, and his fingers found a scale. Small, white, iridescent. It had appeared on his skin, just below his jaw.

Bai Suyin's eyes went wide. "No. That's not possible."

"What? What is it?"

"The mark." She grabbed his hand, her fingers cold. "It's supposed to appear only when the trial is complete. When love is fully given and fully returned." She looked at him with something like wonder. "Xu Ming, you already love me. You've loved me since the moment I walked into your shop."

He opened his mouth to deny it. Closed it. Because she was right.

He had loved her from the first moment. Not just because of her beauty, not just because of her mystery, but because some part of him—some ancient, buried part—had recognized her. Had known her. Had waited for her.

"I don't understand," he said. "If I already love you, why aren't you human?"

"Because the trial requires more than love. It requires choice. A conscious, deliberate choice to bind yourself to me forever." She touched the scale on his neck. "This is a promise. But it has not yet been made complete."

"How do I complete it?"

Bai Suyin's eyes glowed faintly in the moonlight. "Kiss me. Not because you feel compelled, not because of who we were, but because in this moment, you choose to be mine. Choose me as I have chosen you."

The rain had stopped. The moon was bright. In the distance, a temple bell tolled three times—the hour of the ox, the darkest hour before dawn.

Xu Ming stepped forward. He took her face in his hands, felt the warmth of her skin, the slight texture of scales that would never quite disappear.

"Three days," he said. "Fahai is coming in three days."

"Yes."

"If I do this, if I bind myself to you, I'm choosing a war against a monk who has killed gods."

"Yes."

"And I'm choosing you."

Her breath caught. "Yes."

Xu Ming leaned in. Their lips were centimeters apart when the ground exploded beneath them.

A pillar of fire erupted from the temple floor, throwing them apart. Xu Ming crashed against a wall, his head ringing, his vision swimming. Through the smoke and dust, he saw a figure walking toward them.

Not Fahai. Someone else.

title: The Bell Tolls for Her
content:
The golden light bled from Fahai's eyes like molten metal, illuminating every crack in the wooden floor, every bead of rain on the shattered doorframe. Xu Ming's hand was still pressed against Bai Suyin's chest—her heart hammering now, not with the slow eternity of before, but with the frantic rhythm of a trapped animal.

"Let her go," Xu Ming said.

Fahai's smile didn't waver. "You believe you are protecting her. I have seen this before. Every lifetime, there is a man who thinks his love can save a serpent." He stepped forward. His bare feet made no sound on the wet wood. "They are always wrong."

Bai Suyin's fingers dug into Xu Ming's wrist. "Don't listen to him. He will say anything to separate us."

"Us?" Fahai laughed—a sound like stones grinding together. "You have known her for three days, pharmacist. Three days, and already you speak of 'us.' That is her power. That is her poison. She does not love you. She *consumes* you."

Xu Ming felt the truth of his own fear pressing against his ribs. Three days. He barely knew her. But when he looked at her face, he saw something that felt older than time, more familiar than his own reflection.

"I don't care what she is," he said.

Fahai stopped walking. His head tilted slightly, like a predator reassessing its prey. "Interesting. Most men break when they see the truth." He reached into his robe and pulled out a small bronze bell, no larger than his fist. "Then perhaps you need to see more."

Bai Suyin's breath caught. "No."

"Abandon your cultivation," Fahai said, raising the bell. "Return to your animal form. I will let the mortal live."

"You will kill him anyway." Her voice was steel. "You always do."

Fahai shrugged. "True. But I will make it quick."

He rang the bell.

The sound was not a sound. It was a pressure that filled the room, that pushed through Xu Ming's ears and into his skull, vibrating his teeth, his bones, the fillings in his molars. The bottles on the shelves began to shatter one by one—cracking like eggs, their contents spilling into the floor.

Bai Suyin screamed.

It was not a human scream. It was something deeper, something that scraped against the walls of reality. Her form flickered—woman, snake, woman—her skin rippling with scales that emerged and retreated in waves. She dropped to her knees, both hands pressed to her ears.

"Stop!" Xu Ming shouted.

Fahai rang the bell again.

The floorboards beneath Bai Suyin split. Her shadow grew, swelling outward, and Xu Ming saw it—the shape she had warned him about. Massive coils, thicker than tree trunks, filling the space behind her. The bell's resonance was forcing her true form to surface.

"Every thousand years," Fahai said, ringing the bell a third time, "a snake can attempt the transformation. And every thousand years, I am there to remind them what they are."

Bai Suyin's eyes turned vertical. Her mouth opened, and her tongue forked—black and split, tasting the air. She was losing control. The woman was dissolving, and something ancient and terrible was clawing its way through her skin.

Xu Ming grabbed the bell.

Fahai's eyes widened. The monk had not expected a mortal to move. Xu Ming's hand closed over the bronze, and the vibration passed into his arm, shaking his muscles, rattling his joints. He felt his teeth crack. Blood flooded his mouth.

But he did not let go.

"You—" Fahai began.
