Xu Ming twisted. He was not strong. He was a pharmacist, not a fighter. But he had spent years grinding herbs, mixing compounds, developing the kind of grip strength that came from repetitive labor. The bell slipped from Fahai's fingers.

It hit the floor.

Silence returned—broken only by Bai Suyin's ragged breathing, the rain hammering the roof, and the distant rumble of thunder over West Lake.

Fahai looked at the bell. Then at Xu Ming. His expression shifted from amusement to something colder. "You have made a grave error."

"Leave," Xu Ming said. "Now."

"I cannot." Fahai picked up the bell, tucking it back into his robe. "The White Serpent is bound to me by a vow she made eight hundred years ago. She agreed that if she ever attempted the transformation again, she would submit to my judgment." He looked past Xu Ming, to where Bai Suyin knelt, still trembling. "Tell him, serpent. Tell him the truth you have hidden."

Bai Suyin raised her head. Her eyes were human again, but her skin was pale as snow. "I was young. I was arrogant. I thought I could beat him."

"You made a deal," Fahai said. "Your freedom for a single chance. If you failed the heart trial, you would return to the mountains and never seek humanity again." He smiled. "And you have failed."

"No." Xu Ming's voice was raw. "The trial isn't over."

"It is." Fahai pulled a scroll from his sleeve—yellowed, ancient, sealed with red wax. "I have watched you for three days. You have not confessed your love. You have not even kissed her." He unrolled the scroll. Characters glowed gold, pulsing with light. "The trial requires genuine love, freely given, before the third moon. Tonight is the third moon."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. "Is this true?"

She nodded slowly. "I thought... I thought if I could make you care, if I could earn your trust, the love would follow. But he is right. You have not said it. You have not felt it." Her voice cracked. "I forced myself into your life. I lied about why I came. And now I have doomed us both."

Fahai raised his hand. The scroll began to burn—flames licking its edges, consuming the golden characters. "The deal is sealed. The trial is void. You will return to your true form, White Serpent, and you will never walk among humans again."

"No," Xu Ming said.

Both of them turned to look at him.

He walked to Bai Suyin. He knelt in front of her, ignoring the blood still dripping from his mouth, ignoring the shattered glass cutting into his knees. He took her face in his hands—her cold, scaled, inhuman face—and he looked into her serpent eyes.

"Three days," he said. "I have known you for three days. But I have been waiting for you my entire life." His thumb traced her cheek, following the line of a scale. "I did not say it because I was afraid. Afraid of what you are. Afraid of what it would mean." He leaned closer. "But I am not afraid anymore."

He kissed her.

Her lips were cold. They tasted like rain and copper and something older, something that smelled of forests and moonlight and the deep places where the world still remembered magic. For a moment, nothing happened.

Then her heart beat.

Once.

Twice.

The space between each pulse was an eternity—but this time, Xu Ming felt it. He felt her power flowing into him, felt her spirit wrapping around his, felt something ancient and beautiful and terrifying opening inside his chest like a flower blooming in the dark.

The scroll stopped burning.

Fahai's face went blank with shock.

Bai Suyin's scales began to fade. Her skin warmed. Her eyes shifted from vertical to human, and she looked at Xu Ming with an expression he could not name—something between wonder and grief and desperate hope.

"It worked," she whispered. "You actually..."

"I love you," he said. "I don't know how or why. But I do."

The bronze bell in Fahai's pocket began to ring on its own—not the terrible sound from before, but a high, clear note, like a temple bell at dawn. It grew louder, louder, until the walls shook, until the remaining bottles shattered, until the very air seemed to vibrate with the sound of something breaking free.

Fahai's composure cracked. "Impossible. The trial requires genuine love. A mortal cannot love a serpent."

"Apparently," Xu Ming said, "I can."

The bell shattered.

Bronze fragments exploded outward, embedding themselves in the walls, the ceiling, the floor. Fahai stumbled back, his robes torn, his golden eyes wide with something Xu Ming had not seen before.

Fear.

"你做了什么？" Fahai demanded. *What have you done?*

Bai Suyin stood. She was different now—taller, her hair longer, her skin glowing with a pale luminescence. Power radiated from her like heat from a furnace. "He has freed me," she said. "The vow is broken. The trial is complete." She raised her hand, and the rain outside stopped. The clouds parted. Moonlight poured through the shattered door. "I am human now."

Fahai's hands curled into fists. "This is not over, serpent. There are other vows. Older laws. I will find a way to bind you again."

"You will try." Bai Suyin's voice was calm, steady, absolute. "And you will fail."

She turned to Xu Ming. Her eyes were soft, human, filled with tears. "I have waited a thousand years to feel this. To feel *real*." She took his hand. "Thank you."

Xu Ming opened his mouth to speak—

And the ground shook.

Not from thunder. Not from Fahai. Something else. Something *underneath* them, deep in the earth, rising.

Bai Suyin's face went pale. "No."

"What?" Xu Ming asked.

"The third moon." She looked at the sky through the broken door. "I forgot. The third moon is not just the deadline. It is the *opening*." Her grip tightened on his hand. "The gates to the underworld. The prison where the ancient spirits were sealed. When the third moon rises, the barriers thin."

Fahai's fear turned to horror. "You did not tell me this."

"Because I did not know." Bai Suyin's voice shook. "The old texts were destroyed. My teacher died before she could teach me." She looked at Xu Ming, and her eyes held a terrible understanding. "The trial was never just about love. It was about *timing*. Someone wanted me to complete the transformation tonight. Someone wanted the gates to open."

The ground split.

A crack ran through the floor of the pharmacy, through the street outside, through the very fabric of the city. From the darkness below, something laughed.

It was not a human laugh.

It was old. Hungry. Patient.

And it knew Bai Suyin's name.

Xu Ming held her hand as the world broke apart around them, as Fahai chanted prayers that did nothing, as the thing in the darkness began to climb.

"Whatever happens," Xu Ming said, "I am not letting go."

Bai Suyin looked at him—her human eyes, her human face, her heart beating in perfect rhythm with his.

"Then hold tight," she said. "Because I am not the only one who has been waiting a thousand years."

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Weight of Scales

The golden light from Fahai's eyes cast long shadows across the shattered back door. Xu Ming's hand was still pressed against Bai Suyin's chest, feeling her heartbeat accelerate from eternity-paced to something almost human in its panic.

"Step away from the serpent, pharmacist." Fahai's voice carried no anger, only the cold certainty of a man who has already decided the outcome. "This does not concern you."

Bai Suyin's fingers curled around Xu Ming's wrist, not to pull him closer, but to push him away. "Run," she whispered. "The back room. There's a passage—"

"I'm not leaving."

The words came out before Xu Ming could think. They hung in the air between them, foolish and final. He was a pharmacist. He mixed herbs. He had no weapons, no training, no understanding of the forces that now filled his small shop with the smell of burning incense and ozone.

But Bai Suyin looked at him like he had just declared war on heaven itself.

"Foolish mortal," Fahai said, but there was something like respect in his voice. "You cannot protect her. You cannot even understand what she is."

"I understand enough." Xu Ming stepped forward, placing himself between Fahai and Bai Suyin. "She told me about the mountain. About her teacher. About the eight hundred years you've been hunting her."

Fahai's golden eyes flickered. "She told you about the mountain?"

"Yes."

The abbot laughed. It was not a kind sound. "Then she did not tell you everything. Ask her, pharmacist. Ask her what happened on that mountain. Ask her whose idea it was to burn it down."

Bai Suyin made a sound like a wounded animal. "Don't."

"Truth is the only weapon that matters, serpent." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he stepped further into the room. "Tell him. Or I will."

"Bai Suyin?" Xu Ming turned to look at her. Her face had gone pale, not with fear, but with something older. Something like guilt. "What is he talking about?"

"The mountain where I was born." Her voice was barely audible. "Where my teacher raised me. Where I learned to cultivate." She swallowed. "It was... it was a prison, Xu Ming. A cage made of ancient seals and blood wards. My teacher did not raise me to become human. She raised me to become a weapon."

"A weapon against what?"

"Against everything." Fahai answered for her. "The serpent clans of the West Lake were growing too powerful. They threatened the balance between mortal and spirit realms. The Celestial Court decreed that their power must be broken. But no god could strike them down without starting a war." He smiled. "So they found a child. A white snake with potential. And they raised her to destroy her own kind."

"That's not true." Xu Ming shook his head. "She told me—"

"She told you what she needed you to believe." Fahai's voice softened, almost gentle. "I did not hunt her for eight hundred years because I hate her. I hunt her because I helped create her. And I have been trying to clean up my mistake ever since."

The shop felt smaller. The walls pressed in. Xu Ming's hand was still on Bai Suyin's chest, and he could feel her heart racing now, each beat a confession.

"Is it true?" he asked.

"Parts of it." Bai Suyin's eyes were wet. "I was raised to be a weapon. But I refused. I chose to become human instead. That is why Fahai hunts me. Because I abandoned my purpose."

"You abandoned your purpose by choosing mercy," Fahai corrected. "And mercy is the most dangerous thing a weapon can learn. You were meant to kill the serpent clans. Instead, you warned them. You helped them escape. You burned the mountain to destroy the evidence of your creation."

"To free myself!"

"To free yourself, yes. And in doing so, you freed a hundred spirits who now feast on mortal souls in the shadows of this city." Fahai's prayer beads stopped clicking. "Every death they cause is on your hands, serpent. Every family they destroy. Every soul they consume."

Xu Ming's grip on Bai Suyin loosened. "Is this true?"

She didn't answer. She didn't need to. The silence was answer enough.

"I see." Xu Ming pulled his hand away. "You came to me for protection. For love. But you didn't tell me that you unleashed a plague on this city."

"Because I was trying to fix it!" Bai Suyin grabbed his hand, pressed it back against her chest. "Every night, while you sleep, I hunt them. The ones I freed. I track them through the sewers and the abandoned buildings. I send them back to the spirit realm one by one. But there are too many, and I am not strong enough alone."

"Then why not ask for help?"

"Who would help a serpent?" She laughed bitterly. "The mortal police would see a monster. The spirit hunters would see a target. The Celestial Court would see a criminal." She looked at Fahai. "And he would see an excuse to finish what he started."

"She is not wrong about that." Fahai's golden eyes dimmed slightly. "But I am not here to kill you, Bai Suyin. I am here to offer you a choice."

"What choice?"

"Return with me to the Celestial Court. Stand trial for your crimes. Accept whatever punishment they deem fitting." He paused. "Or continue this path, and I will be forced to destroy you along with everything you have built."

"And if I choose a third option?"

"There is no third option."

Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's. Their fingers interlaced. "There is always a third option."

She closed her eyes. Her skin began to glow.

"No." Fahai's voice cracked. "You would not dare."

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice was calm now. Peaceful. "I need you to trust me. Completely. Without reservation. Can you do that?"

"Trust you? You just admitted to—"

"I know. And I will spend the rest of my existence making amends." She opened her eyes. They were no longer brown. They were white. Pure white, like fresh snow. "But right now, I need you to trust me."

The glow intensified. The bottles on his shelves rattled. The floor beneath his feet began to crack.

"What is she doing?" Xu Ming demanded, looking at Fahai.

The abbot's face had lost all color. "She is summoning her true form. In the middle of a mortal city. She will kill thousands."

"Not thousands." Bai Suyin's voice echoed now, layered with something ancient and vast. "One. Only one."

"Who?"

She looked at Xu Ming. Her white eyes held galaxies. "The serpent clans were not the only prisoners on that mountain. My teacher kept something else. Something older. Something that has been sleeping beneath Hangzhou since before the city was built."

Fahai's prayer beads snapped. They scattered across the floor like teeth. "No. You cannot. The seal—"

"I broke the seal eight hundred years ago." Bai Suyin's form began to shift. Scales rippled across her skin. Her spine elongated. "I have been feeding it my power ever since. Keeping it dormant. Protecting the city from what lies beneath."

"Protecting?" Fahai staggered backward. "You have been feeding an ancient evil for eight centuries?"

"Not an evil. A guardian." Bai Suyin's voice softened. "The first white snake. The one who chose to become human before any other. She has been waiting for someone worthy to carry her power."

She turned to Xu Ming. Her face was still hers, but something else moved beneath the surface. Something vast and patient and infinitely old.

"I cannot fight Fahai alone. I cannot hunt the spirits I freed alone. But if you trust me, if you accept what I am about to give you, we can face them together."

"What are you giving me?"

"Everything." She pressed her forehead against his. Her skin was cold. "My power. My memories. My curse." She kissed him. Her lips tasted like rain and thunder. "My heart."

The world exploded into white.

Xu Ming felt himself falling. Falling through layers of time, through centuries of memory, through the lives of every white snake who had ever walked the mortal realm. He saw the mountain before it burned. He saw the serpent clans in their glory. He saw Bai Suyin as a hatchling, learning to speak, learning to dream.

He saw her teacher. An old woman with kind eyes and a cruel purpose.

He saw the moment Bai Suyin chose mercy over destiny.

And then he saw what lay beneath Hangzhou. A vast presence, coiled in darkness, patient as stone. It opened its eyes and looked at him.

*You are worthy,* it said. *But are you willing?*

The white light faded.

Xu Ming opened his eyes.

He was still in his shop. The back door was still shattered. Fahai was still standing in the doorway, his golden eyes wide with horror.

But everything was different.

He could feel the city. Every heartbeat. Every breath. Every spirit hiding in the shadows. He could feel Bai Suyin beside him, her power flowing through his veins like a second bloodstream.

And he could feel something else. Something rising from beneath the earth.

"No." Xu Ming's voice was not his own. It was layered, ancient, terrible. "What have you done?"

Bai Suyin smiled. Her teeth were sharp. "I gave you what you asked for. The truth." She stepped back, and he saw her clearly for the first time. Not as a woman. Not as a snake. But as a bridge between worlds, holding back something that should never be released. "And now we have to deal with the consequences."

The ground shook. A crack split the floor. From deep below, something laughed.

Fahai raised his hand, golden light gathering at his palm. "You have doomed us all."

"No." Xu Ming raised his own hand. White light answered. "I have given us a chance."

The shop's walls exploded outward.

And the ancient white snake rose from beneath Hangzhou.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the weight of his presence did not diminish. He stood in the shattered doorway, water pooling at his feet, prayer beads clicking with mechanical precision.

"A demon," he repeated, savoring the word. "A serpent who has worn human skin for so long she has forgotten it is a costume."

Bai Suyin's hand found mine in the dark. Her fingers were cold. Not human cold. Dead cold.

"She is my patient," I said, surprised by the steadiness of my own voice. "Whatever conflict you have with her, it does not belong in my shop."

Fahai laughed. The sound was dry, like leaves scraping concrete. "Your shop. Your *life.* You think this is a sanctuary because you have hung herbs from the ceiling and called it medicine?" He stepped forward, and the floorboards groaned beneath him. "She has already poisoned you, pharmacist. Her presence in your life is a venom that spreads with every breath you share."

"I am not poisoned."

"No?" He stopped three feet from me. Up close, I could see the scars crossing his face—thin, white lines that looked like they had been made by claws. "Then why are your hands shaking? Why does your heart race when she touches you? Why have you already begun to love something that is not human?"

The words hit harder than they should have.

"I do not need to answer your questions."

"No. You need to answer hers." He turned to Bai Suyin. "Tell him, serpent. Tell him what you have not told him. About the trial. About what happens when it is complete."

Bai Suyin's grip tightened on my hand. "He does not need to know."

"He *deserves* to know." Fahai's smile widened. "If the heart trial succeeds, you become human. Permanently. Your cultivation ends, your power fades, and you live a mortal life until death claims you. That is the reward, yes?"

She said nothing.

"But if the trial *fails*—if you fall in love but he does not truly love you in return—your cultivation breaks. You become a beast again. No memory. No language. No soul. Just a snake, living in the mud, eating rats until something larger eats you."

I looked at Bai Suyin. She would not meet my eyes.

"Is this true?"

"It is incomplete," she said quietly. "He is leaving out the third option."

Fahai's smile vanished. "There is no third option."

"There is always a third option." She released my hand and stepped between me and the abbot. "If the trial is *interrupted*—if I am killed before it resolves—my cultivation scatters. A thousand years of power, released into the air. Any spirit that breathes it in will grow stronger. Any mortal who touches it will die."

"A cursed death," Fahai said. "Her power is tainted with her own blood. It would poison the land for generations."

"That is why he has never killed me outright." Bai Suyin turned to face me fully. "He needs me to complete the trial. To fail it. To destroy myself so that my power dies with me, uncontaminated."

"Or," Fahai said softly, "I could simply wait. Watch. Let your love bloom and wither. You are a snake, Bai Suyin. You have never loved anything without consuming it."

Something broke inside me. Not my courage. Not my will. Something smaller. A thread I had been holding since the moment she walked into my shop.

"I want you both to leave."

They turned to me.

"Now," I said. "Get out of my shop."

"Xu Ming—" Bai Suyin started.

"You lied to me." My voice cracked. "Every word. Every touch. You told me this was about love. About choosing. But it was always about *surviving.*"

"Both can be true."

"No." I pointed at the door. "Get out."

She looked at me for a long moment. Then she walked past Fahai without looking at him, stepped through the shattered doorway, and disappeared into the rain.

Fahai did not follow her immediately. He studied me with those terrible eyes.

"You have done the right thing, pharmacist. The only thing. But she will return. Spirits always return to the places where they have fed." He placed something on my counter—a small brass bell, old and tarnished. "Ring this if she comes back. I will hear it. I will come."

He stepped into the rain and was gone.

I stood alone in my ruined shop. The bell sat on the counter, waiting.

---

Three days passed.

I did not ring the bell. I did not call the police. I swept the broken glass, boarded the door, and tried to return to my life.

But my life no longer fit me.

Customers came. I sold herbs. I counted money. I smiled. But every time the door opened, my heart stopped. Every time I saw white fabric pass the window, I held my breath.

She did not come.

On the fourth day, I found a note tucked under my door.

*The Lotus Hotel. Room 17. Come alone.*

No signature. But I knew her handwriting. I had watched her write prescriptions in her elegant, old-fashioned script.

I went.

---

The Lotus Hotel was a crumbling building near the train station, the kind of place that rented rooms by the hour. The stairs creaked. The walls were thin. I could hear televisions and arguments and the wet sounds of intimacy through every door.

Room 17 was at the end of a dark hallway.

I knocked.

The door opened a crack. One brown eye peered out at me.

"You came." Her voice was raw, like she had been crying. Or screaming.

"You left a note."

"I did." She opened the door wider. The room was small, dim, the curtains drawn. She wore old clothes—a borrowed sweater, jeans that did not fit. She looked smaller than I remembered. Less like a spirit. More like a woman who had not slept in days.

"Come in," she said. "Please."

I stepped inside. She closed the door behind me, locked it, and sat on the edge of the bed.

"I have something to show you."

"I do not want to see your true form."

"No. Something else." She pulled up her sleeve. Her arm was covered in scales. Not the shimmering white of before, but something darker. Cracked. Dying.

"What happened?"

"The trial is weakening." She touched the scales with her other hand. "When you rejected me, it did not break. But it wounded me. I am losing my hold on human form."

"How long do you have?"

"A week. Maybe less." She looked at me with desperate eyes. "I did not tell you everything because I did not want you to choose out of pity. I wanted you to choose because you *wanted* me."

"I did want you."

"Past tense."

I sat down in the chair across from her. "I do not know what I want anymore. You asked me to love you. You did not ask me if I was ready to die for you."

"I would never ask that."

"You did not have to. Loving you means being hunted by Fahai. It means my shop is a target. It means my life is forfeit if he decides I am a liability."

She was silent.

"I am a pharmacist," I said. "I treat coughs and fevers. I do not fight monks who have golden eyes and a thousand-year grudge."

"You do not have to fight him."

"No. I just have to love you while he hunts you. That is somehow worse."

She laughed. It was a broken sound, full of cracks.

"I should not have come to you," she said. "I was selfish. I was desperate. I saw your kindness and I wanted to wrap myself in it like a blanket."

"You did."

"I still do." She reached out, but did not touch me. Her hand hovered in the air between us. "I love you, Xu Ming. Not because of the trial. Not because I need your protection. I love you because you are the first person in a thousand years who looked at me and saw *me.* Not the serpent. Not the power. Not the legend."

"Then why did you not tell me the truth?"

"Because I was afraid." Her hand dropped. "I have lived longer than your country's history. I have seen dynasties rise and fall. I have watched everyone I loved die. And I have never been so terrified as I am right now, sitting in this ugly hotel room, waiting for you to leave."

"I am not leaving."

"Then stay." Her eyes glistened. "Stay with me tonight. Not as a lover. Not as a spirit and a mortal. Just stay. So I do not have to face the dark alone."

I should have left. I should have walked out, rung the bell, and let Fahai finish what he had started.

Instead, I moved to the bed and sat beside her.

She leaned into me. Her head rested on my shoulder. I could feel her breathing, slow and steady, like she was memorizing the rhythm of my body.

"Tell me about your teacher," I said.

"Ling Yun." Her voice was soft. "She found me when I was still a snake, still learning to think. She taught me to read. To write. To shape my energy into human form. She said I had potential."

"What happened to her?"

"Fahai." The name came out like poison. "He caught her during the Southern Song Dynasty. He burned her at the stake of her own mountain. I watched from a cave. I was too weak to stop him."

"Is that when he started hunting you?"

"Yes. He wanted to complete his collection. A white serpent who had cultivated for a thousand years—that was his ultimate prize."

"Collection?"

She pulled away from me. Her face was pale.

"He does not only hunt spirits. He *collects* them. Their bones. Their scales. Their power. He has a temple somewhere, hidden, filled with the remains of every being he has destroyed."

I felt cold. "He wants your bones."

"He wants everything I am. And he will not stop until he has it." She looked at me. "Unless I complete the trial. Unless I become human. Then my power dies, and I become worthless to him."

"But you would lose your magic."

"I would gain a life." She touched my face. "With you."

I kissed her.

It was not gentle. It was not romantic. It was desperate, hungry, two people clinging to each other in a room that smelled of mildew and rain.

She pulled back first. Her lips were red. Her eyes were dark.

"Are you sure?" she whispered.

"No," I said. "But I want to be."

She kissed me again, and I let myself fall into her.

---

I woke in the dark.

The bed was empty beside me. The window was open, rain blowing in, curtain dancing like a ghost.

"Bai Suyin?"

No answer.

I sat up, heart pounding. Her clothes were gone. Her scent was fading.

On the pillow where her head had been, there was a single white scale.

And a note.

*I love you. That is why I have to leave. Fahai will not stop hunting me, and he will use you to get to me. I cannot let that happen. Do not look for me. Do not ring the bell. Live your life. Forget me.*

*But if you ever see a white snake in the grass, know that I am still thinking of you.*

*— Bai Suyin*

I read the note three times.

Then I went to the window.

Down in

title: The Blood Debt Comes Due
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. He stepped over the splintered remains of Xu Ming's back door, his wet robes leaving dark trails across the floor. The prayer beads in his hand clicked with each step—a sound that made Bai Suyin flinch.

"I have waited eight hundred years to speak those words to your face," Fahai said. "白素贞. The White Snake of Mount Emei. The disciple of the traitor Li Shan."

"Li Shan was no traitor," Bai Suyin said. Her voice was steady, but Xu Ming felt her hand shaking where she gripped his arm. "He was the only one who saw the truth."

"The truth?" Fahai laughed—a hollow sound that echoed off the medicine cabinets. "The truth is that spirits should remain spirits. The truth is that a snake cannot become a woman any more than a stone can become a mountain. You have been chasing an impossible dream for a thousand years, and I have been sent to wake you from it."

"Sent by whom?" Xu Ming asked. The words came out before he could stop them. Both of them turned to look at him—Fahai with amusement, Bai Suyin with fear.

"Ah. The pharmacist." Fahai's smile widened. "You have no idea what you've gotten yourself into, do you? She has told you nothing of the blood she has spilled. The lives she has taken."

"Don't," Bai Suyin said. "Fahai, leave him out of this. He is innocent."

"No mortal who aids a demon is innocent." Fahai reached into his robes and pulled out a folded piece of yellow paper. Even from across the room, Xu Ming could see the intricate red characters painted on it—a talisman. "He has given you shelter. Given you herbs. Given you his heart, perhaps. He has made himself guilty by association."

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice was barely a whisper. "When I tell you to run, you run. Do not look back. Do not stop."

"Run where?" Fahai asked. He held up the talisman, and it began to glow. "There is nowhere on this earth that I cannot find you, snake. I have your scent. I have your name. I have your—"

He stopped. His eyes narrowed.

"What is this?" He looked at Xu Ming, then back at the talisman. "There is another spirit here. A fresh one. A human transformation, incomplete but begun." His nostrils flared. "You fed him your blood."

Bai Suyin said nothing.

"You gave him your essence." Fahai's voice dropped to something cold and terrible. "You marked him. Claimed him. Bound him to your cultivation."

"It was the only way to protect him."

"Protect him?" Fahai laughed again, but this time there was no humor in it. "You have doomed him. Every spirit in the city will sense him now. Every hunter will track him. And when I kill you, the bond will break in the worst way possible—his soul will shatter trying to follow yours into death."

Xu Ming's blood ran cold. He looked down at his wrist, where Bai Suyin had cut him days ago. The scar was still there, pink and fresh. He had thought it was just a wound. A strange ritual. Not a death sentence.

"Is it true?" he asked her.

She wouldn't meet his eyes.

"Answer me."

"Yes." The word came out broken. "But I did not have a choice. Fahai was already hunting me. If I did not mark you, he would have found you anyway and used you to get to me. The bond at least gives you some of my strength. Some of my protection."

"Some," Fahai repeated. He tucked the talisman back into his robes and drew out something else—a small bronze mirror, ancient and tarnished. "You overestimate your own power, snake. Your protection means nothing against the Mirror of Truth."

Bai Suyin's face went pale. "You found it."

"I never lost it." Fahai held up the mirror. "Do you know what this does, pharmacist? It reflects the true nature of any being. Not their appearance, not their disguise, but their soul. Let me show you what your lover truly is."

He angled the mirror toward Bai Suyin.

She screamed.

The reflection in the mirror was not a woman. It was a massive white serpent, coiled and writhing, its scales glistening with something dark. Its eyes were not eyes—they were pits of ancient hunger, older than civilization, older than humanity itself. And wrapped around the serpent's body, tangled in its coils, were skeletons. Dozens of them. Human skeletons, their bones cracked and hollow.

"That is what she hides from you," Fahai said. "Every life she has taken to fuel her cultivation. Every mortal she has drained to maintain her form. She is not a woman who became a snake. She is a snake who learned to wear a woman's skin."

"Stop," Xu Ming whispered.

"Look closer. Those bones—that one there, with the jade bracelet still on its wrist. That was a merchant she seduced in the Tang Dynasty. She drained him over three months and left his body in a ditch. This one, with the cracked skull—a scholar she promised to marry. She ate his heart on their wedding night."

"Stop it!" Xu Ming grabbed the mirror from Fahai's hand and threw it against the wall. It shattered into a dozen pieces, each shard reflecting fragments of the terrible image.

Fahai watched him calmly. "You cannot destroy truth by breaking its vessel."

"That is enough," Bai Suyin said. Her voice had changed. It was deeper now, resonant, as if something else was speaking through her. "You have had your fun, Fahai. You have shown him your version of the truth. Now let me show him mine."

She stepped forward, and Xu Ming saw the transformation begin—not the partial scales from before, but something more controlled. More deliberate. Her eyes turned silver. Her hair lifted as if caught in a wind that only she could feel. The air in the room grew heavy, thick with the smell of wet earth and river stones.

"I did not kill those people," she said. "Not the way he described. The merchant was dying of plague when I found him. I stayed with him until the end, and when he died, I absorbed his residual qi so it would not be wasted. The scholar was a murderer who had killed three women before he met me. I gave him a chance to repent. He refused. I took his heart, yes—but it was already black with rot."

"Lies," Fahai said.

"Truth." She turned to Xu Ming. "I will not pretend to be innocent. I have killed. In a thousand years of cultivation, I have taken lives—some justified, some not. I have made mistakes. I have been cruel when I should have been kind. But I have also saved lives. I have healed the sick. I have protected villages from floods and fires and worse things than me."

"Why should I believe you?" Xu Ming asked. His voice sounded hollow even to himself.

"Because I am standing here, in your shop, asking you to." Her silver eyes held his. "Because I could have killed you the first night I came here. I could have drained you dry and moved on. But I didn't. I stayed. I talked to you. I let myself care about a mortal who mixes herbs for a living, who reads poetry by candlelight, who has never hurt anyone in his entire gentle life."

Fahai made a sound of disgust. "Pretty words from a pretty snake. But words cannot change what you are."

"Neither can mirrors," Bai Suyin said. She raised her hand, and the shattered pieces of the Mirror of Truth rose from the floor, floating in the air around her. "You have used this to torment me for centuries, Fahai. You have shown everyone I have ever loved the worst parts of me. But you have never shown them the whole truth."

She touched one of the shards, and it began to glow.

"You want to see my true nature?" Her voice rang through the shop like a bell. "Then look."

The shards arranged themselves in the air, forming a larger image—not the serpent of before, but a woman standing at the edge of a cliff. Behind her, a city burned. In front of her, a sea of demons advanced. She was wounded, bleeding, one arm hanging useless at her side. But she did not run.

"She held the line for three days," Bai Suyin said quietly. "Against an army of corrupted spirits that would have destroyed everything. She saved ten thousand lives. And when the battle was over, she crawled into a cave and wept because she could not save ten thousand more."

The image shifted. Now it showed her kneeling beside a child with a fever, pressing cool cloths to its forehead. Then her hands, covered in mud, pulling survivors from a collapsed temple. Then her face, streaked with tears, as she held a dying woman and promised to protect her daughter.

"I have done terrible things," Bai Suyin said. "But I have also done good things. I have loved. I have lost. I have tried, every day for a thousand years, to become something better than what I was born as. That is the truth that Fahai will never show you, because it does not fit his narrative."

Xu Ming looked at Fahai. The monk's face was stone.

"Is any of this true?" he asked.

"The images are true," Fahai admitted. "But they are irrelevant. Good deeds do not erase evil. A murderer who saves one person is still a murderer."

"And a hunter who kills a thousand innocents in the name of purity is still a monster," Bai Suyin said. She let the shards fall. They clattered to the floor, harmless glass once more. "Tell him, Fahai. Tell him about the village you burned because you thought I was hiding there. Tell him about the children you drowned because their mother was a water spirit. Tell him about the woman you loved, and what happened when she refused to join your crusade."

Fahai's composure cracked. Just a fraction, just for a moment—but Xu Ming saw it. A flicker of something human beneath the gold.

"Do not speak of things you do not understand."

"I understand perfectly." Bai Suyin's voice softened. "I know about Xiao Yan. I know why you really hunt me. It was never about the law of heaven. It was never about purity. It was about her."

"Silence."

"She was a fox spirit, wasn't she? Beautiful. Kind. And she chose to die rather than let you become the monster you are today."

Fahai's hand shot out, and a beam of golden light struck Bai Suyin in the chest. She flew backward, crashing into the medicine cabinet, sending bottles and herbs scattering across the floor. Xu Ming started toward her, but Fahai raised his other hand and a wall of invisible force stopped him.

"Do not interfere," Fahai said. His voice was calm again, but his eyes were burning. "This is between me and the snake."

Bai Suyin pulled herself upright. Blood—red, human blood—dripped from her lip. "You could have been great, Fahai. You could have been a true protector. But you let your grief turn you into something worse than any demon."

"I will end this." Fahai drew a sword from his robes—no, not a sword, a staff of polished wood, wrapped in golden wire and inscribed with sutras. "I will end you,

title: The Burning Gold
content:
The rain hammered against the broken doorframe. Fahai stood in the gap, his wet robes clinging to a body that seemed too still, too composed for someone who had just kicked down a solid wooden door. His prayer beads clicked once. Twice. The sound was louder than it should have been.

Xu Ming's hand was still pressed against Bai Suyin's chest. Her heart hammered beneath his palm, fast and irregular, like a bird trapped in a cage of bone. He could feel the scales rising beneath her skin again, rough ridges forming and dissolving in rapid succession.

"Let go of me," she whispered.

He didn't.

Fahai took a step forward. His bare feet left no prints on the wet floor. "The White Snake has always been clever. Hiding among mortals. Playing at humanity." His golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming. "But she has never been clever enough to hide her scent. Incense and serpent. It clings to everything she touches."

Xu Ming's pharmacy training kicked in. Assess the situation. Identify the threat. Find the exit. The back door was gone. The front door was fifteen feet away, past Fahai. The windows were barred against thieves. They were trapped.

"Get behind me," he said.

Bai Suyin's head snapped up. "What?"

"Get behind me." He stepped forward, putting himself between her and the abbot. His hands were shaking, but his voice held steady. "You're in my shop. You're my patient. I don't care what he calls you."

Fahai's smile widened. "Brave. Foolish, but brave." He raised one hand, and the prayer beads caught the light. Each bead was carved from bone, yellowed with age, etched with characters Xu Ming couldn't read. "Step aside, pharmacist. You have no idea what you're protecting."

"I know exactly what I'm protecting." Xu Ming's mind raced. Fahai was confident. Too confident. That meant he had power he hadn't shown yet. But confidence also meant arrogance. Arrogance meant openings. "She came to me for help. I gave my word. That's how this works."

"Your word means nothing against eight hundred years of duty."

"Then we have a problem."

Fahai laughed. It was a dry sound, like leaves scraping concrete. "You mortals are all the same. You think love can save you. You think conviction matters." He stepped closer. "I have burned forests that sheltered her kind. I have drowned caves where they bred. I have scattered the ashes of her teacher across seven rivers so that she could never be reborn. And you think your *word* will stop me?"

Xu Ming felt Bai Suyin's hand grip his shoulder. Her fingers were cold, her nails sharp against his skin.

"Don't," she breathed. "He wants you to fight. It gives him permission."

"Permission for what?"

"To kill you."

Fahai's prayer beads stopped clicking. The shop fell silent except for the rain. "She understands. She has always understood. I do not harm mortals unless they force my hand. Step aside, and you live. Protect her, and you become an obstacle." His golden eyes narrowed. "I remove obstacles."

Xu Ming reached into his pocket. His fingers found the small cloth bag he always carried—the one with the dried mugwort and salt, a traditional remedy for nightmares that his grandmother had taught him to make. It was useless. Pathetic. But it was all he had.

"I'm not stepping aside."

Fahai moved.

One moment he was ten feet away. The next, his hand was around Xu Ming's throat, lifting him off the ground. The old pharmacist slammed against the wall, bottles rattling on their shelves, the breath crushed from his lungs.

"You are nothing," Fahai said, his face inches from Xu Ming's. The golden eyes were not just bright—they were *hot*. Heat radiated from them like coals. "Your entire life is the blink of an eye to me. Your ancestors were dust when I began my hunt. Your great-grandchildren will be dust when I finish it. You exist in a single moment, and you dare to stand in my way?"

Xu Ming couldn't breathe. His vision was darkening at the edges. But his hand still held the cloth bag, and with the last of his strength, he shoved it into Fahai's face.

The abbot recoiled.

His grip loosened, just enough.

Xu Ming dropped, gasping, and scrambled backward. The cloth bag lay on the floor between them, split open, mugwort and salt scattered across the tiles. Fahai stared at it with something that might have been recognition.

"Mugwort," he said. "Salt. You think peasant superstitions can harm me?"

"No," Xu Ming coughed. "But I thought they might surprise you."

Behind Fahai, Bai Suyin moved.

She didn't run. She flowed—her body becoming liquid shadow, sliding across the floor, reforming behind the abbot with her hand pressed against his spine. Her fingers had become claws. White scales traced her forearm like living tattoos.

"Touch him again," she said, "and I will show you what a thousand years of cultivation looks like from the inside."

Fahai didn't turn. "You would kill a man in front of a mortal? You would break your own trial?"

"I would break *you*."

"For what? For him?" Fahai laughed again. "He will be dead in sixty years. Less, if his lungs are weak. And you will still be here, still a snake, still running. Killing me changes nothing."

"It changes tonight."

"Tonight is nothing." Fahai raised his hand. The prayer beads glowed. "I have tracked you across dynasties. I have found you in the bodies of empresses and beggars. I have pulled you from the Forbidden City and from rice paddies. You cannot hide. You cannot run. And you cannot win."

He turned.

Bai Suyin's claws raked across his chest.

They passed through him like smoke.

Fahai's form dissolved, reforming three feet to the left. The prayer beads swung in his hand, and Xu Ming realized what they were—not just decoration, but a weapon. Focus. A container for power.

"Your teacher taught you that trick," Fahai said. "She used it the night I burned her. It did not save her then. It will not save you now."

Bai Suyin's face was a mask of pure hatred. "You do not get to speak of her."

"I speak of all the serpents I have killed. She was not special. She was simply the one who thought she could hide you." Fahai's eyes burned brighter. "But I have always found you, Bai Suyin. Always. Because you make the same mistake every time."

"What mistake?"

"You fall in love."

He snapped his fingers.

The prayer beads exploded outward, each bead becoming a chain of golden light that wrapped around Bai Suyin's arms, her legs, her throat. She screamed—a sound that was half human, half something far older and far more terrible. The chains burned against her skin, leaving black marks where they touched.

"Love makes you predictable," Fahai said, walking toward her. "It gives you a shape. A form I can grasp." He grabbed her chin, forced her to look at him. "Every lifetime, you find a mortal to cherish. And every lifetime, I use that mortal to find you."

Xu Ming's hand found a glass bottle. He didn't know what was in it. He didn't care.

He threw it at Fahai's head.

The bottle shattered against the abbot's skull. Liquid splashed across his face—some kind of liniment, strong-smelling, probably containing camphor and menthol. Fahai staggered, his concentration broken, and the golden chains flickered.

Bai Suyin moved.

She tore free of the chains, scales rippling across her body, her eyes turning vertical and gold. Her voice dropped an octave, becoming something that resonated in Xu Ming's bones.

"Run," she said. "Now."

Xu Ming didn't argue.

He grabbed her hand and pulled her toward the front door. She was heavy—heavier than she looked, dense with power she couldn't fully contain. But she ran with him, her bare feet slapping against the wet floor, the chains reforming behind them as Fahai shook off the liniment.

They burst through the front door into the rain.

The street was empty. The storm had driven everyone inside. Streetlights cast yellow pools across the wet asphalt, and the wind tore at their clothes.

"This way," Bai Suyin said, pulling him left. "There's a temple. Three blocks. I can—"

A wall of golden light erupted in front of them.

They skidded to a stop. The light was inscribed with characters—Sanskrit, Xu Ming realized, or something like it. The words burned in the air, forming a barrier that stretched across the entire street.

"He's sealed the area," Bai Suyin said. "He's been planning this. He knew I would come here."

"Then we fight."

"We can't fight him. Not yet. Not here." She turned to face him, and for the first time, he saw fear in her eyes. Real fear. "He's too strong. He's had eight centuries to perfect his methods. I've had eight centuries of running."

"Then what do we do?"

She looked at him. The rain plastered her hair to her face, and the scales were receding, leaving her skin pale and human and vulnerable.

"We give him what he expects," she said. "We run."

She pulled him into an alley. The golden light behind them flickered as Fahai stepped out of the pharmacy, his robes untouched by the rain, his eyes burning like twin suns.

"白素贞!" His voice echoed across the street. "你逃不掉的!"

Bai Suyin didn't stop. She dragged Xu Ming through the alley, over a collapsed fence, through a garden that someone had abandoned to weeds. Her hand was cold in his, but her grip was iron.

"I can hide us," she said between breaths. "But only for a little while. He'll find the gap eventually."

"How long?"

"Three days. Maybe four."

"And then?"

She stopped. Turned. Looked at him with those ancient eyes that held a thousand years of running.

"Then you have to decide," she said. "Do you love me enough to die for it?"

The rain fell between them. The golden light grew closer. And Xu Ming realized that his small, safe, predictable life had ended the moment she walked into his shop.

He just hadn't known it yet.

"I don't know if I love you," he said. "But I know I'm not leaving you."

Something shifted in her face. Hope, maybe. Or fear of hope.

"That's enough," she said. "For now."

She pulled him into the darkness, and the golden light closed behind them like a door slamming shut.

title: The Price of Shelter
content:
The rain had stopped, but water still dripped from the shattered doorframe, each droplet counting seconds that felt like years. Fahai stood in the wreckage, golden eyes fixed on Bai Suyin with the patience of a hunter who had waited eight centuries.

Xu Ming stepped forward. "She's not a demon. She's my—"

"Your what?" Fahai's smile didn't reach his eyes. "Your patient? Your lover? Your salvation?" He clicked his prayer beads once. The sound cracked through the room like thunder. "You know nothing of what she is, pharmacist. You see a pretty face and imagine a future. I see scales beneath silk and a hunger that has devoured seventeen men across three dynasties."

"That's not true." Bai Suyin's voice was steady, but her hand found Xu Ming's wrist. "You know the law, Fahai. I have never taken a human life. Not once in a thousand years."

"Not directly." Fahai stepped closer. His robes swept across broken glass without making a sound. "But the men who loved you? They died. Every single one. Your heart trial consumed them. You drained their years, their vitality, their very existence—"

"I loved them."

"You fed on them." He was close enough now to touch. Close enough that Xu Ming could smell the incense burning in his robes, could see the faint scarring across his shaved scalp. "You are a serpent, Bai Suyin. Serpents do not love. They coil. They constrict. They swallow."

Bai Suyin's composure cracked. Something flickered beneath her skin—scales, gold and white, rising like anger made visible. "You would know about swallowing, wouldn't you, Fahai? You swallowed your own humanity when you took that monk's name. You are no more a holy man than I am. You are a butcher wearing robes."

Fahai's smile vanished.

"You dare—"

"I dare." She stepped forward, releasing Xu Ming's wrist. Her fingers elongated slightly, nails catching the dim light like knives. "Because I remember, Fahai. I remember you. Before the robes. Before the mission. You were a farmer's son who watched your mother die of a snakebite. That is what drives you, isn't it? Not righteousness. Revenge."

The silence that followed was absolute.

Fahai's hand moved so fast Xu Ming didn't see it—just a blur, then Bai Suyin was pinned against the wall, the monk's forearm pressed across her throat. Her feet dangled six inches off the ground.

"You remember nothing," Fahai hissed. "I was eleven years old when I found her in the field. Her face was black. Her veins had turned to poison. And the snake that bit her was white. White as snow. White as your cursed skin."

"Put her down." Xu Ming grabbed Fahai's shoulder. The monk didn't move. It was like pushing against a statue. "I said put her down!"

Fahai turned his head. Those golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming, and the pharmacist felt something cold slide down his spine—a presence pressing against his consciousness, trying to find cracks in his mind.

"You love her already." It wasn't a question. "I can smell it on you. That sweet, foolish mortal devotion. You would die for her, wouldn't you? You would kill for her."

"I would ask you to leave my shop."

"Your shop." Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "Your shop is built on a ley line. Do you know what that means, pharmacist? Every spirit in Hangzhou can feel this place. Your shop is a lighthouse for the supernatural. You never noticed because you were blind. But she noticed." He jerked his chin at Bai Suyin. "The moment she walked through your door, she tasted power. That is why she chose you. Not your gentle hands. Your location."

Bai Suyin clawed at Fahai's arm. Her nails left gouges in his skin, but the wounds closed as fast as they appeared. "Don't listen to him, Xu Ming. He twists everything."

"Does he?" Xu Ming's voice was quiet. "You did find me pretty fast. The same day I first saw you, you were in my shop asking for herbs that haven't been sold in centuries. How did you know I had them?"

Bai Suyin's eyes widened. "I—"

"My father kept them. Old recipes. Strange ingredients." Xu Ming looked at her, and for the first time, doubt crept into his voice. "Nobody knew about them. Not even my assistants. But you walked in and asked for them by name. How?"

Fahai smiled. "There. The first crack in the illusion. Keep pulling, pharmacist. You'll find the truth beneath her pretty lies."

"I'm not done." Xu Ming turned back to the monk. "And you. You've been hunting her for eight hundred years. You say she's killed seventeen men. But she's still alive. If you're so powerful, why hasn't she been caught?"

Fahai's smile faltered.

"If she's a monster," Xu Ming continued, "then a holy man like you should have destroyed her centuries ago. But she's still here. Which means either you're not as holy as you pretend, or she's not as monstrous as you claim. Which is it?"

Bai Suyin stared at him. Something shifted in her eyes—surprise, yes, but also something deeper. Respect, perhaps. Or fear.

Fahai released Bai Suyin. She dropped to the floor, gasping, her hand going to her bruised throat.

"You are cleverer than you look," Fahai said. "I will grant you that. But cleverness will not save you from what is coming." He reached into his robes and withdrew a scroll. Old paper, yellowed with age, bound with a red silk cord. "Do you know what this is?"

Xu Ming shook his head.

"This is an exorcism decree, signed by the Heavenly Court itself. It gives me authority to bind, banish, or destroy any spirit that threatens the balance of the mortal realm." He held it up. The characters on the scroll glowed faintly gold. "I have carried this for seven hundred years. And in all that time, I have never once used it on a spirit that did not deserve its fate."

"Then why haven't you used it on her?"

"Because she has not yet broken the law." Fahai's eyes narrowed. "But she will. They always do. And when she does, I will be there. I will always be there, Bai Suyin. Every city. Every lifetime. Every man you try to love. I will find you, and I will remind you what you truly are."

He turned and walked through the shattered door. At the threshold, he paused.

"Three days. That is how long you have before the ley line here attracts something far worse than me. Something that does not care about decrees or balance. Something that will devour this entire district if it wakes." He looked over his shoulder. "You wanted a shelter, serpent? You found one. But every shelter has a price. And yours is about to come due."

Then he was gone.

The silence that followed was deafening.

Bai Suyin slumped against the wall, her carefully maintained composure crumbling. She looked small. Human. Terrified.

"Xu Ming..." She reached for him.

He stepped back.

"Don't."

"Please. I can explain—"

"You said you'd never killed anyone. But seventeen men loved you. Seventeen men died." His voice cracked. "Those are not the same thing, Bai Suyin. Tell me the truth. Did you kill them?"

She closed her eyes. When she opened them, they were wet.

"No. I did not kill them. But I did not save them either." She hugged herself, arms wrapped tight as if holding her body together. "The heart trial requires me to fall in love with a mortal and have that love returned. If I succeed, I become human. If I fail..." She swallowed. "The trial consumes the mortal. Their life force. Their soul. Everything they are, burned away to fuel my transformation."

Xu Ming felt the floor tilt beneath him. "You're telling me that loving you is fatal."

"Yes." The word came out broken. "That is why Fahai calls me a demon. Because no matter how pure my intentions, the trial demands sacrifice. I have loved seventeen men. I have tried to be good. I have tried to be worthy. But every time, something goes wrong. The trial accelerates. My control slips. And they die."

"Then why are you here?" His voice rose. "Why choose me? Why drag me into this?"

"Because I am tired of failing!" She slammed her fist against the wall. The plaster cracked. "I have spent a thousand years trying to become human. I have studied. I have meditated. I have starved myself of power, refused to hunt, lived among mortals like a beggar hoping for scraps. And still, I cannot complete the trial. Still, I am a serpent wearing a woman's skin."

She turned to face him fully. Her tears had stopped. In their place was something harder. Desperate.

"Fahai is right about one thing. The ley line beneath this shop is powerful. It is why I came here. But not to hide. To finish the trial. To draw enough power from the earth itself that I can complete the transformation without consuming you."

Xu Ming stared at her. "You want to use me."

"I want to survive." She stepped closer. "And I want you to survive with me. That has never been an option before. Every other man I loved, I watched wither. I watched the light leave their eyes. I held their hands as they died, knowing I was the poison that killed them." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "I cannot do that again. I will not. So I found another way."

"Using the ley line."

"Yes. The earth's energy to substitute for your life force. It has never been attempted. It may kill me instead. But it is the only chance we have." She reached out, stopped herself inches from touching his face. "I know you do not trust me. I know you have no reason to. But Fahai is not the only thing coming. He spoke of something worse. Something that will wake in three days."

"What is it?"

"I do not know. But I can feel it." She pressed her hand to her chest. "Beneath the city, something ancient is stirring. Something that has been sleeping since before the first dynasty. And when it wakes, it will not care about mortals or spirits. It will consume everything."

The shop's lights flickered.

Xu Ming looked around at the shattered door, the scattered herbs, the life he had built that was now lying in pieces at his feet. A thousand-year-old snake spirit. A monk with golden eyes. An ancient evil waking beneath Hangzhou. And him—a pharmacist who couldn't even kill a spider without feeling guilty.

"What am I supposed to do?" he asked, his voice barely audible.

Bai Suyin finally touched his face. Her fingers were cold, but her eyes were warm.

"Trust me. Just a little. Give me three days, and I will show you a way out of this. I will show you who I truly am—not a demon, not a serpent, but a woman who has spent a thousand years trying to earn a human heart."

She leaned in. Her lips brushed his forehead.

"I choose you, Xu Ming. Not because of your shop. Not because of the ley line. Because when you looked at me in that temple, you did not see a monster. You saw someone worth saving

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Price of Scales

The gold in Fahai's eyes flickered like candle flames as he stepped fully into the shop. His robes were simple gray cotton, soaked through from the rain, but he moved with the weight of someone who had walked centuries. Each step left a wet footprint on Xu Ming's clean floor.

"I expected more resistance," Fahai said, tilting his head at Bai Suyin. "You've grown weak, hiding among mortals."

Bai Suyin stood between them, her body rigid. Xu Ming could see the muscles in her back trembling, but whether from fear or fury, he couldn't tell. She spoke without turning. "Xu Ming. Get behind the counter."

"Don't—"

"Get behind the counter."

His feet moved before his brain caught up. The pharmacist in him, the man who had spent ten years learning to follow instructions, carried him to the safety of his workspace. He hated himself for it. The herbs and bottles rattled as his hip knocked against the wooden counter.

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "You still protect them. After all these years, you still believe mortals deserve your sacrifice." He shook his head slowly. "They will never accept you. They will never love you. You are a serpent wearing a woman's skin, and when they see what lies beneath, they will burn you."

"You know nothing about love," Bai Suyin said.

"I know it is a weakness. A distraction." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he rolled them between his fingers. "I have seen you fall for the same trick a dozen times. A kind face. A gentle voice. You think each lifetime will be different, and each lifetime, you are wrong."

Xu Ming's hand found a ceramic jar on the shelf behind him. Not a weapon. He didn't know what he was doing. But his fingers wrapped around the cool clay, and he held it like a talisman.

"I am not wrong this time," Bai Suyin said.

"You say that every time."

Fahai moved. One moment he was at the door. The next, he was three feet closer, and Xu Ming hadn't seen him walk. The Buddha beads had stopped clicking. The air had gone still.

Bai Suyin's hand shot out, palm open. A wall of white energy shimmered between them, glowing like moonlight on water. Fahai's advance stopped as if he'd hit glass.

"Impressive," Fahai said. "You've recovered more strength than I anticipated. The mortal must be feeding you well."

"Leave. Now. And I will let you walk away."

"Let me?" Fahai laughed again, louder this time. The bottles on Xu Ming's shelves vibrated. "You forget yourself, serpent. I am not the one who has been running for eight centuries."

He pressed his palm against the barrier. Where his skin touched the light, the energy hissed and smoked. The smell of burning incense filled the shop. Not the pleasant kind that monks burned in temples. This was sharp. Chemical. Wrong.

Bai Suyin's arm trembled. Sweat beaded on her forehead.

"You're still weak from the transformation," Fahai said, almost gently. "You poured too much of yourself into that human shape. You have nothing left to fight with." He pressed harder, and the barrier cracked. A single line, thin as a hair, carved through the light. "I have been hunting you since the Tang Dynasty. You think a mortal pharmacist will save you now?"

Xu Ming's hand tightened on the ceramic jar. The Tang Dynasty. That was over a thousand years ago. Fahai had been hunting her for longer than most civilizations had existed.

*He is not a monk. He is a hunter.*

Bai Suyin's knees buckled. The barrier flickered. Fahai's hand broke through, fingers reaching for her throat.

Something inside Xu Ming snapped.

He threw the jar.

It was a stupid, desperate move. A ceramic container of dried chrysanthemum petals, worth maybe twenty yuan, sailing through the air toward a thousand-year-old monster. Fahai didn't even look. He caught it without breaking eye contact with Bai Suyin, crushed it in his fist. Petals and ceramic dust rained to the floor.

But it was enough. The moment of distraction. Bai Suyin twisted, dropped low, and swept her leg across the floor. Fahai stumbled. Not fell. Just stumbled. But it created space.

She grabbed Xu Ming's arm. "Run."

They ran.

The back door was already destroyed, so they went through the front. Xu Ming's shoulder slammed into the wooden frame as they burst into the rain-soaked street. Hangzhou at midnight. The city was never truly dark, its lights reflecting off the low clouds, but the rain had driven everyone indoors. The streets were empty.

"Where?" Xu Ming gasped.

"The lake."

"Are you insane? He'll—"

"The lake," she repeated, and her voice carried an edge he hadn't heard before. Not desperation. Authority. The voice of someone who had lived a thousand years and made a thousand decisions. "Trust me."

They ran through the narrow alleys of the old city, past shuttered shops and sleeping neighborhoods. Bai Suyin moved like water, flowing around corners, never slipping on the wet stones. Xu Ming stumbled behind her, his lungs burning, his shoes soaked through.

Behind them, he heard the Buddha beads. Click. Click. Click. Fahai was walking, not running. And he was keeping pace.

"He's still coming," Xu Ming gasped.

"He will always be coming. That is his nature."

They burst out of the alley onto the shore of West Lake. The water stretched before them, black and silver in the rain, the famous causeways empty of tourists. The pagodas on the islands were dark silhouettes against the storm clouds.

Bai Suyin didn't stop at the edge. She ran straight into the water.

Xu Ming skidded to a halt at the shore. "What are you—"

She turned back. Her face was different. Not the subtle shifts he'd seen in the shop. This was something else. Her eyes were no longer brown. They were vertical slits, pupil and iris gone, just black and gold and ancient. "I am going to show you what I truly am."

She sank.

The water swallowed her without a splash. One moment she was there, standing on the surface, her wet dress clinging to her body. The next, she was gone.

Xu Ming stood at the edge of the lake, rain pounding against his face, his chest heaving. He had seconds. Fahai was coming. He could hear the prayer beads. Click. Click. Close now.

He looked at the water. Black. Empty. She had asked him to trust her. She had said she needed him to love her. But she had also said that if he could see what she truly was, it would break him.

Fear was a cold hand around his throat.

The lake rippled.

Something rose from the darkness beneath the surface. Not a hand. Not a face. A shape. Massive. Coiling. The water bulged upward as if a whale were surfacing, but this was no whale. This was longer than a bus. Wider than a house. Scales caught the dim city lights, shimmering white and silver and blue, each one the size of Xu Ming's palm.

His mind refused to process what he was seeing.

The head broke the surface.

It was beautiful. That was the worst part. It was utterly, terrifyingly beautiful. A serpent's face, wider than his entire body, with eyes that glowed like liquid mercury. The mouth was closed, but he could see the outline of fangs beneath the scales, each one longer than his forearm.

The serpent looked at him.

And in that look, he saw Bai Suyin. The same intelligence. The same sadness. The same desperate hope.

*This is what I am. This is what I have always been. Can you still love me?*

The prayer beads stopped clicking.

Xu Ming turned. Fahai stood at the edge of the lake, his robes dry now, his eyes burning gold. He held a staff in his hand. Xu Ming hadn't seen him pick it up. It was black iron, carved with characters that glowed red in the darkness.

"So," Fahai said, his voice carrying across the water. "She finally showed you."

The serpent behind Xu Ming let out a sound. Not a hiss. A rumble, deep enough to feel in his bones. The water around her rippled outward.

"She is beautiful," Xu Ming said. His voice cracked. He didn't care.

Fahai's expression shifted. Something flickered in those gold eyes. Surprise. Then disgust. "You are already lost."

"I'm not lost. I'm standing exactly where I want to be."

The serpent's head lowered, coming to rest beside Xu Ming. Close enough that he could feel the heat radiating from her scales. Close enough that he could see the individual patterns, each scale a work of art, a thousand years of growth.

Fahai raised his staff. The red characters blazed like fire. "Then you will burn together."

The lake exploded.

Not figuratively. The water erupted upward in a column that reached the clouds. Xu Ming was thrown backward, tumbling across the wet grass, his head cracking against a stone bench. Stars exploded in his vision.

When he looked up, he saw what the lake had hidden.

She was no longer just a serpent. She was a mountain of white scales, coiling and uncoiling, filling the lake and rising above the treeline. Her head was as large as a car. Her body stretched across the water, across the causeway, wrapping around the pagoda on the island. The rain had stopped falling around her, evaporating into steam where her body heat touched it.

This was what she had warned him about. This was the truth beneath the skin.

And she was still growing.

Fahai stood on the water, his staff held before him, his robes untouched by the spray. He looked small against the serpent's size. But his voice carried.

"Finally," he said, and there was hunger in his voice. "Finally, you stop pretending."

The serpent opened her mouth.

White fire gathered in her throat, brighter than the sun, hot enough that Xu Ming felt his skin blister even from twenty meters away. The fire didn't burn. It was something else. Something older. Spirit fire. Soul fire. A thousand years of cultivation compressed into a single breath.

Fahai raised his staff and spoke a single word.

The light died.

The serpent's head snapped back as if struck. Her body convulsed, sending waves across the lake, cracking the stone causeway. Xu Ming heard her scream. Not a snake's hiss. A woman's scream. Bai Suyin's scream.

"NO!" He scrambled to his feet, ran toward the water, toward her.

Fahai turned. His eyes found Xu Ming. "You want to save her?"

"Yes."

"Then you must be willing to sacrifice everything."

The serpent's body went still. Her head hung limp above the water, eyes half-closed, breath coming in ragged gasps that stirred the lake into storm waves.

Fahai walked toward Xu Ming, his footsteps silent on the water. When he reached the shore, he stood face to face with the pharmacist, close enough that Xu Ming could smell the incense on his robes. Old blood. Burning temples.

"I will make you a deal," Fahai said. "Your heart for her freedom."

"What?"

"You love her. I can see it in your eyes. You would die for her." Fahai's smile was not cruel. It was worse. It was patient. "So give me your heart. Literally. Your beating heart, cut from your chest and offered to

title: The Price of a Heartbeat
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the weight of what Xu Ming had seen remained—a predator wearing a monk's robe, standing in the wreckage of his back door.

"I am not harboring anyone," Xu Ming said, stepping between Fahai and Bai Suyin. His voice cracked on the last word, but he didn't move. "This is my shop. My customer. You have no right—"

"Your customer." Fahai's smile didn't reach his eyes. He walked a slow circle around the room, prayer beads clicking, robes brushing against broken glass. "Do you know what she is, pharmacist? Have she told you the truth, or only the pretty lies they always tell?"

"She told me enough."

"Did she tell you about the village in Jiangxi? 1643. The Year of the Goat." Fahai stopped walking. His hand went to his chest, fingers pressing against something beneath his robes. "She seduced a scholar there. Made him love her. And when the trial was complete, she devoured his soul to fuel her cultivation."

"That's not true." Bai Suyin's voice was sharp, cutting through the rain. "I never—"

"You consumed him." Fahai pulled a scroll from his robes, unrolled it with a snap. The paper was ancient, yellowed, covered in characters that seemed to shift as Xu Ming looked at them. "I have records. Eighteen lifetimes. Eighteen mortal men who loved you, and eighteen souls that you took when you abandoned them."

"Lies," Bai Suyin hissed. Her eyes flickered, scales appearing at her temples. "He writes his own history, pharmacist. He burns what doesn't fit his narrative, and he carves the rest into scripture with his own knife."

Xu Ming's hands were shaking. He looked at Bai Suyin—at the woman who had stood in his shop two weeks ago, asking for thousand-year herbs with the rain still wet in her hair. He thought of her laugh by the lake, the way she'd held his hand, the desperate honesty in her voice when she'd shown him the scales beneath her skin.

Then he looked at Fahai—the gold eyes, the burning certainty, the thick file of accusations that spanned centuries.

"I need to know the truth," Xu Ming said.

"They will never give it to you." Fahai tucked the scroll away. "The snake does not know what truth is. She has worn so many faces, lived so many lies, that she has forgotten her original shape entirely. But I remember." His hand went to his chest again. "I remember every life she has taken. Every love she has poisoned. Every soul that now screams in the void because of her hunger."

"Show me."

The words came from Xu Ming's mouth before he knew he was speaking them. Fahai's eyebrows rose.

"Show me the records," Xu Ming continued. "The villages. The deaths. If you have proof, real proof, then I want to see it."

"Xu Ming—" Bai Suyin started.

"No." He turned to face her. "You came to me. You asked me to love you. You said it was the only way you could survive. But you also said you needed me to trust you, and I can't do that if I only know one side of the story."

Her face went still. The scales at her temples began to fade. "If he shows you his records, they will be forged. Twisted. Painted with the brush of a fanatic who has been hunting me for eight centuries because I refused to let him kill my teacher."

"Then you show me yours."

The silence stretched. Rain hammered against the broken door. Water pooled on the floor, mixing with the dust of shattered wood.

"There are no records," Bai Suyin said quietly. "Not written ones. My teacher taught me in the mountains, under the stars. He never wrote anything down. He said that truth should live in memory, not in ink that can be burned."

Fahai laughed. It was a dry sound, like stones grinding together. "Convenient."

"Shut up." Xu Ming's voice surprised even himself. Both of them stared at him. "Both of you. Shut up and listen."

He walked to the counter. His hands found the mortar he used for grinding herbs—the same one he'd used for years, mixing medicines for old women with arthritis and children with fevers. The stone was worn smooth from decades of use, passed down from his grandfather's teacher.

"You want me to choose," Xu Ming said. "You both want me to pick a side based on stories. On ancient grudges. On records that could be lies or memories that could be false." He gripped the mortar so hard his knuckles went white. "But you're both forgetting something."

"What?" Fahai asked.

"I'm human."

He set the mortar down. Picked up a packet of herbs from the shelf—white peony, the one Bai Suyin had asked for on her first visit. He held it up.

"You need this," he said to her. "For your cultivation. For the trial. Right?"

She nodded slowly.

"And you." He turned to Fahai. "You need me to not help her. To reject her. To send her away so you can do whatever it is you came here to do."

"To purify," Fahai said. "To end a curse that should have ended a thousand years ago."

"Right. Purify." Xu Ming tore open the packet. The herbs spilled across the counter, white petals scattering like snow. "Here's what's going to happen. I'm going to make a choice. But not tonight. Not while you're both standing in my shop with your teeth bared."

He looked at Fahai. "You want me to believe she's a monster? Fine. Prove it. Bring me names. Dates. Villages I can visit, people I can talk to. Evidence that doesn't depend on your word or your scrolls."

He looked at Bai Suyin. "You want me to love you? Fine. Show me who you are. Not what you're running from, not what you need from me. Just you. The woman who asked for peony root and knew the old names for herbs that no one uses anymore."

Neither of them spoke.

"I'm giving you both seven days," Xu Ming said. "One week. In that time, no hunting. No killing. No magic in my shop. You," he pointed at Fahai, "stay away from her. You," he pointed at Bai Suyin, "stay away from him. And I will figure out what I believe."

"You don't understand," Fahai said. "She is a predator. A snake does not change its nature because a mortal asks nicely. If I leave her for seven days, she will find another host, another heart to corrupt—"

"I said no hunting." Xu Ming's voice was iron. "If she breaks the terms, I'll know. And I'll help you myself."

Bai Suyin flinched. The movement was small, almost invisible, but Xu Ming caught it.

"Fine." Fahai's robes swirled as he turned. "Seven days. But I will be watching. And if she so much as breathes in a direction I don't like, I will return, and I will not be asking permission." He stepped through the broken door into the rain. "Remember, pharmacist. Snakes do not love. They consume. And when she has taken everything from you, you will understand why I have hunted her for so long."

He disappeared into the storm.

The shop fell silent. Water dripped from the ceiling. Bai Suyin stood motionless, her hands clasped in front of her, her face unreadable.

"You shouldn't have done that," she said.

"I know."

"He won't keep his word. He's never kept his word. He's already planning something—I can feel it, the way he's weaving his energy through the rain, setting traps—"

"Then tell me how to stop him."

She looked at him. Really looked, like she was seeing him for the first time. "You believe me."

"I don't know what I believe. But I know what I saw in his eyes. And it wasn't holiness."

A long breath escaped her. The tension in her shoulders eased, just slightly. "Seven days is not enough time. I have been cultivating for a thousand years. There are things I need to teach you, to explain to you, that take decades to understand."

"Then start now."

Xu Ming walked to the back of the shop. He pulled out a chair, set it in the middle of the floor, and sat down.

"Tell me everything. From the beginning. The real beginning, not the version you think I want to hear."

Bai Suyin stared at him. The rain continued to fall. Somewhere in the distance, a temple bell began to ring, the sound carrying through the storm like a warning.

She opened her mouth to speak.

And then the pain hit.

It came without warning—a gasp, a stagger, her hands flying to her chest. Her eyes went wide, pupils dilating, the whites filling with blood vessels that burst and spread like cracks in ice.

"Bai Suyin!"

Xu Ming was on his feet, reaching for her, but she threw out a hand to stop him.

"Don't—don't touch me—" Her voice was ragged, tearing. "He already—the incense—he must have put it in the air when he came in—"

"What? What did he do?"

She fell to her knees. Her skin was changing, scales spreading up her arms, over her face, her eyes shifting from brown to vertical slits to something else entirely—something ancient and cold and terrible.

"He's poisoning me," she whispered. "With sacred incense. It's meant to burn out my core." She looked up at him, and her eyes were full of pain, but also something else. Fear. Real fear, the first time Xu Ming had seen it in her. "Xu Ming. If I go to sleep tonight, I won't wake up."

"No. No, we can fix this. There has to be a way. What do you need? What herbs? What medicine?"

"There's no medicine for this." Her voice was fading. The scales were spreading, covering her throat, her jaw. "The only cure is impossible."

"Tell me anyway."

She looked at him. For a long moment, she said nothing.

Then: "The heart of a human who loves me. Beating. Fresh. Willing."

Xu Ming froze.

"A heart transplant," he said slowly. "You need a—"

"Not a transplant. A sacrifice." Her eyes met his. "A human must choose to die so that I can live. Must give me their heart while it still beats. That is the only thing that burns out the incense poison."

The rain hammered down. The temple bell kept ringing.

Xu Ming looked at her—at the thousand-year-old snake spirit dying on his floor—and felt the weight of what she was asking settle on his chest like a stone.

"How long?" he asked.

"Until dawn."

He checked his phone. 2:47 AM.

Three hours.

"Tell me there's another way."

"There isn't."

"Tell me you're lying."

"I'm not."

Xu Ming stood up. His legs were shaking. His hands were shaking. Everything was shaking, except for the one thought that had crystallized in the center of his mind, cold and clear as ice.

"I need to think," he said.

"You have until sunrise."

He walked to the broken door. Stood in the frame. Watched the rain fall on the empty street, on the darkened buildings, on the city that had no

title: The Blood Price of Heaven
content:
The golden glow faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary again. Brown. Human. But Xu Ming knew better now. Nothing about this man was ordinary.

"You're wrong," Xu Ming said, stepping between Fahai and Bai Suyin. "She's not a demon."
