"Then you are blind." Fahai's robes dripped water onto the broken floorboards. The rain had soaked through every layer, but he didn't shiver. Didn't blink. "A serpent who has stolen human form. Who feeds on mortal qi to maintain her illusion. Who has seduced countless men across the centuries."

"I have not," Bai Suyin said quietly.

"没有?" Fahai's laugh was soft, almost gentle. "Then explain the scholar in Suzhou. The merchant in Nanjing. The soldier at the Great Wall. All dead, all drained, all found with your scent upon their corpses."

Xu Ming felt her hand grip his arm. Her fingers were cold.

"Those were not me," she said. "You know this. You have always known."

"I know what I have seen." Fahai reached into his robe and withdrew a bronze mirror, ancient and tarnished. "And I know what this mirror shows. Every life you have taken. Every soul you have consumed. They cry out still, trapped in the reflection."

He held it up.

Xu Ming saw nothing. Just his own frightened face, Bai Suyin's pale features behind him.

But Bai Suyin recoiled as if struck.

"You keep them," she whispered. "You trap their souls instead of releasing them. For eight hundred years, you have held them prisoner."

"For eight hundred years, I have preserved evidence of your crimes."

A strange calm settled over Xu Ming. The fear was still there, buzzing beneath his skin, but something else had begun to grow beside it. Anger.

"You're lying," he said.

Fahai's eyebrows rose. "Excuse me?"

"I said you're lying." Xu Ming stepped forward, away from Bai Suyin. His hands were shaking, but his voice was steady. "I've been watching you since you walked in. The way you stand. The way you speak. You're not here to judge her. You're here to destroy her. There's a difference."

"An interesting distinction from a man who knew nothing of spirits three days ago."

"Three days is enough to learn who to trust."

Fahai's smile vanished. The temperature in the shop dropped. Frost crawled across the broken window frames, spreading like white veins across the glass.

"You trust a snake," he said.

"I trust the woman who saved my life tonight." Xu Ming held his ground. "The woman who could have killed me a hundred times but didn't. The woman who came to me for help, not harm."

"Because she needs your love to complete her trial. Did she tell you that? That once she achieves full humanity, the bond between you will be broken? That she will leave you, as she has left every other man she has used?"

"Enough."

Bai Suyin's voice cut through the air like a blade. She stepped past Xu Ming, standing between him and Fahai. Her posture had changed. The vulnerability was gone. In its place was something ancient and cold.

"If you want to speak of crimes," she said, "then speak of yours. The nun you burned alive in Hangzhou. The fox spirit you staked through the heart in Fuzhou. The child—"

"Silence." Fahai's hand moved. A golden thread shot from his sleeve, wrapping around Bai Suyin's throat before Xu Ming could blink. She choked, clawing at the glowing cord.

"Let her go!" Xu Ming grabbed Fahai's arm.

The monk didn't even look at him. A casual flick of his wrist sent Xu Ming flying across the room. He crashed into the medicine cabinets, splintering wood, bottles shattering around him. Pain exploded through his ribs.

"Your compassion is your weakness," Fahai said, tightening the golden thread. Bai Suyin's face was turning blue. "It makes you predictable. It makes you protect what you love, even when love is a weapon used against you."

"And your cruelty is yours."

Fahai paused.

Xu Ming pulled himself up, blood running from a cut on his forehead. "You think you're different from her. You think you're righteous. But you're the same. You both wear masks. Hers is flesh. Yours is faith. And neither of you has shown me the truth."

Bai Suyin's eyes met his. There was something in them he hadn't seen before. Not fear. Not desperation. Recognition.

"You want truth?" Fahai laughed. "Then see."

He released the golden thread. Bai Suyin collapsed, gasping. But before she could recover, Fahai pressed the bronze mirror against her forehead.

She screamed.

The shop dissolved.

Xu Ming was no longer in Hangzhou. He was standing in a mountain forest, ancient and dark, where the trees grew so tall they blotted out the sky. Smoke rose from a distant peak. The air smelled of burning joss paper and blood.

And there was Bai Suyin.

But not the Bai Suyin he knew. This was a woman in white robes, her hair unbound, her eyes slit-pupiled and serpentine. She stood before a burning temple, and at her feet lay bodies. Dozens of them. Monks. Warriors. Men in golden armor.

"Is this the truth you wanted?" Fahai's voice echoed from everywhere and nowhere. "This is what she was. What she still is."

Bai Suyin—the vision of her—turned. Her face was streaked with blood. Her hands were red. She looked at Xu Ming through the vision, through time itself, and her expression was not remorseful.

It was satisfied.

"No," Xu Ming whispered.

"The temple refused her entry," Fahai said. "Refused to allow a spirit to worship. So she burned it. Every monk inside. Every soldier who tried to stop her. She was already three hundred years old then. Already powerful. Already a killer."

The vision shifted.

Xu Ming saw a young man, handsome, seated at a writing desk. A scholar. Bai Suyin entered the room, smiling, carrying tea. He drank. He convulsed. He died.

"The scholar in Suzhou," Fahai said. "She was bored of him. So she poisoned him and moved on."

Another shift.

A merchant in Nanjing, wealthy, laughing with friends. Bai Suyin danced for him. The next morning, he was found in his bed, his heart stopped, his face frozen in ecstasy.

"The merchant. She drained his life force to heal a wound she received in a fight with a rival spirit."

Another shift.

A soldier at the Great Wall, strong, young. Bai Suyin appeared to him as a maiden in distress. He defended her. He died screaming, his soul ripped from his body and devoured.

"The soldier. She needed his qi to survive the winter."

Vision after vision. Death after death. Dozens. Hundreds. Xu Ming saw them all, felt them all—the terror of the dying, the cold satisfaction of the killer, the endless hunger of a creature that had been alive for centuries and would live for centuries more.

"Stop," he said.

"I am showing you the truth," Fahai said.

"You're showing me her past." Xu Ming's voice cracked. "You're not showing me who she is now."

The visions shattered.

Xu Ming was back in the shop, on his knees, his ribs screaming, his head pounding. Bai Suyin lay nearby, the bronze mirror fallen beside her. She was awake. She had seen everything he had seen.

"That was me," she said. Her voice was barely audible. "I did those things. I killed those people. I was a monster."

"Were."

She looked at him.

"Were," Xu Ming repeated. "Past tense. You said it yourself—you've been cultivating for a thousand years. You're not the same creature you were eight hundred years ago. You're not the same creature you were yesterday."

"You do not know that."

"I know you didn't kill me." He crawled toward her, each movement sending fire through his ribs. "I know you saved me from drowning. I know you came to me for help, not harm. And I know that whatever you were, whoever you were, you're trying to be something else now."

"Sentiment," Fahai said. "Weakness. She has infected you with her poison, and you cannot see clearly."

"I see perfectly clearly." Xu Ming reached Bai Suyin, took her hand. It was warm now. Human. "I see a monk who has spent eight hundred years hunting one woman. Who has killed innocents in her name. Who traps souls instead of freeing them. Tell me, Abbot—who is the real demon here?"

Fahai's face went still.

Not angry. Not offended. Still, like a pond before a storm.

"You have made your choice," he said.

"Yes."

"Then you will die with her."

Fahai raised his hand. The golden light returned to his eyes, brighter than before, blazing like twin suns. The air in the shop grew heavy, thick, hard to breathe. Xu Ming felt his heart stutter, skip, struggle to beat.

"I will tear this city apart to find every spirit hiding within it," Fahai said. "I will burn every den, every sanctuary, every pocket of darkness where they crawl. And when I am done, I will return for you both. You will watch her die. And then you will join her."

He turned. Walked through the shattered door. Disappeared into the rain.

The golden light faded.

Xu Ming collapsed.

When he woke, he was in Bai Suyin's arms. She had dragged him behind the counter, propped him against the cabinets. She was pressing a cloth to his head.

"Your ribs are cracked," she said. "You will live."

"Great." He coughed. Tasted blood. "What now?"

"He will return. He will bring others. The Shaolin have long hunted with him. They will surround the city, and they will find us."

"Then we run."

"There is nowhere to run." She looked at him, and for the first time, he saw something like defeat in her eyes. "I have run for eight hundred years. I am tired."

"Then we fight."

"With what? You are a pharmacist. I am a serpent who cannot even show her true form without destroying everything around her. We have no weapons, no allies, no—"

"I have something."

She stopped.

Xu Ming reached into his pocket. His fingers closed around something cold and metallic. He pulled it out.

It was the jade pendant he had worn since childhood. The one his mother had given him before she died. The one with the carvings he had never been able to decipher.

But now, in the dim light of the ruined shop, the carvings glowed.

And Bai Suyin's face went white.

"Where did you get that?" she whispered.

"My mother. Why?"

"The carvings. The pattern." She reached out, trembling, and touched the pendant. "This is not jade. This is a spirit-sealing talisman, carved in dragonbone. And the characters—" Her voice broke. "They are from the Heavenly Court."

Xu Ming stared at her. "What does that mean?"

"It means your mother was not human." Bai Suyin looked up at him, her eyes wide with something he had never seen before. Fear. True fear. "It means you are not fully human. It means—"

The pendant pulsed.

And Xu Ming remembered.

He remembered a woman with silver hair, standing in a garden of clouds. He remembered a voice that

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Blood in the Water

The needle slipped from Xu Ming's fingers, clattering against the ceramic tray. He stared at Bai Suyin's arm, where a thin line of red traced down her pale skin.

"You're bleeding," he said.

She looked at the wound as if surprised to find it there. "That's impossible."

"It's right there." He reached for a cotton ball, but she caught his wrist.

"No. You don't understand." Her eyes darkened. "I have not bled in eight hundred years."

The rain hammered against the shop's windows. Three days had passed since Fahai's visit. Three days since Xu Ming had lied to a monster's face, claiming Bai Suyin was nothing more than a customer who'd left before the monk arrived. Three days since he'd hidden her in his apartment above the shop, feeding her steamed buns and watching her pace like a caged animal.

And now she was bleeding.

"It's a small cut," he said, trying to sound calm. "Maybe you scratched yourself on—"

"A spirit's body does not break." She pressed her palm against the wound. When she pulled it away, the blood continued to flow. "Not unless something is wrong. Something is very wrong."

Xu Ming's pharmacy training kicked in. He grabbed sterile gauze, antiseptic, bandages. "Let me clean it at least."

"You are not listening." Her voice had an edge he'd never heard before. Not fear. Something older. Colder. "This should not be possible. My cultivation sealed my physical form centuries ago. I am beyond mortal injury."

"Clearly not."

"Then something has changed." She stared at her own blood as if it were an enemy. "Something has weakened me."

The shop bell rang downstairs.

They both froze.

"Stay here," Xu Ming whispered. "Don't make a sound."

He crept down the stairs, his heart pounding so hard he could feel it in his throat. The shop was dim, lit only by the grey light filtering through rain-streaked windows. A figure stood at the counter, umbrella dripping onto the floor.

Not Fahai. A woman.

She was young, maybe mid-twenties, with short black hair and sharp features. She wore a simple grey dress that looked expensive in an understated way. When she turned, Xu Ming saw her eyes were the colour of honey.

"Are you the pharmacist?" she asked.

"Yes. How can I help you?"

She smiled. It was a pleasant smile, completely normal. "I need herbs for my mother. She has trouble sleeping."

Xu Ming felt himself relax slightly. A customer. Just a customer. "I have some excellent valerian root. And chamomile blend—"

"I don't want those."

The pleasantness in her voice didn't change, but something in the air shifted. The temperature dropped. Xu Ming's breath misted in front of his face.

"What do you want?"

"Tell me about the snake."

His blood went cold. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"The white snake. The one who lives upstairs." The woman tilted her head, studying him like a specimen. "She's bleeding now, isn't she? For the first time in centuries."

"How do you—"

"Because I made it happen." She held up her hand. A thin scratch ran across her palm, identical to the one on Bai Suyin's arm. "Mirror wounds. A small trick, but effective."

Xu Ming's hand moved toward the phone on the counter.

"I wouldn't." The woman's voice was still pleasant. "I'm not here to hurt you. I'm here to warn you."

"Warn me about what?"

"About her." She stepped closer. Her perfume smelled like gardenias and something metallic underneath. "What has Bai Suyin told you about her trial?"

"That's none of your business."

"It's entirely my business." The woman's eyes flickered gold for just a moment. "I was her teacher's student. Before Fahai burned the mountain. Before everything."

Xu Ming's throat tightened. "You knew her teacher?"

"I was there when he died." No emotion in her voice. None at all. "I saw what the white snake did to save herself. The bargain she made."

"What bargain?"

"She didn't tell you." The woman laughed, and it was the saddest sound Xu Ming had ever heard. "Of course she didn't. She never does."

The shop's back door creaked. Bai Suyin stood in the doorway, bandaged arm held against her chest. Her face was white.

"Little Qing," she whispered. "You're alive."

The woman—Little Qing—turned slowly. "Did you think I wasn't?"

"I saw you burn. I saw—"

"You saw what I wanted you to see." Little Qing's voice cracked for the first time. "You left me, sister. You ran, and you left me to die."

"I had no choice. Fahai—"

"Fahai was a distraction. You know what I'm talking about." Little Qing's eyes blazed gold. "The blood sacrifice. The one you made to complete your cultivation. The one that cost my master his life."

Xu Ming looked between them. "What is she talking about?"

"Nothing." Bai Suyin's voice was sharp. "She's lying."

"I'm lying?" Little Qing laughed again. "Tell him, sister. Tell him what happened on Mount Emei. Tell him whose blood you drank to become human."

The silence stretched.

"I didn't have a choice," Bai Suyin finally said. "The ritual required—"

"A willing sacrifice. A master's blessing." Little Qing's voice rose. "Instead, you took what wasn't offered. You killed him while he slept, and you drank his blood like wine."

"No." Xu Ming shook his head. "That's not—she wouldn't—"

"Look at her face, pharmacist." Little Qing pointed. "Look at her eyes. She knows what I'm saying is true."

Bai Suyin's face was stone. But her hands were shaking.

"How many?" Little Qing asked softly. "How many have you killed to reach this point? Your master. The monks who guarded the sacred pool. The family who took you in during the Ming Dynasty. I've been tracking you for five hundred years, sister. I know every corpse you've left behind."

"Each of those deaths was necessary."

"Necessary." Little Qing spat the word. "That's what monsters always say."

"I am not a monster." Bai Suyin's voice rose. "I am trying to become human. Every spirit who attempts this must make sacrifices. You know the laws of cultivation as well as I do."

"I know that you are a coward." Little Qing stepped forward. "You take shortcuts. You cheat. You steal. And now you've found a mortal to hide behind while Fahai closes in."

"Leave him out of this."

"Too late. He's already in it." Little Qing turned to Xu Ming. "Do you know why she chose you? Not for love. Not for destiny. Because you're weak. Because you're forgettable. Because when she finishes her trial and becomes human, she will discard you like she discards everything else."

"That's enough." Bai Suyin's voice was ice. "Leave. Now."

"Or what? You'll kill me?" Little Qing smiled. "You've tried before. Remember? The night you pushed me off the Thousand Buddha Cliff. I survived, sister. I always survive."

Xu Ming's world was spinning. Everything he thought he knew was cracking. The woman he'd hidden, protected, begun to care for—she was a killer. A murderer who had drunk her teacher's blood to gain power.

"Is it true?" he asked. His voice sounded distant to his own ears.

Bai Suyin looked at him. For a moment, her mask slipped, and he saw something raw underneath. Guilt. Shame. Fear.

"Yes," she said quietly. "But there's more to the story."

"I've heard enough." Little Qing pulled something from her pocket. A jade pendant, carved in the shape of a coiled snake. "Fahai will be here within the hour. I've already sent word to his temple. He knows exactly where you are."

"You betrayed me." Bai Suyin's voice was barely a whisper.

"I didn't betray you. I'm finally doing what should have been done centuries ago." Little Qing walked toward the door. "I'm ending this."

She paused at the threshold. "One more thing, pharmacist. She's not just using you for protection. She's using you for your blood."

Xu Ming's blood ran cold. "What?"

"The heart trial. It requires a mortal connection, yes. But the final stage requires something more." Little Qing's smile was cruel. "A willing sacrifice. Someone whose love for her is strong enough that they would give their life to complete her transformation."

"That's not—" Bai Suyin started.

"She's already planning it, Xu Ming. The night she becomes fully human, she will drain you dry." Little Qing stepped into the rain. "And you will thank her as you die."

The door closed.

The shop was silent except for the rain.

Xu Ming couldn't move. Couldn't breathe. He stared at Bai Suyin, this woman he had trusted, hidden, protected. This creature who had killed for power and would kill again.

"Is it true?" he asked for the second time.

Slowly, she nodded.

"Every word."

The rain stopped.

The silence that followed was worse than any confession.

"There's something else," Bai Suyin said. Her voice was hollow. "Something I haven't told you."

"I don't think I want to hear it."

"You need to." She took a step toward him. He stepped back. The pain in her eyes was real, but so was the blood on her hands. "Fahai isn't just hunting me because I'm a spirit."

"Then why?"

"Because I'm his daughter."

The words hung in the air like poison.

"Eight hundred years ago," she continued, "before he became a monk, before he took his vows, Fahai was a mortal man. And he loved a snake spirit. She bore him a daughter—me."

Xu Ming's legs gave out. He sat down hard on the floor.

"I was raised in both worlds. Human and spirit. But when my mother was killed by cultivators, Fahai blamed me. He said my existence was a sin, a corruption of his sacred path. He took his vows and swore to destroy every spirit who tried to become human—starting with me."

"That's why he's so determined," Xu Ming whispered. "That's why he never stops."

"Because I am his greatest failure. His proof that mortals and spirits should never mix." Her eyes were wet. "And now, you are proof of the same thing."

The shop door burst open.

Fahai stood in the entrance, golden eyes blazing. Behind him, Little Qing watched with cold satisfaction.

"白素贞," Fahai said. "It ends tonight."

Prayer beads clicked in his hand. Each bead was carved from bone—Xu Ming realized with horror that they looked like human finger bones.

"Step aside, pharmacist. Your part in this is over."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. She was trembling, her face white, her bandaged arm bleeding through.

He should step aside. He knew he should.

But he didn't.

"She's under my protection," he said.

Fahai's smile was terrible. "Then you will die with her."

The prayer beads began to glow.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The thunder came three times before the rain.

Xu Ming stood at the window of his second-floor apartment, watching the storm gather over Hangzhou's western hills. The sky had turned the color of old bruises, and somewhere in that darkness, he knew Fahai was watching. The monk had not attacked since that night in the shop. Three weeks of silence. Three weeks of waiting.

Three weeks of Bai Suyin sleeping on his couch.

"You're thinking too loud."

Her voice came from behind him, soft and amused. He turned to find her sitting cross-legged on the floor, a cup of tea cooling beside her. She had stopped wearing the expensive dresses. Now she wore his old university hoodie and sweatpants, her hair tied in a messy knot. She looked almost human.

Almost.

"I'm thinking about the storm," he said.

"You're thinking about him."

Xu Ming didn't deny it. Since the night Fahai had shattered his door, since Bai Suyin had pulled him through the back exit and into the alleys of the old city, they had been running. Not far—just across town, to this small apartment that belonged to a cousin who was traveling in Europe. But far enough that Xu Ming had lost count of the sleepless nights.

"I went back to the shop yesterday," he said.

Bai Suyin's eyes snapped to his face. "You what?"

"Just to look. From across the street." He sat down heavily on the bed, the springs groaning under his weight. "The door was boarded up. Someone had spray-painted the word 'witch' on the wall."

"Fahai's work. He wants to isolate you. Make you afraid to help me."

"It's working."

She was quiet for a long moment. Then she stood, crossed the room, and sat beside him on the bed. Her shoulder pressed against his. Her warmth seeped through the thin fabric of his shirt.

"You can still leave," she said. "I can erase your memory. You'll wake up tomorrow with no knowledge of me. No danger. No—"

"Don't." The word came out harder than he intended. "Don't offer me that again."

"Why not? It's the smart choice."

"Because I already made my choice." He turned to face her. Her eyes were brown today—human brown, warm and deep and full of something he was afraid to name. "I chose when I didn't run that first night. I chose when I let you stay. And I'm choosing now."

"You barely know me."

"I know enough."

Her lips parted. For a moment, she looked like she might argue. Then she did something she had never done before—she reached up and touched his face. Her fingers traced the line of his jaw, feather-light.

"You have no idea what you're promising," she whispered.

"Then show me."

"Show you what?"

"Everything." He caught her hand, held it against his cheek. "No more pieces. No more fractions. I want to see what you really are."

The air in the room changed. The temperature dropped. Bai Suyin's eyes flickered—brown to gold, gold to brown, like a light bulb short-circuiting.

"That is not a small request," she said.

"I know."

"If I show you my true form, even in part, it will leave a mark on you. You will never be fully human again. You will see things that mortals are not meant to see. You will smell the spirit world in every crowd, hear its whispers in every silence."

"I know."

"And Fahai will smell it on you. He will know that you've been touched by the serpent. He will have no mercy."

Xu Ming took a breath. Then another. His heart was hammering, but his voice came out steady.

"I don't want mercy. I want the truth."

Bai Suyin looked at him for a long, searching moment. Then she smiled—not her practiced human smile, but something older, stranger, full of sadness and sharp edges.

"Close your eyes."

He obeyed.

The first thing he felt was heat. Not fire, but the heat of a body too large to exist, pressing against the boundaries of the room. The second thing was scent—jasmine and rain and something metallic, like blood after rain.

"Open your eyes."

He opened them.

She was still Bai Suyin. Still his face, his hoodie, his sweatpants. But she was also something else. Something that existed in layers, like smoke trapped in glass. Behind her, visible only in the corner of his vision, coiled a shape that made his primitive brain scream *predator*. Scales the size of dinner plates. A body that twisted through dimensions, folding space around itself. Eyes—dozens of them, scattered along the length of that impossible form—all fixed on him with ancient, patient intelligence.

"You see," she said, and her voice came from everywhere, from the walls and the floor and the storm outside. "This is what a thousand years looks like."

Xu Ming couldn't speak. His throat had closed. His hands were shaking.

But he did not look away.

"Most humans who see this," she continued, "go mad. Their minds cannot reconcile the scale. They spend the rest of their lives in asylums, drawing pictures of snakes."

"I'm still here."

"Yes." Something shifted in the air—approval, perhaps. Or curiosity. "You are."

The vision flickered. For a moment, he saw her true face: not beautiful, not human, but ancient beyond measure, her features carved from jade and moonlight. Then the image collapsed, folding back into the woman he knew.

Bai Suyin swayed. He caught her before she could fall.

"That took more out of me than I expected," she murmured, leaning against his chest. "I have not shown myself to a mortal in three hundred years."

"Why show me now?"

She looked up at him. Her eyes were exhausted, ringed with shadows that hadn't been there moments ago.

"Because I am tired of being afraid," she said. "And because I think I might—"

The window exploded.

Glass sprayed across the room. Xu Ming threw himself over Bai Suyin, shielding her with his body. When he looked up, a figure stood silhouetted against the lightning-torn sky.

Not Fahai.

A woman.

She was tall, dressed in black robes that seemed to drink the light. Her hair was white, cut short and sharp, and her eyes were the color of dead embers. In her hand, she held a chain made of human finger bones.

"A-Yin," she said, her voice like grinding stones. "You've been hiding."

Bai Suyin went rigid beneath Xu Ming. He had never seen fear on her face before. He saw it now.

"Mei," she whispered. "You're supposed to be dead."

"Disappointed?" The woman—Mei—stepped through the broken window. The bone chain rattled as she walked. "Fahai locked me in a golden cage for seventy years. But cages rust. And I have been looking for you ever since."

"You were my sister. My cultivation partner. We swore—"

"We swore to transcend together." Mei's voice cracked like ice. "And then you fell in love with a mortal. You abandoned our path. You left me to face him alone."

"That was eight hundred years ago."

"Time means nothing to a snake." Mei smiled, and her teeth were filed to points. "I have waited. I have hunted. And now I have found you."

Xu Ming stepped forward, putting himself between them. "Get out of my apartment."

Mei's dead-ember eyes slid to him. She tilted her head, curious.

"A mortal. And he knows what you are." She laughed—a dry, rustling sound. "You always did have terrible taste, A-Yin."

"Leave him out of this."

"No." Mei raised the bone chain. The air grew thick, heavy, hard to breathe. "I want him to watch. I want him to see what happens to snakes who break their oaths."

She spoke a word that Xu Ming's ears could not process. The chain unwound, each bone glowing with sickly green light. The temperature dropped below freezing. Frost crawled across the walls.

Bai Suyin pushed past him, her form flickering between human and serpent. "Run," she said. "Now. I'll hold her off."

"I'm not leaving you."

"You don't understand. She's not like Fahai. She's not a hunter. She's a spirit-eater. She will consume everything I am and leave nothing behind."

"Then we fight together."

Bai Suyin looked at him. For a moment, her mask cracked, and he saw the creature beneath—not the monster, but the woman. The one who had been alone for a thousand years.

"Stay behind me," she said. "And whatever happens, do not let her touch your eyes."

Mei laughed again, the sound swallowed by the storm.

"Touch his eyes?" She lunged. "I'm going to eat his heart while it's still beating."

The bone chain whipped through the air. Bai Suyin met it with a wall of white light. The impact shook the building. Plaster rained from the ceiling.

And somewhere in the distance, a bell began to toll.

Mei froze.

Bai Suyin's face went pale.

"Thunder Bell," Mei breathed. "He's coming."

Fahai.

Xu Ming grabbed Bai Suyin's hand. "Back door. Now."

They ran.

Behind them, Mei's laughter followed, high and mad and full of promise.

"I'll find you again, A-Yin. And next time, I won't be alone."

The bell tolled again. Closer.

And the rain began to fall in earnest, washing the blood from the broken glass, erasing all traces of what had happened in the small apartment above the city.

But the traces remained—in Xu Ming's eyes, which now saw the spirit world lurking behind every shadow. In Bai Suyin's heart, which had cracked open despite every wall she had built.

And in the darkness beneath Hangzhou's streets, where something ancient and patient had finally awakened.

The storm had only just begun.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: THE PRICE OF SCALES

The fire burned for three days.

Xu Ming stood at the window of his ruined shop, watching smoke curl from the embers that had once been his life's work. Shelves reduced to ash. Herbs scattered like blackened bones. The mortar he had inherited from his grandfather—cracked in half, its thousand-year-old patina destroyed.

"The insurance won't cover this," he said, his voice hollow. "Arson is excluded. Act of God. Something."

Behind him, Bai Suyin sat cross-legged on the floor, her eyes closed, her breathing slow. She had not moved since Fahai vanished into the flames. Since she had thrown herself between Xu Ming and the monk's golden staff, taken a blow meant to shatter his skull, and then—somehow—extinguished the fire with a single, whispered word.

"Bai Suyin."

She opened her eyes. They were not brown. They were pale gold, with vertical pupils that contracted in the morning light.

"I heard you," she said.

"Then answer me. What was that? What did he mean when he said I was already marked?"

She stood slowly, her movements deliberate, as if testing each joint. The wound on her shoulder had closed, but the skin around it was discolored—a patch of white scales that refused to fade.

"The Heart Trial is not a choice," she said. "It is a binding. When I selected you as my trial, my essence touched yours. Fahai can see that connection. He can track you now as easily as he tracks me."

"So I'm a target."

"More than a target." She walked to him, placed her palm against his chest. "You are bait."

Xu Ming pushed her hand away. "I need the truth. All of it. No more poetic half-answers about cultivation and destiny. Why do you need me to love you? What actually happens when you complete the trial?"

She was silent for a long moment. Then she walked to the broken window, her reflection ghosting across the shattered glass.

"Every spirit that seeks humanity must face the Heart Trial," she said. "It is the final gate. To pass through it, a spirit must experience love—true, mortal love, with all its capacity for betrayal and loss. But the trial does not end with love. It ends with sacrifice."

"What kind of sacrifice?"

"I must give up my cultivation. A thousand years of power. Every scale, every fang, every memory of what I was. I become human completely, with nothing left of the serpent."

"That doesn't sound like a sacrifice. That sounds like what you want."

"The sacrifice is not mine." She turned to face him. "It is yours."

Xu Ming's blood went cold.

"When the trial completes, the power I shed does not vanish. It transfers. To the one who loved me. You would receive a thousand years of cultivation in a single moment—enough to kill an ordinary man. Your body would burn from the inside out. Your soul would be consumed."

"So if I love you—"

"I kill you."

The words hung between them like smoke.

"Then why," Xu Ming said, his voice barely a whisper, "would you ask me to love you?"

"Because there is another way." She stepped closer. "If you refuse the power. If you reject the cultivation. It returns to me, incomplete, and I remain a spirit. But alive. And so do you."

"Then why didn't you just tell me this from the beginning?"

"Because Fahai is listening." She touched her ear. "He has ways of hearing what should not be heard. If he knew there was a way to stop the trial without destroying me, he would have already used it. He would have taken you, broken your will, forced you to reject me."

"Broken my will?"

"Fahai does not hunt with fire and staff alone. He hunts with fear. With doubt. With every shadow you have ever carried." She looked at him with something like pity. "He will come for you again. And when he does, he will not try to kill you. He will try to make you hate me."

Xu Ming sat down on the charred floor, his legs giving out. His shop was destroyed. His life was ashes. And the woman he was falling for had just told him that loving her would either kill him or damn her forever.

"What do we do?"

"We run."

"For how long?"

"Until the trial completes. Until I become human or we both die trying." She knelt beside him, took his hands. "Xu Ming. I have lived a thousand years. I have seen dynasties rise and fall. I have watched mountains wear down to hills. But I have never—never—met anyone who made me want to give it all up."

"You barely know me."

"I know your hands." She lifted them, pressed them to her cheek. "I know they are gentle. I know they have never killed. I know they mix medicine for strangers and expect nothing in return. In a thousand years, I have learned that gentleness is the rarest thing in the world."

Xu Ming looked at his hands. They were shaking. They had been shaking since the fire.

"Where do we run?"

"South. To the mountains where I was born. There is a cave there that Fahai cannot enter. It is warded with old magic—older than him, older than his temple."

"And then what?"

"And then we wait. We survive. We let the trial run its course."

A sound came from outside. Soft. Rhythmic.

_Pat. Pat. Pat._

Footsteps. Walking through the wet ash.

Xu Ming scrambled to his feet. Bai Suyin's eyes went gold again, her pupils slitting to vertical lines.

"He's back," she whispered.

The footsteps stopped at the doorway. A figure stood silhouetted against the gray morning sky—but it was not Fahai.

It was a woman.

She was tall, with hair the color of moonlight and skin so pale it seemed to glow. She wore a white dress that had no business being clean in a city of smoke and rain. And her eyes were the same shade of gold as Bai Suyin's.

"Sister," the woman said, her voice like wind through bamboo. "You have been hiding."

Bai Suyin went rigid. "Xiao Qing."

"Who is this?" Xu Ming asked.

"My junior." Bai Suyin's voice was ice. "The green snake I trained for three hundred years. The one I thought was dead."

Xiao Qing smiled. It was a beautiful smile, full of teeth. "Dead? No. I simply found a more worthy teacher."

She stepped aside, and Abbot Fahai walked through the doorway behind her. His robes were untouched by ash. His golden eyes were fixed on Bai Suyin.

"Your apprentice betrayed you," Fahai said. "She came to me three centuries ago, begging for protection. Begging to be saved from your shadow."

"You lie," Bai Suyin hissed.

"Does she?" Xiao Qing tilted her head. "You were always so focused on your own cultivation. On your own trial. You never saw that I wanted more than to be your reflection."

"You wanted to be human."

"I wanted to be _you_." Xiao Qing's voice cracked, just slightly. "Everything I did, every technique I mastered, every prayer I offered—it was never enough. You were always the white snake. The chosen one. The one the heavens smiled upon."

"So you joined my enemy."

"I joined the winning side." Xiao Qing turned to Fahai. "Master. The trial is incomplete. The pharmacist still lives. If we sever their bond now, she will be trapped in her serpent form forever."

Fahai nodded. "Then we sever it."

He raised his hand. Prayer beads glowed with golden light.

Xu Ming grabbed Bai Suyin's arm. "Can you fight them both?"

"No."

"Can you run?"

"Not fast enough."

"Then what do we do?"

Bai Suyin looked at him. For the first time since he had met her, she looked afraid.

"There is one thing," she said. "But it will cost us everything."

She pressed her forehead to his. Her skin was cold. Her breath was warm.

"Xu Ming. Do you trust me?"

He thought about his shattered shop. His burned herbs. His small, safe life that was now ashes on the wind.

"Yes."

"Then close your eyes."

He did.

And the world turned to fire.

---

He felt his body dissolve. Not die—_dissolve_, like salt in water. His bones became light. His blood became wind. His consciousness scattered across a thousand points of awareness.

He was in the shop. He was on the mountain. He was in a cave that smelled of damp earth and ancient bones.

And he was inside Bai Suyin.

Her memories flooded him. A hatchling emerging from an egg. A snake learning to breathe the air. A woman watching the first Qin emperor ride past on a warhorse, her heart full of wonder and hunger and terrible, beautiful loneliness.

_This is what I am,_ she whispered in his mind. _This is what I have always been._

He saw her kill. He saw her weep. He saw her kneel before a thousand altars, begging heaven to let her be human, to let her love, to let her die like everything else in the world.

And then he saw her meet him. Saw herself through her own eyes—a small man in a dusty pharmacy, his hands gentle, his heart full of quiet courage.

_You are my last chance,_ she said. _My only chance._

Xu Ming opened his eyes.

He was no longer in the shop.

He was standing on a mountain peak, clouds swirling at his feet. Bai Suyin stood beside him, whole and unharmed. Below them, miles away, a tiny dot of smoke marked where Hangzhou had been.

"What did you do?"

"Something I should not have." She looked at him with new eyes—no longer gold, but the brown he had first seen in his shop. "I merged our souls. Just for a moment. Long enough to escape."

"Is that permanent?"

"No. But it left a mark." She touched her chest. "You are part of me now. And I am part of you."

"Is that bad?"

"I don't know." She smiled, and it was the saddest smile he had ever seen. "In a thousand years, I have never done this before. I have never let anyone this close."

"That's not an answer."

"I know."

She turned to look at the mountains stretching before them. Somewhere in those peaks was the cave she had spoken of. Somewhere was safety.

But somewhere, too, was Fahai. And Xiao Qing. And the impossible choice that waited for them both.

"We should keep moving," she said.

Xu Ming nodded.

But as he followed her down the mountain path, he could still feel her inside him—a warmth beneath his skin, a second heartbeat pulsing alongside his own.

The trial had changed.

And he had the terrible feeling that it was only beginning.

title: The Monastery of Mirrors
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the threat remained, coiled in his posture like a snake ready to strike.

"You have three seconds to step aside," he said to Xu Ming.

Bai Suyin moved before Xu Ming could respond. She placed herself between them, her wet dress clinging to her body, water pooling at her feet. "He has nothing to do with this."

"He has everything to do with this." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he stepped further into the shop. "You have chosen him as your heart trial. That makes him a participant in your sin."

"Sin?" Bai Suyin's voice cracked like ice. "I have cultivated for a thousand years. I have saved lives. I have healed the sick. What sin have I committed except wanting to be human?"

"The sin of presumption." Fahai reached into his robe and withdrew a small bronze mirror. "You forget, serpent, that I have seen your true nature. I have watched you shed your skin and crawl through the mud of this world. You are no more human than the worms beneath the earth."

Xu Ming's hands found a mortar and pestle on the counter. It was useless against a man who could explode doors with his presence, but it was something. "Get out of my shop."

Fahai laughed. It was a dry sound, like leaves scraping concrete. "Brave words from a mortal who doesn't understand what he's protecting."

"I understand enough."

"Do you?" Fahai turned the mirror toward Xu Ming. The bronze surface rippled like water. "Look into the mirror, pharmacist. See what your beloved truly is."

Xu Ming's eyes were drawn to the reflection despite himself. At first, he saw only himself—tired, frightened, clutching a stone pestle like a weapon. Then the image shifted. Behind him, Bai Suyin's reflection changed. Her skin became scales. Her eyes became slits of gold. Her mouth opened and fangs descended, dripping venom that burned through the floorboards.

"Stop it," Xu Ming said, but he couldn't look away.

"Show him the truth," Fahai intoned. "Show him what lies beneath the skin you kiss."

The reflection twisted further. Bai Suyin's human form collapsed entirely, replaced by a massive white serpent that filled the shop. Its coils crushed shelves. Its scales scraped the ceiling. Its head lowered to Xu Ming's level, and it spoke with Bai Suyin's voice: "I am not this. He is lying."

But the mirror showed what it showed.

Xu Ming wrenched his gaze away. His hands were shaking. The pestle clattered to the floor. "That's not her."

"It is her," Fahai said softly. "That is her true form. The shape she wore when she devoured my predecessor eight hundred years ago. The shape she will wear again when she tires of playing human."

"I never—" Bai Suyin started.

"You devoured him." Fahai's voice rose. "I found his bones in your den. I found his robes woven into your nest. You consumed him and grew stronger, and now you pretend to be a creature of love?"

Bai Suyin's face crumpled. "That's not what happened. He attacked me. I was defending myself. I didn't mean to—"

"Mean to?" Fahai's laugh was sharper now. "Intent does not matter. You are a predator. You kill because that is what snakes do. You love because that is what snakes do to their prey before they swallow it whole."

Xu Ming's mind raced. He thought of the herbs Bai Suyin had requested—the exact combination that could paralyze a large creature. He thought of the way she'd touched him, soft and desperate. He thought of the transformation she'd shown him in the shop, the scales and claws that had emerged from her human skin.

Was Fahai right? Was he just prey that didn't know it yet?

"No." The word came from Bai Suyin, not to Fahai, but to Xu Ming. "Don't listen to him. He twists everything. He makes monsters out of those who only want peace."

"Peace?" Fahai raised the mirror higher. "Then why did you come to Hangzhou? Why did you seek out this particular pharmacist? Why him and not any other mortal in this city of millions?"

Bai Suyin's silence was answer enough.

"Tell him," Fahai said. "Tell him why you chose him specifically. Or shall I?"

"Don't."

"His bloodline." Fahai's smile was terrible. "The Xu family has a rare property. Their blood contains a substance that accelerates cultivation. It's why his grandmother lived to ninety-seven without a single illness. It's why his father survived a car crash that should have killed him instantly. And it's why you, serpent, chose him for your heart trial."

Xu Ming felt the floor tilt beneath him. "What is he talking about?"

Bai Suyin wouldn't meet his eyes. "It's not... it's not like that."

"Then what is it like?" Xu Ming's voice cracked. "Tell me. Right now. Why me?"

She opened her mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. "Because I smelled you. Three months before I came to your shop. I was across the city, and I smelled your blood on the wind, and I knew... I knew you were special."

"Special how?"

"Your blood accelerates cultivation. If I complete the heart trial with you, I don't just become human. I become something more. Something that can fight Fahai. Something that can survive."

Xu Ming stepped back. His hip hit the counter. "You chose me because I'm useful."

"I chose you because I love you."

"You don't even know me. We've known each other for weeks."

"I know you better than I've ever known anyone." Tears streamed down her face. "I've lived a thousand years, Xu Ming. I've watched empires rise and fall. I've seen the ocean swallow cities and the mountains spit fire. And in all that time, I have never once felt what I feel when I'm with you. Yes, your blood matters. Yes, I knew about your family. But that's not why I stayed. That's not why I showed you what I am."

Fahai clapped slowly. "Beautiful. A performance worthy of the stage. But we both know the truth."

The mirror in his hand flared with light. The beam struck Bai Suyin, and she screamed. Her body convulsed. Scales erupted across her skin. Her eyes turned golden, and her fangs elongated.

"Fahai, stop!" Xu Ming grabbed the monk's arm.

Fahai threw him across the room. Xu Ming crashed into a shelf of dried herbs. Glass shattered. Pain exploded through his ribs. When he looked up, Bai Suyin was on the floor, writhing, her human form flickering like a candle in the wind.

"This is what she is," Fahai said, walking toward her. "This is what she has always been. A beast wearing a woman's face. A monster pretending to love."

"Please." Bai Suyin's voice came out half-human, half-serpent. "Xu Ming. Please don't let him take me."

Xu Ming tried to stand. His ribs screamed. His vision swam. But he forced himself up, grabbed the pestle from the floor, and charged.

Fahai turned. Caught the pestle mid-swing. Crushed it to powder with one hand.

"Brave," he said. "Stupid, but brave."

He backhanded Xu Ming across the face. Xu Ming hit the ground and didn't get up.

When he opened his eyes, the shop was empty. The door hung open. Rain poured in. Bai Suyin was gone. Fahai was gone. Only the bronze mirror remained, lying on the floor where the monk had dropped it.

Xu Ming crawled to the mirror. His hands were bloody. His vision was blurred. But he saw something in the reflection that made his blood run cold.

The mirror didn't show the shop.

It showed a monastery. Ancient. Stone. Surrounded by mountains. And in the center of the courtyard, chained to a pillar, was Bai Suyin. Her human form was intact, but her eyes were closed, and her chest barely moved.

Around her, monks chanted. Their voices rose in a rhythm that made the air itself tremble. And at the head of the circle stood Fahai, holding a golden staff that hummed with power.

The reflection shifted. Xu Ming saw writing appear on the mirror's surface. Ancient characters, burning like fire:

*The Monastery of Mirrors. Three days' journey west of Hangzhou. Come if you wish to save her. Come if you wish to die.*

The writing faded. The mirror went dark.

Xu Ming clutched it to his chest. His ribs burned. His face was swollen. He had no weapons, no training, no idea how to fight a monk who could crush stone with his bare hands.

But Bai Suyin was out there. Chained. Terrified. Waiting for him.

He pulled out his phone. Dialed the only number he could think of.

"Grandma?" His voice broke. "I need your help."

The line was silent for a long moment. Then his grandmother's voice came through, old and sharp and knowing:

"Fahai found her, didn't he?"

"Yes."

"Then you need to know the truth, Ming. About your blood. About the things your grandfather never told me. About why our family has been hunted for three hundred years."

Xu Ming's hand tightened on the phone. "Tell me."

"Come home. Bring the mirror. And pray that we're not too late."

The call ended.

Xu Ming stood in the ruined shop, rain soaking through his clothes, the bronze mirror cold against his chest. Outside, the storm raged. But inside, something was changing. Something that had been sleeping in his blood for three centuries.

He could feel it now.

A heat. A hunger. A power that had nothing to do with herbs or medicine.

Fahai wanted a monster?

Xu Ming smiled, and it was not a pleasant smile.

He would give Fahai one.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Price of Scales

The sword in Fahai's hand was not metal. Xu Ming realized this as the blade caught the shop's dim light and *absorbed* it, swallowing illumination like a black hole made solid. The weapon looked carved from dried blood and compressed shadow, and it hummed with a frequency that made his teeth ache.

"Dharma weapon," Bai Suyin whispered. "He forged it from the bones of my kind."

"Not just bones." Fahai stepped forward, and the shattered door frame groaned. "I used the spine of your teacher. The one who taught you to deceive mortals. She screamed for three days before I harvested her essence."

Bai Suyin's composure cracked. Something ancient and furious surfaced in her eyes — not human anger, but the cold rage of a predator being taunted. "You lie. I would have felt her death."

"You felt nothing because I trapped her soul in a bell." Fahai reached into his robes and produced a small bronze handbell, no larger than his thumb. He shook it once. The sound that emerged was not a ring but a *scream* — a woman's voice, distorted by a thousand echoes, crying out in agony. "She has been my companion for four hundred years. Every time I ring this bell, she remembers how she failed to protect you."

Xu Ming saw Bai Suyin's hands curl into fists. Her nails lengthened, turning black and sharp. The temperature in the shop dropped. Frost crept across the floorboards, and his breath misted.

"Don't," he said, stepping between them. "Not here. There are people in the apartments above. There's a noodle shop next door. You said you wanted to protect them."

She looked at him. For a moment, he saw the calculation in her eyes — the thousand-year-old mind weighing collateral damage against survival. Then the frost stopped spreading. Her nails retracted.

"Clever mortal," Fahai said, almost admiring. "You've learned to manage her. Perhaps you've earned your death as something other than a warning."

"Nobody's dying tonight." Xu Ming's voice was steadier than he felt. "This is my shop. My home. You want to fight her, you do it somewhere else."

Fahai laughed. It was a pleasant sound, warm and genuine, which made it infinitely more terrifying. "You think you have authority here? I have executed spirits in thirty provinces across nine centuries. I have burned temples, flooded caves, and strangled serpents in their nests. I answer to no mortal law."

"Then answer to mortal witnesses." Xu Ming pulled out his phone. "I've been recording since you broke the door. It's uploading to cloud storage. There are three backup copies. You kill me, this goes to every news station in Hangzhou."

Fahai's smile faded. "You would threaten me with technology?"

"I would threaten you with *attention*. You operate in shadows. You hunt in darkness. The moment you become a story on social media — the crazy monk with the sword — your entire system breaks down. How many spirits can you kill when every security camera in the city is watching you?"

Silence. The rain hammered the roof. Water dripped through the broken door, forming a puddle that crept toward Xu Ming's shoes.

"You are more dangerous than I anticipated," Fahai said quietly. "The serpent chose well."

Bai Suyin stepped forward, placing herself beside Xu Ming. "He's not a weapon. He's not a shield. He's a man I chose."

"Chose, or manipulated?" Fahai's golden eyes narrowed. "Tell me, pharmacist — did she ever mention the nature of her trial? What happens to you when it completes?"

Xu Ming felt a cold knot form in his stomach. "What do you mean?"

"She can't become human without passing the heart trial. That requires genuine love from a mortal. But do you know what happens to the mortal once the trial succeeds?"

Bai Suyin's face went rigid. "Stop."

"The mortal's soul is consumed." Fahai's voice was clinical, detached. "Their essence becomes fuel for her transformation. Everything you are — your memories, your personality, your very existence — will be absorbed into her cultivation. You won't die, exactly. You'll simply *stop being you*. She'll carry fragments of you forever, but you'll be gone."

"That's not true," Xu Ming said. But his voice cracked on the last word.

"Ask her. Look at her face and ask her if I'm lying."

He turned. Bai Suyin's eyes were wet. Not with tears — with something thicker, more viscous. Serpent blood. "It was supposed to be different," she said. "I found a way to complete the trial without consuming you. There's an alternative method, an older ritual—"

"There is no alternative." Fahai's sword lowered slightly. "You've been lying to yourself for a thousand years, serpent. Every time you've attempted the trial, every mortal you've seduced — you told yourself this time would be different. But it never is. You consumed them all. Every last one."

Xu Ming's world tilted. "Every time? How many times have you tried this?"

Bai Suyin's silence was answer enough.

"Seven," Fahai said. "Seven attempts over eight centuries. Seven mortals who loved her completely, and seven souls that now exist only as echoes inside her cultivation core. She carries them like parasites. Their love made her stronger. Their lives made her more human. And their deaths made her a monster."

"I am not a monster." Bai Suyin's voice was barely human now — layered, harmonic, filled with the resonance of something that had been speaking longer than Mandarin had existed. "I am a woman trying to become what I love. And I have paid for every life I've taken with my own suffering."

"Suffering doesn't bring back the dead."

"No." She turned to Xu Ming. "But I can show you the truth. All of it. Every memory I've carried. Every face I've failed. If you want to know what I am, really know, I'll open myself completely and let you see."

Fahai raised his sword. "Don't trust her. She's buying time. While she speaks, she's weaving poison into the air. Look at your hands."

Xu Ming looked down. His fingers were turning blue. Not cold — *blue*, like ink spreading under his skin.

"What—"

"A paralysis toxin," Bai Suyin said. "Odorless. Colorless in the air. It takes effect within minutes of exposure. It won't kill you, but it will keep you still while I deal with him."

"You poisoned me?"

"I protected you. If he kills me, the antidote dies with me. He can't harm you without losing his leverage."

Fahai laughed again. "Even now, she manipulates. She's turned your own body into a hostage, pharmacist. And you still want to defend her?"

Xu Ming's legs gave out. He hit the floor hard, his knees cracking against the wood. The blue had spread to his wrists now, creeping toward his elbows. He couldn't feel his fingers anymore.

Bai Suyin crouched beside him. Her hand cupped his cheek, and her touch was warm — too warm, feverish. "I'm sorry," she said. "I meant to tell you. I meant to find another way. But he found me first, and now I have to choose between your life and your freedom."

"You're going to kill me anyway." His voice was slurred. "That's what Fahai said. The trial consumes me."

"The trial *can* consume you. But it doesn't have to. If I complete it without taking your soul — if I force the transformation using only my own cultivation — I'll lose half my power. I'll be weaker than I've been in five hundred years. He'll kill me within a month."

"Then don't complete it."

"I have to. If I don't, the trial fails permanently. I'll revert to my true form and never be able to take human shape again. I'll become a beast in truth." Her eyes were desperate. "Do you understand? This is my last chance. I have been trying to become human for a thousand years. If I fail now, I will spend eternity as a snake, watching humans live and love and die, never able to join them."

Xu Ming tried to speak, but his tongue was numb. The blue had reached his chest. He could feel his heart slowing, each beat a labor.

"You don't have to decide now," Bai Suyin said. "I'll take you somewhere safe. I'll keep you alive until you can choose freely."

"Choose freely," Fahai repeated. "While bound by poison. While your life depends on her whim. That's not freedom, serpent. That's a cage with prettier bars."

He raised his sword. The blade began to glow — not with light, but with *absence*, a darkness so complete it hurt to look at.

"Last chance, 白素贞. Surrender your cultivation core, and I'll let the mortal live. Refuse, and I'll cut through both of you."

Bai Suyin stood. She turned to face him fully, and Xu Ming saw something change in her posture. The fear was gone. The desperation was gone. What remained was ancient and cold and absolutely certain.

"No," she said. "I have run from you for eight hundred years. I have hidden in every shadow, worn every mask, abandoned every home. I am done running."

She raised her hand. The shop's lights flickered. The rain outside stopped — not gradually, but instantly, as if the sky itself held its breath.

"Tonight, I will complete my trial. Tonight, I will become human. And you"—her eyes flashed white, serpentine—"will watch."

Fahai charged.

The sword swung.

And Xu Ming, paralyzed on the floor of his ruined shop, saw Bai Suyin's body explode into light.

Not scales. Not a serpent. Something else entirely — something that had been waiting inside her for a thousand years, hidden beneath the human face she wore. It emerged as a column of white fire, and in its center, Xu Ming saw shapes moving. Faces. Seven of them, each turning to look at him with eyes that were not quite alive.

The souls of her previous loves.

And one of them — a young man with Xu Ming's own face — reached out and touched his chest.

The blue receded.

The poison retreated.

And Xu Ming understood, in that single moment of contact, that he was not the first Xu Ming. He was not even the first pharmacist. He was the seventh incarnation of a soul that Bai Suyin had been chasing across centuries — a mortal soul so perfectly matched to hers that it kept being reborn, kept finding her, kept loving her and dying for her.

*You always choose to die for her,* the ghost whispered. *Every time. And every time, she forgets to tell you the price until it's too late.*

Then the light consumed everything.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them a flat, mundane brown. He stepped fully into the shop, his wet robes dragging across the shattered wood, and stopped precisely at the boundary where the light from Xu Ming's prescription counter met the shadow of the broken door.

"You've grown careless, Bai Suyin," he said. "A thousand years of hiding, and you choose a pharmacy in Hangzhou. Did you think I would not check every herb seller within a hundred miles of West Lake?"

Bai Suyin's hand found Xu Ming's wrist—not to pull him close, not to protect him. Her fingers pressed against his pulse point, counting his heartbeats. Measuring something.

"Let him go," Xu Ming said. His voice cracked on the last word.

Fahai tilted his head. "Go? He was never caught. I am not here for the mortal."

"Then why—"

"I am here to offer a trade."

Bai Suyin's grip tightened. "No."

"You have not heard the terms."

"I know your terms," she spat. "They are always the same. My cultivation for my life. My scales for another century of hunting." She stepped forward, placing herself between Fahai and Xu Ming. "I will not bargain."

Fahai reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll. Yellowed silk, black characters that seemed to writhe in the dim light. He unrolled it with deliberate slowness, revealing a series of seals stamped in vermilion ink.

"This is a writ of sanctuary," he said. "Signed by the Abbot of Jinshan Temple, sealed by the Bureau of Heavenly Affairs. It grants one spirit immunity from pursuit for a period of thirty years, provided they surrender a single scale from their true form and submit to a geas of non-interference in mortal affairs."

Xu Ming's eyes moved between them. "What does that mean?"

"It means I stop hunting her," Fahai said. "She stops interfering with you. She leaves Hangzhou tonight and never returns. In thirty years, the geas lifts, and she is free to continue her cultivation elsewhere."

"Or?" Bai Suyin's voice was flat.

"Or I complete what I started at West Lake eight hundred years ago." Fahai's eyes flickered gold again. "I will bind you in the foundations of Leifeng Pagoda, where your spirit will slowly dissolve over the next millennium. The mortal will be released with a memory charm. He will forget you ever existed."

The silence stretched. Xu Ming watched Bai Suyin's face, looking for any sign, any crack in her armor. Her jaw was set, her gaze locked on Fahai. But her hand, still gripping his wrist, was trembling.

"Take the deal," Xu Ming said.

She looked at him. "What?"

"Take it. Leave. Live." He pulled his wrist free and stepped around her, facing Fahai directly. "She takes the deal. I'll forget. That's fine. Just let her go."

"You do not understand what you are offering," Bai Suyin said.

"I understand that he's going to kill you if you don't take this chance."

Fahai smiled. "The mortal is wiser than you, snake. Even he can see the inevitable."

Bai Suyin's eyes went dark. "You want a scale." She raised her left hand, flexed her fingers. The nails elongated, turning black and sharp. "You can have it. But I will not leave Hangzhou."

"That is not the deal."

"Then the deal changes." She pressed her palm against her own forearm and *pulled*. Xu Ming heard it before he saw it—a sound like tearing silk, like ripping canvas. A strip of skin came away in her hand, translucent and shimmering, covered in patterns that hadn't been visible a moment before. Scales. Tiny, overlapping scales that caught the light and scattered it into rainbows.

She held it out. "One scale. Freshly taken. More than enough for your collection."

Fahai's composure cracked. For just a moment, raw hunger flickered across his face—the expression of a man who had spent centuries chasing a single prize. He reached for the scale.

Bai Suyin dropped it on the floor.

"I would rather it touch dirt than your hands," she said.

Fahai's face went still. He bent down, picked up the scale with two fingers, and examined it. Then he looked at Xu Ming. "Do you know what she has done?"

"She gave you what you wanted."

"She gave me a piece of her mortality." He held up the scale. "Every spirit that seeks to become human must shed their scales one by one. It is the most painful process in cultivation. Each scale represents a year of progress. A hundred years of growth. By tearing this free, she has undone a century of her work."

Xu Ming turned to Bai Suyin. "Is that true?"

She did not answer. Her arm was already healing, the raw skin knitting together, but her face was pale and she was swaying on her feet.

"She has made herself weaker," Fahai continued. "Weaker than she has been in five hundred years. And for what?" He laughed. "For a mortal pharmacist who cannot even see what she truly is."

"Take the scale and leave," Xu Ming said. "You got what you came for."

Fahai's smile vanished. He tucked the scale into his robe, then reached into his sleeve again and pulled out a second scroll. "I have not presented the full terms."

Bai Suyin's eyes widened. "That is not part of the writ."

"No. This is separate." He unrolled it. More seals, more vermilion characters. "The Bureau of Heavenly Affairs has also authorized a second option. One I did not think you would be foolish enough to force me to use."

He read aloud: "In the event that the spirit refuses full compliance, the hunter is authorized to claim a collateral bond—a mortal who has knowingly associated with the spirit, and who may be detained for questioning regarding the spirit's activities and intentions."

Xu Ming felt the floor drop out from under him. "You're arresting me?"

"I am detaining you for questioning," Fahai said. "There is a difference."

"There is no difference," Bai Suyin whispered. "His cells are warded. Written in blood and scripture. No spirit can reach them. No prayer can enter. He will keep you in darkness until you forget which way is up."

"You will not take him," she said.

"You cannot stop me. You just tore out a century of cultivation. You are weak. I could crush you with a single spell." Fahai stepped forward. "You have until midnight to leave Hangzhou. If I find you here tomorrow, I will not offer another deal. I will simply destroy you."

He grabbed Xu Ming by the collar and pulled him toward the broken door.

Xu Ming fought. He was not a fighter, but he was a man who had spent years lifting heavy mortar jars and carrying sacks of herbs. He twisted, threw an elbow, connected with something solid. Fahai did not even flinch.

"Be still," the abbot said, and his voice carried a weight that pressed down on Xu Ming's chest like a physical force. His legs gave out. His arms went numb. He could not move.

Bai Suyin screamed—a sound that was not human. It was a shriek that shattered the remaining glass in the shop windows and sent bottles crashing from the shelves. Her form flickered, scales rippling across her skin, eyes turning to vertical slits.

"Let him go, and I will leave," she said. "I will leave Hangzhou. I will never return. Just let him go."

"Too late for bargains," Fahai said. "You had your chance."

He dragged Xu Ming through the broken door, into the rain. The water soaked them both instantly. Xu Ming's head banged against the doorframe, and stars exploded across his vision.

"Bai Suyin!" he shouted. "Run! Just run!"

She appeared in the doorway, her face a mask of fury and anguish. For a moment, she was fully human. Then her eyes shifted, and he saw the snake behind them—ancient, patient, calculating.

"I will find you," she said. "I will tear down every wall he puts between us. I will burn every ward, break every seal, shatter every prayer he has ever spoken." Her voice dropped. "Wait for me."

Fahai laughed. "Empty threats from a dying snake."

He pulled Xu Ming around the corner, and the last thing Xu Ming saw was Bai Suyin standing in the rain, bleeding from her wounded arm, her silhouette flickering between human and something far older, far darker.

Then the abbot spoke three words in a language Xu Ming did not recognize, and the world went silver and silent and cold.

---

He woke in darkness.

Not the darkness of a room with the lights off. This was absolute. Complete. The kind of darkness that pressed against his eyeballs and made him wonder if he had gone blind.

He was sitting on a stone floor. His wrists were bound behind his back with something rough—rope, maybe, or chains. The air smelled of old incense and dust and something metallic that he did not want to identify.

"Hello?" His voice echoed. The space was large. A hall, maybe. Or a cave.

Footsteps. Slow. Deliberate.

A light bloomed in the darkness—a single candle, held by a figure in white robes. But it was not Fahai.

The figure stepped closer, and Xu Ming saw a woman. Young, maybe twenty-five. Long black hair. A face so beautiful it hurt to look at. She wore white robes embroidered with silver thread, and around her neck hung a jade pendant shaped like a coiled serpent.

"Xu Ming," she said. Her voice was soft. Familiar. "I am sorry we had to meet this way."

"Who are you?"

She knelt before him, set the candle on the floor. The light caught her face, and he saw that her eyes were not quite human. They held the same ancient weight he had seen in Bai Suyin's.

"I am her teacher," the woman said. "Or I was, once. Before Fahai burned the mountain."

"But Bai Suyin said—"

"I died." She smiled. "But death is not the end for spirits. It is only a change of address."

She reached out and touched his forehead with two fingers.

"Listen carefully. Fahai will come soon to question you. He will try to break your mind, peel back your memories, find everything Bai Suyin has told you. You must not resist. Let him take what he wants."

"Why?"

"Because while he is inside your head, he cannot watch his own."

Her smile widened, and Xu Ming saw that her teeth were not human teeth. They were fangs. Row after row of fangs, curving back into her throat.

"I have been waiting nine hundred years for him to make a mistake," she said. "And you, pharmacist, are going to be the bait that finally catches him."

The candle flickered.

The woman was gone.

And in the darkness, something began to crawl.

title: The Debt of Scales
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes swept across the shop like a searchlight. Xu Ming threw himself in front of Bai Suyin, a ridiculous gesture—what could he do against a man who'd spent centuries hunting spirits?

But Bai Suyin moved faster.

She grabbed a handful of dust from the floor—crushed herbs and plaster and wood splinters—and blew it into the light. The particles caught the glow and exploded into a blinding cloud. Fahai threw up an arm. In that half-second of cover, Bai Suyin seized Xu Ming's wrist and yanked him through the back door into the alley.

Rain hit them like a wall.

They ran. Her grip was iron. She knew these streets—slipping through gaps between buildings, over a collapsed fence, through a courtyard where laundry hung limp and dripping. She moved like water, finding paths that shouldn't exist.

"Where—" Xu Ming gasped.

"Don't talk. Run."

They burst onto a main road. Headlights cut through the rain. A taxi's horn blared as Bai Suyin stepped directly into its path. The driver slammed his brakes, cursing in angry Shanghainese. She pulled open the back door, shoved Xu Ming inside, and slid in beside him.

"Drive," she said.

"Where the hell—"

"Just drive. I'll pay double." She pulled a wad of cash from somewhere—Xu Ming had no idea where—and threw it into the front seat. The driver looked at the money, looked at them, and decided he didn't want to know.

The car pulled away. Xu Ming watched the shop disappear through the rain-streaked rear window. His shop. His life. Everything he owned was back there.

"My medicine cabinet," he said quietly. "My grandmother's mortar and pestle. The prescription records."

"Things can be replaced." Bai Suyin's voice was flat. Controlled. But her hands were shaking. He saw them trembling in her lap, the fingers curled into fists.

"You're cold."

"I'm terrified."

The admission stunned him. She'd faced a hunter of eight centuries with defiance. She'd blown dust in his eyes like a street magician. And now, in the back of a stranger's taxi, she admitted fear.

"He saw me," she whispered. "He confirmed my location. By now, every spirit hunter in the province knows where I am. The only reason we escaped is that he wanted us to."

"What?"

"He could have stopped us. He chose not to." She pressed her palm against the window, watching the city blur past. "He wants me to run. He wants to see where I go. Who I turn to. He's not hunting me—he's tracing my connections."

Xu Ming's stomach dropped. "You mean I led him to everyone you know."

"Yes."

The word hung between them, cold and final.

The taxi driver dropped them at a run-down hotel near the train station. Neon sign flickering. Half the letters dead. Bai Suyin paid with more cash and led him through a lobby that smelled of mildew and cheap cigarettes. The room was small, two narrow beds, a bathroom with a toilet that ran constantly.

"This is where you stay?" Xu Ming asked.

"When I'm hiding." She sat on the edge of the bed, finally letting her shoulders slump. Water dripped from her hair, pooling on the stained carpet. "I have safe houses across the city. This one is three weeks old. By tomorrow, it's burned."

"How many safe houses do you have?"

"Eighteen in Hangzhou. Forty-seven in Shanghai. A hundred and twelve across China." She looked up at him. "I've been running for eight hundred years, Xu Ming. I have a system."

"Then why did you come to my shop?"

She was quiet for a long moment. The toilet gurgled.

"Because I was tired," she said. "Because I wanted to stop running. Because when I walked into your shop and you looked at me like I was just a woman—not a spirit, not a demon, not a prize—I wanted to stay."

Xu Ming sat on the other bed. The springs groaned. Rain hammered the window.

"That monk," he said. "Fahai. You said he killed your teacher."

"Her name was Qing Jie. She was a green snake. Three thousand years of cultivation. She raised me from a hatchling." Bai Suyin's voice went very soft. "She taught me that humans were not our enemies. That we could become like them. That love was the path to transcendence."

"Fahai didn't agree."

"He found our mountain when I was four hundred years old. He was young then—younger than you. But his faith was already absolute." She closed her eyes. "He burned the forest. He killed every spirit who tried to flee. He pinned Qing Jie to the earth with a staff made from the Bodhi tree and asked her, as she died, to renounce her pursuit of humanity."

"She refused."

"She told him that love was stronger than fear. That one day, a mortal would love her student enough to break the cycle of death." Bai Suyin opened her eyes. They were wet. "Then he cut off her head."

Xu Ming couldn't speak.

"I have tried to love mortals before," she continued. "Seven times. Seven lives. Each time, Fahai found them. Each time, they died. Poison. Accident. Illness. Once, a heart attack that came from nowhere while he was walking to meet me."

"Why doesn't he just kill you?"

"Because death would be mercy. He wants me to suffer. He wants me to learn that love is a lie, that I can never be human, that the only path for a snake is solitude." Her voice cracked. "He wants me to give up."

"Are you going to?"

She looked at him. Really looked. Her eyes traced his face, the rain still dripping from his hair, the fear he couldn't hide, the trembling in his hands.

"No," she said. "Because you are different."

"How?"

"Your heart." She touched her own chest. "I can hear it. Eight hundred years of listening to mortal hearts, and I have never heard one like yours. It beats in a pattern that doesn't match the world."

Xu Ming didn't know what that meant. He wasn't sure he wanted to know.

The hotel room's light flickered.

Bai Suyin tensed. Her head snapped toward the door.

"What is it?"

"Someone's here." She rose in a single fluid motion. No sound. No wasted movement. "Stay behind me."

The door handle turned.

Xu Ming's heart stopped.

But the door didn't open. Instead, a piece of paper slid underneath, thin and white, settling on the dirty carpet. Bai Suyin approached it like it might explode. She picked it up, read it, and her face drained of color.

"What does it say?" Xu Ming asked.

She handed him the paper. The handwriting was elegant, brush strokes precise, characters ancient:

*白素贞—*
*You have three days to surrender yourself to the Temple of Thunderous Peace. Bring the mortal. If you do not come, I will burn every safe house. Every ally. Every life you have touched in this city.*
*Your teacher's skull hangs above my altar.*
*Come collect it.*
*—Fahai*

Xu Ming's hands shook so hard the paper rattled.

"He wants me to surrender with you," another voice said from the doorway—but it wasn't Fahai.

The door was still closed.

The voice came from inside the room.

Xu Ming spun. A woman stood in the corner near the bathroom, where there had been no one a moment before. She was tall, dressed in green silk, her hair long and black, her eyes the color of jade. She smiled, and her teeth were too sharp.

"Hello, sister," she said to Bai Suyin. "Long time no see."

Bai Suyin's face went white. Then red. Then white again.

"Xiao Qing."

"The same." The woman bowed mockingly. "I heard you were in trouble. I came to help."

"You came to collect a debt."

"Debt is such an ugly word." Xiao Qing walked forward, her movements sinuous, hypnotic. She stopped in front of Xu Ming and circled him, studying him like a piece of meat. "So this is the heart-trials. He's pretty. I can see the appeal."

"Stay away from him."

"Or what? You'll fight me?" Xiao Qing laughed. It was a beautiful sound, like breaking glass. "Sister, you can barely stand. You've been suppressing your true form for so long that you've forgotten how to be dangerous. Fahai will eat you alive."

"He has my teacher's skull."

"I know. I saw it." Xiao Qing's smile faded. "That's why I'm here. Not for you. For Qing Jie. She was my teacher too."

Bai Suyin's eyes widened. "You—"

"I know where Fahai's temple is. I know its defenses. I know the rituals he uses to suppress spirit power. And I know how to get inside." Xiao Qing turned to Xu Ming, her jade eyes boring into his. "But I need bait. A mortal. Someone he wants."

"No," Bai Suyin said.

"Yes," Xiao Qing countered. "Or your heart-trial dies anyway, along with everyone you love. Those are the choices, sister. Trust me, or watch him burn."

The rain continued to fall.

The toilet gurgled.

And Xu Ming realized, with terrible clarity, that he was standing between two snake spirits, one of whom he loved, and one of whom he was absolutely certain was lying.

But he didn't know which was which.

title: The Blood of the Mountain
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes swept across the shop like a blade. Xu Ming felt it pass through him—cold, searching, hungry. Behind him, Bai Suyin made a sound he had never heard before. A whimper. Small and broken.

"You have grown careless, 白素贞." Fahai stepped over the shattered doorframe. His robes were dry now, despite the rain. The wood fragments on his shoulders crumbled to dust as he moved. "Eight hundred years of hiding, and you choose a pharmacy in Hangzhou. Did you think I would not check the old places?"

Xu Ming's hand found the counter's edge. His fingers brushed against a mortar, a pestle, jars of herbs that suddenly seemed laughably useless. "This is my shop," he said. His voice cracked but held. "You have no right—"

"Quiet, mortal." Fahai didn't even look at him. "Your voice is meaningless here."

Bai Suyin stepped forward. Her transformation had fully reversed—she looked human again, pale and trembling, but her eyes held a cold fire that Xu Ming had only glimpsed once before, when she had shown him her scales. "He is not meaningless. He is mine."

"Yours?" Fahai laughed. It was a terrible sound, like stones grinding together. "You claim a mortal as property now? How far you have fallen, daughter of the mountain."

"I claim him as my heart trial," Bai Suyin said. Her voice rose. "And by the old laws, you cannot touch me while the trial is incomplete."
