The golden eyes flickered. For just a moment, Xu Ming saw something cross Fahai's face—not doubt, but interest. A predator's curiosity at unexpected prey.

"The old laws," Fahai repeated. "You would invoke them? You, who broke every law the heavens set down when you chose to cultivate instead of die?"

"I chose to live."

"You chose to steal." Fahai's hand moved, and the prayer beads around his wrist began to glow. Each bead was carved bone, ancient and yellowed. "You stole the breath of the mountain. You stole the wisdom of your teacher. You stole the form of humanity, which was never meant for your kind."

"And you stole nothing?" Bai Suyin's voice sharpened. "You took your power from the same mountain. You drank from the same spring. We were sisters once, Fahai. We studied under the same master."

The air in the shop changed. Xu Ming felt it—a pressure building, like before a thunderstorm. The bottles on his shelves began to rattle.

"Do not speak of her," Fahai said. His voice dropped to a whisper that somehow filled the entire room. "You are not worthy to speak her name."

"She was my teacher too."

"She was my mother."

Xu Ming's breath caught. He looked between them—the cold monk with the burning eyes and the snake spirit who had pressed his palm to her heart. Sisters. Teacher. Mother. The pieces shifted in his mind, forming a picture he didn't want to see.

"Your mother," Bai Suyin said softly, "chose me. She saw something in me that she never saw in you. And that is why you have hunted me for eight centuries. Not because I broke the laws. Because she loved me more."

Fahai moved.

He crossed the shop in less than a heartbeat, his hand closing around Bai Suyin's throat. She didn't resist. She didn't even flinch. She simply looked at him with something that might have been pity.

"You should have died in the burning," Fahai hissed. "You should have turned to ash with the rest of her legacy."

"I would have," Bai Suyin said. "But she gave me something before she died. Something you never knew about."

Fahai's grip tightened. "Lies."

"She gave me her heart."

The golden eyes went wide. For the first time, Fahai's composure cracked. His hand fell from her throat as if burned. "Impossible. I watched her die. I held her body as the fire consumed her."

"You held her shell." Bai Suyin touched her own chest. "Her heart has been beating inside me for a thousand years. It is why I can become human. It is why your laws cannot bind me. It is why, brother, you will never be able to kill me."

The word hung in the air like smoke. Brother. Xu Ming's mind raced, trying to keep up. The hunter and the hunted. The monk and the snake. They shared blood. They shared a mother.

And Bai Suyin had been carrying their mother's heart inside her for a millennium.

"You're lying," Fahai said again, but his voice wavered. "You've always been a liar,白素贞. You lied to our mother. You lied to the mountain. You will lie to this mortal until he gives you what you want, and then you will devour him like you devoured everything else."

"I have never devoured anything." Bai Suyin's eyes glistened. "I have only survived. Can you say the same, brother? How many lives have you taken in our mother's name? How many spirits have you burned? How many hearts have you crushed, telling yourself it was holy work?"

Fahai's prayer beads shattered.

They exploded outward, bone fragments flying across the shop like shrapnel. Xu Ming threw up his arms, felt pieces tear through his sleeves, his skin. Blood welled from a dozen small wounds. But Fahai didn't notice. He was staring at his empty wrist, at the beads that had been his only connection to the mother he had lost.

"You took everything," Fahai whispered. "Her teachings. Her love. Her heart. And now you stand in this filthy little shop, pretending to be human, pretending to be innocent, while the sins of a thousand years cling to you like rot."

"I carry no sins," Bai Suyin said. "I carry only what she gave me freely."

"Then prove it."

Fahai raised his hand, and the golden light returned to his eyes—brighter now, almost blinding. The air around him began to shimmer. Xu Ming felt the temperature drop. Frost formed on the windows.

"If you truly have her heart," Fahai said, "then show me. Open your chest. Let me see it beat. Let me hold it in my hands one last time."

Bai Suyin's face went pale. "You know I cannot."

"Because you would die?"

"Because it would kill you."

Fahai laughed again, but this time the sound was hollow. "You think I fear death? I have walked through it a hundred times. I have killed my own students, my own followers, my own blood when they stood between me and my purpose. You think your mother's heart could destroy me?"

"I think," Bai Suyin said softly, "that you have been running from it your entire life. Just like I have been running from you."

Xu Ming stepped forward. His legs were shaking. His arms were bleeding. But he stepped between them anyway, facing the monk who had just destroyed his shop with a gesture.

"Get out," Xu Ming said.

Fahai looked at him. Really looked at him, for the first time. His eyes narrowed. "You are either very brave or very stupid, pharmacist."

"Probably both." Xu Ming's hands were still shaking, but he kept them at his sides. "This is my shop. That woman is under my protection. And you—" He swallowed. "You are going to leave, or I will call the police."

"The police." Fahai's smile returned, thin and cruel. "You think mortal laws can touch me? I have lived longer than your entire civilization. I have watched empires rise and fall. Your police are insects to me."

"Then treat us like insects." Xu Ming reached into his pocket and pulled out his phone. "But insects can still sting."

Fahai's gaze dropped to the phone. Something flickered in his eyes—not fear, but recognition. "You would call them? Knowing what I am?"

"I would call them knowing that you just broke into my shop and assaulted my customer. The rest—" Xu Ming's thumb hovered over the call button. "The rest is your problem."

For a long moment, no one moved. The rain hammered against the roof. Frost still clung to the windows. Bai Suyin stood frozen behind Xu Ming, her breath coming in short, sharp gasps.

Then Fahai laughed.

It was a different laugh this time. Lighter. Almost amused. "You love her already," he said. "That's why you're willing to die for her. You've been caught in her web, just like all the others."

"There are no others," Xu Ming said.

"Of course there are. Did she not tell you?" Fahai tilted his head. "Every hundred years, she chooses a mortal. Every hundred years, she tries to complete her trial. And every hundred years, she fails—because she cannot love truly, and no one can love her truly once they see what she really is."

"That's enough," Bai Suyin said.

"Did she tell you about the scholar in the Southern Song? He threw himself into the river when he learned the truth. The merchant during the Ming dynasty? He tried to kill her in her sleep. The painter in the Qing? He went mad and painted her face on every wall in his house before hanging himself."

Xu Ming's thumb pressed down on the call button.

"I know what she is," he said. "She showed me."

Fahai's amusement vanished. "She showed you?"

"Her scales. Her eyes. The form she keeps hidden." Xu Ming's voice was steady now. "I saw the truth. I'm still here."

Something shifted in Fahai's expression. For just a moment, he looked almost human. Almost vulnerable. "Then you are more foolish than I thought. Or more doomed."

He stepped back. The frost on the windows began to recede. The golden light in his eyes dimmed.

"I will not kill you tonight, pharmacist. Not because of your threats, but because I want you to see. I want you to understand what you have bound yourself to." He looked past Xu Ming, at Bai Suyin. "I will be watching. And when your heart trial fails—as it always fails—I will be there."

"Get out," Xu Ming said again.

Fahai turned. He walked through the shattered doorframe, into the rain, and vanished. Not into the street. Not into the darkness. He simply dissolved, like smoke in the wind, until there was nothing left but the sound of rain and the smell of burning incense.

Xu Ming's legs gave out.

He collapsed against the counter, gasping. His hands were shaking so badly he dropped his phone. Blood dripped from his arms onto the floor, mixing with rainwater and shattered glass.

Bai Suyin was beside him in an instant, her hands on his face, her eyes searching his. "You should not have done that. You should have run."

"And leave you?"

"I have survived eight hundred years without you, Xu Ming. I could have survived one more night."

He looked at her. Really looked. The woman who had walked into his shop asking for thousand-year-old herbs. The spirit who had shown him her true form. The daughter of a mountain, carrying her mother's heart, hunted by her own brother across centuries.

"I don't care," he said. "I don't care how many lives you've lived before. I don't care how many times you've failed. I don't care that your brother wants to kill you and that I'm just a pharmacist who mixes herbs for coughs."

He took her hand. Pressed it to

title: The Blood Price
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes swept across the shop like a searchlight. Xu Ming threw himself sideways, pulling Bai Suyin behind the counter as shelves exploded behind them. Glass shards rained down. Herbs scattered into the air, filling the room with the smell of crushed ginseng and dried chrysanthemum.

"Running already?" Fahai's voice carried over the destruction. "After eight hundred years, I expected more fight."

Bai Suyin pressed herself against Xu Ming's back. Her breath was hot against his neck. "The back window. If we can reach the alley—"

"He'll follow."

"Then I'll fight him there, away from you."

Xu Ming grabbed her wrist. "No. You said you need me to survive. So we survive together or not at all."

Her eyes widened. For a moment, the ancient coldness in them flickered, replaced by something younger. Something almost human.

Another blast of golden light tore through the counter. Wood splintered. Xu Ming felt the heat pass inches from his face, felt his eyebrows singe. Then Bai Suyin was moving, dragging him toward the back room where the explosion had started.

They stumbled through the shattered doorframe. The back room was destroyed—his mixing station overturned, jars broken, the floor slick with medicinal oils. Rain poured through a hole in the ceiling where Fahai had entered.

"He's herding us," Xu Ming realized. "He could have hit us. He's pushing us somewhere."

"Clever mortal." Fahai's voice echoed from the front of the shop, closer now. "But cleverness without power is just noise."

Bai Suyin's hand found his. Her fingers were cold, scaled. The transformation was starting again without her control. "There's a basement. Your storage cellar."

"It's a dead end."

"It's our only chance." She pulled him toward the trapdoor beneath the ruined rug. "I can lay a barrier there. Buy us time to think."

Xu Ming's hands shook as he lifted the heavy wooden door. The cellar stairs descended into darkness. He could smell the damp earth, the stored herbs, the rat poison he kept in the far corner. His sanctuary. His small, safe world.

Fahai's footsteps approached.

They descended. Bai Suyin pulled the trapdoor shut above them, and the darkness swallowed them whole.

For a long moment, there was only breathing. Xu Ming's heart hammered so hard he could feel it in his teeth. Bai Suyin pressed both palms against the trapdoor, whispering words in a language that sounded like wind through bamboo. Ancient. Hollow.

A faint blue light bloomed from her hands, spreading across the wood like frost.

"It won't hold long," she said. "But it will hide our presence for a few hours."

"A few hours? That's it?"

"That's everything." She turned to face him, and in the dim light of her own magic, he saw how exhausted she was. The scales had receded, but her skin was pale as paper. Dark circles shadowed her eyes. "Fahai has been hunting me for centuries. He knows every trick. Every escape. Every hiding place across every province."

"Then how do we win?"

She was silent.

"Bai Suyin. How do we win?"

"I don't know." The words came out raw. Broken. "I have spent a thousand years planning for this moment, and I still don't know how to defeat him. He has killed every teacher I ever had. Every ally. Every spirit who tried to help me." Her voice cracked. "He killed my sister."

Xu Ming's breath caught. "You had a sister?"

"Xiao Qing. A green snake. Three hundred years younger than me." Bai Suyin's hands curled into fists. "Fahai caught her in Suzhou, during the Song Dynasty. He buried her alive beneath Leifeng Pagoda. I could hear her screaming for three days before the stone finally silenced her."

The image hit Xu Ming like a physical blow. He had read the legends. He knew the stories. But hearing it from her lips, in this dark cellar, with the rain pounding above them—it made it real in a way the old texts never could.

"Is that what he wants to do to you?"

"That is the kindest version." She looked at him, and for the first time, he saw fear in her eyes. Real fear. "He wants to use me. My cultivation. My essence. He believes that if he can consume a thousand-year spirit, he will achieve enlightenment. He will become something beyond human."

"Consume you?"

"Eat my heart while I still live. Absorb my power. My memories. My centuries." She swallowed. "For eight hundred years, I have been running from a man who wants to devour me."

Xu Ming's mind raced. He was a pharmacist. He knew toxins. He knew the human body, the herbal remedies that could heal or harm. But none of that mattered against a monster like Fahai.

Unless.

"Your blood," he said suddenly.

"What?"

"Your blood. The day we met, you said it had properties. That it could heal. That it was the source of your power."

Bai Suyin's eyes narrowed. "What are you thinking?"

"Can I use it? If I mix it with certain herbs—certain compounds—could I create something that affects you? Something that affects spirits?"

She stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a sharp, bitter sound. "You want to poison me?"

"No. I want to make you stronger. Or make him weaker." He grabbed her shoulders. "You said you need my love to win. But love isn't a weapon. It's a shield. I need a weapon, Bai Suyin. Something I can use to protect you."

"Xu Ming—"

"I'm not asking for permission." His voice was hard. "I'm asking if it's possible."

She studied his face. Whatever she saw there made her breath catch. "It is... possible. But dangerous. My blood is volatile. If you mix it wrong, it could kill me. It could turn me feral. It could attract every spirit hunter within a thousand li."

"I need to know how."

"Xu Ming—"

"Tell me."

The trapdoor above them groaned. The blue barrier flickered. Fahai's footsteps paced overhead, slow and deliberate, like a predator circling a cage.

"Fine." Bai Suyin's voice dropped to a whisper. "But you must understand what you're asking. My blood carries my essence. My memories. My pain. If you mix it, you will taste every death I have witnessed. Every century of loneliness. Every moment of fear." She touched his face, her fingers cold. "It will change you."

"Will it kill me?"

"No. But you might wish it had."

Xu Ming thought of his quiet life. His small shop. His predictable days. He thought of the woman standing before him, ancient and terrified and more alive than anyone he had ever known.

"Do it," he said.

She closed her eyes. When she opened them, they were serpentine—vertical pupils, gold irises, ancient and terrible. She bit her own wrist, hard enough to draw blood. It welled up dark and thick, almost black in the dim light.

"Use your herbs," she said, holding out her bleeding arm. "Mix them with this. Create something that can hurt him."

Xu Ming's hands were steady as he reached for the storage shelves. He grabbed dried angelica, ginseng, cinnabar. He found a ceramic bowl, still intact, and began grinding the herbs with his mortar.

Bai Suyin's blood dripped into the mixture, and the room filled with a smell like thunderstorms and old copper.

The trapdoor groaned again. The barrier flickered.

"Hurry," she whispered.

Xu Ming worked faster. His hands knew these herbs. His body knew these motions. He had done this a thousand times—but never with blood like this. Never with a woman's life in every drop.

The mixture turned dark red. It pulsed, like a heartbeat.

"What now?" he asked.

"Drink it."

"What?"

"Half of it. You need to survive the backlash. The other half we use as a weapon."

He looked at the bowl. The liquid swirled with colors he couldn't name. It smelled like death and possibility.

"You said it would change me."

"It will."

Xu Ming raised the bowl to his lips. He thought of his mother, who had taught him the old ways. He thought of his father, who had died too young. He thought of the small, safe life he had built, and how it had never really been his.

He drank.

The liquid hit his throat like fire. It spread through his chest, his limbs, his skull. He felt everything—Bai Suyin's memories crashing over him like a tidal wave. A thousand years of pain. A thousand years of running. A thousand years of watching everyone she loved die.

He screamed.

The sound was not human. It was something older, something that had learned to scream before language existed.

And somewhere in the darkness, he felt her. Bai Suyin. Not as a woman, not as a spirit, but as a presence. A warmth. A hand reaching for his in the void.

*I'm here,* she said, and her voice was inside his skull. *I'm always here.*

The trapdoor exploded.

Golden light flooded the cellar. Fahai stood in the opening, his robes billowing, his eyes twin suns of divine fury.

"Clever," he said, looking at the bowl in Xu Ming's shaking hands. "But not clever enough."

Xu Ming's body moved before his mind caught up. He threw himself forward, the remaining mixture splashing across Fahai's chest.

The monk screamed.

For the first time, Xu Ming saw him stumble. Saw pain flash across those golden eyes. Saw smoke rise from where the blood-herbs had touched his skin.

But Fahai did not fall.

He raised his hand, and golden light gathered in his palm. "You have made a grave mistake, pharmacist. You have bound yourself to a demon."

"And you," Xu Ming said, his voice strange and layered, like two people speaking at once, "have made a grave mistake threatening what is mine."

Fahai's smile was terrible.

"I wasn't threatening," he said. "I was beginning."

The golden light exploded outward.

And the world went white.

---

Xu Ming opened his eyes to darkness.

He was lying on cold stone. His body ached. His mouth tasted of copper and ash. Somewhere above him, he could hear water dripping.

*Bai Suyin.*

He tried to move, but his limbs would not obey.

A voice spoke from the shadows. Not Fahai's. Someone else. Someone familiar.

"So," the voice said, "you finally found her."

Xu Ming's blood ran cold.

He knew that voice.

He had heard it every day for the past five years.

It was Chen Wei. The old man who sold tea at the stall across the street.

"Old Chen?" Xu Ming whispered. "What—"

"Not Old Chen." The figure stepped into the dim light, and Xu Ming saw eyes that were not human. Not golden like Fahai's, but black. Entirely black. "I am the one who told Fahai where to find you."

"Why?"

The thing that wore Chen Wei's face smiled.

"Because I am the one who has been hunting Bai Suyin longer than Fahai has ever lived. And I have waited a thousand years to taste her heart."

title: The Price of a Heart
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, but the memory of it burned in Xu Ming's vision like afterimages from staring at the sun. He stood between the abbot and Bai Suyin, his body a fragile barrier of flesh and bone.

"Demon?" Xu Ming's voice cracked. "She's more human than you."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. It was the laugh of someone who had heard every argument, crushed every defense, and grown bored of mortal ignorance. "You mistake kindness for truth, pharmacist. Let me show you what she really is."

He reached into his robe and pulled out a bell. Bronze. Ancient. The surface was covered in characters so small they seemed to crawl across the metal like living things.

Bai Suyin gasped. "No. Not that."

"The Golden Bell of Leifeng," Fahai said, holding it up. "Forged from the iron of a thousand temples. Blessed by a thousand monks. It has been waiting for you across eight centuries, Bai Suyin. I found it in the ashes of your last refuge."

He rang it once.

The sound was not loud. It was soft, almost gentle, like a wind chime on a summer afternoon. But Bai Suyin screamed. She fell to her knees, clutching her head, and Xu Ming saw scales ripple across her skin—not slowly this time, but violently, as if her body was trying to tear itself apart from the inside.

"Stop!" Xu Ming lunged at Fahai.

The abbot didn't move. He simply raised one hand, and Xu Ming hit an invisible wall. He bounced off it, landing hard on the broken floor, his nose bleeding.

"Your mortal love cannot protect her from me," Fahai said. "Love is the very thing that makes her vulnerable. Every time she cares for someone, she gives me a weapon. Every time she builds a life, she builds her own cage."

Xu Ming pushed himself up. His vision swam. "You're wrong."

"Am I?" Fahai rang the bell again.

Bai Suyin's scream became a roar. Her true form flickered in and out of existence—a massive white serpent coiling through the shop, shattering shelves, crushing herbs into powder. Xu Ming saw her eye, bigger than his head, filled with pain and rage and something else.

Fear.

She was afraid.

"I will tell you a story, pharmacist," Fahai said, walking slowly around the writhing serpent. "A thousand years ago, a white snake saved a mortal scholar from drowning. She carried him to shore, breathed life into his lungs, and fell in love with him. She thought love would save her. She thought love would make her human."

Another ring of the bell. The serpent thrashed, knocking a hole in the wall.

"But love is not salvation," Fahai continued. "Love is chains. The scholar was taken from her. The child she bore was taken from her. And when she finally broke free of her earthly attachments, I was waiting. I locked her beneath Leifeng Pagoda for five hundred years. She learned nothing."

Xu Ming crawled toward Bai Suyin. His hands found her face—scales and all, cold and smooth and terrifyingly real. She looked at him with eyes that were no longer human, and he saw something ancient in them. Something that had watched dynasties fall and rivers change course.

"Xu Ming," she whispered. Her voice was a thousand voices layered together. "Run."

"No."

"Run. I cannot—" She convulsed, her body splitting between forms. "I cannot protect you when the bell rings. He will use me to kill you. Please. Run."

Fahai raised the bell for the fourth time. "She speaks the truth, pharmacist. The bell does not just reveal her true form. It awakens her primal nature. The serpent that eats mortals. The beast that devoured an entire village in 1178."

"That was an accident," Bai Suyin snarled. "You caused it. You herded them into my path."

"Does it matter whose hand held the staff?" Fahai's voice was gentle now, almost kind. "You still tasted their blood. You still remember how sweet it was."

Xu Ming saw the change in her eyes. Hunger. Ancient and terrible, waking up after centuries of sleep. Her pupils dilated. Her tongue forked. She looked at him and did not see a lover.

She saw meat.

"Bai Suyin," he said, gripping her face. "Listen to me. You are not that thing."

"I am," she breathed. "I have always been that thing. I just learned to hide it."

"No." He pressed his forehead against hers. "You are the woman who saved Old Chen's daughter from a fever. You are the woman who spent three hours teaching me to make that stupidly complicated tea. You are the woman who told me that love is not a trial, it is a choice. And I choose you."

The bell rang again.

But this time, something broke.

Not the shop. Not the walls. Something inside the sound itself. The pure tone cracked, split, became discordant. Fahai looked down at the bell in confusion. A hairline fracture ran across its surface.

"What—"

"You were wrong about one thing, Abbot." Xu Ming stood up, still holding Bai Suyin's face. "Love isn't chains. It's armor."

The explosion of light that followed was not golden. It was white, pure white, like the sun had decided to visit earth. Bai Suyin screamed again, but this time the sound was triumphant. Her human form snapped back into place, but it was different now. She glowed. A faint luminescence clung to her skin, her hair, her eyes.

She looked at her hands. "Impossible."

Fahai's composure cracked. "You completed the trial. Here. Now. Without ritual or preparation—"

"You said love makes her vulnerable." Xu Ming's nose was still bleeding. His ribs ached. He had never felt more alive. "But you were wrong. Love makes her stronger."

Bai Suyin rose to her feet, and the air around her hummed with power. The shattered bottles on the floor lifted, suspended in midair. The pages of scattered herb manuals turned themselves. The shop's lights flickered and died, then came back brighter than before.

"I am human," she said, and the words carried weight. Reality itself seemed to agree with her. "I am human, and I am loved, and you have no power over me anymore, Fahai."

The abbot's face twisted. The serene mask fell away, revealing something ugly beneath. "You think this changes anything? You think love protects you from me? I have hunted you across eight hundred years, Bai Suyin. I have killed your teachers, burned your sanctuaries, erased your children from the face of the earth. Do you think one mortal pharmacist will stop me?"

He threw the broken bell at her feet. "This was just a tool. I have others. Tools you have never seen, horrors you cannot imagine. And I will use every single one of them to drag you back to the darkness where you belong."

"You will try." Bai Suyin's voice was steel. "And you will fail. Because for the first time in a thousand years, I have something worth fighting for."

She turned to Xu Ming. Her eyes were wet. "You should not have done that. You do not understand what you have tied yourself to."

"I tied myself to you." He took her hand. "That's all I need to understand."

Fahai watched them with cold fury. Then, slowly, he smiled. It was the worst expression Xu Ming had ever seen on a human face.

"Very well. If you insist on protecting her, pharmacist, then you will carry the burden of her sin." He reached into his robe again and pulled out a scroll. Yellowed. Bound with red silk. "This is the True Record of Bai Suyin's crimes. Every life she has taken. Every soul she has consumed. Every village she has destroyed."

"Those were not her crimes," Xu Ming said. "You drove her to them."

"Does it matter to the dead?" Fahai unrolled the scroll. Names covered it. Thousands of names, written in blood-red ink. "The law of heaven does not care about intent. It cares about balance. And she owes a debt that cannot be paid."

He looked at Xu Ming with something almost like pity. "By completing the trial, she has transferred her karmic debt to the one who loves her. To you."

The scroll burst into flames. The fire was cold, blue, and it did not burn Fahai's hands. It leaped from the paper and struck Xu Ming in the chest.

He felt nothing at first. Then the names began to etch themselves into his skin. Thousands of them, appearing on his arms, his chest, his face. Each name was a burn. Each name was a life.

He fell.

Bai Suyin caught him. "No. No, no, no—" She turned on Fahai with murder in her eyes. "Reverse it. Reverse it now or I will tear you apart molecule by molecule."

"It cannot be reversed," Fahai said. "The debt is transferred. Every life he sees in his dreams, every death he feels in his bones, will be a reminder of what you are. What you have always been. And in time, he will hate you for it."

He turned and walked toward the shattered door.

"Where are you going?" Bai Suyin's voice cracked.

"To find your master's grave." Fahai paused at the threshold. "I hear she left something for you. Something you will need if you want to save your precious pharmacist from the weight of a thousand sins."

He disappeared into the rain.

Xu Ming lay in Bai Suyin's arms, gasping. The names burned. They whispered to him in voices he did not recognize. A woman. A child. An old man. A soldier. Each one telling him how they died. Each one blaming him.

"I can fix this," Bai Suyin said, but her voice was hollow. "I can find a way. My master knew things. She prepared for everything."

"The scroll," Xu Ming managed. "What was on the scroll?"

She was silent for too long.

"Bai Suyin. What was on the scroll?"

"It was not a record of crimes," she whispered. "It was a prophecy. The names of everyone who will die because of me. Everyone Fahai will kill to get to me."

Xu Ming looked at his arms. The names were still appearing. Thousands of them. And at the very top, written in letters that burned brighter than the rest, he saw two words.

His own name.

And beneath it, in fresh ink that dripped like blood:

*Bai Suyin.*

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Third Eye Opens

The golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming with an intensity that made his bones feel thin.

Fahai stepped fully into the shop, his wet robes leaving dark trails across the floor. He was taller than Xu Ming remembered from the temple — broader in the shoulders, harder in the jaw. The prayer beads in his hand clicked with each step, a sound like bones breaking.

"Release her," Fahai said. Not a request. A command.

Xu Ming's hand was still pressed against Bai Suyin's chest. Her heart hammered beneath his palm, rapid and desperate. He could feel the cold radiating from her skin, the tremble in her muscles.

"No," he said.

The word came out before he could think. It surprised him. It surprised Fahai too — the golden eyes narrowed slightly.

"You do not understand what she is."

"I understand she's bleeding." Xu Ming's voice steadied. "I understand she came to me for help. And I understand you broke my door."

Fahai's smile did not reach his eyes. "She has woven illusions around you, pharmacist. Made you feel special. Chosen. Did she tell you that you are her 'heart trial'? That she needs your love to become human?"

"She told me."

"And you believed her." Fahai laughed — a dry, hollow sound. "Every century, she finds a new fool. Every century, she drains them dry and moves on. You are not her first. You will not be her last."

"That's not true." Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "I never—"

"You never what?" Fahai's prayer beads stopped moving. "You never consumed their lifeforce? You never left them hollow shells, wandering the streets with no memory of what they'd lost?" He turned to Xu Ming. "Ask her about the scholar from Suzhou. The painter from Nanjing. The soldier from Beijing. Ask her why they all died young."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. Her face was sheet-white, tears streaming silently.

"Is it true?"

"I was weaker then." Her voice barely carried. "I took too much. I didn't know how to control it. But I learned. I changed. I spent fifty years in meditation, purifying my cultivation, so I would never harm another mortal—"

"Lies." Fahai's voice cracked like a whip. "A serpent cannot change its nature. You are poison. You have always been poison. And I have spent eight hundred years proving it."

He raised his hand. The prayer beads glowed — not with light, but with absence. The air around them turned cold, dark, as if the beads were drinking warmth from the room.

Bai Suyin shoved Xu Ming aside.

The beads struck her chest.

She flew backward, crashing through the counter, sending jars of herbs exploding across the floor. She landed in a heap among the debris, gasping, a black stain spreading across her dress where the beads had touched her.

"Bai Suyin!" Xu Ming started toward her.

"Stay back." She held up a hand, struggling to her feet. Blood dripped from her lip. "The beads are consecrated. They burn through spirit energy. If they touch you—"

"They will not touch him if he steps aside." Fahai advanced, another bead glowing in his palm. "This is your last warning, pharmacist. I do not wish to harm a mortal. But I will not let a demon escape on my watch."

"She's not a demon."

"She is a snake who has killed hundreds to prolong her existence."

"I've killed no one in three hundred years." Bai Suyin spat blood. "Can you say the same, Abbot? How many spirits have you destroyed? How many innocent cultivators burned alive because they refused your 'mercy'?"

Fahai's face twitched. For a moment, something dark passed behind his golden eyes.

"Those who refuse mercy choose destruction."

"You killed children." Bai Suyin's voice rose. "Fox spirits. Crane spirits. A litter of kits who had never harmed anyone. You burned their den with them inside and called it purification."

"Contamination must be excised completely. Even the seed of evil—"

"They were BABIES."

The shop shook. Bottles rattled. Xu Ming felt the vibration in his teeth.

Bai Suyin was changing.

Her eyes shifted first — from brown to vertical slits, gold and black and ancient. Scales rippled across her cheeks, catching the fluorescent light in rainbows. Her body elongated, twisted, grew beyond the bounds of human form.

"Bai Suyin, don't—" Xu Ming started.

"She wants to see a demon?" Her voice layered over itself, human and serpentine, young and impossibly old. "Let her see what a thousand years of cultivation looks like."

The transformation exploded.

Her human form dissolved into a column of white light, spiraling upward, crashing through the ceiling. Wood splintered. Plaster rained down. The fluorescent lights exploded in showers of glass.

And then she was there.

Not a woman. A serpent.

White scales, each as large as a dinner plate, gleaming like polished bone. Her body coiled through the ruined shop, filling the space, pressing against the walls. Her head — if it could be called a head — was bigger than Xu Ming's entire torso, jaws wide enough to swallow a man whole.

Her eyes found Fahai.

"You wanted my true form." Her voice rumbled through the floor. "Here it is."

Fahai did not flinch. He reached into his robes and withdrew a small brass bell. He rang it once.

The sound was wrong. It hurt. It felt like needles in Xu Ming's ears, like something scraping against his skull.

The serpent screamed.

Her body convulsed, scales rattling, coiling tighter and tighter until Xu Ming thought she would crush herself. Blood appeared between her scales — thin lines of red that dripped onto the ruined floor.

"The Bell of Binding," Fahai said calmly. "Forged from the bronze of a thousand temple bells. It resonates with the frequency of demonic essence. It tears you apart from the inside."

He rang it again.

The serpent thrashed. Her tail smashed through the front window, sending glass spraying into the street. Her body slammed against the back wall, cracking the brickwork. Blood flowed freely now, pooling beneath her.

"Stop!" Xu Ming grabbed Fahai's arm. "You're killing her!"

" That is the intention ."

Fahai shook him off with casual strength. Xu Ming stumbled, fell, hit his head against a broken shelf. Blood trickled into his eye.

He looked up at the serpent — at Bai Suyin — writhing in agony, scales cracking, blood everywhere.

And he understood something.

He did not want her to die.

Not because of the trial. Not because of her beauty or her mystery or the way she had looked at him by the lake. He did not want her to die because she had trusted him. Because she had come to him for shelter. Because for one moment in this small, safe life, someone had looked at him and seen something worth protecting.

He stood up.

"Fahai." His voice was quiet. "I'm going to ask you one more time. Leave. Her. Alone."

"And I will give you one more answer." Fahai raised the bell. "No."

Xu Ming moved.

He was not fast. He was not strong. He was a pharmacist who spent his days measuring herbs and counting pills. But he was between Fahai and the serpent, and that was enough.

"Get out of the way, boy."

"No."

The golden eyes blazed. "I will strike you down where you stand."

"Then strike me down." Xu Ming's hands shook, but his voice did not. "But know this. If you kill me, you will have murdered a human being. Not a demon. Not a spirit. A man. And whatever authority you claim to have, whatever divine mandate you believe in, it will not protect you from that."

Fahai's hand tightened on the bell.

For a long moment, no one moved.

Then Fahai laughed.

It was not a pleasant sound. It was the laugh of someone who has been amused despite themselves, who finds a fly's defiance charming before they swat it.

"You have courage, pharmacist. Stupid courage, but courage nonetheless." He tucked the bell back into his robes. "Very well. I will give you one day."

Xu Ming's knees nearly buckled with relief.

"One day," Fahai continued, "to see the truth. To understand what she is. And when you do — when you see her for the predator she is — you will invite me back to finish what I started."

"I won't—"

"You will." Fahai turned toward the broken door. "Because by this time tomorrow, she will have shown you her real face. The one she hides behind pretty words and wounded eyes. And you will beg me to save you."

He stepped through the doorway, into the rain.

Xu Ming stood frozen, listening to his footsteps fade.

The serpent behind him shuddered, contracted, collapsed in on itself. Scales dissolved into skin. Massive coils shrank into limbs. And then Bai Suyin lay on the floor, naked, covered in blood, barely breathing.

Xu Ming grabbed his coat and covered her.

"I'm sorry," she whispered. "I'm sorry. I shouldn't have—"

"Shh." He lifted her, cradling her against his chest. She was light. Almost impossibly light, as if her bones were hollow. "Let's get you somewhere safe."

"The shop—"

"The shop is destroyed. I have an apartment upstairs. We'll go there."

He carried her through the back, up the narrow stairs, into his small one-bedroom apartment. The space was cluttered with books and drying herbs and half-finished tea. He laid her on his bed, grabbed his first aid kit, and began cleaning her wounds.

"You saved my life," she said.

"I made him leave. That's not the same thing."

"It is to me." She caught his hand. "Xu Ming. I need to tell you something. About the others. The ones Fahai mentioned."

"You don't have to—"

"Yes. I do." Her eyes were human again, dark and tired. "He was right about some of it. I did take too much from them. I was young, foolish, desperate. I thought love was something you could consume, like food, like air. I didn't understand that it was supposed to be given, not taken."

She closed her eyes.

"But I learned. I changed. I spent three hundred years alone, meditating on what I had done. And when I finally emerged, I swore I would never harm another mortal. I would rather die."

"Then why did you come to me?"

"Because I was lonely." Her voice broke. "Because a thousand years is a very long time to be alone. And because when I saw you in that shop, measuring herbs with such careful hands, I thought—" She laughed weakly. "I thought maybe this time could be different."

Xu Ming sat beside her, holding her hand.

Outside, the rain continued to fall. Somewhere in the city, Fahai was waiting.

And in exactly twenty-four hours, he would return.

"Rest," Xu Ming said. "We'll figure this out."

She nodded, already fading into sleep.

Xu Ming watched her breathe.

And for the first time, he noticed something strange.

On her forehead, just above her brows, a faint golden mark was beginning to glow.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Weight of Scales

The rain had stopped, but the water still dripped from the broken doorframe, each drop striking the floor like a countdown. Fahai stood in the gap, his golden eyes fixed on Bai Suyin with the patience of a hunter who had tracked his prey across centuries.

"You always choose the interesting ones," Fahai said, stepping over the splintered wood. His robes brushed the floor without making a sound. "The last time, it was a scholar. Before that, a soldier. Now a pharmacist. You have a pattern, Bai Suyin. You always seek the gentle ones."

Bai Suyin moved between Xu Ming and the monk, her body tensed like a spring. "He has nothing to do with this."

"He has everything to do with this." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he rotated them. "He is your anchor. Your mortal shield. Kill him, and you lose your grounding. Your cultivation falters. Your heart trial fails." He smiled. "I have read the texts."

Xu Ming's hand found the counter behind him, searching for something—anything—that could serve as a weapon. His fingers brushed against a mortar and pestle. Worthless. A jar of dried ginseng. Useless.

"You won't touch him," Bai Suyin said. Her voice was flat, but Xu Ming caught the tremor beneath it. Not fear. Something else. Rage, held in check by a thin chain.

"I don't need to touch him." Fahai reached into his robe and withdrew a small bronze mirror, its surface clouded with age. "I only need him to see."

He held the mirror up, angled it toward Xu Ming.

The world lurched.

Xu Ming was no longer in the shop. He stood on a mountainside, smoke curling from burning trees, ash falling like snow. Below him, a village burned. Above him, something massive moved through the clouds—a shape of scales and fury, its body winding through the sky like a river of moonlight.

The white serpent.

*She* was beautiful. Terrifying. Her eyes were the color of jade, and they swept across the burning village without emotion, without pity. She was a force of nature, and the people below were ants.

"Watch," Fahai's voice whispered. "Watch what she truly is."

The vision shifted. Xu Ming stood in a temple courtyard, swords clashing, robes flying. A younger Fahai—his eyes still human, his face unlined—fought a woman who moved like water, her body flowing around his strikes. Bai Suyin. But different. Harder. Her face was carved from ice, and when she spoke, her voice was not the gentle tone he knew.

"You cannot cage me, monk," she hissed. "I have lived a thousand years. I have loved and lost and killed. What is your lifetime to me?"

"You killed my master," Fahai said, blood running from a cut on his forehead.

"Your master was a fool who thought he could bind the heavens."

The vision shattered.

Xu Ming gasped, stumbling backward, his hand clutching his chest. His heart hammered. His vision swam. When he looked at Bai Suyin, she was watching him with something close to fear.

"Those were half-truths," she said. "Twisted by his mirror."

"The Mirror of Reflection shows only truth," Fahai said. "Your pharmacist saw you for what you are. A killer. A monster wearing a woman's skin."

"He saw me in war," Bai Suyin snapped. "Show him the rest. Show him why I fought. Show him the monastery that was using children for blood rituals. Show him the abbot who was eating human hearts to extend his life." She turned to Xu Ming, desperation cracking her voice. "I did not kill innocent people. I killed monsters who wore holy robes."

Fahai's smile did not waver. "Every killer believes they killed monsters."

"Put down the mirror," Xu Ming said.

Both of them turned to look at him. Fahai's expression flickered with surprise—he had clearly expected more arguing, more revelation, more drama. Bai Suyin looked at him with something like hope.

"I said put it down." Xu Ming's voice was steady. His hands were still shaking, but his voice was not. "I don't know what I saw. I don't know what's true. But I know she came to me for herbs, not violence. I know she held my grandmother's qin like it was sacred. And I know you broke my door."

Fahai's smile vanished. "You are a fool."

"Maybe. But I'm a fool who knows that someone who breaks down doors usually has something to hide." Xu Ming stepped forward, past Bai Suyin, until he stood between her and the monk. "If she's a demon, let her be judged by heaven. Not by a man with a magic mirror and a grudge."

The shop went silent.

Bai Suyin's hand found his, squeezed once. Her fingers were cold.

Fahai looked at them—at the pharmacist standing in front of a snake spirit, at their joined hands—and something shifted in his expression. Not anger. Not triumph. Something closer to... pity?

"You have already fallen," he said softly. "You just don't know it yet."

He raised his hand.

The prayer beads flew.

They wrapped around Xu Ming's throat before he could blink, tightening, burning, searing into his skin like hot iron. He choked, clawed at them, but they were impossibly tight—each bead branded him with symbols he couldn't read, couldn't understand.

"Xu Ming!" Bai Suyin's voice was distant, muffled by the roaring in his ears.

She lunged at Fahai, but the monk was already moving, his hand pressed against her forehead, pinning her in place with a single touch. Her body went rigid. Her eyes widened.

"You forget," Fahai said, "that I have been hunting your kind for eight hundred years. I know your weaknesses. I know the meridians you use to hide your true form." He pressed harder, and Bai Suyin's face twisted in pain. "And I know that if I seal your cultivation, you will be nothing more than a woman. A mortal woman. Barely alive."

"No," she whispered.

"You chose this life. Now you will live it without power." Fahai's fingers traced symbols on her forehead, each one leaving a faint golden glow. "Until you learn what it means to be human. Truly human. Not a spirit playing dress-up."

Xu Ming's vision was darkening. The beads were tightening, compressing, grinding against his windpipe. He couldn't breathe. He couldn't think. He could only hear Bai Suyin's ragged breathing, Fahai's calm chanting, and the distant sound of rain starting again.

Then—nothing.

---

He woke on the floor of his shop, gasping.

The beads were gone. Fahai was gone. Bai Suyin sat beside him, her face pale, her eyes empty. When she saw him awake, she smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes.

"He's gone," she said. "For now."

"What happened?" Xu Ming's voice was hoarse. He touched his throat—the skin was smooth, unmarked, but he could still feel the phantom pressure of the beads.

"He sealed me." She held up her hands, flexed her fingers. "I can feel it. The energy is still there, but I can't reach it. Like trying to grab smoke through glass." She laughed, a hollow sound. "A thousand years of cultivation, and a monk with a charm can undo it in three minutes."

"He said you would be mortal."

"He said I would be nothing." She looked at him, and for the first time, Xu Ming saw her truly afraid. "Without my cultivation, I am vulnerable. Fahai can find me. Anyone can find me. A snake spirit without power is just... meat."

Xu Ming sat up, his head spinning. "Then we leave."

"Where would we go?"

"Anywhere. My grandmother's village. The mountains. Somewhere he can't find us."

"He will always find me." She touched his face, her fingers tracing the line of his jaw. "Unless I am not a snake spirit anymore."

"What does that mean?"

Her eyes met his. "The heart trial. It is not just about love. It is about transformation. If I complete it—if I love you truly, and you love me truly—I will become human. Fully human. No scales, no cultivation, no power. Just a woman." She paused. "And Fahai will have no reason to hunt me."

"Because you won't be a spirit anymore."

"Because I will be beneath his notice. A mortal. A ghost of no consequence." She smiled again, and this time, there was something fragile in it. "I will lose everything I have worked for. My power. My memories. My connection to the heavens. I will become small and weak and mortal."

"But alive."

"But alive."

Xu Ming took her hand, felt her cold fingers, her too-slow pulse. "Then we complete the trial."

"You don't understand." She pulled away, stood up, walked to the shattered door. The rain was falling again, cold and steady. "The trial is not a ceremony. It is not a confession. It is a burning. I will have to give up everything I am. Every power, every memory, every piece of my spirit that made me *her*. And in exchange..." She turned to face him. "I will become someone new. Someone who loves you because she was born to love you, not because she was forced to by a trial."

"I don't understand the difference."

"Neither do I." Her voice cracked. "And that is what terrifies me."

The shop's bell rang.

Both of them froze.

A figure stepped through the broken door, umbrella dripping, footsteps light on the wet floor. She was young—maybe twenty—with dark hair pulled back in a practical bun and glasses that reflected the dim light. She wore a police uniform.

"Xu Ming?" She looked at the broken door, the scattered herbs, the woman in the wet dress. "What happened here?"

"Officer Lin." Xu Ming's heart was racing. "There was—an accident. A break-in. The man who did it ran."

The officer's eyes narrowed. She looked at Bai Suyin, and something flickered in her gaze—recognition? Suspicion? "And you are?"

"Bai Suyin. A customer."

"A customer." Officer Lin stepped closer, her hand resting on her belt. "At this hour? In this rain?"

"I needed medicine," Bai Suyin said smoothly. "I have a cough."

The officer's smile was thin. "I see." She turned to Xu Ming. "I'll need a statement. And you'll need to secure this door before morning. Hangzhou has a lot of rats."

She handed him a card, her fingers brushing his. When she pulled away, Xu Ming noticed something on the back of the card—a symbol burned into the paper, small and intricate.

A lotus.

"That's my number," Officer Lin said. "Call if you remember anything else."

She walked out, umbrella snapping open, and disappeared into the rain.

Xu Ming stared at the card, the lotus symbol burning in his palm.

"That was no police officer," Bai Suyin said, her voice tight.

"Then who was she?"

Bai Suyin took the card, examined the symbol, and her face went white.

"She is Fahai's student. His first disciple." She looked up, eyes wide. "She was not here to investigate. She was here to mark us."

"Mark us?"

Bai Suyin grabbed his wrist, her grip stronger than

title: The Chain of a Thousand Years
content:
The golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming with the weight of centuries.

Fahai stepped fully into the shop, his wet robes leaving dark trails across the floor. His prayer beads clicked with each step—bone, Xu Ming realized. Carved from something that had once been alive.

"You have been deceived," Fahai said. His voice was gentle, almost kind. "The snake is skilled at wearing pretty masks. She has had a thousand years to practice."

Bai Suyin placed herself between them. Her body trembled, but her voice was steel. "He knows what I am."

"Does he?" Fahai tilted his head. "Does he know about the village in Jiangxi? The sixty-three souls you consumed in a single night?"

"That was self-defense."

"The magistrate's daughter? She was six years old."

Bai Suyin's breath caught. "I did not—"

"You did." Fahai's voice remained calm, almost pitying. "You were young then. Barely three hundred years of cultivation. Your hunger ruled you. I understand. But understanding does not bring back the dead."

Xu Ming's hands had stopped shaking. They were still now, frozen at his sides.

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "Don't listen to him. He twists everything. He—"

"How did your teacher die, Bai Suyin?"

She went silent.

"Tell him." Fahai stepped closer. The prayer beads clicked. "Tell the pharmacist how your master was burned alive. How you watched. How you did nothing."

"I was chained," she whispered. "He chained me in a cave with suppression talismans. I could hear her screaming. I could smell her flesh burning. But I could not move."

"Because you were weak." Fahai's smile did not waver. "And you have been weak ever since. Running from life to life. Playing at humanity. But a snake is a snake, no matter how many skins it sheds."

Xu Ming found his voice. "Why are you here?"

Fahai's gaze shifted to him. The golden eyes were ancient, carrying a weight that made Xu Ming's knees want to buckle. "Because she has finally done something worth my attention. She has found a mortal willing to love her. This is the final step. If she completes it, she becomes human—truly human, beyond my reach, beyond the laws that bind her kind."

"And you want to stop her."

"I want to save you." Fahai reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "This is a record of every mortal who has been caught in her web. Seven men. Four women. All dead within a year of meeting her. The last one threw himself from a bridge. He left a note: 'The snake's kiss is sweet, but the venom never leaves.'"

Bai Suyin made a sound like an animal wounded.

"That is not—" Her voice broke. "They were not—"

"Were they not?" Fahai unrolled the scroll. Names. Dates. Manner of death. "Zhang Wei, 1127. Drowned. Liu Mei, 1143. Fell from a height. Chen Bao, 1206. Heart failure. Shall I continue?"

"Stop." Xu Ming's voice was barely a whisper.

"Or you could ask her yourself." Fahai rolled the scroll, tucked it away. "Ask her why she failed the heart trial seven times before. Ask her what happened to those who tried to love her."

Xu Ming turned to Bai Suyin.

Her face was wet. Not from rain.

"Every time," she said, her voice raw. "Every time I failed. Because I could not love them enough to save them. Because Fahai found me before the trial was complete. Because they saw what I was and ran."

"Is that what happened?"

"No." Fahai's voice cut through. "They saw what she was and stayed. And she consumed them anyway. Not out of malice. Out of instinct. A snake does not choose to eat the mouse. It simply does what it is."

"Get out." Xu Ming's voice surprised him. It was steady. Firm.

Fahai raised an eyebrow. "Excuse me?"

"Get out of my shop." Xu Ming walked past Bai Suyin, stood between her and the abbot. "You have no right to bring your war into my home. Whatever happened a thousand years ago, it has nothing to do with me."

"Everything to do with you." Fahai did not move. "She has marked you. Her scent is on your skin. Her power is in your blood. Even if I leave, I will find you again. And when I do, I will ask you one more time. But the next time I ask, I will not be kind."

"Then I will answer the same way."

For a long moment, Fahai studied him. Then he laughed—a sound like stones grinding together.

"You would make a fine monk, pharmacist. Pity you chose the snake." He turned toward the shattered door. "I will return in three days. By then, you will have seen the truth. And you will beg me to save you."

He stepped into the rain.

And vanished.

Not walked away. Vanished. One moment he was there, the next the rain fell through empty air.

Xu Ming stood in the silence, heart hammering.

"He let us live." Bai Suyin's voice was hollow. "That means he has already won."

"What are you talking about?"

"He would never leave if he thought he could win tonight. He left because he knows something I don't. Because he has already set his trap." She grabbed his arm, her fingers cold. "Xu Ming, you need to leave. Tonight. Go somewhere far away. Forget me."

"Don't."

"I am serious." Her eyes were wild. "Every word he said was true. I have failed seven times. Seven mortals died because they loved me. I watched them die. I held their bodies. I buried them with my own hands."

"And yet you came here."

"Because I am selfish. Because I have been alone for a thousand years and I cannot bear another century of silence." Her voice broke. "But I will not add you to my list of graves."

Xu Ming looked at her. The rain dripped from her hair. Her dress was torn. She looked nothing like the elegant woman who had walked into his shop days ago. She looked like someone who had been running for centuries and had finally run out of road.

"Why me?" he asked.

"What?"

"Seven mortals. Seven failures. Why try again? Why me?"

Bai Suyin's lips parted. For a moment, she looked like she might lie. Then she closed her eyes.

"Because you remind me of her."

"Your teacher?"

"My teacher." Her voice was barely audible. "She was mortal. A pharmacist in a small village. She found me when I was wounded, nursed me back to health, never asked what I was. She taught me to read. To write. To mix herbs. She taught me that humans could be kind without expecting anything in return."

"What happened to her?"

"Fahai burned her alive." Bai Suyin opened her eyes. "He chained me in a cave so I would have to listen to her scream. And when it was over, he told me that this was the price of loving mortals. That I would always bring death to those I cared for."

"He was wrong."

"Was he?" She gestured at the scroll on the floor, where Fahai had dropped it. "Seven names. Seven deaths. Seven times I tried to build a life and seven times he tore it down. And he is right about one thing. The last one—Chen Bao—he jumped. Because he saw my true form and could not live with what he had loved."

Xu Ming picked up the scroll.

It was heavy. The names were written in elegant calligraphy, each entry detailing a life cut short. He read them all. When he finished, he looked up.

"These people died because of Fahai. Not because of you."

"What?"

"Read it again." He held out the scroll. "Zhang Wei was drowned. The entry says he was pulled into a river by an 'unseen force.' Liu Mei fell, but the entry says she was 'pushed.' Chen Bao jumped, but the entry before it says he was 'shown the truth' by a monk three days prior."

Bai Suyin's face went pale.

"He made them die." Xu Ming's voice was shaking, but not from fear. "He killed them. And he made you think it was your fault."

The shop was silent except for the rain.

"He has been doing this for a thousand years," Xu Ming continued. "Not hunting demons. Creating them. He needs you to be a monster so he can be a hero. If you ever became human, his purpose would be gone."

Bai Suyin stared at him.

"You read that in three minutes."

"I'm a pharmacist. I'm trained to notice details." He stepped closer. "Bai Suyin. You are not a monster. You are a woman who has been tortured by a madman for centuries. And I am not going to run."

"You should."

"I know." He reached out, touched her face. Her skin was cold. "But I won't."

Her hand came up, covered his. Her eyes were wet again, but this time she was smiling.

"Xu Ming. You are either the bravest man I have ever met or the most foolish."

"Probably both."

She laughed—a real laugh, surprised out of her. It transformed her face. Made her look young, human, alive.

"I have an idea," she said. "A way to break his hold. But it will be dangerous."

"More dangerous than a thousand-year-old snake spirit and a monk with golden eyes?"

"Much more."

"Tell me."

She took a deep breath.

"I need to show you my true form. Not a fraction. All of it. And you need to see it and not run."

"That's it?"

"That's it." Her eyes were serious. "Fahai's power comes from fear. He makes mortals see the monster so they reject the spirit. If you see me—truly see me—and stay, his hold on this trial is broken. I become human."

"And if I run?"

"Then I fail. Again." She looked down. "And this time, I will not try again. I will let him kill me."

"Don't say that."

"It is the truth." She met his eyes. "I am tired, Xu Ming. A thousand years is too long to be alone. If you run, I will not chase you. I will let him end it."

Xu Ming thought of his small shop. His quiet life. His predictable days.

Then he thought of her standing in the rain, shaking, asking him to see her.

"Do it," he said.

"Here?"

"Here. Now. Before I lose my nerve."

Bai Suyin stepped back. Her eyes began to glow—not the faint shimmer from before, but a deep, burning white.

"Remember," she said. "You asked for this."

The lights went out.

Not the electricity. The light itself. The room plunged into darkness so complete that Xu Ming could not see his own hands.

Then the pressure began.

It pressed against his skull. His chest. His lungs. The air grew thick, heavy, impossible to breathe. Somewhere in the dark, something moved. Something vast. Something that had no business existing in a world of walls and ceilings and small pharmacies.

Xu Ming fell to his knees.

"Do not look away," her voice came from everywhere. Not from in front of him. From above. From below. From inside his own mind. "Do not close your

title: The Price of a Thousand Years
content:
The golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming with the weight of centuries. Behind him, Bai Suyin had gone completely still—not the stillness of fear, but of a predator calculating its next move.

"Officer Chen," Fahai said without turning around, "you may enter now."

The front door creaked open. Officer Chen stepped inside, his uniform soaked, his face unreadable. He held a manila envelope in his hands.

"Sergeant Xu," Chen said, his voice carefully neutral. "I'm going to need you to step away from the woman."

Xu Ming didn't move. "You know her?"

"I know *of* her." Chen's eyes flicked to Bai Suyin, then back to Xu Ming. "There's a file on her. Thick as my fist. Goes back decades." He tapped the envelope. "The department keeps it locked in a safe that requires two keys and a retinal scan to open."

"What does it say?"

"It says she doesn't age. It says she's been sighted in every major city in China over the past sixty years, always under a different name, always leaving behind a trail of dead men."

"That's not true," Xu Ming said.

"Three of them were monks," Chen continued, his voice flat. "Two were Taoist priests. The last one was a professor of folklore studies at Peking University who got too close to something he shouldn't have."

Bai Suyin's voice cut through the tension like a blade. "They were Fahai's agents. Every single one of them. They found me, and they tried to kill me. I defended myself."

"Defended yourself by burning them from the inside out?" Chen's hand moved to his holster. "I've seen the autopsy photos. Whatever you are, you're not human."

"Neither is he." Bai Suyin pointed at Fahai. "Ask him what he really is. Ask him about the gold in his eyes. Ask him about the children he's taken from temples across China, children with gifts, children he trains to hunt things like me."

Fahai smiled. It was not a kind smile. "I train them to protect humanity from predators who wear human skin."

"Children with gifts," Xu Ming repeated. The words hit him like a physical blow. "You mentioned a teacher she killed. Was he one of those children?"

The shop went silent. Rain hammered against the roof. Somewhere in the distance, thunder rolled across the sky.

Fahai's smile vanished. For the first time, something flickered in those golden eyes. Something ancient and wounded.

"My teacher was a man named Xuanzang," Fahai said. "He found me when I was six years old, abandoned in a ditch, dying of a fever that should have killed me. He carried me to his temple. He fed me. He clothed me. He taught me to read and write and meditate."

"He also taught you to kill," Bai Suyin said softly.

"He taught me to recognize evil when I saw it." Fahai's voice rose. "He taught me the old ways, the true ways, the methods that had been passed down from master to disciple for two thousand years. And then you came to our temple, pretending to be a pilgrim seeking wisdom, and you murdered him in his sleep."

"I did not murder him."

"You drained his qi. You left him a husk. I found him the next morning with his eyes open and his mouth frozen in a scream that never came out."

"That was not me." Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "That was my sister. Qing. And she killed him because he was about to bind us both in golden chains and bury us under the mountain for eternity."

"He was protecting the realm from your kind!"

"He was torturing spirits for sport!"

The shop trembled. Bottles rattled on their shelves. Xu Ming felt the temperature drop ten degrees in a single breath.

"Enough," Chen said, drawing his gun. "Everyone needs to calm down."

Fahai turned to him. "That weapon will not harm me, officer."

"It's not for you." Chen leveled the gun at Bai Suyin. "I've seen what happens when your kind fights. Civilians die. Buildings collapse. I'm not letting that happen in my city."

Bai Suyin laughed. It was a hollow, broken sound. "You think your bullet can hurt me? I have survived lightning strikes that would vaporize steel. I have been crushed by mountains, drowned in rivers, burned in fires that consumed forests. Your little gun—"

"Is loaded with silver blessed by a living Buddha."

The laughter stopped.

Xu Ming watched the color drain from Bai Suyin's face. For the first time since he'd met her, she looked genuinely afraid.

"Officer Chen," she said slowly, "where did you get those bullets?"

"Department issue. Special circumstances division." Chen's hand was steady. "We've been watching you for three years, Bai Suyin. We know about the trial. We know about the heart bond. We know that if you complete it, you become fully human, and we can't touch you anymore."

"That's right," she said. "I will become human. And then all of this—the running, the hiding, the centuries of fear—it will be over."

"If you complete it." Chen's finger tightened on the trigger. "But you haven't completed it, have you? Because the heart bond requires genuine love, and you've never managed to find a man who could love you back. Not truly. Not enough."

"He loves me." Bai Suyin's voice was barely a whisper. She turned to Xu Ming, her eyes pleading. "Tell him. Tell him you love me."

Xu Ming opened his mouth. The words were there. He could feel them, sitting on his tongue like stones. But they wouldn't come out.

Because he didn't know if they were true.

He barely knew her. He'd known her for three days. Three days of impossible revelations, of ancient hatreds, of a war that had been raging for a thousand years. How could he say he loved her? How could anyone love someone they'd just met, no matter how beautiful, no matter how tragic her story?

The silence stretched.

Bai Suyin's face crumbled.

"I see," she said. "I see."

Fahai stepped forward. "The bond is incomplete. She is vulnerable. Officer Chen, if you would be so kind as to—"

"No." Xu Ming moved between them. "You're not going to shoot her."

"Sergeant Xu," Chen said warningly, "get out of the way."

"I said no." His hands were shaking, but his voice was steady. "This is my shop. This is my city. And I'm not going to let you execute someone in front of me, no matter what she is."

"She's a snake spirit who has killed dozens of people."

"She killed people who were trying to kill her. There's a difference."

"Is there?" Fahai's voice was silk over steel. "Tell me, pharmacist. If she completes her trial, if she becomes human, do you think the killing stops? Do you think she simply walks away from eight centuries of blood and violence and starts a new life as a normal woman?"

"Yes," Bai Suyin said. "That's exactly what happens. The trial purifies me. The love cleanses me. I become what I was never allowed to be."

"And the power?" Fahai asked. "Your cultivation, your abilities, your connection to the natural world? What happens to all of that?"

"It fades. Slowly. Over time. I become mortal."

"Liar." Fahai spat the word. "I have studied the old texts. I have consulted with masters across Tibet and Japan and India. The trial does not strip you of power. It anchors you to mortality, yes, but it also gives you something else. Immortality. True immortality. The kind that cannot be killed because it is bound to the soul of a human who loves you."

"That's not—"

"Don't lie to me, serpent. I know what you're trying to become. Not human. Something better. Something that can never be destroyed."

The room went cold again. Colder this time. Frost formed on the windows.

Bai Suyin's eyes had changed. They were no longer brown. They were gold. The same gold as Fahai's.

"You want the truth?" she said, and her voice had changed too. It was deeper now, older, filled with the weight of centuries. "Fine. Here is the truth."

She turned to Xu Ming. "He's right. The trial will not make me fully human. It will make me something that has never existed before. A spirit with a mortal heart. Immortal in body, mortal in soul. Powerful enough to challenge the heavens themselves, but vulnerable enough to love."

"You lied to me," Xu Ming said.

"I omitted." Her eyes softened. "I did not lie. Everything I told you was true. I need your love to complete the trial. I need your protection to survive Fahai. But I also need you to understand what you're agreeing to."

"And what am I agreeing to?"

"Eternity." She reached out and touched his face. Her fingers were cold. "If you love me, truly, your soul becomes bound to mine. When you die, you will not pass on. You will remain with me, a ghost tethered to my heart, until I find a way to bring you back. And I will find a way. I have a thousand years of knowledge. I will find a way."

Xu Ming's breath caught in his throat. "You're asking me to give up my afterlife."

"I'm asking you to give up everything." Her tears were freezing on her cheeks. "I'm asking you to love me so completely that death itself cannot separate us. I'm asking you to become part of a war that has been raging since before your ancestors built the Great Wall. I'm asking you to sacrifice your peace, your safety, your very soul, for a woman you met three days ago."

She let her hand drop.

"So now you know. Now you can make your choice with open eyes."

The shop was silent. Rain dripped through the broken door. Fahai watched with cold satisfaction. Chen watched with his finger on the trigger.

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. At the thousand-year-old snake spirit who had walked into his shop looking for herbs that hadn't been used in a millennium. At the woman who had shown him the moon and the mountains and the hidden world that existed just beneath the surface of reality.

He thought about his small life. His quiet days. His predictable nights.

And then he thought about her. About the way she laughed. About the way she smelled like rain and jasmine. About the way she made him feel like he was standing on the edge of something vast and terrifying and beautiful.

"I need time," he said again.

"We don't have time." This time, it was Fahai who spoke. "The rain is stopping. The clouds are clearing. In exactly seven minutes, the moon will break through, and I will perform the binding ritual that will seal her power forever."

"Binding ritual?" Bai Suyin's head snapped around. "You wouldn't dare."

"I have dared for eight centuries." Fahai reached into his robes and pulled out a chain. At the end of it hung a golden bell, ancient and tarnished. "This bell was forged from the metal of a thousand captured spirits. When I ring it, it will call to every soul she has ever consumed, every life she has ever taken, and they will rise up and drag her back to the underworld where she belongs."

"You'll destroy everything within a mile radius!"

"Small price to pay."

Chen lowered his gun. "Wait. Civilians—"

"Will be

title: The Curse of the Jade Pendant
content:
The golden light from Fahai's eyes faded, plunging the shop back into the dim glow of a single overhead bulb. Xu Ming's heart hammered against his ribs as he stared at the broken door, at the man who had stepped through it, at the prayer beads that clicked with a sound like bones breaking.

Bai Suyin moved faster than Xu Ming's eyes could track. One moment she was beside him, the next she stood between him and Fahai, her body tense, her fingers curled into claws.

"You will not touch him," she said. Her voice was different now. Deeper. Older. It carried echoes of wind through mountain caves, of rivers flowing underground.

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "I have no interest in the pharmacist. He is merely a distraction. A mortal crutch you lean on because you are too weak to face me alone."

"I am not weak."

"You are terrified." Fahai stepped forward. His robes whispered against the floor. "I can smell it on you. The same fear you carried when I burned your mountain. The same fear you carried when I killed your teacher. The same fear that has followed you across eight centuries like a shadow."

Bai Suyin's hands trembled. Xu Ming saw it. He saw the cracks in her composure, the way her human mask flickered at the edges.

"Get out," she said.

"Make me."

The air changed. Pressure built in Xu Ming's ears. The bottles on his shelves began to vibrate, glass chiming against glass. Bai Suyin's shadow stretched across the floor, growing, twisting, taking shapes that had no place in the human world.

Then she stopped.

Her eyes went wide. Her hand flew to her chest.

"What's wrong?" Xu Ming asked.

She didn't answer. She was staring at Fahai's hand, at the prayer beads wrapped around his fingers. They were glowing. Not with golden light, but with something darker. Something that pulsed like a second heartbeat.

"Do you recognize them?" Fahai asked softly. "They belonged to your teacher. I carved them from her spine after she died."

Bai Suyin made a sound. It was not human. It was the cry of something wounded, something that had been carrying a wound for so long it had forgotten what it felt like to be whole.

Xu Ming moved without thinking. He stepped around Bai Suyin, put himself between her and Fahai, and said the stupidest words he had ever spoken.

"Leave her alone."

Fahai's golden eyes fixed on him. The pressure in the room doubled. Xu Ming's knees buckled, but he didn't fall. He forced himself to stay standing.

"You are brave," Fahai said. "Or foolish. I have not decided which."

"Does it matter?"

"It does when you are about to die."

Bai Suyin grabbed Xu Ming's arm and pulled him backward. Her grip was iron. Her eyes were wild. "Don't. He will kill you without a second thought."

"Then we fight together."

"We cannot fight him here. Not in this form. Not with these walls around us." She looked at the ceiling, at the narrow space of his shop, at the broken door that led to the alley. "I need room. I need sky."

"Then we run."

Fahai laughed again. "Run. Yes, that is what snakes do. Slither away into the dark. It is what you have always done."

Bai Suyin's jaw tightened. For a moment, Xu Ming thought she would attack. But she didn't. She pulled him toward the back of the shop, toward the storage room, toward the emergency exit he had never used.

"Go," she said.

They ran.

The alley was wet with rain. The street beyond was empty, the city asleep, the streetlights casting pools of yellow light on the asphalt. Bai Suyin moved like water, flowing around corners, dragging Xu Ming behind her.

"Where are we going?" he gasped.

"The lake."

"The lake is miles away."

"Then we need a car."

Xu Ming fumbled for his keys. His truck was parked three blocks away, an old Ford that had belonged to his father. They reached it just as Fahai appeared at the end of the street, walking with the unhurried confidence of a man who knew he would catch his prey.

"Get in," Xu Ming said.

The engine turned over on the third try. He floored the accelerator. The truck lurched forward, tires screeching, and they sped away into the night.

For a long moment, neither of them spoke. Xu Ming's hands were shaking on the wheel. Bai Suyin was watching the rearview mirror, her face pale, her breath shallow.

"He let us go," she said finally.

"What?"

"He let us go. He could have stopped us. He chose not to."

"Why would he do that?"

"Because he wants me afraid. He wants me to run. He has been hunting me for so long that the chase has become the point." She closed her eyes. "He could have killed me a hundred times. A thousand. But he never does. He lets me escape. He lets me rebuild my life. And then he takes it all away again."

"That's sick."

"It is his religion. He believes that suffering purifies. That by making me suffer, he is saving my soul."

Xu Ming gripped the wheel tighter. "Where does he get that right? Who gave him the authority to judge you?"

"He gave it to himself. That is what zealots do."

They drove in silence. The streets of Hang scrolled past, wet and empty and beautiful. Xu Ming had lived in this city his whole life. He had thought he knew it. But now it felt alien, like a stage set that had been rearranged while he wasn't looking.

"Pull over," Bai Suyin said.

They were on a side road near the lake, lined with willow trees that drooped their branches into the water. Xu Ming stopped the truck. Bai Suyin got out and walked to the edge of the lake, her reflection rippling on the surface.

Xu Ming followed her. "What are we doing?"

"I need to show you something."

She turned to face him. The rain had stopped. The clouds were breaking apart, revealing slivers of moonlight that fell across her face like silver scars.

"When I told you about my heart trial, I did not tell you everything."

"I figured."

"There is a cost. To falling in love with me." She reached into her dress and pulled out a pendant. It was made of jade, carved into the shape of a coiled snake, with eyes that gleamed red in the moonlight. "This was given to me by my teacher before she died. It carries a curse."

"A curse?"

"Whoever loves me will die. It is not my curse to control. It is a price I pay for my cultivation. Every mortal who has ever loved me has died within a year of confessing their feelings."

Xu Ming's blood went cold. "Every one?"

"Every one. I have loved eight men across eight lifetimes. Each time, I thought it would be different. Each time, I was wrong." Her voice cracked. "They died in accidents. In wars. In illnesses that came from nowhere. One of them was struck by lightning on a clear day. The universe finds a way to collect its debt."

"Then why are you telling me this now?"

"Because Fahai is not the only danger. I am the danger. The curse I carry will destroy you just as surely as his golden eyes."

Xu Ming stared at her. The woman who had walked into his shop three weeks ago, asking for herbs that hadn't been used in a thousand years. The woman who had made him feel alive for the first time in years. The woman who had stolen his heart without asking permission.

"I don't care," he said.

"You should."

"I don't."

Bai Suyin stepped closer. Her hand touched his cheek. Her eyes were wet. "Xu Ming. Please. I cannot bear to lose another one."

"Then don't lose me. Fight the curse. Fight Fahai. Fight whatever comes." He took her hand. "I am not going anywhere."

"You do not understand. The curse is not something I can fight. It is woven into the fabric of my existence. Every step I take toward becoming human is a step that endangers the people I love."

"Then stop becoming human."

She blinked. "What?"

"Stop the cultivation. Stop the heart trial. If becoming human means losing everyone you love, then stay as you are. Stay a snake. Stay a spirit. Stay whatever you want to be. Just stay."

"That is not how it works. Once the trial begins, it cannot be stopped. I must complete it or I will lose everything I have gained. My power. My form. My mind."

"Then we find another way."

"There is no another way."
