"There is always another way." Xu Ming's voice was steady. Stronger than he felt. "I am a pharmacist. I solve problems. This is just a problem."

Bai Suyin laughed. It was a broken sound, half joy, half despair. "You are impossible."

"I have been told that before."

She kissed him. It was soft at first, then desperate, her hands in his hair, her body pressed against his. The world narrowed to the taste of her lips, the smell of her skin, the warmth of her breath.

When she pulled back, her eyes were human again. Vulnerable. Afraid.

"I do not want to lose you," she whispered.

"Then don't."

The pendant around her neck pulsed once, twice. A crack appeared in the jade, thin as a hair, dark as blood.

Bai Suyin looked down. Her face went pale.

"What is it?" Xu Ming asked.

"The curse," she said. "It has awakened."

Before he could ask what that meant, the ground shook. The lake surface rippled. The willow trees swayed without wind.

And from the darkness behind them, a voice emerged.

"Finally."

Xu Ming turned. Fahai stood at the edge of the trees, his golden eyes fixed on Bai Suyin, his prayer beads glowing like embers in the dark.

"I have been waiting eight hundred years for this moment."

"For what?" Bai Suyin asked.

"For you to bind yourself to a mortal. For you to give the curse a target." Fahai smiled. "Do you know why I never killed you? Why I always let you escape?"

"Tell me."

"Because I was waiting for you to fall in love. The curse activates on love. And when it activates, it becomes vulnerable."

"Vulnerable to what?"

Fahai raised his hand. The prayer beads uncoiled like a living thing, stretching, growing, wrapping around his arm in spirals of bone and sinew.

"To destruction."

He lunged.

Bai Suyin pushed Xu Ming aside. Her form exploded outward, shedding skin and shape, becoming something vast and white and terrible. The snake rose above her, a thousand-year spirit made flesh, her scales gleaming like armor, her eyes burning like twin suns.

Fahai crashed into her. The impact sent shockwaves across the lake. Water erupted. Trees shattered. Xu Ming was thrown backward, his head cracking against a stone.

He lay there, dazed, blood running down his face, watching the battle through blurred vision.

Bai Suyin was losing.

Fahai moved like a demon, his prayer beads striking her scales with the force of hammers, cracking them, breaking them, drawing blood that steamed in the moonlight.

"You are weak," Fahai said. "You have always been weak. You cling to mortal love because you cannot stand alone."

"I am not weak." Bai Suyin's voice was thunder. "I am

title: The Blood on His Hands
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. He stepped fully into the shop, his robes dripping rainwater onto the shattered wood. Behind him, the alley was dark and empty. No sirens. No witnesses. The rain had swallowed everything.

"Close the door," he said.

Xu Ming didn't move. His hand was still pressed against Bai Suyin's chest, feeling her heart race like a trapped bird. The front door hung crooked on its hinges. The back door was kindling. The shop that had been his sanctuary for twelve years was now a ruin.

"The door," Fahai repeated.

Bai Suyin's voice came out cold. "You broke them both. If you want privacy, you should have knocked."

Fahai laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. It was the laugh of a man who had heard every possible defiance and crushed them all the same.

"Courage," he said, "is a quality I admire in demons. It makes the breaking more satisfying." He turned to Xu Ming. "You have a choice, pharmacist. You can step aside and live. Or you can protect her and die. The law is clear. Harboring a spirit is punishable by—"

"By what?" Xu Ming's voice surprised him. It was steady. "What law? Yours?"

Fahai's eyes narrowed. "The law of heaven. The law of earth. The law that separates man from monster." He gestured at Bai Suyin with his prayer beads. "She is not what she appears. Under that skin, she is a serpent. A creature of instinct and hunger. She has killed before. She will kill again."

"Liar." Bai Suyin's voice cracked. "I have never taken a human life. Not in a thousand years."

"No?" Fahai reached into his robe and pulled out a scroll. Yellowed paper, red seals, characters written in what looked like dried blood. He unrolled it slowly, deliberately, like a prosecutor presenting evidence. "Then explain this."

He held it up. Xu Ming saw names. Dozens of them, written in a tight column. Each one had a date beside it.

"These are the men who loved you," Fahai said. "Every mortal who passed your heart trial before this one. You used them. Drained their life force. Left them hollow shells." He tapped a name near the bottom. "This one threw himself off a bridge. This one drank poison. This one—"

"Stop." Bai Suyin's face was pale. "Those were not my fault. They failed the trial. The backlash—"

"The backlash killed them," Fahai finished. "You knew the cost. You accepted it. Every time." He turned to Xu Ming. "She will do the same to you. It is her nature. A snake cannot become a dragon. It can only writhe in the mud and drag others down with it."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. Her eyes were wet. Not from rain.

"Is it true?"

She didn't answer.

"Bai Suyin. Is it true?"

"I didn't kill them," she whispered. "But I knew what the trial could do. I knew the risks." She grabbed his hand. "I told you from the beginning. I told you this was dangerous. I never lied about that."

"You omitted."

"I protected you."

"You let me walk into something that could kill me."

"Yes." Her voice broke. "Because it was the only way. Because I am tired of being a monster. Because I want to be human more than I want to breathe." She pressed his palm harder against her chest. "Do you feel that? My heart. It beats like yours now. It didn't, before. I grew this heart for you. I grew these lungs for you. I am becoming something new, and you are the only one who can finish the transformation."

Fahai laughed again. "Beautiful lies. She has had a thousand years to perfect them."

"Shut up." Xu Ming turned on him. "You broke into my shop. You threatened me. You expect me to just hand her over because you have a scroll and a tragic story?"

"I expect you to be smart." Fahai's voice dropped. "I have killed a hundred spirits. I have exorcised a thousand. I have burned mountains, dried rivers, shattered temples. Do you think your little pharmacy can stop me?"

"No." Xu Ming stepped forward, placing himself between Fahai and Bai Suyin. "But my body can slow you down."

"That is suicide."

"Maybe." Xu Ming's hands were shaking, but his voice was steady. "But I've been reading her books. The old texts. And I know something you don't."

Fahai's eyes flickered with curiosity. "Oh?"

"A mortal who willingly gives his life force to a spirit creates a bond that cannot be broken by exorcism. It's called the Heart-Knot. It's in the fifth chapter of the *Treatise on Spirit Marriage*." Xu Ming pulled a folded paper from his pocket. "I signed it. Thirty minutes ago. While you were walking through the rain."

Bai Suyin's breath caught. "You didn't."

"I did." Xu Ming unfolded the paper. It was covered in characters he'd copied painstakingly from her book, followed by his name in English and Chinese. Below it was a red thumbprint. His blood. He'd pricked his finger with a needle from the shop. "My life force is already bonded to hers. If you kill her, you kill me. If you exorcise her, the backlash destroys my soul. I am collateral damage, Abbot. And I don't think your heaven allows you to kill innocents."

Fahai's face went still. For a long moment, the only sound was rain and the drip of water from the broken doorframe.

"You gambled," he said finally.

"You forced the game."

Fahai looked at Bai Suyin. Something passed between them. Old hatred. Old history. Xu Ming couldn't read it, but he felt the weight of it, centuries of violence compressed into a single glance.

"Clever," Fahai said. "But you misunderstand something, pharmacist." He stepped closer. Close enough that Xu Ming could smell him: incense and old blood, just like Bai Suyin had said. "I have killed innocents before. Heaven forgave me. Because the goal is worth the cost."

He reached out and touched Xu Ming's forehead with one finger.

The world went white.

Xu Ming saw everything.

He saw a mountain covered in snow. A woman with silver hair standing on a cliff. Below her, an army of monks, their robes red, their hands forming seals. Fire erupted from the mountain. The woman screamed. She turned into a snake so huge she wrapped around the peak three times. The monks did not stop. They chanted. The fire grew. The snake burned.

Then the vision shifted.

He saw a city, ancient and walled. A young man in scholar's robes walked through a garden. A woman in white waited by a pond. She smiled. He smiled. They embraced. The vision skipped. The man was old now, lying in a bed. The woman held his hand. He whispered something. She wept. When he stopped breathing, she kissed his forehead and walked into a lake. The water closed over her head.

Another shift.

A different city. Different clothes. A woman in white again, this time selling tea on a street corner. A soldier bought a cup. They talked. They laughed. They fell in love. The vision skipped. The soldier was bleeding on a battlefield. She found him too late. His eyes were open but empty. She screamed at the sky. The sky did not answer.

Life after life. Death after death. Xu Ming watched Bai Suyin love and lose across centuries. He watched her grow colder. He watched her try not to care. He watched her fail every time.

And then he watched her walk into his shop on a rainy afternoon, wearing a sundress, pretending to be a tourist, asking for herbs that hadn't been used in a thousand years.

The vision released him.

Xu Ming collapsed to his knees. His nose was bleeding. His head throbbed. Bai Suyin was there instantly, her hands on his face, her voice desperate.

"Xu Ming. Xu Ming, look at me."

He looked at her.

"I'm sorry," he whispered.

"For what?"

"For every life I couldn't save." He touched her cheek. "I saw them. I saw all of them."

Her face crumpled. For the first time since she'd walked into his shop, she looked like she might break.

"That was not for you to see," Fahai said. "That was a punishment. A taste of the misery she spreads wherever she goes."

Xu Ming stood up. His legs were weak. His vision swam. But he stood.

"You're wrong," he said. "That wasn't misery. That was love. A thousand years of it. She gave them everything she had, and she lost them every time. And she still came back. She still tried again." He turned to face Fahai fully. "That's not a monster. That's the bravest person I've ever met."

Fahai's expression didn't change, but something shifted in his eyes. Uncertainty. Crack.

"Then you are a fool," he said. "And fools die young."

He raised his hand. The prayer beads glowed. The air in the shop grew heavy, pressing down on Xu Ming's chest. Bai Suyin grabbed him and pulled him backward, her body shifting, scales rippling across her skin.

"Run," she said.

"Where?"

"Anywhere."

The beads burst into flame. Fahai spoke a word that cracked the windows. The glass shattered inward. Xu Ming threw himself over Bai Suyin, shielding her from the shards.

When he looked up, Fahai was gone.

The shop was empty. The rain had stopped. The only evidence of what had happened was the wreckage, the broken glass, and the faint smell of incense.

Bai Suyin was shaking beneath him.

"He let us go," she whispered.

"Why?"

"Because he wants me afraid. He wants me to run. He wants to hunt." She looked up at him, her eyes dark and ancient. "And because he knows something I didn't."

"What?"

She held up the folded paper. The Heart-Knot contract. It was burning at the edges, the characters smoking as they curled into ash.

"The bond only works if both parties are human," she said. "I am not yet. So the contract is void."

Xu Ming stared at the ash falling between her fingers.

"Then what do we do?"

Bai Suyin closed her eyes. When she opened them, they were gold.

"We finish the transformation. Tonight."

"How?"

She looked at him with an expression he couldn't read—hunger, desperation, and something that might have been love.

"The old way," she said. "The way I've never done before."

Her hand moved to his throat. Her claws were out.

"I have to consume you. Not kill you. But consume your essence entirely. If I take too much, you die. If I take too little, I stay trapped between forms." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "I have never risked this before. Because I was afraid of what I would become if I failed."

"Then why now?"

"Because Fahai will be back in three days. And next time, he won't give us a choice." She pressed her forehead against his. "Trust me, Xu Ming. One more time."

He thought about the visions. The centuries of loss. The woman who had loved and lost and loved again, never giving up, even when heaven itself stood against her.

"I trust you," he said.

She kissed

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: THE BURNING GARDEN

The hospital room smelled of antiseptic and fear.

Xu Ming sat in a plastic chair beside An Xin's bed, watching her chest rise and fall beneath thin white sheets. Her face was pale, her wrists wrapped in gauze where she'd clawed at herself during the seizure. The doctors said she'd be fine. Physically. But they couldn't explain what had caused her brain to spike like a lightning rod, couldn't understand why her pupils had turned vertical for three full minutes before the sedatives kicked in.

"I saw her," An Xin whispered, not opening her eyes.

Xu Ming leaned forward. "Who?"

"The woman in your shop. The one with the white dress." An Xin's fingers twitched. "She was standing in my head. Looking at me through my own eyes. She said... she said I was in her way."

Cold dread pooled in Xu Ming's stomach. He'd left Bai Suyin at the shop, hidden in the storage room behind the herb cabinets, wrapped in layers of salt and iron filings she'd insisted on. The Abbot Fahai had retreated after their confrontation, but his parting words echoed in Xu Ming's memory: *I will return when you are ready to see the truth.*

"You need to stay away from her," Xu Ming said.

"I need to stay away from *you*." An Xin opened her eyes. They were normal now—brown, tired, human—but there was something hard in them he'd never seen before. "Whatever you've gotten yourself into, Xu Ming, it's not safe. She's not safe."

"She's not dangerous."

"She entered my mind without touching me. She made my own brain turn against my body." An Xin's voice cracked. "What do you call that?"

Xu Ming had no answer.

His phone buzzed. A text from an unknown number: *THE WEST LAKE PAGODA. MIDNIGHT. COME ALONE OR SHE DIES.*

He showed it to An Xin. Her face went gray.

"That's Fahai's number," she said. "I saw it in his file. He's been using burner phones since the 90s."

"His file? How do you have his file?"

An Xin closed her eyes. "I've been investigating him for six months, Xu Ming. Ever since my grandmother died. She was the one who told me about the snake spirits, about the war between heaven and earth that's been happening right under everyone's noses." She laughed bitterly. "I thought she was senile. I thought it was dementia talking. But then I found her journals. Photographs. Newspaper clippings going back to 1920. Women with snakes coiled in their hair. Men with golden eyes. A monk who never ages."

"You knew about Fahai before tonight?"

"I knew he existed. I didn't know he was real until he walked into your shop." She grabbed his wrist with bandaged hands. "Don't go to the pagoda. Whatever he wants, it's a trap. He doesn't negotiate. He doesn't show mercy. He just *removes* things."

Xu Ming pulled his hand free. "Bai Suyin is there."

"She's a snake, Xu Ming. A thousand-year-old snake who's been using you for her trial. You said it yourself—she needs you to love her. That's not romance. That's *survival*."

"Even if that's true, I can't let him kill her."

"Then you're a fool."

He stood up. "Maybe. But I'm her fool."

The walk to West Lake took forty minutes. The rain had stopped, leaving the streets slick and gleaming under streetlights. The pagoda rose from the island in the center of the lake like a black finger pointing at the moon. Xu Ming rented a boat from a confused old man who kept muttering about ghosts, and rowed across water that was too still, too dark, too *deep*.

He could feel something watching him from beneath the surface.

The pagoda's ground floor was empty. Dust and prayer scraps. A broken statue of Guanyin with her head replaced by a golden coin. The air smelled of incense, old blood, and something else—something cold and mineral, like a cave where snakes had nested for centuries.

"You came."

Fahai stood at the center of the room, his robes dry despite the humidity. His golden eyes reflected the moonlight like a cat's. Beside him, Bai Suyin knelt on the stone floor, her hands bound in front of her with prayer beads that seemed to pulse with a faint red light. Her face was bruised. One eye was swollen shut.

"Let her go," Xu Ming said.

"Let her go?" Fahai laughed. The sound echoed off the stone walls, multiplied by some acoustics that shouldn't exist. "I have spent eight hundred years hunting this creature. I have lost brothers to her venom. I have watched her devour villages and seduce kings and poison wells. You ask me to let her go as if she were a stray dog."

"She's not a demon."

"She is a serpent who has stolen human form. The difference is academic." Fahai walked around Bai Suyin, his fingers trailing across her shoulders. She flinched but didn't speak. "She told you about her trial, yes? The heart trial. Love a mortal, become human. Kill a mortal, lose your cultivation." He stopped, turned to face Xu Ming. "Did she tell you what happens if the mortal dies *before* she completes the trial?"

Xu Ming's blood went cold.

"I see she didn't." Fahai smiled. "If you die, Xu Ming, her cultivation shatters. All her power, all her centuries of work, gone in an instant. She becomes nothing more than a snake. An ordinary snake, cold and dumb, slithering through the mud until someone steps on her."

"That's why you want to kill me."

"No." Fahai's smile widened. "I want you to kill *her*."

He tossed something at Xu Ming's feet. A knife. Ancient bronze, covered in green patina, its edge still gleaming sharp. The handle was wrapped in dried leather.

"That blade was forged in the Tang Dynasty," Fahai said. "Tempered in the blood of a thousand snakes. One cut, and her spirit is severed from her body. One cut, and she dies permanently. No reincarnation. No rebirth. Just *nothing*."

Bai Suyin raised her head. Her good eye met Xu Ming's, and he saw something in it he'd never seen before: fear. Real, naked, animal fear. The same fear a mouse feels when the cat has it pinned.

"Don't," she whispered. "Xu Ming. Please."

"She's begging you now," Fahai said. "But she won't beg when she kills you. She won't hesitate when she drains your life force to complete her trial. She's been lying to you since the moment you met. Every word, every touch, every moment of tenderness—all calculated. All part of the game."

"She came to me for medicine."

"She came to you because you're weak. Because you're alone. Because no one would notice if you disappeared." Fahai crouched beside Bai Suyin, grabbed her chin, forced her to look at Xu Ming. "Tell him the truth, serpent. Tell him why you really chose him."

Bai Suyin's lips trembled. "I chose him because..."

"Because *what*?"

"Because he looked at me like I was human." Her voice broke. "The first time I walked into his shop, he asked if I was in pain. He offered me tea. He treated me like a *person*." Tears spilled down her cheeks. "Do you know how long it's been since anyone looked at me that way? A hundred years? Two hundred? Everyone else sees a demon, a monster, a thing to be feared or used. But Xu Ming saw *me*."

Fahai dropped her chin. "Lies. All lies."

"Then prove it," Xu Ming said.

Both of them turned to look at him.

He picked up the knife. The weight was strange—heavier than it should be, as if the blade was made of something denser than bronze. The handle fit his palm perfectly, like it had been waiting for him.

"If she's lying," Xu Ming said, "then let me test her. You said she needs me alive to complete her trial. You said she'll lose everything if I die." He raised the knife, pressed the blade against his own throat. The edge was so sharp it drew blood with barely any pressure. "If I kill myself right now, her cultivation shatters. She becomes nothing. You win."

"No!" Bai Suyin lunged forward, but the prayer beads flared red and slammed her back to the floor.

Fahai's golden eyes narrowed. "You would sacrifice yourself for a demon?"

"I would sacrifice myself for someone who made me feel like I mattered." Xu Ming pressed harder. Blood trickled down his neck, staining his collar. "But if you stop her before she can stop me, that proves she's only using me. That proves everything you've said is true."

"Xu Ming, don't." Bai Suyin's voice was raw. "Please. I don't care about the trial. I don't care about becoming human. I care about *you*."

"Then prove it."

He tightened his grip on the knife.

The blade bit deeper.

And Bai Suyin screamed.

It wasn't a human scream. It was raw and ancient and full of anguish that spanned centuries. The sound shattered the windows of the pagoda. The prayer beads around her wrists cracked. Red light exploded outward, and Fahai stumbled back, shielding his face.

Bai Suyin rose.

She was no longer pretending to be human. Her skin had gone pale as moonlight, scales rippling across her cheeks and hands. Her eyes were vertical slits, burning with white fire. The coils of her true form pressed against the walls of the pagoda, cracking the stone.

"Put the knife down," she said, and her voice was not one voice but a chorus, layered and ancient. "Put it down, or I will destroy this entire city to save you."

"See?" Fahai laughed from the corner. "See her true nature? Violence. Always violence. She cannot help herself."

Bai Suyin turned to face him. The air around her shimmered. "You took everything from me. My teacher. My mountain. My first life. My second. My third. You will not take this one."

"Then kill me, serpent." Fahai spread his arms. "Prove to your mortal that I am right. Prove that you are nothing but a beast wearing a human face."

Bai Suyin's hand shot out—

And stopped.

Her claws hovered an inch from Fahai's throat. Her breath came in ragged gasps. The white fire in her eyes flickered.

She was fighting herself.

Xu Ming saw it clearly. She wanted to kill Fahai. Every instinct in her thousand-year-old body screamed for his blood. But she was holding back. For him. To prove that she could be more than a monster.

"Xu Ming," she said, not looking away from Fahai. "Walk away. Go back to your shop. Forget you ever met me."

"And let him kill you?"

"He won't kill me. He can't. Not without the knife." Her voice dropped. "I am the White Snake of West Lake. I have survived dynasties. I will survive him too."

"You'll be alone."

"I have always been alone."

Fahai's hand moved. Faster than Xu Ming's eyes could track. He grabbed Bai Suyin's wrist, twisted, and slammed her to the ground. The pagoda shook. Dust

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Blood Price

The golden light from Fahai's eyes faded as quickly as it had appeared. He stood in the shattered doorway, water pooling around his sandaled feet, his robes untouched by the rain that still hammered the alley outside.

Xu Ming positioned himself between the monk and Bai Suyin. A futile gesture. He knew it. But his body moved before his mind could stop it.

"Step aside, pharmacist." Fahai's voice carried no anger. No threat. Just the absolute certainty of a man who had never been refused. "You have been deceived. The creature behind you is not what she appears."

"She's a patient," Xu Ming said. His voice cracked. He tried again. "This is a place of healing. Whatever dispute you have, take it outside."

"A healer." Fahai's lips curved. Not quite a smile. "You mix herbs for fevers. You wrap bandages around scraped knees. Do you truly believe your little bottles can protect you from what follows in her wake?"

Behind him, Xu Ming heard Bai Suyin's breathing. Shallow. Rapid. The sound of someone calculating odds that grew worse by the second.

"I have no quarrel with you," Fahai continued. He stepped forward. One step. Two. The splintered wood of the doorframe crunched beneath his feet. "Give her to me, and I will forget this night ever happened."

"And if I refuse?"

The golden light flickered in Fahai's eyes. "Then I will take her through you."

Xu Ming's hand found the jar of powdered ginseng on the counter. Not a weapon. But it was something to hold. Something solid in a world that had suddenly become liquid.

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice was barely a whisper. "Don't."

He turned. She had pressed herself against the back wall, her wet dress clinging to her frame. But it was her eyes that stopped him. They were no longer brown. They had shifted to something else—a deep, ancient gold that mirrored Fahai's own.

"If you fight him," she said, "he will kill you. Not because he wants to. Because you are in his way, and he has never let anything stand between him and his purpose."

"Then what do you suggest?" Xu Ming heard the edge in his own voice. "Surrender? Let him drag you back to whatever hell he's been keeping you from?"

"He cannot drag me anywhere." Her voice hardened. "I have survived eight hundred years of his hunting. I will survive this night too."

She stepped forward, moving past Xu Ming until she stood face to face with the abbot. They were the same height. Same posture. Two predators circling each other in a space too small for either of them.

"Fahai," she said. "We have done this dance before. In Suzhou. In Nanjing. On the slopes of Emei Shan where you burned my teacher alive." Her voice didn't waver. "Each time, you have failed. Each time, I have escaped. What makes you think tonight will be different?"

"Because tonight," Fahai said, "I have something I did not have before."

He reached into his robe and withdrew an object that caught the dim light of the shop's single bulb. A bell. Small, brass, ancient. Worn smooth by centuries of handling.

Xu Ming saw Bai Suyin's face change. For the first time since she'd walked into his shop, he saw fear in her eyes. Real fear.

"Where did you get that?"

"From your teacher." Fahai held the bell up. It did not ring. It did not move. But the air around it seemed to tremble. "Before she died, she tried to hide it. She failed."

"That bell belonged to my mother."

"No." Fahai shook his head slowly. "It belonged to the one who created your mother. The original white snake who first learned to walk as a human. She gave it to your teacher for safekeeping, and your teacher passed it to you before I caught up with her in Nanjing."

Xu Ming watched Bai Suyin's hands clench into fists. Her nails bit into her palms. Blood dripped onto the floor.

"You killed her."

"I purified her." Fahai's voice was gentle. "As I will purify you."

He raised the bell. And for the first time, he rang it.

The sound was not loud. It was not sharp. It was a single, clear note that seemed to hang in the air like smoke. But Xu Ming felt it in his bones. In his teeth. In the spaces between his thoughts.

Bai Suyin screamed.

She fell to her knees, hands pressed to her ears. Her body convulsed. Scales rippled across her skin—not the controlled transformation she had shown Xu Ming before, but something violent. Uncontrollable. Her form flickered between human and serpent, human and serpent, faster and faster until Xu Ming couldn't tell which was real.

"Stop!" Xu Ming lunged forward. He didn't have a plan. He didn't have a weapon. He just moved.

Fahai caught him with one hand. The abbot's grip was iron. His fingers closed around Xu Ming's throat and lifted him off the ground.

"You are a distraction," Fahai said, his voice still calm. "Nothing more. She chose you because you were weak. Because she thought she could control you. Because she thought your love would save her."

Xu Ming clawed at the monk's hand. His vision darkened at the edges.

"But love is not salvation." Fahai's eyes glowed brighter. "Love is the trap she set for herself. Every mortal she has ever loved, I have taken from her. Every life she has tried to build, I have burned to ash. Do you know why?"

Xu Ming couldn't breathe. Couldn't speak.

"Because I am the only one who understands what she truly is." Fahai leaned closer. His breath smelled of incense and old copper. "She is not a woman. She is not a spirit seeking enlightenment. She is a wound in the fabric of the world, and I am the surgeon who will cut her out."

He released Xu Ming's throat.

Xu Ming fell, gasping, his knees hitting the shattered wood. He sucked in air that tasted like blood and rain.

Bai Suyin had stopped screaming. She lay curled on the floor, her body still flickering between forms. Human one moment. Serpent the next. Her eyes were open, but they were not her eyes. They were the eyes of something ancient. Something that had been hunted for so long it had forgotten what it meant to be anything else.

"Now," Fahai said, turning back to her. "Shall I ring it again? Or will you come willingly?"

"Wait." Xu Ming's voice was hoarse. Broken. But he forced himself to stand. "Wait."

Fahai paused. He looked at Xu Ming with something that might have been curiosity.

"You love her."

It was not a question.

"Yes." The word came out before Xu Ming could think. Before he could weigh the consequences. "I love her."

He did not know when it had happened. Perhaps it was the moment she had walked into his shop, rain-soaked and desperate. Perhaps it was the night by the lake, when she had shown him her true face and he had not run. Perhaps it was now, watching her broken on the floor of his ruined pharmacy, knowing he would burn the world down to save her.

"Then you are a fool," Fahai said.

"No." Xu Ming reached into his pocket. His fingers closed around the small vial he had been carrying since the night she had first transformed. He had prepared it without knowing why. A mixture of herbs and minerals, ground fine and suspended in alcohol. He had told himself it was for research. For understanding.

He knew now what it was for.

"I am a pharmacist," Xu Ming said. "And I have been studying you."

Fahai's eyes narrowed. "Studying me?"

"You left a piece of yourself behind. Every temple you've visited. Every spirit you've purified." Xu Ming held up the vial. "Incense ash. Old blood. The residue of your mantras. I collected them. I analyzed them. I found the pattern."

"There is no pattern. I am pure."

"No one is pure." Xu Ming uncorked the vial. "Not even you."

He threw it.

The vial shattered against Fahai's chest. Liquid splashed across his robes. For a moment, nothing happened.

Then Fahai's skin began to smoke.

He looked down at his chest. At the place where the liquid had soaked through his robes. His expression was not pain. It was surprise. Utter, complete surprise.

"What," he said, "have you done?"

"I made a poison," Xu Ming said. "For snakes."

Fahai's head snapped up. His golden eyes blazed.

"I am not a snake."

"No," Xu Ming agreed. "But you have spent eight hundred years hunting them. Killing them. Absorbing their essence into your own cultivation." He stepped forward, his voice growing stronger. "You think I don't know what you are, Fahai? You think I haven't seen the scales beneath your skin? You are not a hunter. You are a cannibal. You have been devouring spirits for centuries, and their poison has become yours."

The smoke rose thicker. Fahai's skin began to crack. Not in lines of blood, but in patterns. Scales. Golden scales, pushing up from beneath his flesh.

"You think this will stop me?" His voice was no longer calm. It was a snarl. "You think a mortal's poison can touch me?"

"It already has."

Fahai looked down. The cracks were spreading. Running up his arms. Across his face. He was transforming, and it was not controlled. It was violent. Painful.

Bai Suyin stirred. Her eyes cleared. She looked at Fahai, then at Xu Ming, then at the vial's fragments on the floor.

"What did you do?" she whispered.

"I told you." Xu Ming's hands were shaking. "I studied him."

"No." She stood, unsteady, her body still flickering. "You couldn't have. He's been hidden for centuries. No one knows his true form."

"I didn't need to know his true form." Xu Ming looked at her. "I just needed to know what he was made of. And you told me."

"Told you?"

"When you said he burned incense. When you said he carried old blood." Xu Ming swallowed. "I traced the compounds. I found the pattern. The poison I made is specific to snake essence that has been corrupted by prolonged exposure to sanctified ash."

Bai Suyin stared at him. "You reversed-engineered a spirit poison from my descriptions?"

"I'm a pharmacist," Xu Ming said. "It's what I do."

Fahai was on his knees now. His robes had burned away, revealing a body that was no longer human. Scales covered every inch of him. His face had elongated. His teeth had sharpened. He was becoming something ancient. Something terrible.

But he was also dying.

"This is not over," he hissed. "I will return. I will always return. And when I do—"

"You won't remember." Xu Ming knelt beside him. "The poison doesn't just break down your body. It breaks down your cultivation. Every spirit you've consumed. Every memory you've stolen. It will all dissolve."

Fahai's eyes widened. For the first time, Xu Ming saw fear in them.

"You cannot."

"I already have."

The abbot's body began to dissolve. Not into flesh, but into light. Golden light, streaming upward like incense smoke. His form grew thinner. More transparent. Until all

title: The Weight of a Thousand Years
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. Too ordinary. The kind of eyes you'd pass on the street without a second glance. That was what made him terrifying.

Xu Ming stood between them, his body a fragile barrier. His pharmacy coat hung crooked on his shoulders. The smell of crushed herbs filled the air—broken bottles, scattered medicine, the sharp tang of ginseng and bitter root.

"You're wrong," Xu Ming said. His voice cracked but held. "She's not a demon."

Fahai's smile didn't waver. "You've known her for three days, pharmacist. I have hunted her for eight centuries. Tell me—which of us is more qualified to name what she is?"

"He's lying," Bai Suyin said from behind him. Her voice was strange. Hollow. Xu Ming turned and saw her pressed against the far wall, one hand over her mouth, the other gripping her own arm so hard her nails drew blood.

She was afraid.

The snake spirit who had casually shown him the edges of her true form, who had spoken of a thousand years of cultivation like it was a mild inconvenience—she was terrified.

"Interesting," Fahai said, stepping over the debris. His robes were soaked, hanging heavy, but he moved like a dancer. Weightless. Deadly. "She didn't tell you about the binding, did she?"

"What binding?"

"Don't." Bai Suyin's voice was a razor.

Fahai's smile widened. "Every spirit who seeks to become human must pass the Heart Trial. But there is a price for failure. A geas. A curse." He touched his own chest, right above his heart. "If she fails—if she cannot make a mortal love her truly before the next full moon—she doesn't just lose her chance at humanity. She loses everything."

"She already told me that."

"No, pharmacist. She told you she would lose her cultivation. She did not tell you what happens to the mortal who fails her."

Xu Ming's blood turned cold. "What happens to me?"

Fahai's golden eyes flickered. "You die."

The silence that followed was absolute.

Xu Ming could hear his own heartbeat. The rain still falling outside. The distant rumble of thunder moving away across the lake.

"Is that true?" he asked, not turning around.

"Yes." Her voice was barely a whisper.

"Why didn't you tell me?"

"Because you would have run."

"She's right about that," Fahai said pleasantly. "He still can. Walk away now, pharmacist. Leave this place. Go to the police. Tell them what you've seen. They won't believe you, but you'll be safe. The binding only activates if you're still with her when the moon dies."

"You're lying." Xu Ming's hands were shaking. "You came here to kill her. Why would you give me an escape route?"

Fahai's expression flickered. Something almost human passed across his face. "Because I am not a monster, Xu Ming. I am a protector. I have spent eight hundred years containing threats that would destroy the mortal world. But I do not kill innocents if I can help it."

He reached into his robes and pulled out a small wooden box. Aged. Worn smooth by centuries of handling.

"This is what I came to show you."

He opened the box.

Inside was a mirror.

Not glass—polished bronze, green with age. The surface rippled like water. Fahai tilted it toward Bai Suyin, and the reflection showed something that made Xu Ming's stomach turn.

She appeared in the mirror as a woman. But behind her—through her—was something massive. Coils upon coils of white scale, thicker than trees, wrapped around a core of ancient, patient darkness. Her true form, compressed into human shape.

"Look at yourself," Fahai said softly. "Really look. You've been lying to this mortal, Bai Suyin. You've been lying to yourself. You are not close to becoming human. You are still a beast wearing skin."

"Shut up." Her voice was venomous.

"Your Heart Trial is a farce. You chose this man not because you could love him, but because he was weak. Easy. A pharmacy owner with no connections, no family, no one who would miss him if he disappeared."

"That's not true."

"Isn't it?" Fahai turned the mirror toward Xu Ming.

He saw himself reflected. Normal. Tired. A man who had spent his whole life being overlooked.

And behind him, wrapped around his heart like a snake coiled around its prey, was a thread of pale white light.

"I'm marked," he whispered.

"Bound," Fahai corrected. "Her essence is inside you now. When she fails—and she will fail—that thread will tighten. It will squeeze your heart until it stops. You will die in your sleep, quietly, and no one will ever know why."

Bai Suyin lunged.

She moved faster than Xu Ming's eyes could track. One moment she was against the wall. The next she was in front of Fahai, her hand transformed into a claw, aimed at his throat.

He caught her wrist without looking.

"I have killed a thousand spirits," he said, his voice utterly calm. "You are not special, Bai Suyin. You are merely stubborn."

He twisted. She screamed.

The sound was not human. It was something older, something that had echoed through caves and forests before humans had learned to make fire. Xu Ming felt it in his bones.

"Stop!" he shouted.

Fahai paused. "I am saving your life."

"You're hurting her."

"She is a snake. She does not feel pain the way you do."

"I said stop."

Xu Ming picked up a broken bottle from the floor. Held it like a weapon. His hands were shaking so badly the glass rattled.

Fahai looked at him with something like pity. "You would fight me for her? After everything I've shown you?"

"I would fight you because you broke into my shop, threatened my—" He stopped. What was she? His patient? His friend? The woman who had kissed him by the lake and made him feel, for the first time in years, like he mattered?

"She's under my protection," he finished.

Fahai released Bai Suyin's wrist. She stumbled back, cradling her hand. The scales faded from her fingers, leaving human skin bruised and bleeding.

"You are a fool," Fahai said. "But an honorable one. I respect that." He closed the box, tucked it back into his robes. "I will give you until the full moon. Three weeks. If she fails, come find me at the Leifeng Pagoda. I will remove the binding before it kills you."

"And if she succeeds?"

"She won't."

He turned and walked out into the rain. The door hung open, swinging in the wind. By the time Xu Ming reached it, the street was empty.

He closed the door. Locked it. Leaned against it.

Bai Suyin was sitting on the floor among the broken glass and scattered herbs. She had pulled her knees to her chest. She looked smaller than he had ever seen her.

"That was not how I wanted tonight to go," she said.

"You lied to me."

"I omitted."

"Same thing."

She looked up at him, and for the first time, he saw cracks in her perfect composure. Exhaustion. Desperation. Something that might have been shame.

"You would have run," she said again.

"I might have."

"Exactly." She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Do you know how many mortals I have approached in the last hundred years? Twelve. Twelve chances to pass the Heart Trial. Twelve times I found someone I thought could love me."

"What happened to them?"

"Eight ran. Three tried to kill me when they found out what I was." She paused. "One succeeded."

Xu Ming's breath caught. "What?"

"I let him. It was easier than watching him hate me." She touched her chest, over her heart. "I died. I cultivated for another fifty years to rebuild my form. And then I started again."

"Why?"

"Because I want to be human." Her voice cracked. "I want to feel the sun on my skin without it burning. I want to taste food and know hunger. I want to grow old and die, Xu Ming. I want to end. A thousand years is too long to exist without meaning."

Xu Ming slid down the door until he was sitting across from her. The rain hammered against the windows. The shop smelled like blood and herbs and something electric—her power, bleeding through the cracks in her control.

"Three weeks," he said.

"Yes."

"That's not enough time."

"No."

"Then how are we supposed to do this?"

She looked at him. Her eyes were brown again, soft, human. But behind them, he saw the weight of centuries. The weight of hope crushed and reborn and crushed again.

"We lie," she said. "We pretend. We make him believe."

"Believe what?"

"That you love me." She reached out and took his hand. Her fingers were cold. "And I pretend to love you back."

"Is that what this is? Pretending?"

She didn't answer.

Xu Ming looked down at their hands. Her palm pressed against his. The thread around his heart pulsed once, warm, alive.

"If I'm going to die in three weeks anyway," he said slowly, "I want to know the truth."

"What truth?"

"The one you haven't told me yet." He met her eyes. "The real reason you chose me. Not because I was weak. Not because I was easy. Why me, Bai Suyin?"

She was silent for a long time.

The rain began to slow.

Outside, somewhere in the darkness, a bell began to toll. Deep. Slow. The sound traveled through the wet streets like a warning.

Bai Suyin's face went pale.

"That's not possible," she whispered.

"What?"

"He's not supposed to be able to—" She stood abruptly, pulling Xu Ming to his feet. "We have to leave. Now."

"What? Why?"

The bell tolled again. Closer.

"He's not alone," she said. Her eyes were wild. "Fahai brought the Temple Guard. They're already here."

The door behind Xu Ming exploded off its hinges.

Standing in the doorway was not Fahai.

It was a woman in white robes, her face covered by a veil of silk. Behind her, a dozen figures in identical robes, their heads bowed, their hands clasped in prayer.

"白素贞," the woman said. Her voice was soft. Feminine. Familiar.

Bai Suyin's entire body went rigid.

"Jade Sister," she breathed.

The woman raised her veil.

She had Bai Suyin's face.

Identical. Every line, every curve. The same eyes. The same mouth. The same elegant features that had made Xu Ming's heart stutter when she first walked into his shop.

"Did you really think," the woman said, "that you were the only snake who wanted to become human?"

The bell tolled a third time.

And the night became war.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The abbot's golden eyes fixed on Xu Ming with the weight of judgement.

"I have watched this city for three days," Fahai said, stepping over the shattered remains of the back door. His robes were dry now. The rain seemed to avoid him, sliding off an invisible barrier. "I smelled her before I saw her. A snake always leaves a trail."

Xu Ming positioned himself between Bai Suyin and the monk. His body moved before his mind caught up. "Get out of my shop."

"Brave. Foolish." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he rotated them. Each bead was carved bone. Human bone, Xu Ming realized with dawning horror. "She has woven illusions around you. Made you believe she is a woman in need of rescue. But I have seen what she truly is. I have been seeing it for eight hundred years."

"Eight hundred years of chasing a woman who just wants to be human." Xu Ming's voice cracked but held. "That sounds like obsession, not justice."

Something flickered in Fahai's golden eyes. Not anger. Recognition.

"You love her already." The monk's tone shifted, becoming almost pitying. "That is unfortunate. It means you will suffer more when I show you the truth."

He reached into his robes and withdrew a mirror. Bronze. Ancient. The surface rippled like water.

"Do you know what this is, pharmacist? It is a reflection-catcher. Forged during the Tang Dynasty by monks who understood that demons hide behind beautiful faces." He held it up. "Would you like to see what she really looks like?"

"Don't." Bai Suyin's voice was sharp with fear. "Xu Ming, don't look."

But Xu Ming was already turning.

The mirror caught Bai Suyin's reflection, and the image warped. Her face remained, but beneath it, layered like sediment, was something vast. Scales covered her skin in patterns that shifted and crawled. Her eyes split vertically, pupil becoming slit, iris bleeding to gold. And behind her, coiling through the reflected space of his tiny shop, was a serpent so massive it could have wrapped around the entire building three times over.

White scales. Each one the size of a dinner plate. A head that could swallow a man whole. Fangs like curved daggers.

"That is what she hides," Fahai said softly. "That is the beast that wears your lover's face."

Xu Ming's knees buckled. He grabbed the counter to steady himself.

"Xu Ming." Bai Suyin's voice was small. Human. "Please. Look at me. Not the mirror. *Me.*"

He turned. She stood there, wet hair plastered to her face, eyes human and brown and terrified. She looked like a woman who had just been stripped naked in public.

"Every word I told you was true," she said. "I am a snake. I have been a snake for a thousand years. But I am also this." She touched her chest. "This heart. These feelings. They are real."

"The beast learns to imitate humanity," Fahai said. "But it does not feel. She needs your love to complete her trial because she cannot generate it herself. You are a tool, pharmacist. A means to an end."

"Then why didn't she just leave after I kissed her?" Xu Ming's voice rose. "Why is she still here, bleeding in my shop, asking me to protect her?"

Fahai's smile faltered.

"Because the trial isn't complete," Bai Suyin whispered. "It can't be forced. I can't take your love and leave. It has to be given freely, and it has to be sustained. If I abandoned you now, the trial would fail. I would lose everything."

"She explains it so neatly." Fahai stepped closer. "But conveniently omits the cost. Do you know what happens when a snake spirit completes her trial, pharmacist? She becomes human. Truly human. Mortal. Vulnerable. And every ounce of her cultivation — a thousand years of power — burns away in the transformation."

Xu Ming frowned. "So she would lose her power?"

"Yes. She would become weak. Helpless." Fahai's eyes gleamed. "A perfect target for every enemy she has made over a millennium. And she has made many."

"Fahai is one of them," Bai Suyin said. "He fears me as a snake. But he would kill me without hesitation as a human."

"Not kill." Fahai's voice dropped to a whisper. "Capture. Study. Dissect. A snake spirit that has become human is a miracle of transformation. The secrets in her blood could advance spiritual cultivation by centuries. Do you think I am the only one who would want such a prize?"

Xu Ming's mind raced. The pieces clicked together with terrible clarity.

"You're not just a hunter," he said slowly. "You're a collector."

Fahai inclined his head. "I am a preserver of balance. Demons should remain demons. Humans should remain humans. She seeks to break the natural order, and I seek to correct it."

"You seek to profit from it."

The gold in Fahai's eyes flickered. For a moment, Xu Ming saw something ugly beneath the holy facade.

"Enough talk." The monk raised his hand, and the prayer beads glowed. "I will take the serpent now. You can return to your quiet life, pharmacist. Forget this ever happened."

"Or what?"

"Or I will show you what happens to those who harbor demons."

Bai Suyin stepped forward. Her hand found Xu Ming's and squeezed. Her skin was cold. Scale-cold.

"Let me fight him," she said quietly. "I can hold him off long enough for you to escape."

"He'll kill you."

"Better than letting him take me." Her eyes met his. "Xu Ming. If I fall—"

"You're not falling." He turned to face Fahai fully. "You said you need my love. That it gives you strength."

"Yes."

"Then take it."

He pulled her close and kissed her. Not gentle. Not tentative. A kiss that was a declaration, a promise, a weapon.

Bai Suyin gasped against his lips. He felt something shift in her body — a warmth spreading where before there had only been cold. Her fingers dug into his shoulders, and when they broke apart, her eyes were no longer brown.

They were silver. Pure silver, like mercury in moonlight.

"What—" Fahai's voice cracked. "That's impossible. The trial cannot advance without ritual completion."

"Then maybe your rules are wrong." Bai Suyin's voice had changed. Deeper. Resonant. It seemed to come from everywhere at once. "Maybe love isn't a transaction. Maybe it's a choice."

She raised her hand, and the air around her shimmered. Scales appeared on her arms, but they were different now — not the cold white of a beast, but the soft iridescence of pearl. Her hair lifted, dry despite the rain still falling through the broken door.

"You have hunted me for eight centuries," she said, her voice carrying the weight of a thousand years. "You have killed my teacher. Burned my home. Murdered everyone I have ever loved." Her silver eyes locked onto Fahai. "But you made one mistake."

"And what is that?"

"You made me stop running."

The shop exploded with light.

Not fire. Not magic in any way Xu Ming understood. It was presence — the sheer weight of a being that had stopped pretending to be small. Bai Suyin grew taller, her form flickering between human and serpent, and the walls of the shop began to crack.

Fahai raised his mirror, chanting in a language older than Mandarin. The bronze surface flared, trying to capture her, contain her.

The mirror shattered.

Shards of ancient bronze rained down like glass. Fahai stumbled backward, blood streaming from his nose. His golden eyes were wide with shock.

"You broke the reflection-catcher," he whispered. "That was blessed by seven generations of abbots."

"Blessings are just words." Bai Suyin stepped forward, and the floorboards splintered beneath her feet. "And words can be broken."

She reached for him, and Fahai moved faster than Xu Ming could track. He threw something — a pouch of powder that burst into white smoke. When it cleared, the back door was empty, and Fahai's voice echoed from outside.

"This isn't over, serpent. I know what you're becoming. And I know who you love."

The rain swallowed his footsteps.

Bai Suyin collapsed.

Xu Ming caught her before she hit the ground, cradling her against his chest. Her skin was burning hot. Her silver eyes were fading, flickering back to brown.

"What happened?" he asked. "What did you do?"

"I... borrowed your love." She laughed weakly. "Used it like fuel. I've never done that before. I didn't know it would work."

"Are you okay?"

"I'm exhausted." Her eyes fluttered closed. "I need to rest. Just... give me a minute."

"Bai Suyin."

"Mmm?"

"Your arm."

She looked down. The scales on her arms had not faded. They remained, iridescent and beautiful, but unmistakably *not human*.

"I can't change them back," she whispered, and for the first time, Xu Ming heard genuine fear in her voice. "I think... I think I used too much power. I think I'm stuck."

"Stuck how?"

She looked up at him, and her silver eyes held an ancient terror.

"I think I'm becoming the snake permanently."

The rain continued to fall through the broken door. Somewhere in the distance, a temple bell began to toll.

And Xu Ming held her tighter, knowing that Fahai would return, knowing that the hunt was far from over, and knowing that the woman in his arms was slipping away one scale at a time.

"Then we find a way to bring you back," he said.

"You don't understand." Her voice cracked. "There's no going back. Once the transformation starts, it doesn't stop. I'll lose myself. I'll become nothing but a beast."

"Then we change the rules."

"How?"

Xu Ming looked at the shattered mirror, the broken door, the city beyond that held a monk with golden eyes and a thousand years of hate.

"I don't know yet," he admitted. "But I'm not letting you go."

Bai Suyin's hand found his. Her fingers were longer than before. Stronger. Claws beginning to push through the nails.

"I love you," she said. "I don't know if it's real or if the trial made me say it. But I love you."

"It's real," Xu Ming said. "Because I love you too. And nothing — not Fahai, not the transformation, not a thousand years of anything — is going to change that."

Outside, the temple bell stopped ringing.

And in the silence that followed, they both heard a new sound.

Footsteps. Rhythmic. Measured.

Coming closer.

title: The Price of Her Heart
content:
The rain had stopped.

Xu Ming stood at the window of his ruined shop, watching dawn creep over the shattered rooftops of Hangzhou. The street below was empty—too empty. No vendors setting up their stalls. No early morning joggers. The kind of silence that meant people had been warned to stay away.

He hadn't slept. Couldn't sleep. Every time he closed his eyes, he saw Fahai's golden gaze burning through the dark.

Three days since the attack. Three days since Bai Suyin had dragged him through the back exit while Fahai's prayer beads turned to chains of fire, whipping through the shop like serpents of their own. They'd fled through alleyways, over walls, across rooftops—her hand clamped around his wrist, her body moving with a fluid grace that was not entirely human.

They'd ended up here. A safe house, she'd called it. An apartment above a closed tea shop in the old quarter, owned by a woman who never asked questions and never came upstairs.

Xu Ming turned from the window. Bai Suyin sat cross-legged on the floor in the center of the room, her eyes closed, her breathing slow and measured. Since the attack, she'd been weaker. The glow beneath her skin had dimmed. Sometimes, when she thought he wasn't looking, he caught her staring at her own hands as if they were strangers.

"It's been three days," he said.

"I am aware."

"He hasn't found us."

"He is waiting."

"For what?"

Her eyes opened. The irises flickered—human one moment, serpentine the next. "For me to make a mistake. For you to leave. For the inevitable."

Xu Ming walked over and sat down across from her. The floor was cold. The room smelled of dust and old tea leaves. "I'm not leaving."

"You should."

"I know."

A ghost of a smile touched her lips. It vanished quickly. "Fahai will not attack directly again. He underestimated me once. He will not make that error twice. The next time he comes, he will bring the full might of his order. Monks who have spent lifetimes training to kill spirits. Artifacts forged in the fires of ancient temples. He will burn this city to find me."

"Then we leave Hangzhou."

"Where would we go? He has hunted me across every province, every dynasty. I have hidden in mountains, in rivers, in the courts of emperors. He always finds me." Her voice dropped. "Because he knows what I am. And what I am leaves a trail that cannot be erased."

Xu Ming reached out and took her hand. Her skin was cold. Always cold. "Then we change what you are."

She looked at him sharply.

"The trial," he said. "You told me you need to love a mortal to complete your transformation. To become fully human. What happens if you succeed?"

For a long moment, she said nothing. Then she pulled her hand away. "You do not understand what you are offering."

"Then explain it to me."

"The trial is not a ritual. It is not a spell I can cast. It is a process that takes root in the soul. It requires—" She stopped. Her jaw tightened. "It requires me to give something in return."

"What?"

"My cultivation."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

"A thousand years of spiritual power," she continued, her voice barely above a whisper. "Every technique I have mastered. Every lifetime of memories. I must surrender them to become human. I would be reborn as a mortal woman—no powers, no immortality, no protection. I would age. I would die. I would be as fragile as you."

"And Fahai?"

"He would sense the moment I begin the transformation. It would draw him like blood in water. He would have one chance to destroy me—while I am caught between forms, neither snake nor human, utterly helpless."

Xu Ming's chest tightened. "How long does the transformation take?"

"Seven days. From the moment the heart trial is complete, I have seven days to shed my old self. During that time, I cannot fight. I cannot flee. I can only endure."

"Then we protect you for seven days."

"You cannot protect me from Fahai. You are a pharmacist."

"I know what I am." His voice came out harder than he intended. "I know I'm not a warrior. I know I can't fight monks with golden eyes and fire chains. But I can hide you. I can keep you safe. I can—" He stopped. Swallowed. "I can love you."

The words fell between them like a stone into still water.

Bai Suyin stared at him. Her expression was unreadable—a mask carved from centuries of survival. But something flickered behind her eyes. Something raw.

"You barely know me," she said.

"I know you came into my shop and asked for herbs that haven't been used in a thousand years. I know you saved my life when Fahai attacked. I know you could have killed me a dozen times over, but instead you held my hand and told me the truth." He leaned forward. "I know that when you smile, it changes your whole face. I know that you hum old songs when you think no one is listening. I know that you're terrified of becoming human because you've spent so long pretending to be one that you've forgotten what it actually means."

A single tear slid down her cheek. She wiped it away quickly, almost angrily. "You are a fool."

"Probably."

"A dangerous fool."

"The best kind."

She laughed. It was a broken sound, like glass cracking. "I have not laughed in three hundred years."

"Then I'm already doing something right."

She reached out and touched his face. Her fingers traced the line of his jaw, the curve of his cheek. Her touch was light, almost reverent. "If I do this—if I begin the transformation—there is no turning back. I will gain mortality. I will lose everything else. And if Fahai finds us before the seven days are complete, he will kill us both."

"I understand."

"No. You do not." Her hand dropped. "There is one more thing. A price I did not tell you about."

Xu Ming's stomach tightened. "What price?"

"The heart trial requires balance. I must love you—truly, completely—but you must love me in return. Not as a spirit. Not as a curiosity. As a woman." She paused. "If your love is not genuine, the trial fails. I am trapped between forms forever. Neither snake nor human. A ghost in my own skin."

"And if my love is genuine?"

"Then I become human." Her eyes searched his. "But you will never be the same. Loving me will change you. It will open your eyes to a world you cannot unsee. You will carry the weight of what I was, what I gave up. Every day, for the rest of your mortal life, you will remember that I was once something more."

"I can live with that."

"Can you?" She stood. Her movements were fluid, graceful, but there was a tension in her shoulders that hadn't been there before. "I have loved before, Xu Ming. In my thousand years, I have loved three times. Each time, it ended in blood. Each time, Fahai found them first."

She turned to face him fully. Her eyes were wet. "He does not just kill them. He makes me watch. He makes me hold their bodies as they grow cold. He tells me that this is the price of loving a demon. And then he lets me go, so I can carry the grief for another century."

Xu Ming stood. He crossed the distance between them in three steps. He took her face in his hands and pressed his forehead against hers.

"I am not them," he said.

"You do not know that."

"I know that I'm still here. That you told me the truth. That you trusted me." He pulled back just enough to meet her eyes. "Start the transformation."

"Xu Ming—"

"I'm not afraid."

"You should be."

"I'm not."

She closed her eyes. Her breath shuddered. And then, slowly, she nodded.

"Seven days," she whispered. "Starting now."

The air changed. Something shifted in the room—a pressure, a warmth, a scent of rain and flowers. Bai Suyin's skin began to glow, soft and white, like moonlight through clouds. Her form wavered, flickered, grew solid again.

When she opened her eyes, they were brown. Human brown.

And behind her, the faint outline of massive coils faded into nothing.

She swayed. Xu Ming caught her.

"It has begun," she breathed.

And then the apartment door exploded off its hinges.

Fahai stood in the doorway, but he was not alone. Behind him, a dozen figures in dark robes stood in perfect silence. Their eyes were not golden. They were empty. Hollow. Like dolls waiting for a puppeteer.

"You made a mistake, serpent," Fahai said. "I felt the transformation begin. I felt your power bleed away." His smile was cold and terrible. "You are weak now. Vulnerable. And I have brought my finest hunters."

He stepped forward. The robed figures moved with him, a single dark wave.

"Seven days," Fahai said. "But I will not give you seven hours."

Bai Suyin pressed herself against Xu Ming's chest. Her heart beat once. Twice. Fragile. Mortal.

And Xu Ming realized, with a clarity that cut through everything, that he had just promised to protect a woman who could no longer protect herself.

He looked at Fahai. Looked at the hunters. Looked at the woman trembling in his arms.

Then he reached into his pocket and pulled out a small vial—one of the herbs she had taught him to mix, back in the shop that now lay in ruins.

"Seven hours," Xu Ming said, uncorking the vial. "That's all I need."

He threw the vial at Fahai's feet.

The glass shattered. White smoke erupted—thick, blinding, carrying the scent of a thousand-year-old forest.

And Xu Ming grabbed Bai Suyin's hand and ran.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. Ordinary, except for the centuries of hatred swimming in their depths.

"I expected more resistance," he said, stepping over the shattered remains of Xu Ming's back door. Rain streamed through the opening, pooling on the floor. "The last pharmacist who sheltered her lasted three days before begging for death."

Xu Ming placed himself between Fahai and Bai Suyin. His legs shook. His voice cracked when he spoke. "Get out of my shop."

"Brave words for a man who mixes cough syrup for a living." Fahai's prayer beads clicked as he rotated them — black beads, carved from something that looked like bone. "Do you know what she is? What she's done?"

"I know what you are," Xu Ming said. "A hunter who calls himself holy."

Fahai laughed. The sound had no warmth. "She told you about her teacher? The one I killed?" He tilted his head, studying Bai Suyin like a collector examining a specimen. "Did she tell you why I killed him?"

"Don't," Bai Suyin whispered.

"Because he was teaching her to consume human souls." Fahai's voice dropped to something almost gentle. "Three hundred and forty-seven souls. That's how many she absorbed before I stopped her teacher. That's how much power she needed to climb from serpent to woman."

Xu Ming felt the floor shift beneath him. He turned to look at Bai Suyin.

Her face was stone. "It's not—"

"Not true?" Fahai produced a scroll from his robes, yellowed with age, sealed with red wax. He tossed it at Xu Ming's feet. "Names. Dates. Villages. The mountain pass where she and her master feasted on travelers for seven years before anyone noticed."

Xu Ming didn't pick up the scroll. He didn't need to. The look on Bai Suyin's face told him everything.

"I was different then," she said, her voice barely audible. "I was young. I followed my teacher. I didn't know—"

"You knew." Fahai's beads clicked faster. "You knew every time you tasted a soul. You knew the screams. You knew the faces of the people you consumed. And you continued."

"Why are you telling him this?" Bai Suyin's composure cracked. "To turn him against me? You could have killed me a dozen times tonight. You could have killed me in the street. Instead you come here, to his shop, to speak of old sins—"

"Because I want him to choose." Fahai smiled. "I want him to know exactly what he's protecting. Then I want him to hand you over himself."

"Never," Xu Ming said.

But Bai Suyin saw the hesitation. The way his hands trembled. The way he couldn't quite meet her eyes.

Fahai saw it too. "You have until dawn to decide, pharmacist. Bring her to Leifeng Pagoda, and I will spare your life. Hide her, and I will burn this city to find her. And you will watch." He turned and walked back into the rain, his robes dry despite the downpour, his footsteps silent on the wet concrete.

The door hung open. The rain kept falling.

Xu Ming stood frozen, staring at the scroll at his feet.

"Pick it up," Bai Suyin said.

"I don't want to."

"You need to." Her voice was hollow. "You need to know who I was. So you can decide who I am now."

He bent down. His fingers touched the ancient paper, and it crumbled slightly at the edges. He didn't open it. He held it in his hands like it might bite him.

"How many?" he asked.

"Three hundred and forty-seven. That's what he said."

"I'm asking you."

She closed her eyes. "Four hundred and twelve."

The number hit him like a physical blow. Four hundred and twelve souls. Four hundred and twelve lives, consumed so she could grow stronger. So she could become human.

"When did you stop?"

"The last one was eight hundred years ago." She opened her eyes. "I was trapped in a bottle for three hundred years after that. A monk sealed me in a jar and threw me into the West Lake. I floated at the bottom, unable to move, unable to die, listening to the boats pass overhead. That's where I learned to change."

"That's where you learned to pretend," Xu Ming said.

"Yes." She didn't deny it. "I learned to be human by watching humans. Their kindness. Their cruelty. Their capacity for love. I studied them like a scholar studies texts. And when I emerged, I swore I would never consume another soul. I would earn my humanity honestly."

"Can you?" Xu Ming's voice broke. "Can you earn back four hundred lives?"

"I don't know." Tears streamed down her face. "But I can try. I have been trying. For eight centuries, I have helped the sick. I have protected the weak. I have loved—" she choked on the word. "I have tried to love, truly, even when it meant watching those I love grow old and die while I remained."

"You've done this before."

"Yes. Other heart trials. Other mortals." She wiped her face with the back of her hand. "I failed every time. Because I couldn't let go of my nature. Because when they discovered what I was, they ran. Or they tried to kill me. Or Fahai found them first."

"And you expect me to be different?"

"I expect nothing." She stepped closer to him, her feet soundless on the wet floor. "I came to Hangzhou because I heard about a pharmacist who treated beggars for free. Who stayed open during festivals. Who cried when an elderly patient died of a cough he couldn't cure." She touched his face. "I came because I thought maybe, this time, I could find someone whose heart was pure enough to see past what I was."

Xu Ming's eyes burned. "You lied to me."

"I withheld the truth. There's a difference."

"Is there? You came here to use me. To complete your trial. To hide from Fahai."

"Yes." Her hand dropped from his face. "And now I am telling you everything. Because if you're going to betray me to Fahai, I want you to do it with full knowledge of who you're betraying."

The rain hammered against the roof. The shop's lights flickered.

Xu Ming looked at the scroll in his hands. Four hundred and twelve names. Four hundred and twelve lives sacrificed to power.

He thought about his grandmother, dying of cancer in a hospital bed across the city. He thought about the young couple who came to his shop last week, desperate for medicine for their sick child. He thought about the beggar who slept in the doorway, the one he gave free tea to every morning.

All of them could have been targets, eight hundred years ago. All of them could have been consumed by a serpent who wanted to be human.

And yet.

He looked at Bai Suyin. At the woman who had pressed his palm to her chest and let him feel her heart beat. At the woman who had shown him her true form, terrifying and beautiful. At the woman who was crying, not for herself, but for the lives she couldn't bring back.

"You said Fahai killed your teacher," Xu Ming said slowly. "Why?"

"Because my teacher refused to stop. After I left, after I swore off soul consumption, my teacher continued. He found new students. He taught them the old ways. Fahai tracked him to a mountain temple and burned it to the ground with everyone inside."

"Everyone?"

"My teacher. His students. Twenty-seven monks who had been taken hostage." Bai Suyin's voice hardened. "Fahai didn't check. He didn't care. He burned them all because he believed the ends justified the means."

"Like you believed consuming souls justified becoming human."

"Yes." She met his eyes. "We are both monsters, Xu Ming. The difference is that I know I am one. Fahai believes he is holy."

Xu Ming set the scroll down on his counter. He looked at his hands — hands that had mixed herbs, that had held the dying, that had never killed anything larger than a mosquito.

"Four hundred and twelve," he said.

"Yes."

"That's not nothing."

"I know."

"Can you become human? Truly human? With all the guilt, all the regret, all the weight of what you've done?"

Bai Suyin's expression shifted. For the first time since Fahai's arrival, something like hope flickered in her eyes. "That's what the heart trial is. To love someone so completely that their humanity becomes yours. To share their mortality. To die when they die."

"To suffer for eternity."

"To feel." She touched her chest. "To truly feel, for the first time in a thousand years."

The rain began to slow. The sky outside was still dark, but somewhere, beyond the clouds, dawn was approaching.

"I have conditions," Xu Ming said.

"Name them."

"First, you never consume another soul. Not even to save yourself. Not even if Fahai is about to kill you."

"Agreed."

"Second, you tell me everything. No more secrets. No more half-truths. If I'm going to die for you, I want to know what I'm dying for."

Her lips curved into something that was almost a smile. "And third?"

Xu Ming picked up the scroll. He held it over a candle flame. The ancient paper caught fire, curling inward, the names turning to ash.

"Third," he said, watching the fire consume the evidence, "you help me destroy Fahai. Not because I want revenge. Because people like him, people who think they're holy, they don't stop. They never stop."

Bai Suyin stared at him. "You would kill a monk for me?"

"I would kill a monster for the woman I love." The words came out before he could stop them. He felt his face flush. "Or. The woman I'm beginning to—"

She kissed him. Her lips were cold, like winter rain, but her heart hammered against his chest, faster than any human heart should beat.

"You are a fool," she whispered against his mouth.

"I know."

"A beautiful fool."

"That too."

She pulled back, her eyes glowing faintly in the dark. "Fahai will come at dawn. He will bring his relics. His blessed chains. He will try to trap me in a bottle again."

"Then we won't be here." Xu Ming grabbed his coat. "I know a place. My grandmother's house, in the countryside. No one knows about it. Not even my cousins."

"He will track my essence."

"Then we'll mask it." Xu Ming grabbed bottles from his shelves — herbs, powders, things he'd never used because they were too dangerous. "I've been a pharmacist for ten years. I know how to make a person disappear."

"Not a person," Bai Suyin said. "A snake spirit with a thousand years of cultivation."

"Then I'll learn."

The rain stopped. The silence that followed was absolute.

And in that silence, they heard it — the sound of chanting, distant but growing closer. Monks. Many monks. Their voices rising in a hymn that made the air vibrate.

Fahai had not come alone.

Bai Suyin's face went pale. "He brought the monastery. The entire monastery."

"How many?"

"If he brought the full order, at least two hundred monks. Each one trained in binding rituals. Each one armed with blessed weapons."

Xu Ming's heart dropped. Two hundred monks. His tiny shop. His grandmother's house in the countryside.

They would never make it.
