title: The Blood on His Hands
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving only the cold, patient gaze of a man who had been hunting the same prey for eight centuries. His robes dripped rainwater onto the shattered floorboards. His prayer beads clicked once, twice, three times—each sound like a nail being driven into wood.

"You have thirty seconds to step away from the demon," Fahai said. His voice carried no anger. No threat. Just the flat certainty of a man who had already decided how this night would end.

Xu Ming's hand was still pressed against Bai Suyin's chest. Her heart hammered beneath his palm, a wild drumbeat that told him she was terrified—despite her thousand years of cultivation, despite the power that coiled beneath her human skin, she was afraid of this man.

"No," Xu Ming said.

The word surprised him. It came from somewhere deeper than thought, somewhere primal and stubborn. He had spent his entire life avoiding confrontation. He lowered his head when customers shouted. He apologized when he was right. He had never once stood his ground against anything more threatening than a speeding ticket.

But this. *This* was different.

Fahai's eyebrows rose a fraction of an inch. "You would protect a snake?"

"She hasn't hurt anyone."

"She will." Fahai stepped forward. His bare feet made no sound on the broken glass. "They always do. It is not cruelty that drives them—it is nature. A snake cannot become human any more than fire can become water. The very attempt is an abomination."

"I've heard enough speeches," Bai Suyin said. Her voice had changed. It was deeper now, layered with something that made the air vibrate. She stepped in front of Xu Ming, placing herself between him and Fahai. "Eight hundred years of the same words. *Abomination. Demon. Unnatural.* You murdered my teacher for the crime of wanting to feel sunlight on her skin."

"Your teacher was a serpent who devoured seventy-three mortals before I found her."

"Because you starved her! You trapped her in a cave for forty years with no way to feed except—"

"Enough." Fahai raised his hand. The prayer beads began to glow, each bead lighting up like a miniature sun. "I did not come here to debate history. I came to finish what should have been completed in the Song Dynasty."

He spoke a single syllable. It was not Chinese. It was something older, a sound that seemed to crawl into Xu Ming's skull and set his teeth on edge.

Bai Suyin screamed.

She collapsed to her knees, clutching her head. Her skin rippled—scales appearing and disappearing in waves, her eyes flickering between human brown and serpent gold. The presence behind her, the massive shape that had haunted Xu Ming's nightmares for the past three days, surged forward and was violently yanked back.

"You see?" Fahai said softly. "The sutra binds her. Every lifetime, I have carved these words into her soul. No matter where she runs, no matter what face she wears, I can always find her. I can always *hurt* her."

Xu Ming's body moved before his mind caught up.

He grabbed a mortar from the counter—heavy stone, worn smooth by decades of grinding herbs—and threw it at Fahai's head.

The monk caught it without looking. His fingers closed around the stone and crushed it to dust.

"Foolish," Fahai said. "You are a mortal. Fragile. Temporary. Your entire life is less than a blink to creatures like her. Why would you throw it away for a demon?"

"Because she asked for my help."

"And that is enough?"

"No." Xu Ming stepped forward, his legs shaking. "But she also apologized. She said she was sorry for lying. And she didn't have to say that. She could have taken what she wanted and left. But she stayed."

Fahai studied him for a long moment. Something shifted in his golden eyes—not pity, but a kind of clinical curiosity.

"You love her already."

It was not a question.

"I don't know what I feel," Xu Ming said honestly. "But I know what I *don't* feel. I don't feel afraid of her. Not anymore. And I don't feel like she deserves to die for something she hasn't done yet."

"A beautiful sentiment." Fahai's smile returned, colder than before. "Shall I show you what she has already done?"

He reached into his robe and withdrew a folded piece of paper. It was yellowed, ancient, covered in characters that seemed to writhe when Xu Ming tried to read them.

"This is a record from the year 1247. A village in Fujian. Sixty-three people died in a single night. Their bodies were found with two small puncture wounds on their throats. No blood remained in their veins."

"That wasn't me," Bai Suyin said from the floor. Her voice was weak, strained. "I was in Sichuan that year. I have witnesses."

"Your witnesses were spirits. They would say anything to protect you."

"Then why does the record not name me? If you were so certain, why did you not write my name?"

Fahai's smile flickered.

"Because you did not have a name then," he said quietly. "You were just a white snake with no cultivation, no soul worth recording. I did not hunt you because you were dangerous. I hunted you because you had *potential* to be dangerous. And I was right."

He turned back to Xu Ming.

"Every century, she grows stronger. Every century, she comes closer to completing her transformation. And when she does—when she finally becomes fully human—she will no longer be bound by the laws that restrain spirits. She will be able to kill without leaving a trace. She will be able to walk among mortals as an equal, and no one will ever know what she truly is."

"Unless you stop her."

"Yes." Fahai's voice dropped to a whisper. "Unless I stop her. But I am old, pharmacist. Older than this city. Older than this country. I have killed hundreds of spirits, and each death takes something from me. When I am gone, there will be no one left to hold the line."

He looked at Bai Suyin with something that might have been sorrow.

"I cannot kill her tonight. I came here to warn you. To show you what she is before it is too late."

"Then what do you want?"

Fahai's eyes met his. For just a moment, the golden light faded, and Xu Ming saw something human underneath—tired, broken, desperate.

"I want you to help me complete her trial."

The words hung in the air like poison.

"No," Bai Suyin said. She struggled to her feet, her body trembling. "You cannot. You *cannot*."

"The trial requires true love," Fahai continued, ignoring her. "If the mortal who is her heart trial rejects her—truly, completely, with full understanding of what she is—the trial fails. She loses all her cultivation. She becomes a common snake again, with no memory of what she was."

"You want me to break her heart."

"I want you to save her soul." Fahai stepped closer. "I have pursued her for eight hundred years because I believed death was the only mercy I could offer. But I was wrong. There is another way. A gentler way. If she loses her cultivation, she can live out her natural life as a serpent. No more running. No more hunting. No more fear."

"And she will forget everything," Xu Ming said. "She will forget who she was. She will forget me."

"Yes."

Bai Suyin grabbed his arm. Her fingers were cold, her grip frantic.

"Xu Ming. Listen to me. If I lose my cultivation, I lose everything I am. Every memory. Every lesson. Every life I have lived. I will become a blank slate—a baby snake with no past, no soul, no *self*. That is not mercy. That is annihilation."

"The alternative is death," Fahai said flatly. "I have given you eight hundred years of chances. This is the last one. Choose."

Xu Ming looked at Bai Suyin. Her face was streaked with tears, her eyes wild with fear. She had shown him her true form. She had trusted him with the truth. And now she was asking him to save her—not from death, but from oblivion.

"What if I refuse?" he asked.

Fahai's expression hardened.

"Then in three days, I will return with the full force of my order. We will seal this building. We will burn every herb, every record, every trace of her existence. And we will drag her back to the mountain where she was born and chain her to the earth until her body turns to stone."

"You would do that?"

"I have done it before." Fahai turned toward the shattered door. "Three days, pharmacist. Use them wisely."

He stepped through the wreckage and vanished into the rain.

The shop fell silent.

Bai Suyin collapsed against Xu Ming, her entire body shaking. He caught her, held her, felt her heart racing against his chest.

"I'm sorry," she whispered. "I'm so sorry. I shouldn't have come here. I shouldn't have dragged you into this."

"Stop."

"But—"

"Stop apologizing." He tightened his arms around her. "I'm not letting him take you."

"Then what do we do?"

Xu Ming looked around his ruined shop. The broken door. The shattered bottles. The scattered herbs that had once been his entire world, now reduced to debris. His life had been small and safe and predictable.

And then Bai Suyin had walked in.

"Three days," he said slowly. "He's giving us three days."

"That's not enough time to—"

"It's enough." He pulled back, meeting her eyes. "You said you need me to love you. Truly. For the trial to work."

"Yes."

"Then I need to know everything." His voice was steady now, calm in a way that surprised him. "No more secrets. No more half-truths. Tell me about your teacher. Tell me about the village in Fujian. Tell me about every life you've lived, every person you've loved, every sin you've committed."

"Why?"

He took her hand. Her fingers were warm now, human, fragile.

"Because if I'm going to love you," he said, "I need to know who I'm loving."

Bai Suyin stared at him for a long moment. Then she laughed—a broken, desperate sound that was half sob.

"You are either the bravest man I have ever met," she said, "or the most foolish."

"Probably both."

She leaned forward and pressed her forehead against his.

"Tomorrow," she said. "I will tell you everything tomorrow. But tonight..."

"Tonight?"

"Tonight I just want to be here. With you. Before everything changes."

The rain continued to fall outside. The broken door let in the cold night air. Somewhere in the distance, Xu Ming heard the sound of temple bells, carried on the wind.

Three days.

He held her tighter and tried not to think about what he would have to do if love was not enough.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, leaving them ordinary brown again. But the threat remained, hanging in the air like smoke after a fire.

Xu Ming stepped forward, placing himself between the abbot and Bai Suyin. "She's not a demon."

"Your faith is touching." Fahai's smile didn't reach his eyes. "And your ignorance is dangerous. Do you know what she is? What she has done?"

"I know what she's told me."

"And that is enough?" Fahai laughed, a hollow sound that echoed off the broken shelves. "Ask her about the scholar. The one in the Tang Dynasty. Ask her about the village in Jiangxi. Ask her about the hundred lives she has consumed."

Bai Suyin's voice cut through the darkness. "You dare speak of consumption? You, who burned a forest with three hundred spirits inside?"

"The forest was infected. They were corrupted."

"They were my family."

The air between them crackled with tension. Xu Ming felt it pressing against his skin, making the hair on his arms stand up. He had seen spirits before — minor ones, lingering in the herbal compounds he prepared. But this was different. This was a war between ancient powers, and he was standing in the middle of it.

"Leave," Fahai said, turning to Xu Ming. "Walk away from this shop. Go back to your quiet life. I will cleanse this place, and you will remember nothing."

"You can't do that."

"I can do much more than that." Fahai reached into his robe and pulled out a small bronze bell. It was old — ancient, really — covered in verdigris and characters Xu Ming couldn't read. "This bell has called a thousand spirits to judgment. Do not make me add yours to the count."

Xu Ming's pharmacy training kicked in. He looked at the bell, at the way Fahai held it, at the subtle vibration in the air around it. "That's made from copper and tin. Standard temple alloy. But the characters are Sanskrit — you've bound it with mantras."

Fahai's eyes narrowed. "You know mantras?"

"I know herbs. I know metals. I know that your bell has a frequency that disrupts spiritual energy." Xu Ming reached behind the counter and pulled out a small pouch. "And I know that ground magnetite, when dispersed in the right concentration, can interfere with that frequency."

He threw the pouch. It burst open in mid-air, releasing a cloud of black dust that settled over Fahai's robe. The bell in his hand flickered, its glow dimming.

Bai Suyin moved.

She crossed the distance in less than a heartbeat. Her hand closed around Fahai's wrist, and she twisted. The bell clattered to the ground. She kicked it away, sending it skittering under a shelf.

Fahai didn't flinch. He looked at her with something like pity. "You think that will stop me? I have hunted you across eight centuries. You have run through twenty lifetimes. And still, you do not learn."

"I have learned one thing." Bai Suyin's voice was cold. "I have learned that you are afraid."

"Afraid of what?"

"Of change. Of spirits becoming human. Of a world where the old rules don't apply anymore." She released his wrist and stepped back. "That's why you hunt me. Not because I'm dangerous. Because I represent something you cannot control."

Fahai's composure cracked. For just a moment, Xu Ming saw something beneath the surface — anger, yes, but also something else. Something that looked almost like grief.

"You know nothing of what I fear."

"I know you loved a woman once. A human woman." Bai Suyin's voice softened. "I know she died. I know you blame spirits for her death."

Fahai went still. His entire body became a statue, frozen in the dim light of the shop.

"That was not your story to tell."

"You made it my story when you came for me." Bai Suyin's eyes glistened. "You made it my story when you burned the mountain. When you killed my teacher. When you chased me through every life I tried to build. Your grief became my cage."

Xu Ming watched the exchange with growing horror. This was not a simple battle of good versus evil. This was centuries of pain, layered like sediment, each generation building on the last.

"You killed her." Fahai's voice cracked. "You killed her with your presence. She was pure, and you contaminated her with your... your nature."

"I never touched her."

"You existed. That was enough." Fahai's hand went to his chest, where a small jade pendant hung. "She saw you. She spoke of you. She wanted to help spirits like you. And it killed her."

Bai Suyin's face crumpled. "Lianhua. That was her name."

"Don't."

"Lianhua, who fed stray cats. Who prayed at every temple. Who believed that all living things deserved compassion." Bai Suyin's tears fell freely now. "She was the kindest human I ever met. And she died of fever — not because of me, but because the medicine she needed was too expensive, and the doctors wouldn't treat a poor woman."

Fahai's hand trembled on the pendant. "You could have saved her."

"I was young. I was weak. I had not yet mastered healing arts."

"You could have tried."

"I did try." Bai Suyin's voice broke. "I tried everything. I sat by her bed for three days. I poured my qi into her body. I begged the mountain spirits for help. But she was human, and humans die. That is the rule I cannot break."

The silence that followed was absolute. Even the rain seemed to pause, holding its breath.

Then Fahai laughed.

It was not a kind laugh. It was bitter and broken, the sound of something old and rotten cracking open. "She lies. She always lies. Everything that comes from a serpent's mouth is poison."

Xu Ming stepped forward. "She's not lying."

"How would you know?"

"Because I can see the truth in her eyes." Xu Ming's voice was steady. "And I can see the truth in yours. You're not here to save anyone. You're here because you couldn't save her, and you need someone to blame."

Fahai's face twisted. "You know nothing."

"I know you're in pain." Xu Ming walked toward him, hands open and empty. "I know you've been carrying this for a long time. And I know that killing Bai Suyin won't bring Lianhua back."

"Don't lecture me, boy."

"I'm not lecturing. I'm offering." Xu Ming reached into his pocket and pulled out a small envelope. "This is a mixture of ginseng, astragalus, and lotus seed. It won't bring back the dead. But it will calm your heart. Ease your mind. Give you space to think clearly."

Fahai stared at the envelope. For a moment, Xu Ming thought he might take it.

Then the abbot's hand shot out and grabbed Xu Ming's throat.

"Your kindness is a weakness." Fahai's grip tightened. "She has infected you. You are already lost."

Bai Suyin screamed. She lunged forward, but Fahai raised his other hand and a barrier of golden light slammed into her, throwing her across the room. She crashed into the shelves, sending herbs and bottles flying.

"Let him go," she gasped, blood trickling from her lip.

"Or what? You'll show me your true form?" Fahai tightened his grip. Xu Ming struggled, his vision starting to darken. "Do it. Reveal yourself. Let the entire city see what you are. Let the police come. Let the news cameras arrive. Let the world know that the serpent walks among them."

Bai Suyin's eyes flickered. For a moment, Xu Ming saw something in them — calculation, desperation, and then... fear.

She was afraid.

Not of Fahai. Not of death.

She was afraid of losing him.

"I can't," she whispered. "If I reveal myself here, he dies. The energy would kill him."

"Then choose." Fahai's voice was ice. "Save yourself, or save him."

Xu Ming's vision was going dark. He could feel his pulse weakening, his lungs burning for air. But even as the world faded, he saw Bai Suyin's face.

She was crying.

And she was reaching for him.

"I'm sorry," she whispered. "I'm so sorry."

Then she turned and ran.

The back door slammed open. Rain poured in. And Bai Suyin disappeared into the night, leaving Xu Ming in Fahai's grip.

The abbot released him. Xu Ming collapsed to the floor, gasping, coughing, his throat burning.

"Interesting." Fahai looked down at him. "She chose to run. She chose her own survival over yours." He shook his head. "That is the nature of serpents. They cannot love. They can only consume."

Xu Ming looked up at him, breathing hard. "You're wrong."

"Am I?"

"She ran because she was protecting me." Xu Ming's voice was raw. "You said it yourself — her true form would kill me. She ran to save my life."

Fahai's expression flickered. For just a moment, Xu Ming saw doubt in those golden eyes.

Then it was gone.

"It does not matter. She has revealed her weakness." Fahai turned toward the door. "She cares for you. That means she will come back. And when she does..."

He looked over his shoulder, his smile cold and final.

"I will be waiting."

The abbot stepped into the rain and vanished.

Xu Ming lay on the broken floor of his shop, tasting blood and copper and failure. His phone buzzed in his pocket.

He pulled it out.

A text from an unknown number:

*She will come to the Broken Bridge at midnight. If you want to save her, bring the jade pendant from Fahai's neck. If you want to live, stay home.*

Xu Ming stared at the message.

Outside, the rain continued to fall.

And somewhere in the darkness, Bai Suyin was alone.

title: The Price of Scales
content:
The golden light faded from Fahai's eyes, but the afterimage burned in Xu Ming's vision like a brand. He stood between Bai Suyin and the abbot, his pharmacist's hands raised in a gesture that meant nothing against a man who had killed spirits for centuries.

"You're wrong about her," Xu Ming said, his voice steadier than he felt. "She hasn't harmed anyone since she came here."

Fahai's smile didn't waver. "The snake does not stop being a snake because it coils in a garden. It only waits for the moment to strike." He stepped forward, his wet robes leaving dark footprints on the wooden floor. "Step aside, pharmacist. This does not concern you."

"It concerns me when you break my door."

"Then send me a bill." Fahai's hand moved, and the prayer beads around his wrist began to glow. Each bead pulsed with trapped light, and Xu Ming heard whispers — voices trapped inside, begging, weeping, cursing. "I will reimburse you for the damage. But the serpent comes with me."

"No."

The word surprised even Xu Ming. Bai Suyin's hand found his shoulder, her fingers cold even through his shirt.

"Xu Ming," she whispered. "You can't fight him. He'll kill you."

"Then we run."

"Where?" Her laugh was bitter. "I've been running for eight hundred years. He always finds me."

Fahai watched them with the patience of a predator who knew its prey had nowhere left to flee. "The girl shows wisdom. Your little pharmacy, your mortal life — it means nothing against what I carry." He raised his hand, and the beads spun, forming a circle of light. "I have bound a hundred spirits in these beads. Nine dragons. Twelve phoenixes. The ghost-queen of the Southern Marsh. Your teacher, the white crane of Mount Emei."

Xu Ming felt Bai Suyin flinch.

"You killed my teacher," she said, her voice dropping low, cold, old.

"Your teacher chose to protect a demon. I chose to uphold the law of heaven." Fahai's gold eyes fixed on her. "The beads remember every scream. Every plea. Every promise of reform that turned out to be a lie." He took another step. "You will join them, 白素贞, as you should have a thousand years ago."

Xu Ming moved before he thought. He grabbed a jar from the shelf — dried angelica root, nothing dangerous — and threw it at Fahai's face. The abbot didn't flinch. The jar shattered against an invisible barrier an inch from his skin, the fragments suspended in midair before falling like brown snow.

"Impressive reflexes," Fahai said. "Futile, but impressive."

He snapped his fingers.

The beads exploded with light.

Xu Ming felt it like a physical blow — the pressure of a thousand years of suppressed rage and grief, the collected suffering of every spirit Fahai had ever captured. It slammed into his chest, knocked the breath from his lungs, sent him crashing into the counter. Bottles shattered. Herbs scattered across the floor. His head hit the edge of the sink, and blood ran warm down his temple.

"Xu Ming!" Bai Suyin's voice was distant, underwater.

He forced his eyes open.

She had changed.

Not fully — not into the massive coiled presence he'd glimpsed before — but her skin had taken on a shimmer, scales rising along her cheekbones, her fingers elongating into claws. She stood between him and Fahai, and she was *angry*.

"Touch him again," she said, her voice layered with something that wasn't human, "and I will forget every year of cultivation. I will become the monster you claim I am. I will show Hangzhou what a thousand-year serpent looks like when she stops holding back."

Fahai's smile widened. "Finally. The mask falls."

"You wanted this." Bai Suyin's claws extended. "You've always wanted this. To prove that I'm no different from the others."

"You aren't different. You never were." Fahai raised his hand, and the beads began to spin faster, faster, the whispers becoming screams. "The only difference between you and every other snake I've bound is that you've been luckier. Better at hiding. Better at lying to yourself about what you are."

"I know what I am."

"Do you?" Fahai's eyes locked onto hers. "Then tell me, 白素贞 — how many mortals have you killed?"

The question hit like a blade.

"I have never—"

"How many?"

Bai Suyin's scales flickered. Her claws retracted slightly.

"I have cultivated for a thousand years without taking a single life."

"That's not what I asked." Fahai stepped closer. The beads hummed. "I asked how many you have killed. Not how many you have consumed. Not how many have died because of your presence. I asked about your hands, your fangs, your venom."

"None."

"Liar."

The word cracked through the shop like thunder. Fahai's hand shot out, and the beads wrapped around his fist, glowing with terrible light. He pointed at Xu Ming.

"Ask her," he said. "Ask her about the Year of the Snake. Ask her about the village of Green Bamboo. Ask her about the man she loved before you."

Xu Ming's blood ran cold. He looked at Bai Suyin. Her face had gone pale, the scales retreating completely, leaving her looking young and frightened and terribly exposed.

"Suyin?"

She wouldn't meet his eyes.

"Tell me you've never killed anyone," Xu Ming said. "Look at me and tell me."

"I..." Her voice cracked. "It's not that simple."

"Tell me."

"Xu Ming, please. There are things you don't understand. Things I've done that—"

"Tell me."

The silence stretched. The rain hammered against the roof. Fahai stood watching, his gold eyes glittering with satisfaction.

Bai Suyin's shoulders sagged.

"One," she whispered. "One man. One hundred and twenty years ago, in a village called Green Bamboo." Her hands were shaking. "He was a hunter. He trapped me. Kept me in a cage for three months, cutting pieces of me for medicine, for profit. He thought I was just a snake. A big one, but just an animal."

Xu Ming's stomach turned.

"When I finally escaped," Bai Suyin continued, "I was weak. Half-dead. I had spent centuries cultivating my human form, and he had stripped it from me, piece by piece. I couldn't think. Couldn't reason. I was just... pain. And rage." Her voice dropped to barely a whisper. "I found him in his bed. I killed him. I ate his heart while it was still beating."

Fahai laughed. "There. The truth. The serpent's nature, revealed at last."

"It was one man," Xu Ming said. "He tortured her. He deserved—"

"Deserved?" Fahai's voice rose. "Who are you to decide what a man deserves? You, a mortal, who has never seen what these creatures do when they stop pretending? She killed one man, yes. But her teacher killed twenty-three before I bound her. The nine dragons I carry destroyed three cities before they fell. The ghost-queen slaughtered an entire monastery." He stepped forward, his presence filling the room. "Every spirit I have ever hunted started with one kill. One justification. One mortal who 'deserved it.' And then another. And another. Until the blood runs like rivers and the land is poisoned with their hunger."

"That's not me," Bai Suyin said, but her voice wavered.

"Then prove it." Fahai spread his arms. "Let me bind you. Let me carry you for a hundred years as I have carried the others. If you are truly different, you will survive the beads. You will emerge purified, your demon nature burned away." His smile was ice. "Or you will join the screams. Either way, the world is safer."

"No." Xu Ming pushed himself to his feet, ignoring the blood running down his face. "You're not taking her."

"Xu Ming, stop." Bai Suyin grabbed his arm. "You can't—"

"I don't care what she did." He faced Fahai, his body trembling, his voice raw. "I don't care about a hundred years ago, or eight hundred years ago, or whatever other sins you've dug up. I know who she is *now*. I know what she's trying to become."

"You know nothing."

"I know she cried when she saw an old woman fall in the street. I know she spends hours at the pharmacy helping people she's never met. I know she chose to love me, a boring pharmacist, when she could have found anyone in the world." Xu Ming's hands were shaking, but his voice was steady. "That's not a demon. That's a woman trying to be human."

Fahai's expression flickered — something like pity, something like contempt.

"Foolish," he said. "You've known her for two weeks. I've hunted her for eight centuries. Do you truly believe your judgment surpasses mine?"

"I believe you're wrong."

"Then you believe a lie." Fahai raised his hand, and the beads began to glow again. "But I have no more time for debate. The serpent comes with me, and you will forget this night ever happened."

He snapped his fingers.

The light expanded.

Xu Ming felt it — a force trying to burrow into his mind, trying to find memories and erase them, trying to rewrite the past two weeks as a dream. He fought it, but it was like fighting the tide. His thoughts blurred. His knees buckled.

Then Bai Suyin screamed.

Not in fear. In fury.

The light shattered.

Xu Ming saw her transform — not the partial scales she'd shown before, but something deeper, something that broke the boundaries of the shop. Her body elongated, her skin becoming pure white scales that gleamed like moonlight. Her eyes turned vertical, slit-pupiled, ancient. Her voice became a chorus of whispers.

"You will not take his memory of me."

Fahai stumbled back, his beads flickering. "You're stronger than I thought."

"I have spent a thousand years becoming something you cannot understand." The serpent's voice filled the room. "I have loved. I have grieved. I have made mistakes and paid for them and made more mistakes and paid for those too. And I will not let you take the one person who sees me as I truly am."

"Then you will both die."

Fahai's hand went to his robe, and he pulled out something Xu Ming had never seen — a small golden bell, cracked and ancient, wrapped in red silk.

Bai Suyin's eyes went wide.

"No," she breathed. "You found it. You actually found it."

"The Leiyin Bell." Fahai's smile was triumphant. "Forged from the thunder that struck down the first demon king. Lost for three thousand years." He held it up, and even Xu Ming could feel the power radiating from it — a pressure that made his bones ache, his teeth vibrate. "I spent fifty years searching for this. I killed seventeen spirits to claim it. And now, 白素贞, I will use it to end you."

He rang the bell.

The sound was not a sound. It was a force. It hit Xu Ming like a wall, throwing him across the room, through the back wall, into the alley behind the shop. Wood splintered. Rain soaked him. His ears rang with a tone that felt like death.

He pushed himself up, coughing blood.
